facts: Anovion Technologies announced its first large-scale expansion of manufacturing capacity for premium synthetic graphite anode materials will be in Decatur County, Southwest Georgia.
The project represents an $800 million investment for the new manufacturing facility.
The new facility will span 1,500,000 square feet.
The facility is expected to produce more than 44,000 tons per year of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries once fully operational.
The project will create hundreds of high-quality local clean energy jobs in the surrounding region.

Anovion chose the Decatur County location due to its proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources, short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, a highly valuable skilled workforce, and a business-friendly environment.

Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, stated that the company conducted a thorough search to identify a location addressing energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs to ensure an ecosystem for producing high-quality and sustainable synthetic graphite anode material.
Eric Stopka noted that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will allow Anovion to further reduce its carbon footprint.

In October 2022, Anovion was awarded a grant totaling $117 million from the US Department of Energy under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to help secure the domestic battery manufacturing supply chain.
The new expansion facility will be the first of this scale in North America and is considered a crucial first step to reshoring synthetic graphite anode production.
Anovion will utilize its innovative, sustainable, and technologically advanced manufacturing process at the new facility.
The manufacturing process will rely on the region’s rapidly decarbonizing energy mix to power operations.
The facility will introduce Anovion’s proprietary furnace technologies, which substantially reduce greenhouse gas emissions and lay out a roadmap for achieving a carbon-neutral footprint over time.

Chip Dunn, Executive Chairman of Anovion, stated the company's long-term strategic vision is to focus on growth and innovation, positioning Anovion as a leader in the US energy transition and securing a domestic source for critical materials required for US lithium-ion battery production.

Chip Dunn noted that from continued research and development in electrochemistry to technology improvements in production processes and climate tech initiatives, the goal is to become carbon-neutral over time, mitigate greenhouse gas emissions, and position Anovion as a future climate tech leader.
In addition to the Georgia expansion, Anovion is expanding its Anovion Center of Excellence and its hydro-powered sister manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls, NY.
The Niagara Falls facility is notably one of the only qualified US sources of battery-grade synthetic graphite that is commercially shipping product today.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago and is a US-owned and US-based advanced materials business.
Anovion is currently one of America’s first commercial-scale manufacturers of premium, cleaner anode-grade synthetic graphite across its existing footprint near Niagara Falls, NY, and Clarksburg, WV.
Anovion began commercial production in early 2021.
Anovion is among a limited number of graphite anode producers in America to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.
source_title: Anovian to Bring $800M Manufacturing Facility to SW Georgia

facts: The Oyakhire Lab studies and controls ion and electron transfer at solid-liquid interfaces in electrochemical systems.
Interfacial charge-transfer processes determine the macroscopic performance of batteries, electrocatalytic cells, and related technologies.
Using insights from charge transfer measurements, the lab designs next-generation energy storage and conversion devices.
The lab utilizes hypothesis-informed materials such as thin films and small molecules using tunable methods.
Research focuses on ion- and electron-transfer mechanisms to determine how these materials influence device properties such as efficiency.
Material design is performed recursively using mechanistic insights to optimize performance.
The lab received an ACS Sustainable Futures Early Career Postdoctoral-Faculty Bridge Grant on 02/27/2026.
PhD students Luke Cravey and Gene Koifman passed their PhD qualifying exams on 01/13/2026.
The lab received a Schmidt Science Fellows Catalyst Grant on 12/01/2025.
Dr. Oyakhire welcomed his first set of PhD students, Gene Koifman and Luke Cravey, on 10/01/2025.
The lab was founded in 2025.
Dr. Oyakhire started the lab at Georgia Tech on 08/01/2025.
The lab studies and controls charge transfer at solid-liquid interfaces to advance batteries, electrocatalysis, and electrochemical separations.
During charging, lithium- and sodium-metal batteries undergo electrically driven metal deposition and growth.
Electrodeposition plays a central role in battery performance and safety but remains poorly understood.
Using well-defined thin-film substrates, the lab elucidates key steps in metal growth within batteries to enable long-lasting, stable batteries.
Recycling minerals from waste is essential for environmental stewardship and equitable resource distribution.
Conventional approaches such as chemical leaching, hydrometallurgy, and pyrometallurgy are limited in selectivity and efficiency.
The lab exploits precise control over electron transfer and the tunability of electrochemical free energy to recover valuable resources from complex waste mixtures.
The electric double layer (EDL), solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), and cathode–electrolyte interphase (CEI) mediate dynamic charge-transfer processes in batteries and electrocatalytic devices.
These interfaces have historically been measured using ex-situ, post-mortem techniques.
The lab develops operando spectroscopy and acoustic sensing methods to provide real-time insights into interface evolution.
These methods guide the design of passivation layers that enhance battery cycle life.
In liquid cells, ion transport and electrode passivation are governed by the properties of small molecules.
The vast design space of small molecules necessitates artificial-intelligence tools to identify optimal electrolytes.
The lab develops Hypothesis-Informed, Physics-coupled Artificial Intelligence (HIP-AI) to design new molecules for batteries and resource-recovery cells.
HIP-AI enables discovery of new molecules through filtering and generative capabilities.
HIP-AI provides interpretability via physics-coupled algorithms.
HIP-AI delivers experimentally actionable outputs by linking experimental design parameters to HIP-AI features.
Solomon Oyakhire is the Principal Investigator and an Assistant Professor of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering at Georgia Tech.
Dr. Oyakhire earned his BSc in Chemical Engineering from the University of Lagos, Nigeria, graduating with highest honors as the department's best graduating student.
He completed his PhD in Chemical Engineering at Stanford University working on interfacial electrochemistry with Stacey Bent and Yi Cui as a Knight-Hennessy scholar.
During postdoctoral training at UC Berkeley, he developed artificial intelligence algorithms for high dimensional datasets with Kristin Persson and Gerbrand Ceder as a Schmidt Science Fellow.
PhD student Gene Koifman's undergrad institution is Florida Institute of Technology.
PhD student Luke Cravey's undergrad institution is Auburn University.
The lab is actively recruiting PhD students and postdocs, welcoming applicants from a wide range of scientific backgrounds due to its interdisciplinary nature.
source_title: Oyakhire Lab | Georgia Institute of Technology

facts: On Wednesday, Gov. Brian Kemp announced Hyundai Mobis will invest $926 million into an EV power electric system plant.
Hyundai Mobis is known as one of the world’s biggest auto suppliers.
The investment will add at least 1,500 jobs to the Bryan County area.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “When we celebrated the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, we knew it would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that entire region of our state,”
The plant is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis to build EV power system plant in Bryan County Georgia

facts: The Hyundai electric vehicles plant being built near Savannah in Bryan County has attracted another automotive parts supplier.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the attraction of the new supplier on Tuesday.
Seohan Auto Georgia will invest more than $72 million in a new manufacturing facility.
The new manufacturing facility is located in nearby Liberty County.
The facility will create at least 180 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the Hyundai Metaplant is and will continue to be an economic driver for the entire region.
Kemp noted the plant is creating more jobs for hardworking Georgians in communities like Liberty County.
Kemp welcomed Seohan as the latest supplier in a growing list to invest in Georgia.
Kemp expressed anticipation for the continued growth of this fast-emerging sector.
Seohan Auto Georgia is a Tier-1 automotive parts supplier for Hyundai Motor Group America.
Seohan Auto Georgia is based in South Korea.
The company has production facilities around the world, including multiple facilities in the United States.
Jung Kee Koo, Seohan Auto Georgia’s CEO, stated that sustained growth of the EV market over the past few years suggests accelerated changes to the automotive market are inevitable.
Koo stated that Seohan believes Georgia will be the center of the EV industry.
Koo noted Georgia will be a new frontier for Seohan’s future with limitless opportunities and potential.
Seohan’s new facility in Georgia is scheduled to begin production late next year.
The new manufacturing facility will produce H/shafts, axles, and brake systems.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America broke ground on the Bryan County plant last October.
The Hyundai plant is projected to provide $5.5 billion in direct economic impact to the coastal region.
The economic impact of offsite suppliers announced to date has reached nearly $2 billion.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked in partnership with the Liberty County Industrial Development Authority, the Georgia Ports Authority, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program to land the Seohan project.
source_title: Hyundai EV plant lures new parts supplier to Savannah area | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: Beretta Holding S.A., through its subsidiary Norma Precision Inc., will expand its footprint in Georgia and build a new manufacturing, assembly, and distribution facility in Bryan County.
The $60 million project will create 600 new jobs for which the company is currently hiring.
Governor Kemp stated that it is a testament to Georgia’s strong workforce and economy that companies continually choose to further invest and expand their operations in the No. 1 state for business.
Governor Kemp noted that Norma Precision is already hiring hardworking Georgians on the coast and the new facility will support healthy communities across the Savannah area.
Norma Precision was formerly a U.S. subsidiary of Ammotec Inc. and was recently acquired by Beretta Holding S.A., one of the world’s oldest industrial groups.
Norma Precision will continue to develop and produce high-end ammunition to serve key markets in hunting, sporting goods, optics, military, and law enforcement sectors.

Dr. Pietro Gussalli Beretta, President and CEO of Beretta Holding S.A., stated that Norma Precision manufactured ammunition in the United States for 12 years as RUAG Ammotec USA and experienced steady growth over the past four years.

Dr. Pietro Gussalli Beretta expressed confidence that the long-term partnership with Georgia will be mutually beneficial while focusing on the Norma ammunition brand to better serve customers and industry partners in the United States and abroad.

Paul Lemke, CEO of Norma Precision, stated that the new facility offers a significant opportunity to expand small arms ammunition manufacturing and distribution operations to meet customer expectations in the United States and abroad.

Paul Lemke thanked Bryan County, Chatham County, local communities, and the State of Georgia for their support and expressed excitement about hiring, training, and transferring staff to manufacture, sell, and distribute Norma world-class ammunition and trade goods.
Norma Precision’s new facility will be 300,000 square feet in size.
The facility will be located at Interstate Centre III in Bryan County, which is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certified site.
To earn GRAD certification, a property must undergo due diligence and complete specific certification requirements prior to a visit by a prospective business.
Following completion of the new facility, operations from the Chatham County location will be moved to Bryan County.
Current employees at the Chatham County facility will have the option to transfer to the new facility.
The company is currently hiring for multiple positions and experience levels in manufacturing, distribution, HR, marketing, and sales.
Jon Seagraves, Development Authority of Bryan County Chairman, expressed excitement about Norma Precision locating a new manufacturing facility in Interstate Centre III.
Jon Seagraves noted that Norma Precision is backed by the nearly 500 years of experience and tradition that comes with Beretta Holding.

Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority, welcomed Norma Precision’s growth in the region and highlighted the state’s broad logistics network and ability to reach major domestic and global markets efficiently.

Fernanda Kirchner, Senior Project Manager, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on this project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, the State of Georgia Europe Office, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that projects of this size benefit entire regions of the state through hiring and partnering with local suppliers.
Pat Wilson noted that Bryan and Chatham counties worked together to create an innovative solution to secure Norma Precision’s new jobs for Georgians in the region.
source_title: Norma Precision Inc to Create 600 Jobs, Invest $60 Million in Bryan County - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is constructing a $5.54 billion electric vehicle plant.
The plant site covers nearly 3,000 acres of Bryan County soil, located 30 miles west of Savannah along I-16.
The factory will be 17 million square feet, which is nine times the size of the Mall of Georgia.
Construction is months ahead of schedule according to Pat Wilson, Georgia’s top economic development official.
Hyundai officials are targeting the first half of 2025 for production start.
Production could begin as early as October 2024.
The plant will produce battery-powered Genesis, Hyundai, and Kia vehicles.
Supplier LG Energy Solution is investing $2.1 billion in a battery cell manufacturing facility.
Supplier SK On is investing $2.6 billion in a battery cell manufacturing facility.
Supplier Hyundai Mobis is investing $750 million in an automotive components plant.
Kia is investing $900 million in an EV parts plant.
The project will create 25,000 jobs directly and indirectly.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated the project positions Georgia as a global leader in battery and EV manufacturing.
Pat Wilson noted Hyundai is building an entire ecosystem for suppliers and workforce development.
Construction crews are working around the clock to meet the accelerated timeline.
The site includes infrastructure for logistics, warehousing, and supplier parks.
source_title: Hyundai’s $5B EV plant is ahead of schedule. So are its suppliers

facts: 475 people were detained in an ICE raid on Thursday, September 4 at a factory manufacturing batteries for Hyundai and Kia vehicles.
The facility is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
Multiple federal agencies, including ICE, FBI, and DEA, conducted the judicially authorized enforcement operation.
Steven Schrank, DHS Special Agent in charge of Investigations for Georgia, stated that authorities are actively conducting an investigation into unlawful employment practices.
The $4.3 billion plant is a joint venture between South Korean auto giant Hyundai Motor and battery manufacturer LG Energy Solution (LGES).
Each company holds a 50% stake in the plant, which will supply batteries for Hyundai and Kia vehicles.
The facility is part of Hyundai's broader $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
South Korean media identified 300 of the detainees as South Korean nationals, with some reports citing a higher figure of 560 total detainees.
South Korea’s foreign ministry expressed concern about the impact of the raid on its citizens and companies investing in the United States.
Lee Jae-woong, a spokesperson for South Korea’s foreign ministry, stated that the economic activities of Korean companies in the US must not be unduly violated during US law enforcement operations.
The raid immediately stopped construction at the facility.
Governor Brian Kemp had previously hailed the project as a huge boost for the state's economy and the biggest industrial investment in Georgia's history.
Social media video footage showed officers informing workers they had a search warrant for the whole site and needed all work to cease immediately.
The Atlanta office of the ATF confirmed at least 475 people were arrested.
An ICE official at a Friday press conference stated that a majority of the 475 arrested were Korean nationals.
Detainees are being held at a facility in Folkston, Georgia, until the agency decides their next destination.
The raid is seen as a setback to Hyundai's substantial investment in the US and a dramatic iteration of the Trump administration’s crackdown on immigration.
Observers note latent impact on the inflow of foreign investment due to the administration's immigration stance.
The battery production facility was originally scheduled to start operations at the end of this year according to LGES.
In 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced the $4.3 billion venture.
Hyundai-GA battery company announced full cooperation with authorities and confirmed construction work is paused.
Hyundai Motor stated that its production of electric vehicles at other sprawling sites was not affected by the raid.
The project was announced in March amidst a ramping up of tariffs by the Trump administration.
source_title: 475 people detained in ICE raid on Hyundai, LGES  EV battery factory in Georgia on Thursday – KonnieMoments

facts: HMGMA and Kia America celebrated the production of the Kia Sportage Hybrid in Ellabell, GA on June 2, 2026.
HMGMA welcomed a new fire truck with a traditional wet-down ceremony to strengthen on-site emergency response in Ellabell, GA.
Savannah Technical College and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America commemorated a landmark apprenticeship partnership with an official signing ceremony in Ellabell, GA.
An EV lab sponsored by HMGMA opened at Effingham College & Career Academy in Rincon, GA.
HMGMA donated $100,000 to establish a scholarship at Georgia Southern University in Statesboro, GA.
Hyundai Motor Company issued a statement regarding an immigration enforcement action that took place at a supplier facility in Ellabell, GA.
Chris Susock, Chief Manufacturing Officer of Hyundai Motor North America, stated that HMGMA is extremely thankful for Oscar's leadership, which laid the foundation for the future of the Metaplant.
HMGMA invested $150,000 in United Way to serve coastal Georgia families in Ellabell, GA.
Hyundai will reveal the all-new 2026 Palisade at the New York Auto International Auto Show at 10:30 a.m. on Wednesday, April 16, while HMGMA will not be producing the Palisade.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) hosted its grand opening celebration as part of Hyundai Motor Group's commitment to and investment in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Company announced it has successfully completed a pilot project for Private 5G (P-5G) RedCap (Reduced Capability) technology in partnership with Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.

facts: Hyundai announced a $5.5 billion investment in facilities dedicated to manufacturing EVs and batteries in Savannah, Georgia.
Hyundai will receive an additional $1 billion investment from its suppliers towards the Savannah project.
The Savannah facility will be Hyundai’s first EV-only plant in the US.
Construction at the 2,923-acre Savannah site is expected to begin in early 2023.
Production at the Savannah site is expected to start in the first half of 2025.
The Savannah facilities aim to produce 300,000 vehicles per year, equivalent to 25,000 per month.
The Savannah project will create over 8,000 new jobs.
Stellantis plans to invest over $2.5 billion in partnership with Samsung SDI to build its first US EV battery manufacturing facility in Kokomo, Indiana.
Tesla signed an agreement with Panasonic in 2014 for a US$1.5–2 billion investment in Gigafactory 1, Tesla’s first factory dedicated to producing lithium-ion batteries for EVs.
Ford announced a $11.4 billion investment to bring EVs at scale to American customers.
Ford signed a non-binding MOU with SK On Co., Ltd. and Koç Holding to create one of the largest commercial vehicle battery production sites in Europe.
Toyota announced plans to build a $1.3 billion US battery plant for EVs.
Toyota will spend $3.4 billion on battery investments in the US up until 2030.
GM is investing $6.6 billion on EV plants.
GM created Ultium, its next-generation batteries and EV platform and technologies.
GM estimates that cells from its Ultium platform will be capable of a range of up to 724km or more on a full charge with 0-96kmh acceleration in three seconds.
Mercedes-Benz invested in Sila Nanotechnologies in 2019.
Mercedes-Benz announced a breakthrough with high silicon automotive batteries.
Mercedes-Benz plans to provide the option of equipping the upcoming electric Mercedes-Benz G-Class with next-generation battery cells with silicon anodes starting mid-decade.
The average transaction price for an EV stands at $56,437.
Batteries currently account for around 30% of the final cost for the customer.
Essential battery materials including lithium, nickel and cobalt face supply shortages due to supply chain disruptions and ongoing geopolitical conflicts.
The Tesla Model S has an 85 kWh battery pack that weighs 540 kg and contains 7,104 lithium-ion battery cells divided into 16 modules wired in series.
Addionics’ technology loads more active material into the battery, allowing a longer driving range and reduction of charging time thanks to lower internal resistance.
Addionics’ process is low-cost, drop-in ready, and can seamlessly be added to any manufacturing process.
Addionics could enable the adoption of emerging battery chemistries such as silicon and solid state, and even the next generation of LFP battery chemistry.
source_title: 3 Reasons Why Automotive OEMs Have to Become Battery Makers - Addionics

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that TEKLAS, a Turkish advanced research and development, manufacturing company and supplier of electric vehicle (EV) parts, will invest $6.5 million in opening their first North American facility and headquarters in Gordon County.
The project will deliver 120 jobs to Calhoun.
Founded in Turkey in 1971, TEKLAS designs, develops, and manufactures fluid circulation systems for cooling and heating applications.
TEKLAS produces hoses and pipes from rubber, plastic, and metal for the global automotive industry.
The company uses highly innovative and automated manufacturing processes using advanced water molding technology.
TEKLAS USA will include assembly lines to serve the most prominent original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) in the industry.
Operations at the new facility are scheduled to begin in the spring of 2021.
TEKLAS is headquartered in Turkey and maintains locations in Bulgaria, Serbia, Mexico, and China, along with sales offices in Germany and France.
Regional Director Arcan Ergur stated that the Calhoun location will help the company be closer to its main customers.
The 200,000-square-foot facility and headquarters will be located at 320 South Industrial Boulevard in Calhoun, Georgia.
TEKLAS will hire for positions at a variety of skill levels, including advanced manufacturing, management, and supervision.
Individuals interested in careers with TEKLAS are encouraged to visit the company website for additional information.
Larry Roye, chairman of the Development Authority of Gordon County, applauded state and local partners who helped encourage the company to come to Calhoun.

Gordon County Chamber of Commerce President and CEO Kathy Johnson commended TEKLAS for their research and advancements in electric vehicle manufacturing and contribution to the expansion of the automotive sector of the community's manufacturing base.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Project Manager Fernanda Kirchner represented the Global Commerce division on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Economic Development Authority of Gordon County and Georgia Power.
The Center of Innovation for Logistics, a division of GDEcD, provided freight analysis to support the project and company planning.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that TEKLAS choosing to establish their North American headquarters and build their very first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility in Calhoun exemplifies Georgia's efforts to attract and grow jobs of the future.

Wilson noted that as the automotive industry continues to transform toward electric vehicles and e-mobility, Georgia will work to foster an ecosystem of manufacturers and suppliers on the leading edge of the industry’s transition.
source_title: Electric Vehicle Manufacturer TEKLAS to Open North American Headquarters in Georgia, Create 120 Jobs in Calhoun - Augusta CEO

facts: On May 20, Bojangles debuted its first ever on-site EV charging station.
The initial charging station is located at a single store in Savannah, Georgia.
Bojangles positions itself as an ultra-enthusiastic booster of the "charge-and-dine" model among the nation's leading quick service restaurant chains.
Cracker Barrel, Waffle House, and almost every other leading QSR chain have already launched new EV charging initiatives.

In a press statement, Bojangles explained that the new charging hub signals its evolution beyond traditional quick-service dining and positions the brand as a forward-thinking player at the intersection of mobility and hospitality.
The company declared it is stepping forward as a category leader by transforming charging time into an opportunity for comfort, convenience, and craveable Southern flavor.

Bojangles Chief Information Officer Richard Del Valle stated that with EV charging, time becomes an asset and they are turning the stop into a chance to relax, refuel, and enjoy a true Bojangles experience.
Del Valle emphasized that the initiative is about more than charging vehicles and is redefining the stop along the way.
Bojangles declared that the war on EVs is over by integrating EV infrastructure into its footprint.
The company asserted it is supporting broader adoption of electric vehicles and reshaping expectations for what a quick-service restaurant can be.
Bojangles elaborated that as consumers prioritize efficiency and sustainability, the brand is leaning into hospitality and quality to meet those needs in new ways.
The Southeastern US has proven itself to be EV-friendly territory despite its red-state political environment.
EV sales took a steep downward turn in the Southeast and elsewhere after President Donald Trump and his Republican allies in Congress terminated the $7,500 EV tax credit last September.
The Southeast Alliance for Clean Energy (SACE) anticipated that EV sales in the region would recover despite the sales drop.
SACE noted in its March 2026 update that while sales fell significantly in Q4, data from December already showed early signs of a shift upward toward higher sales figures.
Car-friendly businesses continue to bank on the ability of on-site charging to attract more traffic to their stores.

New-bought EVs will not vanish simply due to a change in federal tax policy, and the widespread availability of used EVs could factor into continued demand for convenient, public EV charging stations.
EV-only startups Slate Auto and Rivian are moving forward on plans for launching new EVs into the market later this year into 2027.
Last month, Rivian nailed down a $4.5 billion loan guarantee from the Trump administration earmarked for constructing its new factory in Georgia.
Bojangles chose a store in Savannah, Georgia to highlight its new EV charging campaign rather than tapping a store in its home town of Charlotte, North Carolina.
In the May 20 announcement, Bojangles affirmed that the Savannah charging station is just the first of a series.
The company currently counts 870 owned and franchised sites on its roster covering 23 states, including 351 stores in North Carolina.
Bojangles' EV charging management partner is XLR8 America, a California-based startup.

In June of 2025, XLR8 announced a new branch of operations consisting of an EV charging infrastructure management service for commercial real estate owners, portfolio managers, municipalities, and others.
XLR8 explained that this service directly addresses the significant operational burdens, unexpected costs, and often low returns experienced by owners managing these assets in-house.
XLR8 currently counts Lincoln Electric, Autel Energy, Atom Power, DG Matrix, EVoke Systems, and Kennedy Power Systems on its partnership roster.
XLR8 CEO Frank O’Connor indicated that the initial Bojangles installation in Savannah represents the tip of the EV charging iceberg.
O’Connor enthused that when a driver pulls in for a Bo-Berry Biscuit and their battery tops off while they dine, it is the charge-and-dine experience made real.
He added that together they are building a national network that makes EV charging effortless and every stop genuinely worth making.
The charging equipment itself comes from the Florida firm Energy and Environmental Design Services (EEDS), which specializes in commercial properties.
EEDS has its eye on new opportunities to install EV chargers at convenience stores, with a particular focus on independent stores through its new “C-Store United” initiative.

The C-Store United Program has been developed to provide support to the independent C-Store owner looking to enter the EV charging market while maintaining their branding, customer data, and a flexible ownership stake.
EEDS notes that it is on a mission to help C-store clients “go on the offensive.”
source_title: EV Charging Meets Chicken Rippers At Bojangles

facts: Ecoplastic Corporation will spend approximately $205 million on a new manufacturing facility in southeast Georgia to supply components to Hyundai Motor Group’s electric vehicle campus.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the investment on Thursday.
Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai and Kia.
The South Korea-based manufacturer produces automotive interior and exterior plastic parts.
The company selected Bulloch County, Georgia, to house its facility.
The facility will be located neighboring Hyundai’s incoming $5.5 billion EV campus.
The new plant will have injection molding and printing capabilities.
The plant will produce parts including bumpers, garnishes, consoles, and trims for EVs.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Ecoplastic has decades of experience as a supplier for Kia and Hyundai.
Wilson noted that officials are building the supplier network for the Hyundai metaplant in Bryan County and are excited to see suppliers spread out across the region.
Hyundai’s facility broke ground in October.
Hyundai’s facility has already attracted more than $1 billion in investments from offsite suppliers flocking to Georgia.
In November, Joon Georgia became the first confirmed supplier for the Hyundai facility.
Joon Georgia plans to invest $317 million in a production facility in Bulloch County.
In late November, Gov. Kemp announced Hyundai Mobis as the second confirmed supplier for the Hyundai facility.
Rivian announced in 2021 that it would spend $5 billion to construct its largest EV production facility in East Augusta, Georgia.
Suppliers including Solvay Specialty Polymers and Denkai America have announced plans to set up shop near Rivian’s East Augusta site to supply key battery parts.
source_title: Third Hyundai supplier sets up factory near upcoming EV plant | Manufacturing Dive

facts: The development of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant just over the county line in Bryan County is having a profound effect in the region, with Bulloch County seeing the most impact aside from Bryan County.
Three Tier-one suppliers to the Hyundai plant are starting construction of facilities in southeast Bulloch near I-16, pacing to match or beat the Hyundai completion date of early 2025.
A fourth automotive parts manufacturer, not directly related to Hyundai, has broken ground in Bulloch County.
Joon Georgia, part of auto parts manufacturer Ajin USA, is expected to bring 630 jobs and a $317 million investment to Bulloch County.
The Korean-owned company Joon Georgia will produce automotive body parts for cars for the Hyundai plant.
Ecoplastic, a Korean-based company, will generate an anticipated 456 jobs with a $205 million investment in Bulloch County.
Ecoplastic will produce plastic car parts such as bumpers for Hyundai.
Hanon Systems will supply vehicle air conditioning systems for Hyundai and also for other manufacturers.
Hanon Systems' $40 million investment is expected to generate 160 jobs.
Aspen Aerogels makes insulation blankets for battery compartments of electric vehicles.
Aspen Aerogels will hire an anticipated 250 employees with $325 million in investment in Bulloch County.
The Aspen Aerogels project was announced ahead of the May 2022 Hyundai plant announcement, and although ground has been broken, construction is currently on hold.
General Motors is a major customer of Aspen Aerogels, and the pause in construction came before the early fall UAW strikes against major automakers.
German-based plastics recycler revalyu Resources (US) is building its first U.S. facility in Bulloch County to recycle PET plastic bottles, accounting for 121 jobs on a $210 million investment.
Ogeechee Technical College (OTC) is expanding its education of industrial employees and currently training Amazon workers on industrial maintenance, with students traveling from across the country.
OTC is constructing a new 37,000-square-foot Georgia Industrial Systems and Industrial Robotics Training Center to triple its training capacity.
The $19.7 million OTC training facility is slated for completion in March 2025.

The two-story training center is being built on land donated by the Development Authority of Bulloch County and will include labs for industrial systems, industrial robotics, and programmable logic controller (PLC) training.

Georgia Southern University's research areas focus on direct impact in Southeast Georgia and include logistics and supply chain, advanced manufacturing, coastal resilience and sustainability, holistic wellness and fitness, and community vibrancy reflected in public health and safety.
Georgia Southern is adding two new doctoral programs in high-demand areas of applied computing and engineering.

The university is expanding the delivery of six graduate degree programs over the coming year to the Education Center at Fort Stewart and Hunter Army Airfield, including an executive MBA, a Master of Public Administration, a Master of Science in IT, a master's degree in professional communication and leadership, and two graduate certificates in cybersecurity and professional communication.
New industries are concentrated in the southeastern portion of Bulloch County, closest to the Hyundai plant in Bryan County, impacting farmland use.
Approximately 1,700 jobs are currently in the process of locating and building in Bulloch County, according to Roy Thompson, chair of the Bulloch County Board of Commissioners.
source_title: Statesboro | Bulloch County: Success Breeds Success - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Toyota will invest an additional $8 billion in its first North American electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility.
The Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina announced the investment, which adds another 3,000 jobs.
The project is dubbed “Toyota Battery Manufacturing North Carolina” (TBMNC).
TBMNC was first announced as a $1.29 billion investment in 2021.
The project saw expansions in 2022 and 2023.
The total project value is now $13.9 billion.
The project will create more than 5,000 jobs.
The project is expected to be completed in 2025.
North Carolina Governor Roy Cooper stated that his recent trip to Tokyo and meeting with President Sato strengthened the partnership with Toyota, culminating in this historic announcement.
Governor Roy Cooper noted that North Carolina’s transition to a clean energy economy is bringing better-paying jobs to support families and communities for decades.
The facility is located on an 1,825-acre megasite in Liberty, North Carolina.
Toyota will manufacture batteries for hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) and battery electric vehicles (BEVs) at the facility.
The facility will use 100% renewable energy for manufacturing.
The facility supports Toyota’s global electrification efforts, which include rolling out 30 BEV models globally by 2030.

Christopher Chung, CEO at the Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina (EDPNC), called the announcement a validation of North Carolina’s national recognition as a hub for innovation and a top location for EV manufacturers and suppliers.
More than 28,000 North Carolinians work for Japanese companies every day.
Policies like the Inflation Reduction Act have helped propel North Carolina to be one of the top four states in electric vehicle investment over the past year.
North Carolina has brought in more than $10 billion in EV investment over the past year.
The state has seen more than $11 billion invested in the electric vehicle industry in the last three years.
VinFast, a Vietnamese auto manufacturer, announced a $2 billion investment and selected Chatham County in 2022 for its first North American automotive assembly and battery manufacturing plant.

The state has become a hub for electric vehicle component manufacturing with investments totaling $760 million from Eplison Advanced Materials, Deere & Company, Kresiel Electric GMBH, and Kempower, Inc. announced in the last eight months.

Wolfspeed, one of the world’s largest producers of silicon carbide wafers used in electric vehicles to increase battery range, announced a $5 billion investment in its newest semiconductor manufacturing plant in 2022.
CNBC named North Carolina its Top State for Business in 2022 and 2023.
Last year saw a record-breaking $19.3 billion invested in the state and nearly 29,000 new jobs announced.
Nearly 29,000 new jobs were announced last year, including 10,275 from 30 foreign direct investment projects worth a combined $5.21 billion.
source_title: Toyota Announces $8B Expansion at its North Carolina-Based EV Battery Plant - EDPNC

facts: North Carolina is suing to regain control of a nearly 2,000-acre Chatham County economic development site from VinFast.
The lawsuit alleges VinFast cannot open its planned car manufacturing facility by July 2026.
The lawsuit alleges VinFast cannot employ 1,750 people at the Moncure site by the end of this year.
These deadlines are part of an option in the state's sales agreement for three parcels that make up the megasite.
By failing to meet the deadlines, the state says it should be able to re-purchase the site.
North Carolina officials point to a clause requiring vertical construction on buildings and structures to begin by Jan. 1, 2024.
The state alleges VinFast has only graded the site and poured some concrete, with nothing built above ground.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson stated VinFast agreed to build a factory and create jobs for North Carolinians but did neither.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson noted that when North Carolina makes a deal, it builds in protection for taxpayers because VinFast broke the deal.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson said the state is using that protection to find a new project for the site that will create jobs.
VinFast has publicly stated it could open the factory by 2028, two years after the agreement deadline.
On May 10, 2024, VinFast asked North Carolina for an extension on the 2026 target.
During the May 10, 2024 meeting, VinFast cited a slowdown in global electric vehicle sales as making it difficult to secure financing.
State officials told VinFast they could not change the agreed-upon timeline during the May 10, 2024 meeting.
The lawsuit expresses skepticism that VinFast can meet its proposed 2028 opening target.
Even if possible, building by 2028 falls outside the maximum 18-month cure period the state can grant for compliance.
State officials allege VinFast cleared trees from the site in 2023 but stopped further work in December 2024.
A site development agreement clause allows the state to claw back site prep funds if work halts for more than 12 consecutive months before 2032.
The lawsuit states VinFast failed to take concrete action, abandoned work for over a year, and made zero progress on buildings or structures.
The lawsuit states VinFast failed to create jobs and never provided photos of upright columns or vertical structures built into building footers.
A request for comment sent to VinFast's general communications portal was not returned Thursday afternoon.
VinFast was founded in 2017 and quickly looked to open a factory in the United States.
The Chatham County factory was announced in 2022, with VinFast expecting to finish the first phase by 2024.
At the time of the 2022 announcement, VinFast called it the largest economic development project in North Carolina history.
VinFast planned to invest $4 billion and employ 7,500 people for the Chatham County project.
The site was planned to feature a car factory, a bus manufacturing facility, and an electric vehicle battery production plant.
VinFast's agreement required starting vertical construction on its first phase by January 1, 2024, which has not happened.
When asked for proof of vertical construction, VinFast sent photos of concrete footers being poured or retaining walls instead of free-standing structures above ground.
North Carolina officials argue that finishing a foundation for the facility would require hundreds more footers to be poured.
The lawsuit states VinFast cannot begin vertical construction until it has completed the underground foundation of the building.
The lawsuit states VinFast has yet to advance beyond the preliminary stage and has run out of time to do so.
VinFast has never actually been issued a building permit for the facility.
Chatham County was poised to issue a building permit on May 29, 2024, but required VinFast to inform the county which contractors would work on the site.
VinFast never responded to the county's request to name contractors.
The January 1, 2024 deadline is critical because a potential 18-month cure period under the agreement has already lapsed.
In a March 6 building permit application for a foundation submitted to Chatham County, VinFast reduced the scope of the project.
The March 6 application stated the facility would cover about 236 acres of the site and be built to employ 1,400 people.
This projected employment is fewer than the 1,750 people VinFast's agreement requires it to employ at a manufacturing facility on the site by this year.
Governor Josh Stein stated VinFast has not fulfilled its commitments and that today's action is about protecting taxpayers.
Governor Josh Stein said the goal is getting the Chatham County mega-site back on the market to support future good-paying manufacturing jobs.
The N.C. General Assembly appropriated $450 million of taxpayer dollars to the project for site preparation as part of the economic development package.
The appropriation included up to $125 million to VinFast, up to $250 million to the N.C. Department of Transportation, and up to $75 million to the City of Sanford for water and sewer extensions.
The site development agreement states that if construction halts for at least a year, the state can claw back any funds sent to VinFast.
North Carolina alleges VinFast failed to do anything at the site for a full calendar year, allowing the state to claw back about $80 million sent for grading and site prep.
Clayco, VinFast's previous contractor, terminated its contract on June 28, 2024, due to delays from VinFast and its failure to prove financing.
In a lawsuit, Clayco officials alleged that while contracted for construction, the facility's design was only 50% complete in June 2024.
VinFast terminated its agreement with construction company Barnhart in December 2024.
In April 2025, VinFast told North Carolina that no work had been done since Barnhart left and said it was considering using the site as a supplier park instead of a manufacturing facility.
On December 11, 2025, VinFast executives met with North Carolina officials about the lack of progress at the site.
During the December 11, 2025 meeting, North Carolina told VinFast it was ready to begin discussions about triggering its option to purchase the megasite.
VinFast officials pushed back during the December 11, 2025 meeting, saying they still planned to build a manufacturing facility on the site.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson sent VinFast a letter on Jan. 6, 2026, formally informing the company that North Carolina believed it was in violation of the economic development agreement.
In the Jan. 6, 2026 letter, Jackson told VinFast the state would seek to claw back $80 million if the company did not comply with the state's exercise of the option.
VinFast officials pushed back in February, arguing work was ongoing at the site, including erosion control.
VinFast officials argued in February that they were working to secure financing and sign new agreements with contractors for the project.
Over ensuing months, VinFast made claims about the scope of the project, potential financing, and work being conducted.
None of VinFast's subsequent claims satisfied North Carolina officials, particularly its insistence that it plans to finish construction in 2028.
The lawsuit states VinFast repeatedly repudiated its agreements throughout its responses rather than assuring the state of its ability to fulfill them.
source_title: NC sues VinFast in effort to regain control of Chatham County megasite | WHQR

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
Friday marked the last working day for exactly 958 plant employees, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted laid-off workers will be paid through May 6.
SK Battery America Inc. opened its $2.6 billion battery manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The Commerce plant notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.

The U.S. electric vehicle market is currently at a standstill as the Trump administration steers federal support away from electrification toward laxer automotive emissions policies and broader support for oil and gas industries.
In December, Ford announced it would scrap the fully electric F-150 Lightning in favor of an extended-range version of the vehicle.
SK Battery America Inc. and Ford had jointly invested $11.4 billion in U.S. battery plants before the battery maker ended their joint venture in December.

SK Battery America Inc. has made significant investments in Jackson County, Georgia, in recent years as automakers planned billions for EV development and the Biden administration supported domestic EV supply chain efforts.
In June 2020, SK Battery America Inc. announced plans to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time of the 2020 expansion announcement, Governor Brian Kemp’s office stated it would create 600 jobs.
SK Battery America Inc. and Hyundai are currently jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, Georgia (northwest of Atlanta).
Georgia has attracted other major EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex.
EV demand has not met automakers' ambitious expectations, accounting for about 8% of new U.S. vehicle sales in 2025, matching the previous year's rate.
Automakers are reevaluating multibillion-dollar electrification plans due to mounting financial losses and shifting consumer demand.

Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis, and other EV supply chain companies have reneged on factory, investment, and product plans, laid off workers, and pivoted efforts toward hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
Mainstream buyers are increasingly favoring hybrids and efficient gasoline-powered vehicles due to concerns over EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
The U.S. Congress under President Donald Trump has eliminated consumer tax credits of up to $7,500 for purchasing new or used EVs.

The Trump administration has announced plans to weaken federal fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, effectively eliminating federal incentives for producing cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: Nearly 1,000 workers laid off at Georgia battery plant as companies cancel EVs and lose incentives | Fortune

facts: SK Battery America Inc. announced the layoffs of 958 employees at its Commerce, Georgia plant.
The company cited shifts in automakers’ electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for electric vehicles (EVs) as reasons for the layoffs.
A Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice was filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
The final day of work for these employees was today.
Impacted workers will continue to be paid through May 6.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery facility in Commerce in January 2022.
The plant notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the truck this past December.

The layoffs follow a significant shift in federal policy under the Trump administration, which has steered support away from electrification toward laxer automotive emissions policies and oil/gas industries.
Congress has eliminated consumer tax credits of up to $7,500 for the purchase of new or used EVs.
The administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules, which essentially eliminates federal incentives for auto companies to prioritize cleaner vehicle fleets.
SK Battery America stated it remains committed to Georgia and its U.S. operations, framing the workforce reduction as a response to current market conditions aligned with long-term success.
SK Battery America is pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
Sen. Jon Ossoff blamed the current White House administration for the layoffs, stating that Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy and causing Georgians to lose jobs.

Sen. Raphael Warnock stated that the administration slashed clean energy investments that brought good-paying manufacturing jobs to Georgia and made manufacturing in Georgia more expensive through harmful tariff taxes.
source_title: SK Battery America lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Commerce plant | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: SKT America Inc is located at 3230 Old Maysville Rd, Commerce, GA 30529.
The company operates Monday through Friday from 8:00 AM to 5:00 AM and remains closed on Saturdays and Sundays.
SKT America Inc maintains an overall rating of 3.0 stars derived from six customer reviews.
Reviewer Eric Baggett dated May 16, 2023, identifies the company as part of the SK battery plant that is destroying the local community.
Reviewer Miguel Cruz dated August 9, 2022, alleges the company treats undocumented workers unfairly and advises against employment there.
Reviewer Manuel Gomez dated October 22, 2023, describes the workplace as terrible and one of the worst places to work.
The business is categorized as a manufacturer in Commerce, GA.
source_title: SKT America Inc in Commerce, GA 30529  - 706-336...

facts: Tanger Commerce features on-site EV charging stations available on a first-come, first-served basis.
Standard charging rates for the facility's EV charging stations may apply based on the provider.
The site is located at 800 Steven B. Tanger Blvd in North Georgia, halfway between Atlanta, GA, and Greenville, SC, off Interstate 85 at Exit 149.
The location is accessible via a 30-minute drive north of Athens via Highway 441.
source_title: Commerce Visitor Information

canonical_name: Jackson County, Georgia Economic Development Alliance
facts: Businesses and commercial real estate developers are making major investments in Jackson County because the perfect location is now more important than ever.
Jackson County, Georgia’s strategic location just northeast of Atlanta on I-85 is one of the main reasons so many businesses have decided to locate in Jackson County.
Commerce Crossroads Building 400 offers 172,000 SF of Class A industrial space with 32-foot clear ceilings, over 50 dock doors, and prime access to I-85 in Jackson County.
Commerce Crossroads Building 400 is located at 22 Cold Sassy Lane, Commerce, GA.
Commerce Crossroads Building 400 spans approximately 172,000 SF and is set up for flexible use.

Commerce Crossroads Building 400 features 32-foot ceilings, over 50 loading docks, secure trailer courts, and built-in office areas fully equipped for efficient distribution or manufacturing operations.
Commerce Crossroads Building 400 is positioned near major manufacturing hubs, making it ideal for strategic partners and suppliers.
Commerce Crossroads Building 400's proximity to the SK Innovation facility makes it particularly suitable for supply chain operations in a thriving industrial corridor.
Leasing options for Commerce Crossroads Building 400 are available, starting at $6.30 per SF per year, scalable to fit tenant needs.
Ridgeway 85 Logistics Center is an available industrial site in Jackson County.
Commerce Exchange is an available industrial site in Jackson County.
McClure Industrial Park is an available industrial site in Jackson County.
Public and private organizations have partnered together to promote and support Jackson County, Georgia’s economic development.
These public and private partners collectively form the Jackson Alliance.
source_title: Jackson County, Georgia | Jackson County, Georgia Economic Development Alliance

facts: A South Korean company plans to build a $72 million factory in Georgia to make parts for electric vehicles, hiring more than 140 workers.
Daesol Ausys announced its plans to build a factory in West Point, making luggage boards and covers for compartments in electric vehicles.
The company supplies parts to Hyundai, Kia and General Motors.
It will be the third factory in the same West Point industrial park for members of the company’s corporate family.
Daehan Solution opened a million factory there in 2008 to make headliners and other sound insulation parts for the nearby Kia plant.
Daesol Material opened a $35 million factory there in 2017 to make automotive fiber padding.
Daehan Solution today employs 350 full-time workers, while Daesol Material employs 50 workers, said Meghan Richardson, an economic developer for the city of West Point.
Daehan Solution also has a plant near Montgomery, Alabama, supplying the Hyundai assembly plant there.

The project is another in a wave of electric vehicle suppliers announcing plants in Georgia after Hyundai said in 2022 that it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai site could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Suppliers have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and to hire 5,000 people.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp said in a statement: “Our state’s automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for the jobs of the future.”
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production in December 2024.
The company would pay $15 to $20 an hour.
The state will pay to train workers.
The state gave $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the site and build a road, funds the county matched using sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and the county are granting eight years of property tax breaks, worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, at $1,250 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
source_title: South Korean auto supplier plans $72 million plant in Georgia to build electric vehicle parts | AutoGraphic's Automotive Report

facts: Joon Georgia Inc. is the first announced supplier for Hyundai’s new electric-vehicle and battery plant in Bryan County.
Joon Georgia Inc. said it would invest $317 million.
Joon Georgia Inc. said it would hire 630 people.
The investment and hiring will take place in Bulloch County, where Statesboro is the county seat.
Joon Georgia Inc. is a subsidiary of Korea’s Ajin Industrial Co. Ltd.
In 2008, the company set up shop near the Georgia-Alabama border to supply body components to Hyundai’s landmark Montgomery factory.
Ajin USA’s first plant is located in Cusseta, Ala., along the Korean automotive corridor that emerged between Montgomery and the Kia Motors plant in West Point, Ga.
The first plant operated under the name Joon LLC, churning out stamped metal assemblies and automotive electronic components.
Joon Georgia's announcement came two weeks after groundbreaking on the $5.54 billion Hyundai Metaplant.
The Hyundai Metaplant will include a battery joint venture.
The Hyundai Metaplant is reportedly considering assembling multiple models in the Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric-vehicle lineup.
Joon Georgia became the first in what is expected to be a wave of Korean suppliers headed to coastal Georgia.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area joint development authority are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Total supplier investment factored in for the new plant is $1.1 billion.
Hyundai said the new plant will bring about 8,100 jobs for Georgians.
The Hyundai Metaplant will sit on a 2,923-acre site jointly purchased by the state of Georgia and the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
The Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority includes the counties of Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham.
Clearing on the Hyundai Metaplant site is nearing completion.
Construction on the Hyundai Metaplant is set to begin in January.
Hyundai plans to begin making vehicles at the site by 2025, if not sooner.
Ajin’s new site will be located in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park in Statesboro.
Georgia Quick Start, the state’s workforce training agency, is set to assist the company with a customized training package to fill available positions.
Hiring for Joon Georgia Inc. is expected to begin in the middle of next year, a year before the planned start of production.
Ajin Industrial Co. Ltd. is based in Gyeongsan, just outside of the metropolitan city of Daegu.
Daegu is an auto parts powerhouse in the southern part of Korea and a sister city of Atlanta.

Ajin USA CEO Jung Ho Sea stated his company’s commitment is to not only be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, but to also be the best employer and corporate citizen and give back to the great community of Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
source_title: With Plans to Invest $317M and Hire 630, First Hyundai Supplier Is No Stranger to the Southeast - Global Atlanta

facts: Carpet manufacturer Ray Anderson cut net carbon emissions by more than 80 percent after reading The Ecology of Commerce in 1994, inspiring the clean energy highway project.
Anderson’s daughters have turned an 18-mile stretch of rural Georgia highway named after him into a living laboratory for clean energy innovation.
The Ray aims to be “a zero carbon, zero deaths, zero waste, zero impact highway.”
Harriet Langford, president of the Ray, stated that the project is fundamentally changing assumptions and expectations of highway infrastructure to make roads safe, sustainable, and regenerative.
Super slender, extra durable, skid-resistant solar panels are installed along the roadway to supply power to the Visitor Information Center in West Point, GA.
A solar-powered charging station at the West Point Visitor Information Center can charge most electric vehicles 80 percent of the way in less than 45 minutes.
A roll-over tire pressure monitor plate at the West Point Visitor Information Center gauges the pressure and tread depth of each tire and delivers a printout with the measurements.
Drivers can fill up their tires for free at a nearby pump, which improves fuel efficiency.
Engineers are looking to build highways with embedded wireless charging lanes that use an electromagnetic field to transmit energy to next-generation electric cars.
Noise-canceling barriers with embedded solar panels are being considered as solar barriers along the highway.
Solar-powered smart studs featuring a small solar panel and battery are being considered to light up at night, urge drivers to slow down in rain, or mark shoulders as extra lanes during rush hour.
The Ray is considering installing roadside wind turbines and additional solar panels.
Wheat farms along the route will produce straw that can be turned into toilet paper and paper towels.
Flower gardens are being planted to serve as habitats for pollinators including birds, bees, bats, butterflies, and other creatures.
The Ray is considering using drones to inspect damage to roads and bridges.
The highway is looking to be repaved using a mix of asphalt and recycled tires to cut down on noise and improve durability.
Harriet Langford stated that these innovations demonstrate her father’s philosophy of doing well by doing good.
source_title: This 18-mile stretch of Georgia highway is a living laboratory for clean energy

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On Battery Manufacturing Facility
facts: A new Hyundai electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility will bring billions of dollars of investment and thousands of new jobs to Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp announced that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On selected a location in Bartow County, Georgia, to supply automotive manufacturing plants across the country.
Officials state the new facility will be one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia history, with an investment of approximately $4 to $5 billion into Bartow County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia's growing automotive industry.
Governor Brian Kemp noted his administration's focus on bringing jobs and opportunity to previously overlooked communities across Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed pride that SK On and Hyundai Motor Group are investing further in Georgia, which he called the No. 1 state for business.
Officials estimate the facility will create more than 3,500 new jobs.
The facility will be located at Bartow Centre, an industrial site on Highway 411.
The facility is expected to begin operations in 2025.
Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed the county's pleasure with Hyundai Motor Group and SK On's decision.
Commissioner Steve Taylor thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre Industrial property.
Commissioner Steve Taylor stated that local officials and the companies pursued the project together as an extraordinary economic development initiative.
Commissioner Steve Taylor noted the project will provide regional benefits for local job seekers and significantly establish Bartow County in Georgia's EV ecosystem.
SK On, the lithium-ion battery division of SK Innovations, currently employs over 2,000 workers at its existing SK Battery America facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The construction of the existing Commerce plant has faced controversy due to FOX 5 I-Team investigations alleging worker safety issues at its construction site.

Earlier this year, Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to invest over $5.5 billion in a separate electric vehicle manufacturing facility in Bryan County, which prompted multiple parts suppliers to announce new facilities to support it.
source_title: Hyundai, SK building new battery plant to Bartow County, bringing 3,500 new jobs | FOX 5 Atlanta

entity_name: Hyundai-SK On EV Battery Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-SK On EV Battery Joint Venture
facts: The Inflation Reduction Act is starting to move things in the EV and battery markets in the US.
Primarily Korean companies were caught off-guard by the IRA announcement and are now in a dire situation.
Hyundai and Kia do not currently make electric vehicles in the US and will not for a while.
The Kia EV9 will be the first US-made EV from Hyundai, coming in 2024 from the West Point, Georgia factory that also builds the Kia Telluride.
Hyundai Motor Group intends to expand US production of electric vehicles under the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
Hyundai Motor Group chose SK On as its battery cell supplier.
Hyundai and SK On have established a joint venture to supply battery cells for local US production.
The Hyundai-SK On joint venture plans to invest $5 billion in a local US battery production facility.
Battery cells from the Hyundai-SK On joint venture will be assembled into battery packs by Hyundai Mobis and supplied to the Group's production facilities.
Hyundai and SK On have chosen Bartow County, Georgia as the location for their US battery manufacturing facility.
The Bartow County facility is located close to Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The annual production capacity of the new facility is initially targeted at 35 GWh.
The 35 GWh capacity is sufficient to support the production of 300,000 electric vehicles.
Battery-cell production at the Bartow County facility is scheduled to start in the second half of 2025.
Hyundai Motor Group intends to expand its US footprint with a new EV production facility in Bryan County, Georgia.
The $5.5 billion EV manufacturing plant in Bryan County will begin production in 2025.
The Bryan County plant will begin production just in time to use batteries produced at the new Bartow County factory.
Hyundai's partnership with SK On is not exclusive.
Hyundai is in talks with LG Energy to establish two additional battery plants in the US, enough to power one million electric vehicles.
The battery cells supplied by SK On are of the pouch type.
Hyundai already uses this pouch cell chemistry for the Ioniq 5, Ioniq 6, and Kia EV6.
Hyundai will utilize the same SK On battery cells in the upcoming Ioniq 7 SUV, Genesis GV70, and Kia EV9.
With both battery and vehicle production in the US, Hyundai Motor Group's EVs will qualify for the IRA tax credit.
The IRA tax credit is considered a significant incentive that can help sales tremendously.
source_title: Hyundai and SK On Choose Bartow County for Their EV Battery Plant - autoevolution

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On
facts: A new electric vehicle battery plant is coming to Bartow County in 2025.
The project will bring more than 3,500 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project on Thursday.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are looking to build their manufacturing facility at Bartow Centre on Highway 411.
About $4 to $5 billion will be invested into Bartow County.
Gov. Brian Kemp called the plant one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia's history.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated in a news release: "Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in our state's ever-growing automotive industry. Since day one, my administration has been focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to communities across the state that may have been overlooked in the past. SK and HMG share this goal, and we're proud they are choosing to invest even further in this No. 1 state for business."
In May, Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to invest $5.54 billion into Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Ground was broken on the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America facility in Bryan County in October.
source_title: EV Battery Plant To Open In Bartow County | Cartersville, GA Patch

facts: Go-Station is presenting at the 2024 Clean Energy Roadshow on July 11th and 12th.
The event will be held in Cartersville at the Savoy Automobile Museum and in Alpharetta on the North Fulton campus of Gwinnett Technical College, GA.
Mike Anderson, Go-Station's Chief Operating Officer, will present information on Georgia's electrification transition efforts and how Go-Station is supporting a sustainable future for the state.
The Clean Energy Roadshow was founded by Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols in 2010 as the Alternative Fuel Vehicle Roadshow™.
The event was originally conceived as a round table of experts to discuss integrating new alternative fuel technologies for governments, workplaces, and enterprise fleets.
The Roadshow has evolved into an annual, statewide series covering benefits, expanding options, and funding programs for zero and near-zero emission vehicles.
Each event includes a showcase and demonstration of the latest technology vehicles.
Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols stated that Go-Station continues to play a critical role in Georgia's transition to alternative energy and their sustainable future.
Go-Station is an EV charging company dedicated to building confidence in owning and driving electric vehicles.
The company serves drivers, multi-family developments, workplaces, parking facilities, healthcare, businesses, and fleet operators seeking to go electric.
Go-Station offers a full range of charging solutions, including turn-key deployments.
source_title: Go-Station to Present at Georgia's Clean Energy Roadshow

facts: Battery Resourcers, a vertically integrated lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered materials company, announced plans to open a commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Covington, Ga.
On January 19, 2022, Battery Resourcers changed its name to Ascend Elements.
The 154,000-square-foot facility is scheduled to be fully operational in August 2022.
When operational, it will be North America’s largest battery recycling facility.
The facility will have capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of discarded lithium-ion batteries and scrap per year.
The processing will return battery-grade lithium, cobalt, and nickel back into the battery supply chain.
Battery Resourcers is investing $43 million in the facility.
The facility is located at 9172 Industrial Drive in Covington.
The project will bring at least 150 jobs to the community.
The site is strategically located near several EV manufacturing hubs and lithium-ion gigafactories.
Industry analysts and the federal government agree the United States needs to build a sustainable battery recycling infrastructure, and the facility opening will help meet that demand.
Michael O’Kronley, CEO and Director of Battery Resourcers, stated that automotive OEMs currently have very few options for responsible and cost-effective disposal of discarded batteries and scrap.
O’Kronley said the company is providing a sustainable solution to help minimize the need for mining while returning valuable, battery-grade materials back into the lithium-ion supply chain.
O’Kronley expressed a desire for the battery recycling industry to bring more recycling capacity online in the coming years.
He stated that the industry needs to match gigafactory production capacity with “gigarecycling” facilities capable of recycling millions of batteries.

O’Kronley noted that while the Covington facility will be the largest in North America at opening, he encourages development of even larger recycling facilities to prevent batteries from going to landfill.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Battery Resourcers is the latest job creator moving to Georgia due to the state’s leadership position in electric vehicle manufacturing.
Governor Kemp welcomed Battery Resourcers as they open their state-of-the-art Covington facility and expressed honor that they chose Georgia for this milestone business venture.
Pat Wilson, Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, noted that Battery Resourcers had many options to consider before choosing Georgia.
Wilson highlighted Georgia’s assets for business growth, including an educated workforce, pro-business climate, high quality of life, and commitment to sustainability.
The opening of the Covington facility marks the first phase of Battery Resourcers’ strategic expansion.
Plans are already in motion to open an additional facility for precursor and cathode-active material production in 2023.
The new facility will use the company’s patented Hydro-to Cathode™ technology.
Compared to mining and production of new materials, the Hydro-to Cathode recycling process is 93% cleaner at a 59% lower cost.
A recent study published in the scholarly journal Joule found recycled cathode from the Hydro-to-Cathode process outperforms new cathode materials in terms of cycle life by as much as 53%.
The company’s long-term plans include opening additional facilities in North America, Europe, and Asia.
These future facilities aim to process up to 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion material globally per year.
Battery Resourcers is based in Worcester, Mass.
The company operates the world’s most efficient lithium-ion battery recycling process.

Unlike other battery recycling companies, Battery Resourcers starts with a mixed stream of used lithium-ion batteries or production scrap and ends with the production of finished battery-ready cathode active materials.
The company is engineering a novel process for graphite recovery and purification to return both cathode and anode active materials back to new battery manufacturers.
Battery Resourcers was founded in 2015 with a mission of returning 100% of battery active materials back into new batteries.
The company currently makes EV-grade, finished cathode active materials that meet or exceed performance requirements set by other industry-leading brands.

links.context: Learn more about closed-loop recycling, cathode manufacturing, Hydro-to-Cathode technology, and expansion plans

source_title: Ascend Elements | North America’s Largest Lithium-ion Battery Recycling Facility

facts: Ascend Elements is based in Massachusetts.
Ascend Elements extracts cathode material from older batteries to be used in newer ones.
Ascend Elements broke ground on a new factory in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The company expects to deliver its first cathode supplies to an unnamed buyer in the fourth quarter of 2024.
The new Kentucky plant is expected to reach full production by 2025.
Ascend Elements has a contract to deliver a minimum of $1 billion worth of cathodes over the next five years.
Ascend Elements is the latest EV-related manufacturer to move to the region known as the Battery Belt, which covers areas from Michigan to Georgia.
The EV battery industry received a boost from tax incentives provided in the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act.
Since the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, companies have announced $242 billion in new clean power capital investments.
These investments include battery plants, wind farms, and EV factories across 41 states.
Center for American Progress Senior Vice President Trevor Higgins reported these investment figures to a subcommittee of the House Committee on Energy and Commerce in April.
Georgia has experienced a boom in EV activity.
Hyundai Motor Group announced a new $4.3 billion EV battery plant located outside of Savannah.
The Hyundai plant is being developed in a partnership with LG Energy Solutions.
The Savannah-area plant will be Hyundai Motor Group's second EV battery plant in Georgia.
The plant will produce power cells for batteries compatible with Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis electric vehicles.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley stated the Kentucky plant is receiving $480 million in matching grants from the Inflation Reduction Act.
Mike O’Kronley noted that the non-diluted funding was extremely helpful and is beginning to translate into commercial contracts.
Ascend Elements was founded in 2015 in Worcester, Massachusetts.
Ascend Elements already operates an existing plant in Covington, Georgia.
The Covington, Georgia facility recycles battery material from discarded consumer electronics.
The Covington, Georgia facility also recycles manufacturing scraps from Honda Motor Co. and Hitachi.
The new Kentucky plant is expected to produce cathodes capable of supplying 40 gigawatt-hours of batteries annually, or enough for 750,000 electric vehicles.
source_title: Another $1B EV Supplier Factory Under Construction In The 'Battery Belt'

facts: Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PAIH) named Ascend Elements’ Project Apex 2 as Manufacturing Investment of the Year.
The facility is an EU-first battery material refining center to fuel Poland’s.
source_title: Ascend Elements | News and Insights

facts: Rivian has officially broken ground on its planned $5 billion electric vehicle factory in Georgia.
The project marks a major step toward expanding production beyond Rivian's existing plant in Normal, Illinois.
Construction activity is expected to accelerate in 2026 after a phased restart of the project.
The project is backed by state incentives and positioned as a major economic development effort.
The factory is intended to support thousands of jobs, broaden Rivian's production capacity, and help the company compete more aggressively in the EV market.
The site is located east of Atlanta near the communities of Stanton Springs and Social Circle.
The Georgia facility is designed as a long-term manufacturing base for next-generation electric vehicles rather than an overflow plant for current models.
The campus is intended to scale production of more affordable, higher-volume vehicles alongside existing R1T pickup, R1S SUV, and commercial van operations in Illinois.
Rivian previously adjusted its near-term capital spending and shifted the initial launch of its R2 SUV to the Illinois plant.
Georgia officials have promoted the project as one of the state's largest clean manufacturing investments.
