facts: Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an EV Power Electric system plant in Bryan County, GA.
The new plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment and project details.
Hyundai Mobis is one of the world’s largest automotive suppliers.
This announcement marks the second supplier to locate in the Bryan County area in just two weeks.
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The West Point plant is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh serves as Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit.
Once it reaches full production, the new facility will be 1,200,000 square feet.

The new facility will supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.
The new facility will also supply 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to those same customers.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The new facility is located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves serves as Chairman of the Development Authority of Bryan County.
The site purchased about two years ago is near available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
Raydient Places provided support and partnership on the project alongside Bryan County and the City of Richmond Hill.
Alyce Thornhill is the Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy for the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power on this competitive project.
Pat Wilson serves as Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
In May, GDEcD predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
That prediction was exceeded in about two weeks following the announcement.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis invests $926M on Georgia EV plant

facts: Hyundai Mobis is opening a new metaplant in Bryan County soon.
The company is looking to hire employees within the community for all types of entry-level positions to start this new project.

Eric Bringert, Production Manager of Electric Power Transplant, stated they need many hands on deck and are trying to get the right people that can work together with the team, grow as a family, and really help us grow, help the community grow.
Donna Wilson, Recruiting coordinator, stated she hopes everyone can understand they are here to do and give back to the families in this area and hopefully provide them another home for their career.
There will be upcoming career fairs as well.
The company believes hiring locally will help start the project and boost the community.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis: New metaplant in Bryan County to boost growth, jobs

facts: Hyundai Mobis unveiled its ‘holographic windshield display’ technology at CES 2025, transforming the vehicle’s front windshield into a transparent display without any additional device.
Kia’s electric vehicle EV9 was used to offer a live demonstration of the holographic windshield display technology to CES 2025 visitors.
The in-vehicle display market is shifting toward user experience innovation, including large screens, high-definition graphics, 3D and AR technologies, privacy modes, and unique interior designs.
Global automakers are increasingly adopting advanced displays with various features to establish distinctive brand images.

The technology projects images and videos anywhere on the front windshield using a special film with an optical component called HOE (Holographic Optical Element) that uses light diffraction to deliver content to driver and passenger eye positions.
The display offers a privacy mode that prevents the driver from seeing the screen before the passenger does.
The holographic film can be placed on the top, bottom, or side of the windshield, with side placement allowing it to function like a smartphone’s curved edge screen.
Hyundai Mobis is teaming up with German optical company ZEISS to develop this advanced, not-yet-mass-produced display technology.
Both companies intend to complete pre-development by the first half of next year and aim to launch the product for mass production as early as 2027.
Lee Gyu Suk, CEO of Hyundai Mobis, and Karl Lamprecht, CEO of ZEISS, met at Hyundai Mobis’s CES 2025 booth to discuss further business cooperation and underscore their partnership.
The companies aim to deepen collaboration by focusing on advanced solutions for driver and cabin monitoring systems as well as innovative augmented reality display technologies.
Hyundai Mobis’s premium in-vehicle display portfolio includes the big and slim high-definition QL Display, the 34-inch curved Swivel Display, and the Rollable Display.
Lee Gyu Suk stated that Hyundai Mobis will target the global market with market-leading technology and high-value-added products while actively introducing various new technologies.

Hyundai Mobis invited global clients to its CES 2025 booth, held over 30 meetings with worldwide clients, and set up a special private zone to showcase 16 strategic products including electrification components, vehicle electronics, chassis, and lamps for global business opportunities.

Hyundai Mobis invited approximately 40 Korean and international students from MIT, Stanford, and the Georgia Institute of Technology to experience its key exhibits, including the holographic windshield display and Human-centric Interior Lighting, as part of a global talent recruiting program aimed at expanding its student network and attracting top future talent.
source_title: [News] Hyundai Mobis Unveils Holographic HUD ? ‘Magic Etched on the Windshield’: Transparent Display for All Kinds of Information - LEDinside

facts: Hyundai Glovis, a logistics subsidiary of the South Korean auto giant, leased 18K SF at Satellite Place, an office park in Duluth that includes a six-story and a single-story office building totaling 820K SF.
Hyundai Glovis is nearly doubling its local footprint and moving from nearby 3675 Crestwood Parkway in Duluth.
Glovis is among eight tenants, totaling 47K SF, tied to the EV industry that have signed leases at Satellite Place, off Satellite Boulevard across from Gwinnett Place Mall.
Georgia has become a magnet for vehicle makers in the EV sector, with Rivian and Hyundai among the brands opening major plants.
Electronics company Ricoh USA also recently inked a 24K SF lease at Satellite Place.
source_title: This Week's Atlanta Deal Sheet: Hyundai Expands EV Arm Into Satellite Place (November 17, 2023)

facts: Hyundai plans to order tens of thousands of Boston Dynamics robots over the next few years.
Atlas humanoids are heading to a Georgia plant in 2028, scaling to 30,000 units a year.
Hyundai Motor Group acquired Boston Dynamics in 2021 for $1.1 billion.
Hyundai Motor Group announced it will invest $21 billion in the U.S., including a $6 billion push to drive new ideas and expand partnerships with American companies.
Part of the investment is earmarked for hardware that does not punch a time clock, with plans to purchase tens of thousands of robots over the next few years.
Hyundai will fold Boston Dynamics into its broader manufacturing operation to help become the world’s top maker of advanced mobile robots.
Hyundai laid out its robotics timeline at CES 2026.
The roadmap includes plans to build 30,000 Atlas humanoid robots a year by 2028 at its sprawling “Metaplant” near Savannah.
The robots will be put to work in factories and warehouses worldwide.
Deployment will start gradually in 2028 at the Savannah plant, where Hyundai builds electric and hybrid vehicles like the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9.
Atlas will initially focus on parts sequencing, making sure vehicle components land in the correct spots on the line when needed.
By 2030, Hyundai will start using Atlas for more complex tasks, including component assembly and jobs involving repetitive motions or heavy lifting.
Spot, the four-legged robot dog, is already operational.
Spot robots are inspecting vehicles on the assembly line at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The Atlas humanoid can charge itself and keep working without stopping.
Atlas runs at a steady pace and automatically walks itself over to a charging station to swap out its own batteries when power is low.
Once a single Atlas learns a new task, that task can be copied across the entire fleet.
The deployment aims to catch defects early and keep tolerances tight on every car that rolls off the line.
Better quality control at the plant means fewer warranty claims, fewer recalls, and stronger long-term durability.
The impact of pouring money into robotics will likely be felt beyond new vehicle production, shaping factory floor inspections and the long-term reliability of certified pre-owned cars.
Tesla plans to reveal the latest version of its Optimus robot in February or March, with production slated to begin by the end of 2026.
Mercedes-Benz bought a stake in robotics firm Apptronik and is testing its robots in a couple of its German factories.
BMW has been trying out Figure AI’s humanoid robots at its Spartanburg, South Carolina plant.
Former employees say Hyundai is pressuring Boston Dynamics to produce “tens of thousands” of robots in the next few years for its automotive plants.
Boston Dynamics is reportedly making just four Atlas robots per month as it figures out how to ramp up large-scale manufacturing.
The production climb to 30,000 a year has reportedly contributed to executive shake-ups inside Boston Dynamics.
Tighter assembly tolerances are expected on Ioniq EVs first, followed by similar gains across Kia and Genesis lineups.
Hyundai will use its Hyundai Mobis auto parts arm and Hyundai Glovis logistics firm to build an end-to-end robotics value chain that can churn out 30,000 robots a year.
Vehicles produced in 2030 are expected to share factory floors with thousands of humanoid coworkers.
source_title: Inside Hyundai’s Massive Robot Push and What It Means for Car Buyers

facts: Georgia is among US states in which public officials have been on a rant against investing that follows environmental, social, and governance principles, or ESG for short.
Georgia has become a key state in the Biden administration’s efforts to step up the pace of decarbonization.
A new $4.3 billion joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution is the latest clean tech investment in Georgia.
The EV battery joint venture will be located next to Hyundai’s forthcoming Metaplant America, which is already under construction in Savannah.
Under the agreement, the 50-50 venture will produce batteries for electric vehicles at a rate of 300,000 EVs per year.
This production rate comes close to half the total number of EVs sold in the US last year, which topped 800,000 for the first time.
Construction for the joint venture is scheduled to start in the second half of 2023.
The joint venture plans to start battery production at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant, then supply them to the Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for production of Hyundai, Kia and Genesis EV models.
The new facility aims to help create a stable supply of batteries in the region and allow the Group to respond fast to the soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.
The new electric vehicle battery factory in Georgia joins six others under Hyundai's wing, including some still in the construction phase.
An Energy Department report indicates Georgia, Kentucky, and Michigan will dominate electric vehicle battery manufacturing in the United States by 2030.
Kansas, North Carolina, Ohio, and Tennessee will also be key players in EV battery manufacturing.
These EV battery-friendly states have been front and center in a Republican-organized movement to protect fossil energy stakeholders from competition by throwing up roadblocks to ESG investing.
Renewable energy is a prominent feature of ESG guidelines.
Georgia, Kentucky, Michigan, Kansas, North Carolina, Ohio, and Tennessee will collectively help electric vehicle manufacturers meet the demand for zero emission mobility.
A Federal Reserve Bank report reported a total of more than $40 billion in planned investments for the North American electric vehicle battery buildout.
There is an overlap between the electric vehicle battery race and states with anti-union “right to work” laws.
About 28 states have laws on the books that restrict the ability of unions to organize, with the aim of driving down labor costs and attracting manufacturers.
Many right-to-work laws go back to the 1940s, long before the mass market for electric vehicles began to take shape.
Overseas competition was a leading factor in union-friendly northern states sliding into the Rust Belt, while right-to-work states continued to attract manufacturers with low labor costs.
The low-cost labor factor has helped set the table for EV production, and Georgia is a leading example.
Fortune reported on Hyundai’s other new electric battery venture in Georgia: an investment of up to $5 billion with the South Korean firm SK On.

A photo caption noted Hyundai has been investing in Georgia for years, referring to a 2010 ribbon-cutting ceremony with former Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue for Hyundai’s first Kia factory in the US, located in West Point, Georgia.
The SK project adds more fuel to Georgia’s competition with fossil energy.
SK already has a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta, with more than 2,000 workers.
The Commerce plant also makes EV batteries for Ford and Volkswagen.
LG Energy Solution promotes Georgia as an epicenter of the decarbonization trend.
LGES aims to ramp up local production to provide innovative products both in scale and with speed, thereby expediting the clean energy transition in the U.S.
Republican officials in Georgia signed on to an anti-ESG open letter organized earlier this year by Florida Governor Ron DeSantis, joining 18 other states.

The letter claims freedom-loving states can leverage state pension funds to force change in how major asset managers invest money of hardworking Americans, ensuring corporations focus on maximizing shareholder value rather than woke ideology.
The letter claims the proliferation of ESG throughout America is a direct threat to the American economy, individual economic freedom, and way of life.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp signed the anti-ESG letter on behalf of his home state.
Hyundai’s new $5 billion EV factory in Georgia is one of the largest economic development deals in state history.
The state of Georgia committed an incentive package worth $1.8 billion for the automotive plant.
The incentive package was described as easily the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised for an automotive plant.
source_title: Georgia To Lead Electric Vehicle Revolution, ESG Or Not

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) announced the formation of a new joint venture.
The joint venture plans to build a U.S. EV battery plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The plant will be capable of supporting the production of 300,000 vehicles per year.
The anticipated investment for the plant is more than $4.3 billion.
The new battery plant will be located adjacent to Hyundai’s Metaplant America.
Hyundai’s Metaplant America is its first full-electric vehicle (EV) facility in the U.S.
Hyundai’s Metaplant America was announced last year and is currently under construction.
Hyundai stated the new facility will help respond fast to the soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.
The new facility aims to create a stable supply of batteries in the region.
Battery packs assembled using cells from the plant will be supplied for the production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
Earlier this year, Hyundai announced a goal to become one of the world’s top 3 EV manufacturers by 2030.
Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.
Jaehoon Chang stated they will create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing a new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
The launch follows an executive order by President Joe Biden in 2021 mandating that zero-emissions vehicles make up half of new vehicle sales in the U.S. by 2030.
The launch follows the allocation of $7 billion from the U.S. Bipartisan Infrastructure Law aimed at strengthening the U.S.’ domestic battery supply chain.
Automotive leaders GM, Ford, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and Stellantis have each announced significant investment plans in EV and battery capacity over the past several months.
LG Energy Solution (LGES) was spun out of chemical giant LG Chem in 2020.
LGES stated it is now concentrating most of its resources in the U.S. to expand battery production capacity.
The new JV brings LGES's number of battery plants currently operating or being constructed in the U.S. to seven.
This includes a $4.4 billion JV launched earlier this year with Honda.
Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, stated that two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands to drive the EV transition in America.

Youngsoo Kwon stated LG Energy Solution will commit its best efforts to offering ultimate sustainable energy solutions to customers by advancing product competitiveness and global operational expertise.
Construction on the new JV’s battery plant is expected to begin later this year.
Battery production at the plant is anticipated to begin as soon as late 2025.
source_title: Hyundai, LG Energy Launching $4.3 Billion EV Battery Plant to Meet “Soaring” U.S. Demand - ESG Today

entity_name: Hyundai and LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant Partnership
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Cell Manufacturing Plant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group revealed plans on Friday for an EV battery cell manufacturing factory that is to be part of the Hyundai Metaplant in Bryan County.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution will partner on a $4.3 billion plant.
The facility is expected to produce 300,000 batteries a year.
The facility is expected to open in late 2025.
The battery plant opening is scheduled just months after Hyundai's EV assembly factory is scheduled to come online.
A supplier building a facility in Richmond Hill, Hyundai Mobis, will also play a role in battery production.
The EV battery factory will be built next to the Hyundai assembly plant in Bryan County.
source_title: Hyundai and LG Energy Solution build EV battery plant in Bryan County

facts: LG Energy Solution will invest $3 billion in its Holland, Michigan battery manufacturing plant.
The investment is part of a new lithium battery supply deal with Toyota.
Batteries from the Michigan plant will be shipped to Toyota’s largest vehicle manufacturing plant in Georgetown, Kentucky.
The batteries will be used in Toyota’s new, North American-assembled battery-powered EV.
This agreement is LG Energy Solution’s largest supply deal outside a joint venture and is scheduled to begin in 2025.
LG Energy Solution is planning an annual battery production capacity of 20 gigawatts.
The supply deal supports Toyota’s goal to produce 3.5 million battery EVs annually by 2030.
Toyota announced plans to triple its annual EV production target to 600,000 vehicles in 2025.
Toyota aims to compete against global EV makers like Tesla and China-based BYD.
Toyota announced plans to update and streamline its EV manufacturing process to reduce production costs.
Toyota’s North American segment sold 64,298 EVs in September, up 81.5% year-over-year.
LG Energy Solution has become a major player in U.S. battery manufacturing over the past year.
LG Energy Solution is partnering with Hyundai to build a $6.3 billion battery manufacturing campus in Savannah, Georgia.
LG Energy Solution is partnering with Honda to build a facility worth up to $4.4 billion in Fayette County, Ohio.
LG Energy Solution and GM’s joint venture Ultium are building three battery production sites across Ohio, Tennessee, and Michigan.
LG Energy Solution is independently investing $5.5 billion in a Queen Creek, Arizona complex to produce cylindrical EV batteries and lithium iron phosphate batteries.
Young Soo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, stated the company will leverage 30 years of lithium-ion battery experience to support Toyota’s EV expansion.
Kwon noted the agreement strengthens LG Energy Solution’s North American production capacity and advances regional electrification progress.
source_title: LG Energy Solution invests $3B in US battery manufacturing expansion | Utility Dive

facts: Aurubis AG, Europe’s largest copper producer, announced it has started production at its new metals recycling plant in Richmond County, Georgia.
The $800 million facility is the first of its kind in the U.S.
The plant will process up to 180,000 tons per year of electronic and industrial scrap, including circuit boards, cables, and other metal-bearing waste.
From that processed material, Aurubis Richmond will output around 70,000 tons of high-purity blister copper (98–99% Cu) annually.
The facility will also produce significant volumes of nickel, tin, and precious metal by-products.
The project aims to bolster domestic supply of these vital materials and reduce America’s reliance on imports.
The U.S. consumes about 1.8 million tons of copper annually but currently imports roughly half.
Aurubis says its Georgia plant will “quickly” cut the need for imports by supplying copper to U.S. manufacturers directly.

An Aurubis spokesperson noted, “We intend to sell the copper primarily to U.S. customers,” pointing to surging demand for strategic metals like copper in electronics and renewable energy infrastructure.
The blister copper produced in Georgia can also be sent to Aurubis’ European smelters for further refining if needed.
By mid-2026 the plant should reach full capacity.
Aurubis’s CEO hinted that given “the high availability of recycling material and good local conditions,” the firm is considering additional U.S. projects down the line.

The investment reflects a broader trend of transatlantic cooperation on critical minerals, with a European company leveraging American scrap supply to strengthen U.S. supply chains while expanding its own global footprint.
source_title: Critical Minerals Report (09.26.2025): The Pentagon Doubles Down on Antimony as Neo’s Magnet Plant Opens in Europe - InvestorNews

facts: Aurubis Richmond is the first US multimetal recycling plant.

The facility recovers strategic metals including copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals from recycling materials for American energy infrastructure, data centers, AI applications, and the defense industry.
American industry currently needs about 1.8 million t of copper per year.
Experts estimate that demand for strategic metals like copper will rise nearly 30% in the next five years.
The US currently imports about half of the copper it processes.
Aurubis Richmond operates as the first and most technologically advanced secondary smelter in the US to process complex recycling materials.
The facility's "First Melt" celebration took place on Wednesday, September 24, 2025, in Augusta, Georgia/US.
Event participants included US Representative Rick W. Allen for Georgia’s 12th District and German Ambassador to the United States Jens Hanefeld.
Aurubis AG CEO Dr. Toralf Haag stated that Aurubis Richmond is the frontrunner in multimetal recycling and processing in the US, matching its position in Europe.
Dr. Toralf Haag noted that high availability of recycling material and good local conditions make the American market appealing for continued expansion of strategic raw material production expertise.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp stated in a video message that Aurubis Richmond is now a significant employer for Georgia and plays a major role in the American multimetal supply chain.
Governor Kemp described the facility as a testament to American manufacturing, independence, and resiliency.
The project involves an investment of about $800 million.
Aurubis is creating more than 240 jobs in Georgia through this facility.
The plant will process up to 180,000 t of complex recycling material annually, including printed circuit boards, copper cable, and other metal-bearing products.
An expansion stage for the facility is scheduled to start in 2026.
Aurubis Richmond recovers critical metals from incoming materials on site, closing the value chain locally.
The facility will be supplied with recycling materials locally to help keep valuable raw materials in the market.

US Representative Rick W. Allen emphasized that Aurubis Richmond is a strategic site in Georgia’s 12th District that reinforces America’s ability to supply itself with metals for national security, infrastructure, and technology leadership.
David Schultheis, President and Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond, described the facility as an important player in the North American multimetal supply chain.
David Schultheis thanked Georgia policymakers and governmental authorities for their strong support, smooth approval processes, and enabling the company to settle quickly in the state.
The new plant is equipped with cutting-edge technology that fulfills the latest environmental standards enacted by Georgia state and federal authorities.
The facility operates with the highest consideration for water, air, and soil quality.
Aurubis Richmond is designed as a flexible “one-stop shop” that will receive a broad range of metal-bearing recycling materials from US suppliers.

facts: South Korean molder and auto interior supplier Duckyang Industrial is set to invest $10 million to open a manufacturing facility in Braselton, Ga.

facts: Mexican nationals recruited to work white-collar jobs at Hyundai and Kia plants in Georgia instead ended up on the factory floor, lawsuit alleges.
Isidro Arellano was recruited by recruiters at Mexico’s Universidad Tecnológica de Torreón looking for engineers interested in career-enhancing jobs in the auto industry in the US South.
Arellano, 26, ended up lugging steering columns and installing bumpers, logging 60-hour-plus weeks at a Kia assembly line in West Point, Georgia.
Arellano returned to Mexico last year after being fired for complaining about what he describes as bait-and-switch practices.
Arellano expected to work in an office, attend meetings with executives, and have a friendly environment as promised.
Arellano is one of nine Mexican engineers who are plaintiffs in a federal racketeering lawsuit against KIA, Hyundai Motor Group, and Hyundai Mobis.
The Mexican nationals allege they were lured to the companies’ assembly lines by Korean-led labor brokers dangling professional jobs that didn't exist.
The case highlights an ongoing battle between business and US agencies charged with enforcing labor laws, including the role of third-party staffing companies in enabling alleged abuse.
Georgia gave its largest incentive package ever, $1.8 billion, to Hyundai for an EV plant near Savannah, promising 8,100 direct jobs and thousands more from suppliers.
Benjamin Botts, law director for Centro de los Derechos del Migrante, questioned why companies make such promises to the state while engaging in deceptive practices at existing facilities.
Staffing companies allegedly coached engineers on how to obtain TN visas and provided employer letters detailing fictitious US jobs.
The plaintiffs were among hundreds of Mexican nationals brought to West Point under the visa program by labor brokers under contract to supply workers to automakers.
Federal officials do not track TN visa holders in the US.
Plaintiffs ended up doing manual labor for less pay and longer hours than their domestic counterparts, the suit alleges.
Kia strongly denies the lawsuit allegations and states it requires business partners to strictly adhere to all applicable laws, including immigration laws.
Hyundai MOBIS strongly denies the allegations, stating it is an equal employment opportunity employer that will vigorously defend the claim.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp set a particularly torrid pace of recruiting and winning EV projects, making promised jobs a centerpiece of his 2022 reelection campaign.
The ceremonial groundbreaking for the $5.5 billion Hyundai EV plant came weeks before Governor Kemp's November victory.
The Hyundai EV plant is the biggest economic development project in state history.
Since October, Kemp announced job creations including 1,500 from Hyundai MOBIS; 630 from Joon Georgia Inc., a subsidiary of Ajin USA; 740 from Seoyon E-HWA; and 456 from Ecoplastic.
There are an additional 3,500 new jobs at a battery manufacturing plant, a joint venture from Hyundai Motor Group and SK On, a spinoff of Seoul-based SK Innovations.
In March, Kemp announced 400 more jobs from a Hyundai supplier.
Kemp stated that in a single month, Georgia’s economic development community announced more than 1,900 new jobs for Georgians, most related to the Hyundai plant.
State economist Jeffrey Dorfman warned there may not be enough Georgians to fill the jobs, telling state legislators in January they need to bring in more people.
Georgia’s unemployment rate was 3.1% in March, consistently lower than the US average.
Nationwide labor crunch has plagued automakers for years, according to Kristin Dziczek, automotive policy adviser for the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago.
Federal data show the unemployment rate for the general transportation equipment sector has been lower than for both manufacturing and overall unemployment nearly every quarter since 2012.
As the national unemployment rate averaged 3.3%, unemployment in transportation manufacturing stood at 1.8%.
The South faces challenges because plants are in rural areas with a far-flung labor pool and lack a dense, experienced manufacturing workforce around the plant.

Legally hiring foreign line workers is difficult due to the lack of a manual labor visa; non-agricultural manual labor visas are for temporary seasonal work, time-consuming, costly, require documentation that no US workers are available, mandate prevailing wage payment, and are capped at 66,000 annually.

The TN visa is granted by the US State Department, typically applied for by workers instead of companies, has no annual cap, and lacks worker protections such as work contracts or prevailing wage and travel reimbursement requirements.
Employers provide a job offer letter to U.S. consular officials for TN visas but nothing else, resulting in scant federal oversight.
There have been more TN visa workers in the US than any other category of temporary visa holder every year except one since 2012.

The TN visa is for Mexicans and Canadians in specific fields including engineers, scientists, lawyers, doctors, dentists, accountants, architects, astronomers, meteorologists, and “scientific technicians” with enough expertise to assist professionals in 10 specified fields.
No agency systematically checks to ensure TN visa holders work in the jobs they were brought to the country to do.
Mexican engineers learned of Georgia opportunities through LinkedIn, job fairs and universities.

A recruiting company identified candidates that met TN visa requirements and coached them through the application process, while an affiliated staffing company provided critical hiring letters attesting to US jobs.

Luis Salazar, an engineer with seven years of experience, was supposed to be a quality control engineer at Hyundai MOBIS applying statistical methods and performing mathematical calculations, but ended up on an assembly line installing bumpers, suspension systems and speakers.

Veronica Olan, an engineer for six years, was supposed to develop, evaluate and improve operations processes, analyze workflows, and help with budget, specifications, schedule, management, start-up, evaluations and presentations, but was put on a KIA assembly line tightening door screws and pouring antifreeze.

Arellano was supposed to be a “scientific production technician” making $40,000 annually with two weeks of paid vacation and a year’s free housing, but learned the reality when landing in a two-bedroom apartment with three other engineers doing manual labor.
Neither state nor local officials would comment on the specific allegations in the lawsuit or the future implications for Georgia’s labor supply.

Officials said they are taking steps to ensure an adequate workforce, including a labor shortage action plan from the Savannah Joint Development Authority, a state rural housing initiative, and continued investment in training related to EVs.
Garrison Douglas, spokesman for Kemp, stated Georgia is leading the way in growing and developing its workforce to fill jobs of tomorrow created by partners like Kia and Hyundai.
An estimated 4 million manufacturing jobs will open over the decade, of which 2.1 million will remain unfilled, according to a 2021 report by Deloitte and the Manufacturing Institute.
Laine Mears, professor of automotive engineering at Clemson University, called labor among the biggest challenges for EV manufacturing, noting the talent pipeline has slowed to a trickle.
The lawsuit raises questions about automaker strategies for staffing assembly lines and company responsibility for violations when workers are recruited and paid by outside firms.

Alabama fined eight companies in Hyundai’s supply chain late last year for child labor violations, seven of which were third-party staffing companies that found and hired the children for Hyundai’s suppliers.

Third-party staffing companies have become staples of the US manufacturing industry, according to David Weil, Brandeis economist and former head of the US Department of Labor’s Wage and Hours Division.
The Obama administration broadened the definition of responsibility to include companies where violations occur along with the third-party recruiter due to high coordination between them.
Former President Donald Trump reversed the provision, which was later restored by President Joe Biden.
Weil noted similar problems arose with the J-1 visa program a decade ago, which also resulted in foreign students ending up on factory floors.
source_title: ‘I Expected to Work in an Office:’ Engineers Recruited by Carmakers End Up on the Assembly Line - Human Trafficking Search

facts: Ascend Elements celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility.
The facility is located in Covington, Georgia.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is North America’s largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
Lithium-ion battery recycling is becoming an increasingly important part of the EV battery materials supply chain as demand for electric vehicles increases.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility now has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
This processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley stated: “This facility is an important part of America’s EV battery infrastructure, and this is just the start.”
Mike O’Kronley added: “As an industry, we need to continue building our closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.”
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.

By recycling used lithium-ion batteries and scrap with its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ process, Ascend Elements can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
Ascend Elements plans to increase headcount to 185 by 2024.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Massachusetts.
Ascend Elements is the leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions.
Ascend Elements produces lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM) at a commercial scale.
Ascend Elements' Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
This technology results in improved economics and lowered greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
The company's operations aim to reduce batteries going to landfill and create a cleaner manufacturing process for the lithium-ion battery industry.
source_title: Ascend Elements Opens North America's Largest Electric Vehicle Battery Recycling Facility in Georgia - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Battery recycling and manufacturing company Ascend Elements (formerly known as Battery Resourcers) is planning to open a battery recycling plant in Georgia by August of 2022.
The facility will recycle lithium, cobalt, and nickel.
The company plans to invest $43 million in the facility.
The facility will span 154,000 square feet.
The facility will be able to process 30,000 metric tons of discarded batteries and scrap per year.
Ascend Elements selected the Covington, Georgia site due to its proximity to EV manufacturing hubs and battery factories.
By 2023, Ascend Elements plans to open a facility to produce cathode active materials.
The new facility will rely on the company’s patented Hydro-to-Cathode recycling technology.
According to the company, Hydro-to-Cathode recycling technology is 93% cleaner compared to mining and the production of new materials.
The Hydro-to-Cathode technology operates at a 59% lower cost compared to mining and new material production.
Based on an article published in the journal Joule, the company states that Hydro-to-Cathode has a 53% longer life cycle than new cathode materials.
Ascend Elements plans to open additional facilities in North America, Europe, and Asia.
These additional facilities will process a combined total of up to 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion battery materials per year.
Ascend Elements CEO and Director Michael O’Kronley stated that the industry needs to match gigafactory capacity with “gigarecycling” facilities capable of recycling millions of batteries.
The Covington facility will be the largest battery recycling facility in North America as of summer 2022.
O’Kronley encouraged the development of recycling facilities even larger than the Covington site to prevent batteries from going to landfills.
source_title: Charged EVs | Ascend Elements to open battery recycling facility in Georgia - Charged EVs

facts: Korean workers recently detained by ICE at a Georgia battery plant had technological expertise not generally found in the U.S.
source_title: ICE raid at Hyundai-LG battery plant was decades in making - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: A new agreement between Ascend Elements and Call2Recycle will bring customized electric vehicle battery management, logistics, and recycling services to automotive dealerships, recyclers, and repair facilities.
The program combines the EV battery recycling infrastructure and advanced technologies of Ascend Elements with the collection, logistics, and tracking capabilities of Call2Recycle.
The program offers online access to GreenTraxEV, an all-in-one cloud-based platform from Call2Recycle designed to seamlessly ship mid-life, end-of-life, and damaged-defective EV batteries.
Working together, the companies aim to collect and recycle the equivalent of over 300,000 EV battery packs by 2030.
Leo Raudys serves as CEO of Call2Recycle.
GreenTraxEV leverages Microsoft Dynamics and Microsoft Power Platform to streamline the scheduling, routing, and logistics of EV battery management.
Ascend Elements and Call2Recycle can tailor the GreenTraxEV workflow to meet the specific requirements of any customer.
Ian Braime serves as Chief Commercial Officer at Ascend Elements.

The program supports customers with lithium-ion batteries from battery electric vehicles, plug-in hybrid vehicles, hybrid vehicles, electric buses, electric aircraft, stationary energy storage units, outdoor power equipment, e-bikes, e-scooters, hoverboards, and power tools.
Ascend Elements' battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia has the capacity to process 30,000 metric tons per year.
The Covington, Georgia facility is one of the largest EV battery recycling facilities in North America.
Call2Recycle, Inc. was founded in 1994 as a not-for-profit organization committed to protecting and preserving the environment through safe and responsible end-of-life battery management.
Ascend Elements was founded in 2015 as a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions.
Ascend Elements commercially produces lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).

Ascend Elements utilizes Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis technology to produce new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions.
source_title: Call2Recycle & Ascend Elements Formalize Agreement to Offer Customized EV Battery Management, Logistics & Recycling Services - CleanTechnica

facts: Anovion Tecnologies, based in Chicago, announced it will invest $800 million to build a factory in Bainbridge, southwest Georgia.
The company plans to hire 400 workers for the new facility.
Production of synthetic graphite anode at the Bainbridge plant is supposed to start in 2025.
Anovion was created last year by combining the graphite businesses of Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials, along with new investment from private equity firm Monomyth Group.
The company has existing facilities in Sanborn, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
Anovion previously announced plans to build a graphite factory with a 35,000-ton yearly capacity near Muscle Shoals in northwest Alabama.
The company won a $117 million grant under the new federal infrastructure law last year to help finance the Alabama plant and improvements at its New York facility.
CEO Eric Stopka stated the company was attracted to Georgia by its workforce, research universities, and growing solar energy production.
Anovion executives have stated they aim to reach 150,000 tons of synthetic graphite production in coming years, starting from almost nothing.

Synthetic graphite is manufactured from carbon products like petroleum coke by heating it to super-high temperatures for long periods, requiring huge amounts of electricity and making power costs a key location factor.
The Bainbridge plant could qualify for a state income tax credit allowing an annual deduction of $3,000 per job from state income taxes for five years.
To qualify for the tax credit, workers must earn at least $31,300 annually, and the total incentive could be worth up to $6 million.
Anovion may also qualify for property tax breaks from the city of Bainbridge and Decatur County.
Republican Governor Brian Kemp said the announcement furthers his goal of making Georgia the nation's electric mobility capital.
Georgia economic development officials report that more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects have been announced in Georgia since 2020, pledging $22 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs.
Most battery graphite is currently manufactured outside the United States.
The Bainbridge announcement marks the second major industrial win in two years for the city, located in the far southwest corner of Georgia.
In 2021, Danimer Scientific announced plans to build a $700 million factory in Bainbridge, hiring 400 employees.
Decatur County Commission Chairman Pete Stephens stated that diversifying the industry mix is necessary for the county to remain competitive and grow.
source_title: $800 million electric battery factory announced for South Georgia

facts: US battery materials company Anovion Technologies selected Decatur County in Georgia for its first and initial large-scale expansion of manufacturing capacity.
The total investment for the expansion is $800 million.
The facility will produce premium synthetic graphite anode materials.
The initial facility will be 1,500,000 square feet.
The site is located near a skilled workforce and other battery and electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing plants.
In October 2022, Anovion was awarded a grant of $117 million from the US Department of Energy under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law aims to help secure the domestic battery manufacturing supply chain.
The facility is the first of this scale for Anovion.
Anovion views the project as a “crucial first step” to reshoring synthetic graphite anode production.
Once fully operational, the company expects to initially produce 40,000 tonnes per annum.
Chip Dunn serves as Executive Chairman of Anovion.

Chip Dunn stated that Anovion's long-term strategic vision is to focus on growth and innovation, positioning the company as a leader in the US energy transition while securing a domestic source for critical materials required for US lithium-ion battery production.
Anovion claims to be one of only a few graphite anode producers in the US to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.
source_title: Anovion selects Georgia for $800 million graphite anode material production facility - Best Magazine

facts: Georgia scored the top spot in Site Selection’s annual Prosperity Cup ranking of state-level successful investment recruiting in the previous year.
Georgia unseated previous #1 North Carolina, which had claimed the top spot in the Site Selection ranking for the past three years.
The news was celebrated this week in press releases from both the Governor’s Office and the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that the award adds to a decade of evidence that the way business is done in Georgia is working, noting job creators get access to global markets through logistics assets like Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and some of the fastest-growing ports in the nation, plus state and local partners.
Georgia attracted 318 new and expanded facilities in 2023, leading all states.
One of the top criteria for the Prosperity Cup is a state’s ability to attract new and expanded facilities in the previous year.
Georgia led all states with 318 new and expanded projects including the expanded scope of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant, the largest project in the history of Georgia.

Originally announced in 2022, the South Korean automaker plans to produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis and Kia electric vehicles (EVs), as well as a new battery manufacturing facility northwest of Atlanta in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA, which will create 8,500 direct jobs for Georgians.
Georgia got a boost in this year’s Prosperity Cup ranking when Hyundai expanded its commitment in 2023 by an additional $2 billion and 400 new jobs.
Anovion Technologies announced an $800-million, 400-job synthetic graphite manufacturing facility in Decatur County.
Meissner Corporation plans to double the company’s Georgia manufacturing footprint with a new $250 million facility in Athens scheduled to open in 2026.
GDEcD Commission Pat Wilson credited the state’s pro-business environment as a solid contributing factor in putting Georgia over the top in this year’s Site Selection ranking.

Pat Wilson stated that taking the top spot in the 2024 Prosperity Cup reaffirms that the Georgia Way works, citing decades of partnerships and laying the groundwork for intentional economic development from the community level to the state level, combined with consistent pro-business policies.
Florida, North Carolina, and South Carolina earned spots behind Georgia in Site Selection’s ranking of “South Atlantic” states due to their pro-business environments.
North Carolina earned the #2 spot in Site Selection’s ranking of “South Atlantic” states attracting new and expanded facilities.
South Carolina earned the #3 spot, followed by #4 Florida.

Other states earning the #1 spot for attracting new investment in different regions included Ohio (East North Central), Texas (South Central), Pennsylvania (Northeast), Kansas (West North Central), and Arizona (Mountain).
source_title: Georgia Earns Top Spot In Site Selection Ranking

facts: Solvay officially opened its New Applications Development Lab and 3D printing technology line at its Research & Innovation Center in Alpharetta, Ga.

The Alpharetta site is part of the global headquarters of Solvay’s Materials business, which provides solutions enabling the future of electric vehicles, the hydrogen economy, aerospace, and other advancements supporting a more sustainable society.
Over the last four years, Solvay has invested more than $30 million in the Alpharetta site, including the new lab.
The new lab serves as a workshop for Solvay and its customers to develop advanced prototyping, virtual engineering validation, special testing, and secondary operations activities.
Customers can engage in real-time with Solvay’s versatile engineering teams at the facility to co-create innovative creative solutions more efficiently than before.
The opening marked the first stop of a seven-day Belgian Economic Mission visiting and recognizing Belgian companies that are growing in the U.S.
The mission was led by Her Royal Highness Princess Astrid of Belgium, Representative of His Majesty the King.

Attendees included Belgian Ministers, high-ranking Belgian officials, Deputy Commissioner Abby Turano of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, Solvay leadership and employees, and other guests.

The event included a ribbon cutting and tour of the new lab as well as the decoration of Mike Finelli, President, Group Growth Initiatives and Chief North America Officer at Solvay, for services rendered to Solvay and to Belgium in its development in the US.
Solvay CEO Ilham Kadri stated the state-of-the-art facility offers the perfect space for scientists to partner with customers to develop tailor-made solutions to meet their unique needs.

Minister in charge of Trade David Clarinval noted the lab exemplifies innovative solutions from a strong business partnership between Belgium and the United States, reinforcing Belgium’s links with Georgia and contributing to economic relations between the two countries.
Clarinval added that the U.S. is the number one export destination outside the EU for Belgium and the largest foreign investor back home.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson congratulated Solvay on the lab opening, noting Georgia continues to lead the U.S. in supporting the transition to e-mobility and celebrating Belgian investments that continue to reinvest and grow in Georgia.

Other Belgian Economic Mission attendees included His Excellency David Clarinval (Minister for the Self-Employed, SMEs and Agriculture, Institutional Reforms and Democratic Renewal, in charge of Foreign Trade); His Excellency Willy Borsus (Vice-President of the Walloon Government, Minister of Economy, Foreign Trade, Research and Innovation, New Technologies, Agriculture and Urban and Spatial Planning); and His Excellency Pascal Smet (State Secretary of the Brussels-Capital Region, responsible for Urbanism and Heritage, European and International Relations, Foreign Trade, Firefighting and Emergency Medical Assistance).
In 2021, North America represented 26% of Solvay’s net sales.

More than 5,400 Solvay employees work in 25 states at 38 industrial sites, 3 R&I Centers, and 3 corporate offices, including the Alpharetta site where the company employs more than 450 full-time workers and contractors.

Solvay is a science company with more than 21,000 employees in 63 countries that creates sustainable shared value through its Solvay One Planet roadmap focused on protecting the climate, preserving resources, and fostering a better life.

The Group’s innovative solutions contribute to safer, cleaner, and more sustainable products found in homes, food and consumer goods, planes, cars, batteries, smart devices, health care applications, water and air purification systems.
Founded in 1863, Solvay ranks among the world’s top three companies for the vast majority of its activities and delivered net sales of €10.1 billion in 2021.

Belgian Economic Missions are organized jointly by the Belgian Foreign Trade Agency, the regional institutions Wallonia Foreign Trade & Investment Agency, Brussels Invest & Export by hub.brussels and Flanders Investment & Trade and FPS Foreign Affairs.
source_title: ​Solvay opens new innovation lab in Alpharetta with HRH Princess Astrid of Belgium | Solvay
