facts: Solvay is accelerating the pace of providing the automotive industry with an entirely new generation of Amodel® polyphthalamide (PPA) materials targeted at higher demands of performance and sustainability for advanced electrical and electronic applications in e-mobility.
In the Supreme range, Amodel® PPA AE 9933 and AE 9950 have been designed for e-motor and inverter busbars operating at 800 volts and higher.
These materials combine best-in-class comparative tracking index (CTI) ratings with high thermal cycle shock resistance from -40 to 150 °C.
As a breakthrough vs. conventional PPA technology, they will also retain their CTI over time even after temporary exposure to peak temperatures above 150 °C.

New halogen-free flame retarded Amodel® Bios HFFR R1-133 and HFFR R1-145 meet with the growing trend of integrating the e-motor, power electronics and the gearbox into one single consolidated electric drive system.
With a CTI of >600 volts, heat resistance of >120 °C and excellent dimensional stability, these grades enable the design of highly compact systems using miniaturized components.
Moreover, they provide UL94 V0 flammability ratings without the need for halogenated flame retardants.
Besides addressing safety concerns in the event of uncontrolled thermal excursion, the halogen-free formulation also minimizes the risk of electronic corrosion.
In addition, Solvay is offering Amodel® Bios AE R1-133, an electro-friendly grade specifically developed for surface mounted data connectors that can be reflow soldered without blistering.

Compared to standard PPA, the higher impact resistance and weld-line strength of the material allows designers to further reduce the wall thickness of connectors, saving up to 50 percent in footprint on the printed board and giving more space for other electronic devices.

Brian Baleno, Head of Marketing, Automotive at Solvay Specialty Polymers, states that Amodel® Supreme and Bios are garnering a high level of interest among designers of e-propulsion systems, including e-motors, power electronics, and electronic coolant pumps.

He notes that beyond adding value by improving the thermal and electrical performance of e-mobility components in these systems, the new additions to the Amodel® polymers family are being more widely considered as manufacturers seek to meet ambitious sustainability targets.
Amodel® PPA Supreme is currently considered the highest performance thermal and electrical PPA on the market.

Amodel® PPA Bios features a partially bio-based long-chain molecular structure with the highest glass transition temperature (Tg) as well as the lowest global warming potential (GWP) compared to incumbent bio-sourced long-chain PPAs.
As part of the company’s One Planet commitment to sustainability, Solvay has reduced the CO2 footprint of Amodel® PPA by 30 percent since 2013.
Solvay uses 100 percent renewable energy in the PPA production at its global manufacturing plant in Augusta, Georgia.
source_title: Solvay Launches New Amodel® Supreme and Bios Grades to Support Further Sustainable Innovation in E-Mobility - Modern Plastics Japan

facts: Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Group, has begun production at a new solar panel factory in Dalton, Georgia.
The facility is a two-factory complex located in northwest Georgia.
The plant can produce enough solar panels to generate 5.1 gigawatts of power yearly.
This output represents almost 40% of U.S. solar panel capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.
Qcells opened its first factory in 2019.
The company has been building an even larger plant in phases since opening its first facility.
The company describes this project as the largest solar investment in American history.
Scott Moskowitz serves as Qcells head of market strategy and public affairs.
The new Dalton plant is the first solar module factory in the U.S. to begin production since President Joe Biden’s signature climate legislation passed.
Qcells made a $208 million investment in the Dalton facility.
Federal incentives are spurring a nationwide boom in renewable energy and electric vehicles.
Industry leaders warn that surging Asian imports could dampen efforts to manufacture more solar components in the United States.
The company’s new plant showcases more efficient equipment and processes as part of a larger investment to bring key steps in solar manufacturing to the U.S.
A solar panel or module is assembled from solar cells, which are most commonly made from wafers cut from ingots of polysilicon.
Qcells currently imports its solar cells from Asia.
Thirty miles south of Dalton in Cartersville, Qcells is building a $2.3 billion complex.
The Cartersville complex will take polysilicon refined in Washington state to make ingots, wafers, and solar cells.
The Cartersville plant will also produce 3.3 gigawatts of solar modules.
The Cartersville plant is scheduled to open in phases starting next year.
Currently, no silicon ingots or wafers are made in the U.S.
Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act offers an extra tax credit on American-made solar equipment.
The IRA allows manufacturers to earn incentives for every unit of polysilicon refined and every wafer, cell, and module produced.
Qcells earns a tax credit of 7 cents per watt for every panel made in Dalton.
This equals $34.30 for every 490-watt residential panel made.
Solar industry leaders warn that factories will struggle to compete with a new spike in cheap Asian imports.
Industry leaders are urging federal officials to investigate whether solar panels are being dumped at unfairly low prices.
Previous investigations led to anti-dumping tariffs on panels made in China and Taiwan.
Mike Carr is executive director of the Solar Energy Manufacturers for America Coalition, a group that includes Qcells.
Carr argues Chinese component makers are pushing out cheap modules from southeast Asian factories to tank panel prices and ensure Chinese dominance while smothering U.S. manufacturing.
Former President Donald Trump is hostile to renewable energy, adding political risk to the industry's dependence on federal policy.
Vice President Kamala Harris visited the Dalton plant earlier in 2023.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp has wrestled with Democratic U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff over political credit for electric vehicle and renewable energy investments flowing to Georgia.
Kemp stated that Qcells chose to operate and expand in Georgia because of the state's unrivaled assets and a competitive package put together by the state.
Ossoff and fellow Georgia Democratic Sen. Raphael Warnock note they authored the component incentives that benefit Qcells.
Ossoff stated that Georgia is emerging as the advanced energy capital of the nation thanks to federal infrastructure and manufacturing policies benefiting the state more than any other.
Qcells praises both state and federal assistance for building these markets and making them work through a whole government approach.
source_title: Solar panel company opens massive $171M plant in Georgia | FOX 5 Atlanta

canonical_name: LG Energy Solution Vertech & Qcells Partnership
facts: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells have announced a multi-year commitment to install American-made storage products on Qcells development projects across the United States.
The agreement includes 5 GWh of lithium-ion energy storage systems for utility-scale projects, as well as lifecycle services from LG Energy Solution Vertech.
In 2024, Qcells entered into battery purchase agreements with LG Energy Solution Vertech totaling 4.8 GWh of BESS projects developed across the United States.
The new 5-GWh partnership secures energy storage systems for Qcells that meet domestic content requirements.
LG Energy Solutions battery energy storage products are made in Michigan, and Qcells solar panels are made in Georgia.
Chris Hodrick, CEO of Qcells EPC, stated: "Qcells is proud to partner with LG Energy Solution on a project that shows whats possible when energy development supports U.S. manufacturing."

The collaboration reflects a shared commitment to building clean energy the right way by creating American jobs, strengthening supply chains, and delivering reliable, affordable power for communities across the country.
The energy storage systems included in this commitment are built using the latest LG Energy Solution containerized lithium-ion battery solutions with LFP cells manufactured at U.S. facilities.
LG Energy Solution is manufacturing up to 16.5 GWh of battery energy storage cells in Holland, Michigan.
Production at LG Energy Solution sites across North America is set to ramp up to around 50 GWh by the end of 2026.
Jaehong Park, CEO and President at LG Energy Solution Vertech, stated: "Were excited to continue our partnership with Qcells as we begin a new phase together."
LG Energy Solution Vertech is committed to supporting projects throughout their lifecycles to enable the long-term success of its customers energy storage programs.
Qcells operates the largest silicon solar panel manufacturing facility in the United States, based in Dalton, Georgia.
The company is building a second factory in Cartersville, Georgia, where it will manufacture ingots, wafers, cells and finished panels.
Once fully operational, Qcells annual solar panel production capacity in the US will reach 8.4 GW.
source_title: LG Energy Solution Vertech & Qcells: 5 GWh U.S. Storage Deal for American-Made Clean Energy - News and Statistics - IndexBox

entity_name: Georgia Clean Energy and EV Supply Chain
canonical_name: State of Georgia Clean Energy and Electric Vehicle Supply Chain
facts: Georgia is potentially facing the greatest loss of clean energy jobs in the nation if the House GOP’s “big, beautiful bill,” which is now in the Senate, is passed.

A new report released by Senator Raphael Warnock estimates that as many as 42,000 jobs, created by 51 clean energy projects that represent more than $28 billion in investments statewide, will be in jeopardy if the clean energy tax credits included in the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) are repealed.
The report highlights Georgia’s position as the top beneficiary of IRA incentives, leading in clean energy job growth with the most announced or advanced projects underway.
Following the IRA’s passage in 2022, Georgia has seen more than $4.50 in private investment for every $1 of federal funding received.

Senator Raphael Warnock stated that Georgia businesses and workers agree that investments should be protected because they are spurring good-paying manufacturing jobs across the state, supporting Georgia jobs, manufacturing, and innovation, but those good-paying clean energy jobs are under threat.

Warnock said we should not be sacrificing Georgia jobs for tax cuts for the well-off and cannot allow political games to derail a generational economic transformation that is already putting people to work and creating economic opportunity.
Georgia has led the nation in renewable energy growth since former President Joe Biden signed the IRA into law in 2022.
Sen. Warnock’s analysis details the Peach State’s massive influx of public and private investment in domestic manufacturing since the beginning of 2023.
Georgia’s $28 billion in investments is second only to New York’s $115 billion.
While 42,000 new jobs have been created in Georgia—the most of any state—that figure is poised to grow significantly in the next few years as more clean energy projects come online.

According to the Department of Energy, Georgia’s clean energy workforce is set to be “turbocharged” by an estimated $180 million investment in large-scale clean power generation and storage through 2030.

The majority of these jobs and investments are primarily in Georgia’s rural areas and counties, where families with incomes below the national median and the lowest rates of higher education levels live.
Most of those areas also happen to be within Republican-led congressional districts.
Three of the top ten GOP districts for clean energy job growth are in Georgia: Rep. Buddy Carter’s 1st District, where a Hyundai Metaplant is located in Bryan County.
Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene’s 14th District is home to a QCells solar factory in Dalton.
A new Qcells solar plant has been built in Georgia's 11th congressional district represented by Rep. Barry Loudermilk.
Rep. Mike Collins’ 10th District is where SK Battery is based in Commerce, Georgia.
More than 85% of funding from the IRA has flowed to red states, according to E2.
According to Sen. Warnock’s report, government funding for clean energy in Georgia jumped from just over $200 million in 2022 to more than $1.1 billion in 2023, and $2.3 billion in 2024.
The report calls out the 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production tax credit as a provision that incentivizes domestic production of clean energy components.
The report calls out the 48C Advanced Energy Project tax credit as a provision that supports investment in facilities that produce these components.
The report calls out the 30D New Clean Vehicle credit as a provision that provides up to $7,500 to qualified buyers of new clean energy (EV) vehicles that are assembled in North America.
These tax credits have been instrumental in onshoring the clean energy supply chain and bolstering factory buildout in Georgia, turning it into the EV and domestic manufacturing hub it is today.
If President Trump gets his way in repealing what he calls the “Green New Scam,” Georgia’s clean manufacturing investment boom could be stopped in its tracks.

The current budget reconciliation bill would eliminate or accelerate the phaseout of many IRA credits to pay for the $4.5 trillion tax cuts for the wealthy and large corporations that Trump is calling for, as well as introduce a number of stricter eligibility requirements.
Among the proposed changes to IRA, the Investment tax credit (48 ITC) would be reduced to 6% by 2030 and eliminated by 2032.
45X credits would begin to phase out by 25% per year for components sold after December 31, 2029, before expiring after 2031.
The consumer tax credit for new EVs would phase out by the end of 2026.

The current legislation would impose new restrictions on project financing, credit transferability, and eligibility for projects with ties to “foreign entities of concern” (FEOCs), such as China, which dominates the supply chain for raw materials and components used in clean energy technologies.

Clean energy projects must begin construction no later than 60 days after the bill’s passage and be placed in service by the end of 2028—a timeline that could be unworkable for many announced or early-stage projects.

Earlier this year, battery manufacturers Freyr Battery and Aspen Aerogels scrapped plans to build new factories in Georgia—projects that would have created 1,400 jobs and brought nearly $3 billion in investment to the state.
These losses constitute just a fraction of the $14 billion in clean energy projects and 10,000 jobs which have been already cancelled since Trump took office in January, according to E2.
Qcells continues to expand its new solar manufacturing facility in Cartersville, which is bringing approximately 3.3 GW of annual solar capacity and up to 2,000 jobs to the state.
In Cedartown, SolarCycle plans to invest $344 million in a solar glass manufacturing facility, the first of its kind in the U.S. to make new solar glass out of recycled materials from solar panels.
The SolarCycle facility is expected to begin operations later this year and create more than 600 jobs.

Hyundai's new $7.6 billion Metaplant America EV factory near Savannah and Qcells’ Dalton plant expansion are already transforming Georgia's manufacturing landscape and proving the economic power of American-made clean energy.
IRA rollbacks would impact local solar installers, nonprofits, and families relying on its funding, who will feel the consequences of a repeal much sooner.

Under the current tax bill, the 25D residential solar tax credit, which provides a credit to U.S. homeowners equal to 30% of their solar installation costs, would be eliminated by the end of the year.

Clean energy credits for individuals such as 25D and 30D, along with a range of other IRA programs—from subsidies enabling low-income households to install rooftop solar to home energy rebates reducing the cost of electrification—are already making tangible impacts on people’s daily lives.

Seth Gunning, CEO of Sunpath Solar, a local Georgia-based provider of solar energy solutions, says an IRA repeal would not only hurt his business, but the communities and homeowners he helps on an everyday basis.

Gunning stated that many projects enabled by churches and organizations of faith around Georgia have been enabled by the Inflation Reduction Act's direct pay or elective pay provision, which allows nonprofits to capitalize on some of the tax incentives that they previously didn't have access to.
Without credits like 25C, Gunning says those in need will be the ones excluded from the clean energy transition.

Gunning noted the real impact is going to be to homeowners, farmers, local small business owners, and nonprofit faith communities who are already struggling to make ends meet and to pay increasingly high costs of power, stating that not having a way to access solar energy resources makes life that much more expensive.

Gunning advised Congress that consistency and certainty are key, calling the immediate elimination of the residential tax credit and the 60-day requirement for commenced construction on the commercial tax credit really abrupt changes that make it hard for homeowners, businesses, nonprofits, and solar installers to effectively plan for the future.
Gunning suggested providing a roadmap, ideally several years, to sunset those credits is much better for the industry to be able to get its feet fully under itself.

The House budget bill would have serious consequences for Georgia if it retains these cuts to clean energy tax credits: ending the Inflation Reduction Act's tax incentives would jeopardize billions in investments, kill good-paying jobs, and raise household electric bills by around $143 more per year for the average American household.
source_title: The top state for clean energy jobs could lose them all to GOP budget bill cuts | EnergySage

facts: Hanwha Solutions’ Qcells is one of the world’s largest and most recognized photovoltaic (PV) manufacturers, known for its high-quality solar cells and modules.
The company selected Gray to build its first U.S. facility, an award-winning solar module assembly plant in Dalton, GA.
Qcells soon selected Gray for a second project for a new facility with 420,000 s.f. of production space, a 400,000 s.f. raw and finished goods warehouse, and a corporate office complex.
Qcells announced plans to partner with Gray on the construction of a production facility that President Biden described as “the largest solar investment in U.S. history.”
The new facility will create thousands of good-paying jobs.
Gray aims to strengthen its relationship with Qcells for the benefit of their companies, communities, and American-made renewable energy.
The newest production facility is located in White, GA.
The project timeline indicates it will be operational in 26 months.
Qcells has a customer relationship with Gray that stretches back to 2005.
The newest manufacturing complex is located in White, GA, just outside of Cartersville and only 35 miles from the Dalton campus.
The complex will be the first in the U.S. to execute the total manufacture of silicon ingots and wafers, solar cells, and finished solar modules.
The facility will boast an annual production capacity of 3.3 gigawatts (GWDC).
This capacity solidifies Qcells' status as the market leader in domestic solar energy.
The project has received national recognition as the largest investment in domestic solar energy production.
The endeavor was largely made possible by the passing of the Inflation Reduction Act and its related incentives for manufacturers of advanced technology to begin U.S. operations.
The Gray team developed a close partnership with Qcells to meet aggressive schedules and deliver a first-class facility.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in mid-2025.
source_title: An Industry-Defining Project | Total Solar Production Solution

facts: FREYR Battery has scrapped plans to build a $2.6 billion battery energy storage system factory in Coweta County, Georgia.
The company told officials in the Atlanta suburb of Newnan on Thursday that it would not build the “Giga America” battery factory in Georgia.
The planned Georgia factory was expected to employ more than 700 people.
FREYR had been incentivized by the Biden administration’s Inflation Reduction Act tax credits and robust renewables growth.
The company moved its headquarters from Norway to Georgia and announced the battery factory in 2023.

FREYR cited climbing interest rates, falling battery prices, and a change in leadership as reasons for canceling the project, or stated it was a “realignment of near-term strategic goals” in a letter to the county authority.
The company entered into a definitive agreement to sell the Georgia factory’s 368-acre site to an undisclosed party for gross sales proceeds of $50 million.
The site sale transaction is expected to close on February 15.
Estimated net proceeds to FREYR from the sale are expected to total $22.5 million following repayment of previously received state and local grants.
In August, then-CEO Tom Einar Jensen told investors that a surplus of cheap Chinese batteries had made it harder to raise money to manufacture batteries.
The company switched its strategic focus from battery manufacturing to solar panel manufacturing.
On December 24, FREYR announced it had closed its acquisition of Trina Solar’s 5-gigawatt (GW), 1.35 million-square-foot solar panel factory in Wilmer, Texas.
The Texas acquisition solidified FREYR’s transformation from a European battery company into a US solar company.
The company announced it is moving its global corporate headquarters to Austin.
source_title: FREYR kills plans to build a $2.6 billion battery factory in Georgia | Electrek

facts: Clean energy manufacturers canceled, closed or downsized nearly $8 billion in projects in the first quarter of 2025.
Clean energy advocacy nonprofit E2 announced the cancellations last week as part of its monthly tracking of clean energy project announcements in the U.S.
This was the first time E2 has opted to include cancellations and downsizes as part of its tracking, as the number of abandoned projects grows across the U.S.
Cancellations included lithium battery maker Kore Power’s cancellation of its planned $1.2 billion factory in Arizona.
Cancellations included Freyr Battery’s cancellation of its $2.6 billion Georgia battery factory.
The cancellations spanned 16 projects in sectors such as wind, solar and electric vehicle manufacturing.

In the years following the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022, manufacturers poured hundreds of billions of dollars into clean energy projects in a bid to cash in on the law’s lucrative tax credits for domestic manufacturing, as well as its direct funding and loan financing.
On his first day in office, President Donald Trump froze IRA funding pending a federal review.
Last week, a federal judge ordered the funding temporarily reinstated while a lawsuit from six climate groups is under consideration.

Trump has been critical of the climate law and what he calls former President Joe Biden’s “electric vehicle mandate,” referring to Biden’s 2021 executive order that called for half of new vehicles sold in the U.S. to be electric by 2030.
The president has also criticized EV tax credits provided under the IRA.
Further compounding this issue are the Trump administration’s tariffs, which could drive up supply costs for everything from solar panels to EV batteries.

For example, the U.S. still largely relies on imports for solar cell components – in 2022, roughly 88% of solar shipments were imports, primarily from Asia, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.
While cancellations and scaled down plans made up the bulk of clean energy announcements in the first quarter of the year, some companies are still opting to invest in the U.S.
March brought $1.6 billion in project investments across six states, including an $850,000 solar cell factory from T1 Energy.
After canceling its Georgia battery factory, Freyr Battery rebranded and relaunched as T1 Energy, with a new focus on solar energy.

E2 Communications Director Michael Timberlake said in a statement that clean energy companies still want to invest in America, but uncertainty over Trump administration policies and the future of critical clean energy tax credits are taking a clear toll.
Timberlake warned that if self-inflicted and unnecessary market uncertainty continues, more projects will be paused, construction halted, and job opportunities disappear.
source_title: Clean energy manufacturers cancel projects as Trump-era policies take hold | Construction Dive

facts: Freyr Battery called off plans to build a $2.57 billion battery cell manufacturing plant in Newnan, Georgia.
The company stated the facility is no longer part of its strategic goals.
Freyr notified Coweta County officials of the cancellation via a letter last month.
The Newnan plant was initially announced in 2022.
The canceled facility would have created 723 jobs.
First-phase production for the Newnan plant was projected at approximately 34 gigawatt hours.
The decision to scrap the Newnan plant occurred a month after Freyr acquired Trina Solar’s 5-gigawatt solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas.
Freyr is pivoting to establish a commercial presence in the U.S. solar market.
Information regarding the pivot was detailed in a November 2024 presentation by the company.
Trina Solar originally announced an investment of more than $200 million to establish the Wilmer plant in September 2023.
Approximately one year later, global banking company Standard Chartered secured a $235 million green loan to finance the development and construction of the Wilmer facility.
The Wilmer plant spans 1.35 million square feet.
Upon completion, the Wilmer facility will create up to 1,500 jobs.
The Wilmer site will source polysilicon from the U.S. and Europe.
The Wilmer facility will feature seven production lines.
Production at the Wilmer plant began on November 1, 2024.
Freyr announced on February 10 that it selected Austin, Texas, as its new global headquarters.
The Austin headquarters location brings the company closer to the Wilmer site in the Dallas-Fort Worth metropolitan area.
Freyr is currently developing a separate 5 GW solar cell manufacturing facility in the U.S.
Freyr plans to announce the location of the new solar cell factory in the coming months.
Production at the new solar cell facility is expected to begin in the first half of 2026.
The company expects to spend $850 million on the new solar cell facility.
The new solar cell facility is expected to create 1,800 jobs.
Both the Wilmer plant and the new solar cell factory are fully eligible for Inflation Reduction Act production tax credits.
Freyr plans to apply for the IRA production tax credits.
The future of these tax credits under President Trump remains unclear due to his vocal opposition to the subsidies.
source_title: Freyr Battery cancels $2.6B Georgia battery factory plans | Manufacturing Dive

facts: FREYR Battery announced Austin, Texas as the location for its new global headquarters to geographically align its workforce with operations and strategy to provide solar modules made in the United States.

The announcement coincides with the ongoing ramp of production at FREYR’s G1 solar module facility in Wilmer, Texas, which already employs more than 1,000 people across the Wilmer and Dallas communities.

Austin is strategically located near FREYR’s operating U.S. solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas, and provides access to an emerging talent pool within the booming, business-friendly Texas economy to support growth aspirations.
FREYR’s global headquarters will be located at 1211 E 4th St., Austin, TX 78746.

Daniel Barcelo, FREYR’s Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer, stated that establishing the headquarters marks an important step in executing a strategic plan to establish an integrated U.S. solar + battery storage manufacturing enterprise built on advanced technology.
Daniel Barcelo noted that Austin is a vibrant and fast-growing hub of renewable energy and advanced technology development, strategically located close to operations.
As part of the strategy to build an integrated American solar + storage manufacturing network, FREYR intends to bring more than 1,000 new American jobs to the Texas economy.
FREYR plans to build teams in Austin and Wilmer, establish a Texas job creation engine, and work with partners across industry and government to invest in critical U.S. infrastructure.

In conjunction with the relocation to Texas, FREYR has entered into a definitive agreement to sell its 368-acre site in Coweta County, Georgia, to an undisclosed party for gross sales proceeds of $50 million.
The sale transaction is expected to close on February 15, 2024.
Estimated net proceeds to FREYR from the Georgia site sale are expected to total $22.5 million following repayment of previously received state and local grants.
FREYR is an energy solutions provider building an integrated U.S. supply chain for solar and batteries.

In December 2024, FREYR completed a transformative transaction, positioning the Company as one of the leading solar manufacturing companies in the U.S., with a complementary solar and battery storage strategy.
Based in the U.S. with plans to expand operations in America, the Company is also exploring value optimization opportunities across its portfolio of assets in Europe.
source_title: FREYR Battery Announces Austin, Texas as Location for New Corporate Headquarters

facts: The electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) industry is approaching a critical milestone in 2026.
Joby Aviation and Archer Aviation are moving through the final stages of FAA type certification for their air taxi aircraft.
If certified, these aircraft will represent the first commercially operated electric air taxis in the United States.
Joby Aviation is backed by Toyota and operates with a Part 135 air carrier certificate.
Joby Aviation has completed thousands of test flights.
Joby Aviation is the furthest along in the FAA certification process.
The company’s five-seat eVTOL aircraft has a range of approximately 150 miles.
The company’s five-seat eVTOL aircraft has a top speed of 200 mph.
The aircraft's speed is fast enough to turn a 90-minute ground commute into a 15-minute flight.
Joby’s partnership with Toyota brings manufacturing expertise that pure aerospace startups lack.
Building aircraft at automotive scale requires different manufacturing processes, quality systems, and supply chains than traditional aviation.
Archer Aviation is focusing on shorter-range urban missions with its Midnight aircraft.
Archer Aviation has secured partnerships with United Airlines.
Archer Aviation is building a manufacturing facility in Georgia designed for high-volume production from day one.
Archer’s strategy mirrors what Tesla did with electric cars by designing for manufacturing from the start rather than adapting a prototype.
Archer’s strategy is a bet that the bottleneck will be production capacity, not demand.
The strategy assumes whoever scales manufacturing first wins.
Electric air taxis need landing pads (vertiports) in urban areas.
Electric air taxis need charging infrastructure.
Electric air taxis need air traffic management systems.
Electric air taxis need public acceptance.
These infrastructure challenges are arguably harder to solve than the aircraft themselves.
Cities like Los Angeles, New York, and Miami are actively planning vertiport networks.
Vertiport construction and permitting take years.
Electric air taxis will not replace cars or trains for most trips.
Electric air taxis are a premium option for time-sensitive travel including airport transfers, cross-city commutes, and emergency medical transport.
Premium technologies have a way of becoming mainstream as costs decrease.
Uber started as a luxury car service and ended up replacing taxis worldwide.
The eVTOL industry represents a $1 trillion potential market by 2040.
Companies that achieve certification first and scale manufacturing fastest will capture outsized value in this emerging market.
Joby and Archer are the frontrunners in the race to dominate the eVTOL market.
The race for eVTOL dominance is far from over.
source_title: Electric Aviation Takes Flight: Where Joby and Archer Stand in 2026 — Taha Salahuddin Abbasi

facts: Archer Aviation announced it will invest $118 million to construct a plant near Atlanta, Georgia.
The company plans to eventually hire up to 1,000 people for the facility.
The aircraft plant will be built adjoining an airport in Covington, Georgia.
Archer Aviation is one of many companies trying to build electric air taxis.
The company is building a battery-powered vertical takeoff and landing craft with six propellers that holds four passengers and a pilot.
The eVTOL craft is designed for short flights, especially in urban areas.
United Airlines announced it would fly the craft from downtown Manhattan to Newark Liberty International Airport in New Jersey.
The planned flight route would cut a trip that can take an hour by car in congested traffic down to 10 minutes.
The U.S. Air Force is evaluating Archer's aircraft for possible use.
Archer Aviation is seeking Federal Aviation Administration certification, with a potential certification date of 2024.
The company aims to produce 650 aircraft annually at the Georgia site.
Annual production capacity at the Georgia site has the potential to rise to 2,300 aircraft per year.
Archer expects to receive an incentive package worth some $40 million from Georgia and local governments.
The incentive package includes the donation of 96 acres of land at Covington Municipal Airport.
The incentive package also includes a cash grant from the state and other tax incentives.
Archer chose Georgia due to the availability of local workers, the ability to conduct test flights, affordable construction costs, and good highway, rail, and air connections.

Archer CEO Adam Goldstein stated that their eVTOL technology can transform how urban and rural communities live and commute, and the factory can create pathways to highly skilled manufacturing hubs and other ladders of social and economic opportunity.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that economic recruiters specializing in aerospace had worked with Archer.
Pat Wilson stated that technology and innovation continue to drive change across strategic industries.
source_title: Archer ok's Georgia for electric air taxi factory

facts: Archer received the certificate of occupancy for its ~400,000 square foot aircraft manufacturing facility—referred to as ARC—in Covington, GA.
Archer held a commemorative ribbon cutting ceremony for the facility.
Tooling load-in is already underway ahead of the planned start of initial production beginning in early 2025.
The goal is to ramp production to a rate of two aircraft per month by the end of the year.

Stellantis and Archer are working to finalize an agreement pursuant to which Stellantis would work with Archer as its exclusive contract manufacturer to mass produce Archer’s Midnight eVTOL aircraft at this facility.
Stellantis is contributing capital, advanced manufacturing technology and expertise and experienced personnel to the partnership.
The goal of the partnership is scaling the facility to 650 aircraft annually by 2030.
ARC is connected to the Covington Municipal Airport in Georgia.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp commented that the choice to build the new manufacturing facility in Newton County marks the next step in an incredible relationship and reaffirms Georgia’s commitment to creating quality jobs and opportunities for years to come.

Congressman Mike Collins of Georgia, who sits on the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, stated that the completion highlights Georgia’s leadership in innovation, advanced manufacturing and job creation.

Archer founder and CEO Adam Goldstein commented that the completion is a testament to the industry shifting from R&D into commercialization and praised the team for getting construction done fast and on budget.

Stellantis Chief Manufacturing and Supply Chain Officer Arnaud Deboeuf stated that supporting Archer’s electric aircraft growth with deep manufacturing expertise is a great opportunity for both companies.
ARC is where Archer plans to produce its all-electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft, Midnight.
The Midnight aircraft is designed to offer passengers a sustainable, low-noise and safe alternative to ground transportation in congested cities around the world.

links.context: SEC filings including Annual Report on Form 10-K and Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q

source_title: Archer Aviation - Archer Completes Construction of High-Volume Manufacturing Facility

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Duckyang will open its first U.S. facility in Jackson County.
Duckyang is an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA).
The company will invest $10 million in the new manufacturing facility.
The new facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, Georgia.

Governor Kemp stated that Duckyang is creating high-paying, advanced automotive manufacturing jobs for Georgians and praised Georgia's workforce training programs and its status as the number one state for business for eight years.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
Duckyang supplies cockpit modules to customers around the world including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.
Cockpit modules are integrated auto parts comprised of more than 130 subcomponents found near the driver’s seat of a vehicle.
The Georgia facility will allow Duckyang to better serve several of its automotive customers within the region.

Dong-in Son, CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd., stated that entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA provides opportunities to cooperate with Hyundai and Kia Motors and expand business to supply EV parts, interior parts, cockpit module, door trim panel module, and other flagship parts to global automakers in the U.S.
The new facility will be 230,000-square-foot.
The facility address is 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton.
Duckyang will be hiring for positions in production, maintenance, logistics, and warehouse management.
Jennifer Scott, Town Manager and Clerk of Braselton, expressed delight at welcoming a new manufacturing facility to the town.

John Scott, Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, noted that companies like Duckyang are helping secure Jackson County and Georgia's place in the electric automotive industry's future.

Yoonie Kim, Director of Korean Investment, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on the project in partnership with the Jackson County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain and highlighted strong relationships with SK Battery America and Duckyang as examples of bringing suppliers to the state to secure local supply.
The State of Georgia facilitated more than $1.3 billion in investment and more than 2,700 jobs from South Korean companies during 2020 alone.
Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing and continues to attract business globally.
source_title: New auto manufacturing facility to arrive in Braselton

facts: Georgia is emerging as a national leader in advanced manufacturing thanks to investment attracted through strong international relationships.
The Jackson Alliance for Economic Development is the central economic development organization for Jackson County, Georgia.
Organized under the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, the Alliance has been successful in attracting investment.

Duckyang, a supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA), announced it would invest US$10 million in its first US manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
South Korean companies are major investors in the state of Georgia.
Government figures put total investments from South Korean firms in 2020 at US$1.3 billion.
South Korean firms created over 2,700 jobs in 2020.
In June 2020, SK Innovation, a developer and manufacturer of lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicles (EVs), increased its investment in its Georgia manufacturing plant by US$940 million.
In 2019, SK Innovation invested US$1.67 billion in a manufacturing plant in the state.
With the new investment, total job creation by SK stands at 2,600.
Jun Kim, CEO of SK Innovation, stated: “SK innovation’s solid partnership with Georgia will allow SK to continue investing in Georgia, the U.S. economy, and the U.S. electric vehicle industry.”
South Enchem Ltd is spending US$61 million to build two new manufacturing plants in Jackson County.
Enchem specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes for rechargeable batteries and electrostatic double-layer capacitors (ELDCs).
Enchem was the first supplier to follow SK Innovation, which broke ground in Jackson County in March 2019 on a US$1.67 billion battery manufacturing plant.
Since its founding in 2012, Enchem has developed multiple new electrolytes and functional additives to improve and extend battery life.
Governor Brian Kemp stated: “I am excited to welcome Enchem to Georgia.”

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “Building on the success of SK Innovation in Jackson County, this is exactly the type of quality project that we hoped to attract on our first international trip in June 2020. As the market for electric vehicles and batteries continues its rapid growth, it’s great to see the industry building a hub here in Georgia. Our strong international partnerships – like the one we have established between South Korea and Georgia – will continue to benefit hardworking Georgians and create opportunities throughout the state in the years ahead.”
Toyota Industries Compressor Parts America (TICA) announced in October 2020 that it would expand its Pendergrass, Georgia, production facility to meet growing demand for onboard power electronics.
Toyota is investing US$5.5 million in a project that will create at least 27 new jobs in Jackson County over the next 18 months.
Toyota set up the TICA facility in 2012 with an initial investment of US$350 million to supply automotive compressors for Toyota Motor Corporation, Chrysler, Ford, General Motors, and others.
Toyota Industries Compressor Parts works in concert with the adjacent TD Automotive Compressor Georgia facility, which assembles parts manufactured at the TICA plant.
The combined facilities encompass over one million square feet of production space and a total investment of more than US$1 billion.

Kota Otoshi, executive officer for the electronics division of TICA’s parent company Toyota Industries Corporation, stated: “We are very pleased to join TICA’s operations in Georgia... We would like to expand our electrical products from this plant and our onboard power electronics to our market in North America. We look forward to being a corporate citizen who can contribute to the economic and social development of Georgia through the production and sale of our onboard power electronics.”
In 2018, GE Appliances invested US$55 million in a new Southeast Region Appliance Distribution Center in Commerce.

GE Appliances had a strong presence in Georgia with 1,960 employees at Roper Corporation, a wholly owned subsidiary in LaFayette, and 50 employees supporting its Factory Service Operations based out of Atlanta.
John Scott is Vice President and Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce.

When complete, SK Innovation's Georgia location will give it a projected annual global capacity of 71 gigawatt hours (together with facilities in Europe and Asia), making it one of the leading makers of EV batteries in the world.
Jackson County is centrally located in the Southeast United States’ growing automotive industry.
The SK Group was among the first Korean investors in Georgia and has a longstanding history in the state.
Georgia works aggressively to ensure companies like SK Innovation have the best resources necessary for success.
Enchem's investment validates Jackson County’s thriving business climate attracting top global companies.
Duckyang's investment helps Jackson County and Georgia secure a place in the automotive industry's future as it moves toward electric vehicles.
Strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies like Duckyang help bring suppliers to the state to secure local supply of needed parts.
Toyota's expansion adds new jobs for Georgians and proves the business climate, workforce investments, logistics infrastructure, and quality of life make a compelling case for growth.
Jackson County’s strategic location, strong workforce, and quality of life continue to attract significant investment from companies like GE Appliances.
GE utilizes Jackson County's highly skilled workforce and business-ready transportation infrastructure to strengthen its competitive edge and reach customers throughout the Southeast.
source_title: Jackson Alliance helping to establish Georgia as advanced manufacturing hub - AI Online -

facts: Georgia is a premier distribution and logistics hub in the heart of the Southeast.
Industry leaders including Amazon, Cargill, Hapag-Lloyd, Hyundai Motor Group, SAIA, and UPS have been attracted to Georgia’s logistics strengths.
In 2025, the state’s exports reached 213 countries and territories, with total trade hitting $210.7 billion.
Ongoing infrastructure expansions include increased warehouse space and extensive cold storage development for perishable goods and agricultural sectors.
Georgia ranks nationally for efficient movement of goods by Area Development in 2025.
The Port of Savannah is the fastest-growing single-terminal port in the nation, according to Georgia Ports Authority.
More than 80% of the U.S. market is within a two-hour flight or two-day truck drive from Georgia.
The Port of Savannah ranks among the fastest-growing deepwater container ports in the U.S., with extensive on-terminal rail access and strong capacity for refrigerated containers.
The Port of Brunswick is the No. 1 port for automotive and heavy machinery Ro/Ro.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the world’s busiest, also operates as a major air-cargo hub.
The airport features cargo complexes exceeding 2 million square feet.
The airport features the Southeast’s only USDA-approved on-terminal perishable-goods facility.
The airport handles over 650,000 metric tons of cargo annually.
Georgia's rail network is the largest in the Southeast, backed by Class I providers CSX and Norfolk Southern.
Georgia's highway network includes 1,200 miles of interstate highways and 20,000 miles of federal and state roads.
Half of the world’s top 100 third-party logistics (3PL) providers operate in Georgia.
Georgia’s warehousing footprint spans hundreds of millions of square feet, including specialized cold storage that supports perishables and high-value products.
One in nine jobs in the state are tied to the logistics industry.
The state consistently ranks high for access to qualified talent, especially in logistics, transportation, and supply chain functions.
Georgia invests heavily in workforce development to support industry growth.

A broad network of logistics programs across the University System of Georgia, technical colleges, and career academies trains students on logistics operations, freight management, supply chain analytics, and more.

Strategic partnerships between educators, employers, and state workforce initiatives ensure that training aligns with industry needs, helping companies recruit and retain skilled workers for today and tomorrow.
source_title: Logistics in Georgia | Georgia
