canonical_name: Toyota Motor Engineering and Manufacturing North America
facts: The Treasure Hunt program’s purpose includes promoting awareness and benefits of energy reduction within industrial plants.
The program aims to find new ways to identify energy efficiency opportunities through facility-wide Treasure Hunts.
The program shares technical resources with other partners through tools, knowledge, and resources.
The program builds strong relationships with national suppliers.
The program’s creation reflects Toyota’s commitment to sustainability.
Toyota believes sustainability creates three values for society: Safety and Peace of Mind, Environmental Sustainability, and Waku-doki (excitement and exhilaration that wows you).
Toyota has placed the environment as a management priority in all stages of vehicle production.

Every five years Toyota establishes the Toyota Environmental Action Plan consisting of three priority themes: contribution to a low carbon society, contribution to a recycling-based society, and environmental conservation and contribution to a society in harmony with nature.
To go beyond zero environmental impact and achieve a net positive impact, Toyota has set six challenges for itself.
Challenge 1 is the New Vehicle Zero CO2 Emissions Challenge.
Challenge 2 is the Life Cycle Zero CO2 Emissions Challenge.
Challenge 3 is the Plant Zero CO2 Emissions Challenge.
Challenge 4 is the Challenge of Minimizing and Optimizing Water Usage.
Challenge 5 is the Challenge of Establishing a Recycling-based Society and Systems.
Challenge 6 is the Challenge of Establishing a Future Society in Harmony with Nature.
Toyota’s Supplier Relations team works closely in the Treasure Hunt recruitment process.
Together with the Purchasing team, Toyota sends out a voluntary application process for suppliers interested in the program.
The Purchasing team functions as the main point of contact for supplier applications.
Suppliers are asked if they are willing to open their facility to Toyota and non-competitive suppliers for a complete energy audit.
Once the Purchasing team identifies four suppliers (one per quarter), Toyota’s facility engineers are selected to facilitate the Treasure Hunt.
The Supplier Relations team helps promote the program through a network of suppliers interested in water and energy efficiency savings.
Preliminary steps before the on-site Treasure Hunt include an introductory call to familiarize the host supplier with the program and Toyota’s facility engineers.
An in-depth Data Collection call is held next, allowing Toyota to do initial research.
The team collects key data metrics including total electricity use, HVAC tonnage, lighting systems, exhaust fans, and hours of operation.
Once data is aggregated and potential resources are identified, a Kick-off Meeting is scheduled.
The Kick-off Meeting is set up over web conference and brings together the host supplier, Toyota’s facility engineers, and other non-competitive Toyota suppliers participating in the Treasure Hunt.
Program logistics include Toyota delivering a Detail Sheet along with specific energy calculation tools.
Teams of 5 to 10 are formed, consisting of a mix of members from every party.
The Treasure Hunt begins on Sunday so teams can monitor the facility while it’s still asleep (production offline yet energy still being consumed).
Teams arrive early Monday morning to see the facility wake up, and return on Tuesday to observe break times (lunch) throughout the day.
Total onsite analysis typically takes over 4 hours.
Each team concludes their findings Tuesday afternoon.
Dozens of savings opportunities are likely identified, but only the most cost-effective projects (3-5) are selected.
Once a presentation is assembled (~10 Slides), the host supplier takes ownership of the project and delivers it to senior management.
Toyota provides a Detail Sheet (calculators) during the first Data Collection call.
The sheet aggregates provided information and runs a multivariate regression analysis.
For every Treasure Hunt, there are typically as many as 150 energy efficiency ideas.
Toyota’s calculation sheet contains a built-in macro which ranks the top energy and water savings opportunities.
Supplier success is measured by potential dollars saved.
For Toyota, a more energy-efficient supply chain can cut down on costs, improve production reliability and transparency, and improve competitiveness.
Toyota bases its success on replicability for other facilities.
Since FY08, 40 Suppliers have hosted training events.
Employees from 180 Tier 1 suppliers have participated and been trained on the Treasure Hunt process.
There has been $4.4 M in total annual energy reduction opportunities identified.
The identified savings are equivalent to 43.5 million kilowatt-hours or 15,200 metric tons of CO2 per year.
A detail sheet tool is used to track energy data and progress towards energy savings.
source_title: Toyota Motor Engineering and Manufacturing North America: Supply Chain-Treasure Hunt Program | Better Buildings & Better Plants Initiative

facts: The 21st Century Truck Partnership addresses national challenges related to medium-duty and heavy-duty truck efficiency, safety, and emissions by fostering research collaboration and technical innovation among government and industry partners.

Industry partners include Allison Transmission Inc., Cummins, Inc., Daimler Trucks North America, Eaton, Ford Motor Company, General Motors Research, GILLIG LLC, International Motors, LLC, Oshkosh Corporation, PACCAR Inc, Robert Bosch NA, Stellantis, and Volvo Trucks.
Federal government partners include the U.S. Department of Defense, U.S. Department of Energy, U.S. Department of Transportation, and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.

Participating national laboratories are Argonne National Laboratory, Idaho National Laboratory, National Energy Technology Laboratory, National Laboratory of the Rockies, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, and Sandia National Laboratories.
Medium-duty and heavy-duty trucks and buses serve as the backbone of America's economy for moving freight and passengers.
These vehicles are essential for construction, building and servicing water and electricity infrastructure, collecting refuse, and maintaining roadways and highways.
The partnership's overall vision is for U.S. trucks and buses to safely and cost-effectively move larger volumes of freight and greater numbers of passengers while emitting little or no pollution.
The partnership accelerates technology development through collaborative, pre-regulatory, and pre-competitive R&D in component and system-level projects.

The partnership focuses R&D efforts on topics of broad interest by providing a discussion forum and organizing consensus building tools such as roadmaps and position papers to help members agree on R&D topics and goals.

The partnership conducts information exchange and dissemination through regular conference calls, meetings, and information dissemination tools to help partners access current industry and government activities.
Technical sector focus areas include Internal Combustion Engine Powertrains, covering engines, fuel, emissions equipment, transmissions, drive axles, and hybrid drive systems.
Technical sector focus areas include Electrification Technologies, covering traction motors, power electronics, energy storage, and charging and infrastructure.
Technical sector focus areas include Freight Operational Efficiency, covering vehicle duty cycle, automation and connectivity, and driver impacts.
Technical sector focus areas include Safety, covering advance driver assistance systems or ADAS, automated driving systems, and safety communications.
The partnership has developed four all-new roadmap documents to provide the technical foundation for research priorities within these technical areas.

links.context: Contact email for more information on the 21st Century Truck Partnership

source_title: 21st Century Truck Partnership | Department of Energy

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on March 18, 2025 that TriNet plans to create 750 new jobs at a new corporate center in Dunwoody over the next five years.
The expansion represents an estimated $15.4 million in investment in DeKalb County.
TriNet provides comprehensive HR solutions, technology, expertise, and access to world-class benefits that enable small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) to attract and develop top-tier talent.
Mike Simonds serves as TriNet President and CEO.
Catherine Wragg serves as TriNet Chief People Officer.
The new corporate center will occupy approximately 150,000 square feet of space in Dunwoody.
TriNet will immediately begin hiring for technology, HR consulting, client management, and sales roles.
The company plans to leverage its increased local presence to grow its regional Atlanta and Southeast customer base.
Dunwoody Mayor Lynn Deutsch highlighted the city's access to a talented workforce and vibrant mixed-use environment as ideal for TriNet's location.
DeKalb County CEO Lorraine Cochran-Johnson stated that the investment reinforces DeKalb as a premier destination for corporate growth and innovation.
Katie Kirkpatrick, President and CEO of the Metro Atlanta Chamber, noted TriNet's unique position to connect businesses with emerging talent from metro Atlanta’s renowned universities.
John Soper, Assistant Director of Statewide Projects, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the City of Dunwoody, Decide DeKalb, Metro Atlanta Chamber, University System of Georgia, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson emphasized that strong partnerships between industry, communities, and higher education drive economic growth in Georgia.
TriNet’s suite of products includes services and software-based solutions to streamline workflows by connecting HR, benefits, employee engagement, payroll, and time and attendance for SMBs.
source_title: TriNet to Create 750 Jobs in Metro Atlanta | Georgia

facts: Published in October 2023, the National Transmission Needs Study is an assessment of existing data and current and near-term future transmission needs through 2040.

The study assesses publicly available information and more than 120 recently published reports considering historic and anticipated future needs given electricity demand, public policy, and market conditions.

It serves as the DOE’s triennial state of the grid report and fulfills a Congressional requirement to conduct assessments of national electric transmission capacity constraints and congestion at least once every 3 years.
The expanded scope considers both historic and anticipated future capacity constraints and transmission congestion that could adversely affect consumers.
Nearly all regions in the United States would gain improved reliability and resilience from additional transmission investments.
Regions with historically high within-region congestion, particularly the Northwest, Mountain, Texas, and New York, need additional strategically placed transmission deployment to reduce congestion.

The largest benefit in new interregional transfer capacity additions is between the three interconnections: Mountain and Plains, Texas and Delta, Southwest and Texas, Plains and Midwest, and Plains and Delta regions.
Significant within-region transmission deployment is needed as soon as 2030 in the Plains, Midwest, and Texas regions.
By 2040, large deployments will also be needed in the Mountain, Mid-Atlantic, and Southeast regions.
In 2030, large relative interregional transmission deployments are needed between the Delta and Plains, Midwest and Plains, and Mid-Atlantic and Midwest regions.
By 2040, there is a significant need for new interregional transmission between nearly all regions.
DOE released a draft of the study to states, Tribes, and regional grid entities in October 2022, with a 30-day comment period.
During the consultation period, DOE hosted six informational webinars and received nearly 180 unique comments from 20 consultation entities.
An updated draft was released in February 2023 for a 45-day public comment period closing April 20, 2023.
A public webinar providing an overview of the findings was held on November 8, 2023.
source_title: National Transmission Needs Study | Department of Energy

entity_name: Trenton Channel Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) Project
canonical_name: Trenton Channel Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) Project
facts: The Trenton Channel Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) Project is located in Trenton, Michigan.
The project entails constructing a 220-Megawatt (MW)/880 MWh lithium iron-phosphate (LFP) BESS.
The project scope includes building a new substation.
The project scope includes building new transmission lines connecting the BESS and the substation.
The DOE Loan Programs Office (LPO) is considering whether to issue a loan guarantee of a funding facility to DTE Electric Company (DTE).
The potential loan guarantee would be issued pursuant to the LPO's authority under the EIR 1706 Program.
The Trenton Channel BESS Project is included in DTE’s recently released Integrated Resource Plan.
The document was published by the Office of NEPA Policy and Compliance on November 21, 2024.
source_title: CX-270751: DTE Electric Company Trenton Channel Battery Energy Storage System Project | Department of Energy

facts: Through the third quarter of fiscal year 2022 (July 1, 2021 - March 31, 2022), GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 251 project locations resulting in more than 35,400 new jobs and $12.9 billion in investment for the state.
Job creation totals account for a 48% increase compared to three-quarters through the previous fiscal year and a 6% increase compared to the prior year’s overall total.
Total investment shows a recorded 54% increase compared to the same time period last year.
Nearly 78% of both projects and investments were located outside the 10-county metro Atlanta area, with over half of the jobs created also outside this region.
Key industry sectors for recent economic development include automotive, advanced manufacturing, logistics and distribution, software and technology, and food processing.
Georgia is actively recruiting the entire EV supply chain to ensure an effective, efficient, and affordable environment for EV production.
Rivian plans to develop a $5 billion campus at Stanton Springs North and an electric vehicle service center in Atlanta that will provide training on EVs.

SK Battery America has partnered with American lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered materials startup Ascend Elements to recycle both cell and module lithium-ion battery manufacturing scrap from SK’s Jackson County facility.
Ascend Elements announced its first commercial-scale battery recycling plant in Covington in January.

Aurubis, a leading recycler of copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials worldwide, will invest $340 million in a ‘first-of-its-kind in the U.S.’ state-of-the-art recycling and secondary smelting facility for multi-metal recycling in Augusta.
Electrification solutions and sustainability leader Aspen Aerogels announced plans to locate a new 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility in Statesboro.
Aspen Aerogels will produce PyroThin materials at its new facility, which provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems.
In March, Moderna announced it will establish a new Enterprise Solutions Hub in Atlanta.
Last August, Intuitive announced it is expanding its Gwinnett County campus.
The Moderna and Intuitive projects represent a combined investment of more than $547.8 million and 1,425 new jobs.

Georgia Quick Start, the No. 1-ranking workforce training program administered through the Technical College System of Georgia for over 40 years, helped secure Pepsi Co.’s expansion in DeKalb County, Remington Firearms’ world-class research and development center in LaGrange, and Titan Steel Doors’ expansion in Hart County.
The Pepsi Co., Remington Firearms, and Titan Steel Doors projects alone represent 1,082 new jobs and $373 million in investment.
Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) deepwater and regional port projects played a key role in securing Serena & Lily Inc.’s new facility in Effingham County.
The GPA has been reporting record-breaking numbers for the past 19 consecutive months as it expedites plans for long-term growth.

The Savannah Harbor Expansion Project (SHEP) was recently completed, deepening a 40-mile stretch between the port and the Atlantic Ocean to accommodate super-sized freighters traveling from the expanded Panama Canal.
Inland ports are attracting investments, such as the Appalachian Regional Port driving Roper Corporation’s decision to invest $118 million in expanding its facilities in Walker County.
Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and the state’s extensive rail network serve as key assets driving Georgia’s growth.
The Georgia Department of Transportation maintains more than 1,200 miles of highway connecting companies to 80% of the country in two days or less of driving time.
This transportation system was integral to landing Ryder System Inc.’s new facility in Henry County that will create more than 250 new jobs.

Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia continues to be the preferred place to do business and emphasized the state's pro-business approach and strong partnership with homegrown and global companies.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted the department's mission to target strategic industries, support existing business expansions, and maintain momentum through the end of the year.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: New Georgia Economic Development Records Set for Jobs, Investment in Just Nine Months | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Lead Performer is Mainstream Engineering Corp., located in Rockledge, FL.
Partner organization is National Laboratory of the Rockies, located in Boulder, CO.
Document date is October 12, 2021.
DOE Total Funding for the project is $199,874.45.
FY20 DOE Funding allocated to the project is $199,874.45.
Project Term runs from June 28, 2021 to March 27, 2022.
Funding Type is Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) Phase 1.
Growing concerns regarding the impacts of CO2 emissions and climate change have prompted both states and the federal government to promote the adoption of renewable energy sources.
Combined with rapidly decreasing costs, the share of intermittent renewable energy generation on the grid, such as wind and solar, has increased rapidly.
Intermittent energy sources do not necessarily align with periods of high electric demand, putting additional stress on the grid.
Electric utilities are required to rely on high-cost, short-term, fossil-fuel power generation during peak demand.
Excess renewable generation capacity exists during specific periods, leading to curtailment of these resources.
No significant grid-level storage technology has yet gained traction.
Utilities are attempting a market-based approach to shift electric demand away from peak periods with Time-of-Use (TOU) pricing.
Consumers can easily shift some electric loads to off-peak periods, but heat and cooling (HVAC) systems must run at specific times of day when climate control is required.
HVAC systems are the single largest electrical load for most residential and commercial customers.
HVAC systems are not able to effectively shift their electrical usage and take advantage of TOU pricing.

Mainstream Engineering and partners at the National Laboratory of the Rockies will develop and demonstrate a low-cost thermal energy storage heat exchanger using water as a phase-change material (PCM).
The PCM heat exchanger (PCM-HX) can be integrated into existing residential and commercial scale HVAC systems.
The PCM-HX will be produced with advanced materials and manufacturing techniques.
During periods of low electrical demand where prices are low, the system charges by freezing the water in the PCM-HX.
During high demand periods the PCM-HX discharges, significantly reducing the electrical power required by the integrated HVAC system.
The product allows consumers to shift their electrical usage to off-peak periods and take advantage of lower TOU prices.
Adoption of this HVAC thermal storage technology will have significant benefits to individual consumers, grid stability, and the further adoption of intermittent renewable energy sources.

Integrating this thermal storage scheme into HVAC systems using either the Thermal Energy Storage Subcooler (TESS) or the Integrated Two-Phase Pump Loop (I2PPL) design will increase costs on the order of $800 to $2,500.
This cost increase represents a 20 to 60 percent increase in the cost of a new HVAC system.
Return on investment for the additional cost could be as low as two to four years depending on TOU rates.
TOU rates are only likely to become more advantageous in the future.
The technology is significantly cheaper than pure battery storage, which can cost on the order of $10,000 for even the most basic or low-capacity systems.

This technology could allow low-to-middle income households to participate in the green energy revolution that has thus far been limited to those who can finance solar and battery systems costing tens of thousands of dollars.

Shifting electrical loads will reduce increasing costs incurred by utilities and customers due to peak electrical demand and the effects of intermittent but low-cost energy sources, benefiting all users.
Energy storage will be required for adoption of significant percentages of renewable energy generation, particularly approaching the 2030 California 50% RPS.
This will become increasingly important as the nation and world attempt to limit carbon emission in the coming decades.
The Phase I effort will develop and demonstrate a novel PCM-HX that can be integrated into either the TESS or I2PPL schemes to achieve load shifting.
DOE Technology Manager is Fredericka Brown.
Lead Performer contact is Jeffrey Milkie, PhD, from Mainstream Engineering.
source_title: Ice Storage and Other Thermal Storage-Related Systems | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) operates the Critical Minerals and Materials Program with a vision to build reliable, resilient, affordable, and secure domestic critical mineral and materials supply chains that support the energy, manufacturing, and transportation economies while promoting safe solutions to meet current and future needs.

The program's strategy focuses on four pillars: Broaden and Expand Supply by securing resources from primary and secondary sources, co-produced materials, and international partners; Develop Alternatives to produce new materials with less disruption potential and design parts requiring little to no critical materials; Improve Materials and Manufacturing Efficiency through atom economy, waste reduction, and efficient mining/manufacturing/recycling; and Reuse and Recycle by remanufacturing, refurbishing, repairing, reusing, recycling, and repurposing materials.

DOE enables these strategy pillars through four cross-cutting functions: Analysis and advanced tools, Market assessment and development, International engagement and standards, and Education and workforce development.
The Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office (AMMTO) and Office of Geothermal (OG) Critical Minerals and Materials Accelerator requires letters of intent due 4/24/26 at 5 p.m. ET.
Full application deadlines for the AMMTO and OG Critical Minerals and Materials Accelerator Topic Area 3 are set for 7/23/2026.
The Office of Science (SC) Energy Frontier Research Centers (EFRC) requires a pre-application due 4/1/26 at 5 p.m. ET.
Full application deadlines for the SC EFRC are due 7/1/2026 at 11:59 p.m. ET.
The Office of Indian Energy Policy and Programs (OIE) Unleashing Tribal Energy Development opportunity is due 7/24/2026 at 5 p.m. ET.
The Office of Science Financial Assistance Program deadline is 9/30/2026.

Additional funding opportunities include CMEI Mines and Metals Capacity Expansion – Topic 1, ARPA-E ROCKS and MAGNITO FY26, ARPA-E SCALEUP Ready program, ARPA-E ROCKS FY25, FECM (FE) TRACE, Office of Science (BES) Energy Frontier Research Centers (EFRCs) FY25, Office of Science (BES/EPSCoR) Building EPSCoR-State/National Laboratory Partnerships FY25, and Office of Science (SC) Early Career Research Program (ECRP) FY25.
An upcoming event on May 21st, 2026 at 2:00 PM EDT is the National Lab Discovery Series: ORNL Critical Mineral Recovery Technologies.
The page lists DOE critical materials resources including the 2023 Assessment, 2021 Strategy, and 2019/2011/2010 Strategy and Assessments.

Resources from the Critical Materials Innovation (CMI) Hub include a 10-Year Retrospective, Bibliometric Analysis of Publications 2013–2022, Patent Portfolio Evaluation, Year 10 Cumulative Report, and Annual Review 2022.

Supply chain resources include an Education and Workforce Development Workshop Report, Domestic Wastes and Byproducts resource, America's Strategy to Secure the Supply Chain for a Robust Clean Energy Transition, Building Resilient Supply Chains report on Large Capacity Batteries, Battery Critical Materials Supply Chain Challenges from the 2020 RFI, Rare Earths Supply Chain Situational White Paper, Minerals and Materials for the Global Clean Energy Transition, Lithium Resources in the Salton Sea Region fact sheet, Wind Energy Technologies materials quantification, 2023 DOE Critical Materials List determination, and Recovery of Rare Earth Elements from Coal and Coal Byproducts.

The page references external resources including CriticalMinerals.gov, Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025, the Inflation Reduction Act, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, and methodology for the 2023 Review and Revision of the U.S. Critical Minerals List.
source_title: Critical Minerals and Materials Program | Department of Energy

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy Vehicle Technologies Office FY2024 R&D Funding Program
canonical_name: Vehicle Technologies Office Fiscal Year 2024 Research & Development Funding Program
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced $51.7 million in funding for 19 projects that will advance research and development (R&D) critical to unlock innovative and efficient mobility options that are affordable for all Americans.
The Fiscal Year 2024 Vehicle Technologies Office Research & Development funding opportunity was originally announced on April 4, 2024.

Projects selected will support the demonstration and deployment of new and efficient mobility options that are affordable for all Americans, in accordance with strategies and plans detailed in the U.S. National Blueprint for Transportation Decarbonization.
Next-generation phosphate-based cathodes received funding for 4 projects totaling $17.01 million.
Advancing the state of the art for sodium-ion batteries received funding for 5 projects totaling $6.42 million.

Developing concepts for decreasing greenhouse gas emissions from off-road vehicles such as construction, agriculture, mining, and forestry vehicles received funding for 4 projects totaling $7.24 million.

Developing and deploying vehicle-to-everything (V2X) technologies that can lead to meaningful savings at the vehicle and transportation system level received funding for 2 projects totaling $7 million.
Developing high-performance, domestically produced electrical steels (E-steels) for use in electrified powertrains received funding for 2 projects totaling $5.6 million.
Addressing critical cybersecurity needs for smart and secure electric vehicle charging received funding for 2 projects totaling $8.46 million.
Selection for award negotiations is not a commitment by DOE to issue an award or provide funding.
DOE and the applicants will first undergo a negotiation and DOE may cancel award negotiations and rescind the selection for any reason.
source_title: Funding Selections: Fiscal Year 2024 Vehicle Technologies Office Research & Development Funding Program | Department of Energy

facts: Georgia Power announced that the 1,114-megawatt (MW) Unit 4 nuclear power reactor at Plant Vogtle near Waynesboro, Georgia, entered into commercial operation after connecting to the power grid in March 2024.
The commercial start of Unit 4 completes the 11-year expansion project at Plant Vogtle.
No nuclear reactors are under construction now in the United States.
Vogtle Unit 3 began commercial operation in July 2023.
The plant’s first two reactors, with a combined 2,430 MW of nameplate capacity, began operations in 1987 and 1989.
The two new reactors bring Plant Vogtle’s total generating capacity to nearly 5 gigawatts (GW).
This surpasses the 4,210-MW Palo Verde plant in Arizona and makes Vogtle’s four units the largest nuclear power plant in the United States.
Construction at the two new reactor sites began in 2009.
The project was originally expected to cost $14 billion and begin commercial operation in 2016 (Vogtle 3) and in 2017 (Vogtle 4).
The project ran into significant construction delays and cost overruns.
Georgia Power now estimates the total cost of the project to be more than $30 billion.
The commercial operating date is when builders hand over a reactor to the plant owner or operator, declaring the reactor to be officially in commercial operation.
With a total installed capacity of about 97 GW, the largest commercial nuclear generating fleet of any country is located in the United States.
The fleet of operating nuclear power reactors accounted for nearly 19% of domestic electricity production in 2023.
Nuclear became the second-largest source of U.S. electricity generation after natural gas, which accounted for 43% of electricity generation in the United States last year.
Electricity generation from nuclear reactors doesn’t produce CO2 emissions and can provide baseload power that would otherwise largely come from coal- and natural gas-fired plants.
Interest in nuclear power as an energy resource to help reduce the carbon footprint of the U.S. electric power sector has increased recently.
Both Vogtle Units 3 and 4 use a newer reactor design, the Westinghouse AP1000.
This reactor has a smaller footprint and simpler design than previous generation reactor technologies.
It also features passive safety systems that are intended to shut down the reactor without any operator action or external power source.
Vogtle Units 3 and 4 are the first and only U.S. deployments of the AP1000 Generation III+ reactor.
Two other Westinghouse AP1000 reactors were planned for a nuclear power plant in South Carolina, but utilities there halted construction in 2017.
Principal contributor to the report is Slade Johnson.
source_title:
Plant Vogtle Unit 4 begins commercial operation -
		U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA)

facts: Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. announced plans to open a new EV parts manufacturing and R&D facility in Dublin, Georgia on June 7, 2023.
The project represents an estimated $18 million investment in Dublin-Laurens County.
The facility will create more than 130 new jobs.
Woory Industrial Company was established in 1989 and specializes in heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) components and systems for the global automotive industry.
The company is currently concentrating on developing new products for electric vehicles (EV) and hydrogen-fueled cars.

Jungwoo Kim, CEO of Woory Industrial Company, stated that the transition to electric vehicles represents a revolution in the automobile industry and identified the U.S. EV market as key among all automotive markets.
Over the past 10 years, Woory has grown into a specialized EV component company through continuous innovation.
The new facility will be located at 404 Kellam Road in Dublin and will serve as the company’s first U.S. manufacturing location.
Operations at the Dublin facility are expected to begin in November 2023.
The facility will produce electrical heaters, control units, and actuators for EV and internal combustion carmakers in North America.
Unlike internal combustion engine vehicles, electric vehicles lack a built-in heating source, requiring heaters to warm the cabin and maintain battery temperature.
Woory's pioneering development of electric heaters over the last 10 years has positioned it as a leader in this market segment.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the project and highlighted Georgia's goal to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Kemp credited the state's highly-skilled workforce and reliable infrastructure network for attracting historic levels of new jobs and investment.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight emphasized that Woory’s commitment to repurposing an existing manufacturing building promotes infill development and creates new career paths.

Dr. Fred Williams, Chair of the Board of Directors of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, noted Dublin and Laurens County's rapidly growing automotive sector driven by its location on Interstate 16 and access to a skilled workforce.

The project involves partnerships with the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team, the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the GDEcD Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia has invested for decades to match competitive projects with prepared communities and sites.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia's pursuit of EV supply chain opportunities has resulted in over $22.7 billion in investment and the creation of more than 28,400 jobs.
Woory currently employs over 1,300 people across its international production plants.
The company has been nominated as a supplier for Tesla, Rivian, Lucid, and Canoo.
Woory is currently supplying the HV heater system used to heat the cabins of EVs.
Area education systems and training providers will partner with Woory to ensure training programs are in place to fill its talent pipeline.

canonical_name: Manufacture of Advanced Key Energy Infrastructure Technologies (MAKE IT) Prize
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced three winners of the Manufacture of Advanced Key Energy Infrastructure Technologies (MAKE IT) Prize Facilities Track on September 30, 2024.

Each of the three MAKE IT Prize Facilities Track winners received $5 million throughout the prize for demonstrating readiness to begin building a manufacturing facility for critical clean energy components.
The DOE is announcing another iteration of the MAKE IT Prize, making an additional $30 million available in the next few months.
The MAKE IT Prize Facilities Track is made possible by the Office of Technology Transitions (OTT) and the Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations (OCED).
The prize required competitors to prove they have shovel-ready manufacturing facilities, including showcasing site control, permits, financing, and community engagement.

DOE Chief Commercialization Officer and Director of the Office of Technology Transitions, Dr. Vanessa Chan, stated the prize will allow winners to fast-track construction of new domestic manufacturing facilities.
Dr. Vanessa Chan noted that critical clean energy components made at these sites will help power the Investing In America agenda to ensure investments are supported by a U.S. supply chain.

OCED Director of Portfolio Strategy, Melissa Klembara, emphasized the prize's importance for building out critical supply chains needed for the next wave of infrastructure projects and future clean energy projects.
The three winners will leverage prize dollars to create more than $100 million of economic activity directly related to the construction of their facilities.
More than 250 jobs will be created to build and operate these three facilities.
The winning companies will build more than 250 hybrid inverters per year to upgrade the electric grid and deploy more than 300 MWh per year of long duration energy storage.
These DOE investments support the Biden-Harris Administration’s efforts to boost domestic manufacturing and competitiveness.

MAKE IT Prize Round 2 will be open to additional critical components in areas including upgrades to the electric grid, long duration energy storage, industrial decarbonization, carbon capture and storage, direct air capture, clean hydrogen, and advanced nuclear power.
DOE intends to publish the new prize rules and open the competition in the fall of 2024.
Stryten Energy of Augusta, GA won a Phase 2 award and plans to repurpose a shuttered facility in Augusta, GA as a new high-volume continuous reactor vanadium electrolyte production facility.
The Stryten Energy project aims to create a cost-effective domestic supply chain for vanadium electrolyte and establish the U.S. as a leader in this critical infrastructure area.
Switched Source, LLC of Chicago, IL won a Phase 2 award and aims to expand production capacity for a power-electronics-based hybrid transformer called "Phase-EQ".
MAKE IT Prize funds will help Switched Source transition from a 5,000 square foot low-volume facility to a ~20,000 square foot manufacturing, high power testing, and warehouse facility.
The expanded Switched Source facility will be capable of producing over 250 Phase-EQ units per year by the end of 2027.
Urban Electric Power of Kingston, NY won a Phase 2 award and plans to build and operate a rechargeable zinc-manganese dioxide battery plant for long duration energy storage applications.
The Urban Electric Power manufacturing technology is designed to meet market demand for safe, reliable, and American-made turnkey battery energy storage systems (BESS).
Winning teams will locate facilities in currently underutilized spaces in disadvantaged communities and work with local partners on workforce development initiatives.
Remaining Phase 1 winners in the MAKE IT Facilities Track still have opportunities to submit shovel-ready plans, with one more Phase 2 award available.
The next submission deadline for Facilities Track Phase 2 is Nov. 5th, with additional deadlines expected in February and May 2025 if funding is available.
DOE may consider making additional prizes available at the November deadline but does not commit to increasing the prize pool at this time.
The three-phase MAKE IT Strategies Track, focusing on community-driven strategies for attracting clean energy manufacturing, is still underway through next spring.
Round 1 competitors in the Strategies Track are currently in Phase 3: Activate, while Round 2 teams are in Phase 2: Create.
Teams in both Strategies Track rounds will submit materials for review by Nov. 1st, with winner announcements anticipated in December.

The MAKE IT Prize was developed by DOE’s OTT, OCED, and Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy (EERE) and is funded by the Technology Commercialization Fund through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
The prize is administered through the American-Made program, which accelerates the transition from ideas into real-world clean energy solutions.

The DOE Office of Technology Transitions (OTT) was founded in 2015 and bolsters the technology industry's market skills to enable clean energy technologies to progress through research, development, demonstration, and deployment.
The Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations (OCED) was established in December 2021 to help scale emerging technologies needed to tackle climate challenges and achieve net zero emissions by 2050.
OCED is managing more than $25 billion in funding to deliver clean energy demonstration projects at scale in partnership with the private sector.

source_title: DOE Awards $15 Million to Prize Winners for Domestic Clean Energy Manufacturing Facilities and Will Launch a New $30 Million MAKE IT Prize | Department of Energy

entity_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America
canonical_name: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America
facts: Yamaha Motor Manufacturing Corporation of America (YMMC) and Yamaha Motor Corp., USA (YMUS) celebrated the assembly of a new 2021 Wolverine RMAX2 1000 Side-by-Side, marking a historic milestone of 4 million vehicles produced in the U.S.
The celebration took place at YMMC’s state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Newnan, Georgia, on August 12, 2021, to recognize employee dedication and manufacturing quality.
YMMC serves as Yamaha’s hub for 4-wheel off-highway vehicle manufacturing, producing all of the company’s Side-by-Sides, full-size ATVs, WaveRunners, and Golf Cars for worldwide distribution.
Over the past 20 years prior to August 2021, Yamaha invested more than $380 million into U.S. manufacturing to establish the world-class Newnan facility.
Taka Imanishi, president of YMMC, stated that the company maintains high quality and environmental standards while supporting community programs through corporate social responsibility.
Since its founding in 1988, the YMMC facility has expanded to cover 280 acres with more than 1.3 million square feet of manufacturing space in Newnan.
Yamaha employs over 2,000 people in Georgia across the YMMC facility and YMUS corporate offices in Marietta and Kennesaw, accounting for more than half of the company’s 4,000+ U.S. employees.
The city of Newnan designated May 18, 2011, as “Yamaha Day” to celebrate the company’s positive economic impacts and ongoing job creation in the area.
Yamaha’s Georgia operations and workforce were featured behind-the-scenes on the season 19 premiere of “Modern Marvels: Machines” on the HISTORY Channel.

Mike Martinez, YMUS vice president of motorsports, noted that Yamaha continues to add personnel and strengthen Georgia operations as demand for outdoor recreation grows and new Side-by-Side models are introduced.
The YMMC facility holds ISO9001 Quality Management System and ISO14001 Environmental Management System certifications for quality, safety, and environmental manufacturing standards.
YMUS maintains corporate offices in California, two in Georgia (Marietta and Kennesaw), facilities in Wisconsin and Alabama, and factory operations in Tennessee and Georgia.
Additional U.S.-based subsidiaries include Yamaha Marine Systems Company, Skeeter Boats, and Yamaha Precision Propeller, alongside a nationwide network of distributors and dealers for YMUS products.
source_title: Yamaha Celebrates 4 Million Units Assembled in the USA

entity_name: Plug Power Energy Loan Borrower, LLC
canonical_name: Plug Power Energy Loan Borrower, LLC
facts: In January 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office announced the closing of a $1.66 billion loan guarantee ($1.55 billion of principal and $107 million of capitalized interest) to Plug Power Energy Loan Borrower, LLC.
The loan will help finance the construction of up to six projects to produce and liquify zero- or low-carbon hydrogen.
The produced hydrogen will supply Plug Power’s national customer base with end-to-end clean hydrogen.
The loan guarantee will support an estimated 100-200 jobs during the construction period.
At full capacity, each location will have at least 50 new full-time jobs.
Together with the DOE Regional Clean Hydrogen Hubs and ongoing research, development, and demonstration in the DOE Hydrogen Program, this project will help strengthen local economies.

The project aims to create and maintain high-quality jobs, reduce greenhouse gas emissions in sectors critical to meeting U.S. net-zero goals, and enhance America’s manufacturing and industrial competitiveness.
The hydrogen fuel is expected to be used for fuel cell electric material handling equipment and in the transportation and industrial sectors.

The project is estimated to achieve an 84% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions compared to conventional hydrogen production, which derives hydrogen from natural gas (CH4) and ultimately produces carbon dioxide (CO2).

Harnessing hydrogen fuel cells in applications such as material handling equipment includes enhanced operational efficiency, reduced environmental impact through zero-emission operations, and increased productivity due to faster refueling times compared to conventional batteries.
Plug will initiate a community outreach program dedicated to promoting awareness, understanding, and utilization of hydrogen as a clean and sustainable energy source.

The outreach aims to engage and empower communities by providing educational resources, interactive activities, and collaborative initiatives that highlight the benefits and potential applications of hydrogen technology.
Plug has a comprehensive workforce strategy including utilizing apprentices at its New York location.

All workforce development strategies and programs would be designed to utilize the comprehensive suite of services offered by the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) network of One-Stop Career Centers.
The project owners are Plug Power Energy Loan Borrower LLC.
Project locations are throughout the U.S.
The loan program is Title 17.
The guaranteed loan amount is $1.66 billion.
The issuance date is January 2025.
Permanent U.S. jobs supported are estimated at up to 300.
The approximate loan amount approved at closing includes principal and any capitalized interest.
Job estimates were made at the time of closing.
source_title: Plug Power | Department of Energy

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Office of Energy Dominance Financing
facts: Gregory A. Beard is the director of the U.S. DOE Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF), appointed in January 2026.
Director Beard was previously appointed by the President in April 2025 to serve as a Senior Advisor to EDF.
Director Beard has extensive experience in natural resources investment, private equity, and capital markets, having sourced and led more than 25 energy investments over more than 30 years.
Before government service, Director Beard was a senior partner on the management committee at Apollo Global Management, leading natural resources investing for 10 years.
Director Beard held a senior energy investment role at Riverstone Holdings LLC.

Director Beard co-founded, chaired, and served as CEO of Stronghold Digital Mining, Inc., launching the first company to integrate waste-to-power generation within co-located data centers to mine Bitcoin.

Director Beard founded and served as Chairman and CEO of Beard Energy Acquisition Corp., raising a $200 million SPAC and evaluating more than 200 potential transactions in the energy transition space.
President Trump and Secretary Wright have made commitments to unleash American energy dominance, with EDF on the front lines of delivering affordable, reliable, secure energy.
Upon taking office in January 2025, DOE senior leadership worked to reverse Biden-era energy policies that led to energy price inflation and grid destabilization.
Under the Biden Administration, electricity prices increased by 30%, which was 13 times faster than the previous seven years.
More than 100 gigawatts (GW) of firm reliable power were projected to retire by 2030 under the Biden Administration.

President Trump took decisive action to redirect DOE's mission through energy-related Executive Orders, including Unleashing American Energy, Reinvigorating the Nuclear Industrial Base, and Strengthening the Reliability and Security of the United States Electric Grid.
DOE has recentered around data-driven, common-sense policies that return the country to energy abundance and prioritize baseload power.
Between 2021 and January 2025, the Biden Administration committed over $104 billion in EDF financing.
Of the Biden-era commitments, $85 billion was closed or committed between Election Day 2024 and Inauguration Day 2025.
DOE and EDF leadership are restructuring, substantially revising, or eliminating over $83.6 billion in loans and conditional commitments, representing over 80% of the Biden-era loan portfolio.
EDF has completed or is in the process of de-obligating over $29.9 billion (approximately 29%) of the $104 billion in Biden-era principal loan obligations.
EDF has completed or is in the process of revising another $53.6 billion (approximately 51%) of the total Biden-era principal loan obligations.
EDF has eliminated approximately $9.5 billion in government-subsidized, intermittent wind and solar projects.
EDF is replacing eliminated wind and solar projects with natural gas and nuclear uprates where possible to provide more affordable and reliable energy.

EDF currently has more than $289 billion in available loan authority, including in the Energy Dominance Financing Program with expanded eligibility criteria thanks to President Trump’s Working Families Tax Cut.
This funding makes EDF the largest energy lender in the world.
EDF is prioritizing six foundational sectors: Nuclear, Coal/oil/gas/hydrocarbons, Critical materials and minerals, Geothermal, Grid and transmission, and Manufacturing and transportation.
EDF financing for manufacturing and transportation is serving as an investment accelerator for U.S. manufacturing and transportation, supporting job creation and supply chains.
In the final months of 2025, EDF closed three new loans totaling $4.1 billion.
One of the new loans was to Constellation for the Crane Restart project (formerly known as Three Mile Island) to reinvigorate the nuclear industry.
Another loan was to a subsidiary of AEP to improve America’s electric grid through reconductoring and rebuilding transmission lines.
The third loan was to Wabash Valley Resources to utilize a coal plant to produce fertilizer in America’s Corn Belt.
In 2026, EDF will prioritize financing energy and manufacturing projects that meaningfully contribute to U.S. energy security, grid reliability, and lowering costs for all Americans.
EDF is partnering with the private sector to strengthen America’s energy foundation and support U.S. leadership in emerging AI technologies.
source_title: Letter from Leadership: EDF 2025 Year-in-Review and Looking Forward to 2026 | Department of Energy

facts: New intelligent technologies at the driver-, vehicle-, and transportation system-level are attracting research attention for their potential to improve energy efficiency, convenience, and environmental impact of current transportation systems.

Connected vehicle technology enables vehicles, roads, other infrastructure, and smartphones to communicate and share vital transportation information through advanced wireless communication technology.
Automated vehicle (AV) technologies use hardware and software to perform some or all driving tasks on a sustained basis with little to no human input.
Connected and automated vehicle (CAV) technology could potentially improve traffic flow and safety, increase vehicle efficiency, and reduce transportation energy in certain applications.
SAE International has defined six levels of driving automation, ranging from level 0 (no driving automation) to level 5 (full driving automation).
With level 5 automation, a vehicle can operate autonomously on all roads in the United States that are navigable by a human driver.
CAVs provide the opportunity to transform the logic, operations, and performance of traffic signal control, thereby reducing congestion and increasing transportation system efficiency.
The potential impacts of CAVs on vehicle miles traveled (VMT), vehicle fuel efficiency, and costs to consumers are unknown.

The U.S. Department of Energy's Transportation Technologies Office, Energy Efficient Mobility Systems (EEMS) program research shows even a modest market share of CAVs reduces congestion and energy consumption in situations such as vehicle merging at highway ramps.
Simulations on I-75 indicate that a 20% light-duty CAV penetration leads to 4% corridor fuel consumption savings for a range of mixed traffic scenarios.
CAVs could facilitate increased rideshare, thereby promoting greater vehicle operating efficiency.
If individually owned, CAVs could substantially increase VMT by allowing riders to do other activities while they spend time in a vehicle, thus reducing the inconvenience of longer trips.
One beneficial application of CAVs is truck platooning, a vehicle-to-vehicle communication strategy that uses sensors to virtually connect two or more trucks in a convoy.
Truck platooning allows vehicles to save fuel by accelerating and braking together and traveling at a closer distance.
DOE's EEMS program has investigated the energy, technology, and usage implications of vehicle connectivity and automation and identified efficient CAV solutions.
Based on test-track data, platoons of three close-following trucks can achieve a combined 13% reduction in fuel use.
If trucks nationwide operated in these CAV three-truck platoons, 2.1 billion gallons of fuel could be saved each year.
A 2017 study analyzed telematics data on 57,000 vehicles traveling 210 million miles and determined that 63% of U.S. interstate and highway miles may be platoonable.

The U.S. Department of Transportation is collaborating with public and private partners, including state and local governments, vehicle and device manufacturers, and academia, to advance connected vehicle development and implementation.
An Automated Mobility District (AMD) is the implementation of CAV technology across a focused area, such as a campus for a university, medical facility, or business campus.
AMDs are characterized by driverless, on-demand, low-speed shuttles that provide direct origin-to-destination service to either individuals or small groups.
An AMD has the potential to reduce total system fuel consumption, but the amount is largely dependent on operating and ridership assumptions.
Much more fuel is required to stop at a red light and then accelerate up to speed rather than cruising through a green light.
Currently, traffic signals use sensing and control to safely coordinate the flow of vehicles, pedestrians, bikes, and scooters, while also prioritizing safe and efficient public transportation.
Modern traffic signal control systems are limited by the information provided to them from sensors.
Advances in CAV technologies provide an opportunity to transform how traffic signals are controlled to reduce delay, conserve energy, and enhance safety at intersections.
Many traffic signals are controlled by software within signal cabinets that run simple pre-timed sequences for certain times and days of the week.
Some traffic signals can respond to changes in demand, varying their timing in response to feedback from infrastructure sensors.
At best, such signals only offer a partial picture of the state of traffic, leaving out details about the location and velocity of all vehicles.

Improvements are possible by pairing CAVs with more sophisticated infrastructure-based sensing that provides the location and speed of all vehicles (and possibly pedestrians, cyclists, and other moving objects) to optimize movement in intersections.

DOE’s EEMS program investigated the potential to reduce delay, conserve energy, and enhance safety with an advanced signal control network (either through vehicles or at the intersection) and control at signalized intersections in the SMART Mobility Urban Science Capstone Report.

Efficient routing is a connected vehicle strategy under which drivers receive information about the most fuel-efficient route taking speed and stops into account before departing for a given destination.

A study using a large-scale, high-resolution dataset from the California Household Travel Survey indicates that 31% of actual routes have fuel savings potential, and among these routes the cumulative fuel savings could reach 12%.
Google Maps now offers a fuel-efficient routing option that extends this service to a much larger audience.
Transportation network companies (TNC) such as Uber and Lyft provide a for-hire transportation mode like taxis and their on-demand service is typically priced similarly to traditional taxi services.
TNCs offer real-time observability through map-based monitoring and mobile phone-friendly payment methods.

The emerging impacts of TNC services have become an increased area of city attention and research, as their increased use has implications for transportation, energy use, parking revenues, and future infrastructure.
Solo rides in TNC vehicles, and especially the driving of empty vehicles to pick up new passengers, may actually increase overall VMT.
Ride-hailing increases energy use by an estimated 41%–90% compared to prior mode.
When ride-hailing is used to complement transit, communities could see increased mobility and transportation efficiencies.
When transportation-on-demand is combined with shared vehicles, such as with Via, Scoop, Bridj, or micro-transit programs, there are improved outcomes for congestion, efficiency, and mobility.

Research has shown that combining high vehicle sharing and high automation decreases total energy consumption by 23% and VMT by 18% and increases network speed by 17% due to fewer vehicles on the road.

Policymakers, transit agencies, and urban planners can use these types of insights to foster opportunities for mobility as a service, such as on-demand transit or public-private partnerships between TNCs and local transit agencies, while ensuring public transit services meet user needs and encourage mode shift.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Intelligent Transportation System Technologies

facts: Irving Tissue announced an expansion at its Macon facility involving nearly $600 million in investment.
The expansion will add at least 100 new jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the expansion on November 21, 2024.
The announcement was celebrated during Irving Tissue’s fifth anniversary celebration in Macon.
Since choosing Macon in 2017, Irving Tissue has invested around $1.5 billion into the community.
Irving Tissue has created 400 well-paying jobs since 2017, with another 100 jobs on the way.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is the No. 1 state for business and emphasized ongoing relationships with job creators.
Irving Tissue’s current facility is located at 1897 Allen Road in the Sofkee Industrial Park.
The facility employs more than 400 people.
The facility produces ultra-premium quality household paper products, including soft bath tissue and high-quality paper towel.
Irving Tissue President Robert K. Irving expressed appreciation for the State of Georgia’s commitment to economic prosperity, jobs, and growth.
Robert Irving credited the state’s drive to promote advantages and foster a positive business environment for making the investment possible.
Robert Irving highlighted the highly skilled workforce in the area and continued support from employees, customers, and suppliers as key factors enabling the expansion.
The expansion includes a third ThruAir Dry paper making machine.
The expansion includes additional converting lines.
The expansion includes a new, fully automated warehouse.
The announcement will increase Irving Tissue’s annual ThruAir Dry capacity by 75,000 tonnes.
The capacity increase is equivalent to 15 million cases.
The total annual capacity at the Macon facility will reach 225,000 tonnes.
The total capacity is equivalent to 45 million cases.
Irving Tissue will be hiring for management, supervisory, and machinist positions.
Macon-Bibb County Mayor Lester Miller stated that Irving has been a true member of the community with a deep commitment to its people.
Lester Miller noted that the expansion speaks volumes about the strong foundation Irving Tissue has built in Macon.
Chairman Robby Fountain of the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority (MBCIA) described working with Irving over the past five years as an incredible journey.
Robby Fountain stated that Irving Tissue became a valued part of the community and its growth is a testament to hard work, dedication, and strong local connections.
Regional Project Manager Candice Scott represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with MBCIA.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Irving Tissue has demonstrated what it means to become part of the community fabric over the last five years.
Pat Wilson noted that each expansion builds on partnerships and collaboration supported by the state’s business-friendly environment and world-class logistics infrastructure.
The logistics infrastructure will connect Irving’s Macon facility to markets across the East Coast.
Irving Tissue produces premium household store brand paper products for many of North America's top retailers.
Irving Tissue also produces some of the top-selling tissue brands in the marketplace.
Irving Tissue is part of Irving Consumer Products, one of North America's leading manufacturers of household paper and baby diaper products.
source_title: Irving Tissue to Expand, Invest $600 Million | Georgia

facts: Club Car is headquartered in Augusta, Georgia.
Club Car announced the launch of its all-new CRU Lithium option for its street-legal CRU Neighborhood Electric Vehicle (NEV).
The CRU Lithium NEV features an advanced lithium-ion battery that delivers faster charging, lighter weight, longer range, and improved power.
The new lithium battery option allows the vehicle to go three times further with more efficient energy use and consistent performance.
The CRU Lithium NEV offers a lightweight design that improves handling and acceleration with reduced battery weight.
The lithium-ion technology eliminates the need for watering and reduces corrosion, resulting in low maintenance requirements.
The lithium-ion batteries are individually encased and tested to applicable standards to ensure safety and reliability.
The CRU Lithium NEV is available now through authorized Club Car dealers.
Club Car has a history spanning over six decades and is known for quality craftsmanship ensuring long-term reliability and customer satisfaction.
Though rooted in golf fleet and operations, Club Car's product portfolio includes vehicles for commercial, personal, and street-legal low-speed use.
The Club Car Vehicle Group also includes the luxury electric vehicle brand Garia and the lightweight electric utility vehicle brand Melex.
Brant Mitchell, Senior Director of Product Management at Club Car, stated that CRU Lithium is a game changer that provides a smarter, longer-lasting power solution fitting customer lifestyles.
source_title: Club Car Launches Lithium Option for Street-Legal CRU Vehicle

facts: Continental Tire the Americas, LLC announced plans to expand its Lancaster County operations.
The company is investing more than $20 million into the expansion.
Founded in 1871, Continental manufactures and distributes a complete line of passenger, light truck and commercial tires for original equipment and replacement markets.
The company also offers technology solutions for vehicles, machines, traffic and transportation.
Located at 1830 MacMillan Park Drive in Lancaster County, the expansion will include a new 88,000-square-foot building constructed next to the existing headquarters.
The new facility will enable Continental to consolidate its footprint while providing space needed to support the continuous growth of its operations.
The expansion is expected to be completed by mid-2022.
The Coordinating Council for Economic Development has approved a $2.6 million Closing Fund grant to Lancaster County for costs related to the project.

Continental Tire the Americas Chief Executive Officer Dr. Jochen Etzel stated that Lancaster County has been home to their tire operations for the Americas Region since 2009 and expressed excitement about investing in their campus to bring their team together in a new space with modern features.

Gov. Henry McMaster celebrated Continental’s decision to invest $20 million into Lancaster County, noting it demonstrates the strength of South Carolina's business-friendly climate and strong workforce.

Secretary of Commerce Bobby Hitt stated that South Carolina is known for making things well, which has been crucial in recruiting companies like Continental, and praised the company's growth and workforce.
Lancaster County Council Chairman Steve Harper congratulated Continental on expanding their Indian Land headquarters, calling them a premier employer and welcoming the new investment and jobs.

Lancaster County Department of Economic Development Executive Director Jamie Gilbert called the expansion a huge win for Lancaster County, noting Continental is an international leader in the automotive industry that chose to remain and grow there due to the outstanding business climate, professional workforce, strategic location, and quality of life.
source_title: Continental Tire the Americas, LLC Expanding Headquarters in Lancaster County | S.C. Governor Henry McMaster

facts: Phenix City Mayor and City Council voted to approve an incentive package for Daechang Seat Corp., USA (DSC) to expand their manufacturing operations in Phenix City, AL.
DSC is currently located at 903 Fontaine Road in Phenix City.
The expansion will include a new facility on 22 acres, purchased from the City, in the Phenix Industrial Park.
The manufacturing operation projects to create an additional 300 jobs over the next 3 years.
The project involves an initial capital investment of approximately $23,366,072.

DSC originally located in Phenix City in 2019 through the effort and support of the State of Alabama Department of Commerce, Alabama Power Company, the Columbus Area Chamber of Commerce, and the Phenix City leadership.
Mayor Eddie Lowe stated that the expansion continues to show Phenix City is a great place to do business.
Daechang Seat Co., USA was established in 1979 in Kyungju, South Korea, and has become one of the largest seat frame producers in the world.
Globally, DSC employs more than 3,000 people across 7 countries.
DSC’s manufacturing process includes fully automatic lines with arc welding and spot welding machines.
Once construction is complete at the new manufacturing facility, DSC will have a larger footprint and greater opportunity for continued growth.
DSC General Manager Mr. Kim expressed pleasure in working with the Mayor of Phenix City and local citizens.
DSC is a car seat manufacturing supplier that currently has 24 factories worldwide.
DSC is the first cooperative company with Hyundai and Kia.
Construction plans are to begin immediately, with operations projected for the 1st quarter of 2023.
Construction is being completed by Marshall Design Build, LLC, an industry leading Design Build Contractor specializing in Industrial Automotive facilities.
Marshall Design Build has completed over 90 Hyundai, KIA and other tier supplier projects.
Marshall Design Build has its headquarters in Montgomery, Alabama, and has been working with tier suppliers for over 19 years.
Travis Marshall, owner of Marshall Design Build, expressed that the company looks forward to continuing the growth of tier suppliers in Alabama and Georgia.

links.context: Phenix City Planning & Economic Development contact

source_title: Media Release: Daechang Seat Corp. USA Announces Expansion Plans in Phenix City | Phenix City, Alabama

canonical_name: Technology for Recovery and Advanced Critical-material Extraction–Gallium (TRACE-Ga) Initiative
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management announced up to $6 million in federal funding available for research and development projects.
The funding aims to establish a secure domestic supply chain for gallium, a critical material for the energy, defense, and semiconductor sectors.
Prototype technologies will leverage existing feedstocks at U.S. metal processing facilities to reduce foreign dependence and increase efficiency of gallium recovery.
The program advances President Trump’s commitment to strengthen America’s critical minerals supply chain.
Tala Goudarzi, Principal Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon Management, stated: “The U.S. has not recovered primary gallium in nearly four decades.”

Goudarzi added: “With this funding, we aim to change that by tapping existing processing streams and supporting innovators who can recover gallium here at home—showcasing how American technology can outpace current methods and unlock a new supply.”
The TRACE-Ga initiative is managed by ENERGYWERX in partnership with DOE through a Partnership Intermediary Agreement set up by the DOE's Office of Technology Commercialization.

Anthony Pugliese, DOE Chief Commercialization Officer and Director of the Office of Technology Commercialization, stated: “The Office of Technology Commercialization’s role is to enable the deployment of technologies that matter most for our nation... By supporting innovations that can restart domestic gallium recovery, we are strengthening supply chain security and ensuring U.S. industry has reliable access to critical materials.”

Projects under TRACE-Ga will test and validate prototype technologies with a goal of producing at least 50 kg of pure gallium from at least one 14-day campaign of continuous operation using a real-world metal industry processing stream like aluminum or zinc.
The objective is to validate a prototype capable of producing at least 1 metric ton per annum scale of gallium.
Successful projects will restart domestic primary gallium recovery for the first time in almost 40 years.

Applicants must qualify as a domestic entity and provide evidence of success recovering gallium from similar feedstock, a letter of support from feedstock owners if applicable, and a description of potential scale-up and market adoption.
An informational webinar for the initiative is scheduled for September 30, 2025 at 3:00 pm ET.
source_title: U.S. Department of Energy Announces $6 Million to Enhance U.S. Supply Chain Security Through Domestic Gallium Production | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) has established technical targets for liquid alkaline electrolysis to guide technology development.
The central goal is low-cost hydrogen production of $2/kg H2 by 2026 and $1/kg H2 by 2031.
Many combinations of performance, efficiency, lifetime, and cost targets can achieve the central hydrogen production cost goals.
The target combinations were developed with input from experts from industry and national laboratories.
The targets are intended as a starting guidepost for technology developers.
Separate technical target tables have been developed for electrolyzer technologies other than low-temperature liquid alkaline electrolyzers.
All performance, durability, and capital cost targets must be met simultaneously on the same stack or system to achieve the levelized cost of hydrogen targets.
The ultimate goal is to demonstrate these targets in stacks and systems at relevant commercial scales.
Current status values are based on traditional liquid alkaline electrolyzer technology available in commercial products for many years.
The 2026 performance target for electrolyzer stacks is 1.0 A/cm2 @ 1.8 V/cell, with an ultimate target of 2.0 A/cm2 @ 1.7 V/cell.
The electrical efficiency target for stacks is 45 kWh/kg H2 (74% LHV) ultimately, down from a 2026 target of 48 kWh/kg H2 (69% LHV).
The average degradation rate target for stacks is 2.1 mV/kh (0.13%/1,000 h) ultimately, with a 2026 target of 2.3 mV/kh (0.13%/1,000 h).
The lifetime target for electrolyzer stacks is 80,000 hours of operation for both 2026 and ultimate goals.
The stack capital cost target is $50/kW ultimately, with a 2026 target of $100/kW.
The system energy efficiency target is 48 kWh/kg H2 (70% LHV) ultimately, down from a 2026 target of 52 kWh/kg H2 (64% LHV).
The uninstalled system capital cost target is $150/kW ultimately, with a 2026 target of $250/kW.
The ultimate hydrogen production cost target is $1.00/kg H2, with a 2026 target of $2.00/kg H2.
Efficiency calculations include a 2.5% efficiency loss due to H2 crossover, used as an upper limit for safety margins.
Status and targets assume an end-of-life performance loss of 10%.
Degradation rate evaluation must account for operation near the stack’s nominal operating point, dynamic operation for duty cycle requirements, and on-off cycling if required by applications.
End-of-life is defined as a 10% voltage loss from beginning-of-life operations following any break-in or conditioning period, measured at the same current density.
Cost targets assume economies of scale have been achieved through high-volume manufacturing.
Installation costs, including engineering, procurement, construction, and permitting, are excluded from system cost values.
Production cost targets assume utilization of low-cost electricity (e.g., ≤$0.03/kWh) with high availability (e.g., >90%).
Scenarios utilizing lower-cost electricity at lower capacity factors, such as from wind or solar, may also meet production cost targets.
source_title: Technical Targets for Liquid Alkaline Electrolysis | Department of Energy
