facts: Trucks that haul gaseous hydrogen are called tube trailers.
Gaseous hydrogen is compressed to pressures of 180 bar (~2,600 psig) or higher into long cylinders that are stacked on a trailer that the truck hauls.
This gives the appearance of long tubes, hence the name tube trailer.
Tube trailers are currently limited to pressures of 250 bar by U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) regulations.
Exemptions have been granted to enable operation at higher pressures (e.g., 500 bar or higher).
Steel tube trailers are most commonly employed and carry approximately 380 kg onboard.
The carrying capacity of steel tube trailers is limited by the weight of the steel tubes.
Composite storage vessels have been developed that have capacities of 560–900 kg of hydrogen per trailer.
Such tube trailers are currently being used to deliver compressed natural gas in other countries.
source_title: Hydrogen Tube Trailers | Department of Energy

entity_name: DOE BENEFIT 22-23 Building Energy Efficiency Funding Program
canonical_name: Buildings Energy Efficiency Frontiers & Innovation Technologies (BENEFIT) Funding Opportunity
facts: DOE released $46 million in funding for 29 projects across 15 states to develop advanced technologies and retrofit practices for buildings that will benefit occupants and the grid through efficient, affordable, sustainable building operations.

On August 7, 2023, DOE released $46 million in funding for 29 projects across 15 states under the Buildings Energy Efficiency Frontiers & Innovation Technologies (BENEFIT) funding opportunity to help buildings electrify while improving energy efficiency and demand flexibility.

Georgia Institute of Technology, Georgia received $2.4 million to design and integrate Thermochemical Energy Storage (TCES) into buildings for load shedding/shifting, developing a high-energy, closed-cell thermochemical energy storage module that connects with a residential heat pump to improve efficiency and store energy during periods of peak demand year-round.

Southern Company Services, Georgia received $1.5 million to deeply integrate Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) into an existing large commercial building automation system alongside HVAC, solar, and EV charging infrastructure to achieve net zero carbon goals.
Southern Company Services will use a supervisory control layer to coordinate local and grid objectives for the BESS integration, breaking traditional BESS and BAS silos.

Selection for award negotiations is not a commitment by DOE to issue an award or provide funding; before funding is issued, DOE and applicants will undergo a negotiation process, and DOE may cancel negotiations and rescind the selection for any reason during that time.
source_title: Meet DOE’s Newest Research Projects from BENEFIT 22-23 | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) released “The National Blueprint for a Clean & Competitive Industrial Sector” on November 18, 2024.
The Blueprint outlines five whole-of-government strategies within a private-sector led and government-enabled framework to fuel continued growth of American manufacturing.
The framework aligns with the Biden-Harris Administration’s agenda to transform U.S. manufacturing to boost competitiveness, reduce pollution, and create good-paying jobs for American workers.

U.S. Deputy Secretary of Energy David M. Turk stated that the manufacturing sector is expanding rapidly under President Biden’s leadership, employing millions of Americans while providing essential materials and products that people use every day.
Implementing the strategies outlined in the Blueprint will improve public health, accelerate innovation to support U.S. international competitiveness, and create even more good-paying U.S. jobs.
The American manufacturing sector contributes 12% to the U.S. Gross Domestic Product annually.
Many manufacturing jobs pay better than average while being accessible to workers without a four-year degree.
Robust domestic manufacturing reduces dependence on foreign suppliers and lowers the risk of supply chain shocks, which is important for national security.
The manufacturing sector is energy intensive and accounts for approximately 20 percent of total annual domestic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
The Blueprint outlines a suite of new levers and five strategies to guide near-term Federal government coordination.
One strategy is to accelerate deployment of commercially available, cost-effective lower carbon solutions in the near term.
Another strategy is to demonstrate emerging solutions at commercial scale to de-risk deployment.
A third strategy is to increase data use to drive emissions reductions and efficiency gains that can significantly improve performance and track progress.
A fourth strategy is to innovate and advance research to develop transformative processes and products for deep GHG emissions reductions.
The fifth strategy is to integrate across the product life cycle to reduce embodied GHG emissions in industrial products and minimize waste.

Investments in low-carbon industrial technology are investments in the future of American manufacturing competitiveness, as corporations and governments across the globe race to dominate emerging energy technology solutions.

The Blueprint lays out a holistic vision of coordinated federal actions that will support pollution reduction across U.S. industry while strengthening broadly shared economic prosperity, public health and national security.
source_title: U.S. Department of Energy Releases First-Ever Blueprint for a Clean and Competitive Industrial Sector | Department of Energy

facts: Fox Factory moved from California to Gainesville, Ga., in 2018.
The company opened a new $50 million facility in Gainesville, Georgia.
Georgia Quick Start provided a customized training program to prepare the team with both theoretical knowledge and operational skills.
Fox Factory manufactures high-performance shock absorbers and racing suspension products.
The company needed to expand its talent space and physical footprint to continue on its growth trajectory.
Joe Cobain serves as the general manager at the Gainesville plant.
During site selection, Fox Factory looked at numerous other states before choosing Georgia.
Quick Start enhanced its existing CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machining program to include a high-level math course that included geometry and trigonometry.
High-quality technical training is critical for Fox due to the precision required to manufacture its products.
Fox Factory is a global leader in the design and manufacturing of premium products that deliver championship-level performance for specialty sports and on- and off-road vehicles.
Lanier Technical College is listed as a training partner or resource associated with the project.
source_title: Fox Factory - Georgia Quick Start

facts: The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) approved a 40-year Part 70 Special Nuclear Material license for X-energy's advanced nuclear fuel fabrication facility in Oak Ridge, Tennessee.
The approval marks the first Category II fuel fabrication license issued by the NRC and the first new fuel fabrication license issued in approximately 50 years.

The TX-1 facility is currently under construction and is the first commercial-scale fuel fabrication facility in the United States focused on high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) fuel to receive NRC approval.
Once complete, TX-1 will manufacture TRISO fuel to support deployment of X-energy’s Xe-100 advanced small modular reactor, as well as other advanced nuclear reactor companies.
The license authorizes TRISO-X, a wholly owned subsidiary of X-energy, to commercially produce nuclear fuel for advanced reactors.
TRISO-X submitted the initial license application for a fuel fabrication facility in 2022.
In September 2025, the NRC issued a draft environmental impact statement for public comment, finding no significant impact for the project.
The license was approved on February 13, three months ahead of schedule.
Building construction on TX-1 began in November 2025 at the 110-acre TRISO-X Campus in Oak Ridge.
The facility is part of X-energy’s participation in the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program (ARDP).
ARDP funding provides up to 50% cost sharing with X-energy for the Xe-100 Advanced Reactor Demonstration Project, including construction of TX-1.
X-energy predicts that the 215,000-square-foot facility will employ around 500 full time staff.
The facility can produce 700,000 of its proprietary TRISO-X fuel “pebbles” per year, which is enough fuel to sustain 11 Xe-100 reactors.
TRISO-X’s fuel pebbles are billiard ball-sized spheres designed specifically to power the Xe-100 high-temperature gas-cooled reactor.
A single pebble contains over 18,000 TRISO fuel particles, each about the size of a poppy seed.

The TRISO particles are made up of a uranium, carbon, and oxygen fuel kernel encapsulated by three layers of carbon- and silicon carbide ceramic-based materials that prevent the release of radioactive fission products.
This unique structure makes TRISO fuels extraordinarily robust, giving them the ability to withstand extreme radiation and temperatures beyond the threshold of current nuclear fuels.
TRISO-X is a proprietary form of TRISO fuel developed by X-energy and TRISO-X in partnership with Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
The companies have operated a pilot facility at ORNL since 2016, adapting and refining established TRISO production processes to ensure high-quality fabrication at full commercial scale.
Irradiation testing of TRISO-X fuel is currently underway at Idaho National Laboratory’s (INL) Advanced Test Reactor facility.
The experiments will evaluate how the fuel performs under various power levels, temperatures, and burnup conditions to simulate a wide range of operating scenarios.

TX-1 is a critical piece of X-energy’s plans to commercialize the Xe-100 small modular reactor, an 80-megawatt-electric, 200-megawatt-thermal high-temperature gas-cooled reactor designed to provide either electricity or high-temperature steam for industrial applications.
In May 2025, the NRC accepted a construction permit application from Dow to build the first four Xe-100 reactors to power its manufacturing facility in Seadrift, Texas.
The Seadrift project is anticipated to be the first-ever manufacturing site in North America to be powered by nuclear energy.
The project is one of two demonstration projects supported by DOE to accelerate the deployment of new reactor technologies.
Fuel fabrication at TX-1 is expected to begin in early 2028.
source_title: TRISO-X Receives NRC Special Nuclear Material License for Advanced Fuel Fabrication Facility | Department of Energy

entity_name: U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Fusion Science and Technology (FS&T) Roadmap
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy released the finalized Fusion Science and Technology (FS&T) Roadmap on June 9, 2026.
The roadmap serves as a national strategy to accelerate the development and commercialization of fusion energy on a rapid timeline.
It aims to support fusion pilot plants and commercial fusion power in the mid-2030s.

The initiative advances President Trump’s energy dominance agenda and reinforces commitments to expanding reliable American energy production, strengthening domestic supply chains, and maintaining U.S. leadership in critical technologies.
DOE Under Secretary for Science Dr. Darío Gil stated that fusion energy has entered a new era defined by extraordinary scientific progress and public-private momentum.
The roadmap was developed with input from more than 800 scientists and engineers across the public and private sectors.
Contributions to the roadmap came from more than 15 private companies, over 10 National Laboratories, and more than 70 universities.
The document identifies critical science and technology gaps that must be closed to realize fusion pilot plants and strengthen U.S. leadership in the global fusion industry.

It establishes a unified strategy built around three primary drivers: building critical infrastructure to close fusion materials and technology gaps, innovating through advanced research/high-performance computing/AI, and growing the U.S. fusion ecosystem through public-private partnerships/supply chain development/workforce growth/commercialization pathways.
The roadmap aligns with DOE’s Genesis Mission and will be implemented through the DOE's newly established Office of Fusion.
More than $10 billion in private investment is already advancing fusion technologies and demonstration projects.
DOE is coordinating a national effort to close remaining technical gaps needed to commercialize fusion energy.
The roadmap's ability to support milestones and timelines is contingent on future public-private partnerships and Congressional appropriations, and it does not commit DOE to specific funding levels.

links.context: Full copy of the Fusion Science and Technology Roadmap

source_title: Energy Department Releases Finalized Fusion Science and Technology Roadmap to Accelerate Commercial Fusion Power | Department of Energy

facts: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the Georgia Center of Innovation for Energy Technology teamed up with DemocracyLab Volunteers to provide collaborative tools for visualizing environmental and economic impacts in local communities, states, and countries.

The visualization tools utilize 24 indicators including job growth, value-added, air quality, energy use, water use, land use, biodiversity, local conservation projects, and sustainable development goals.
Supply chain analysis features show which industries provide inputs and receive outputs from specific sectors.
State commodity and job totals are tracked using Pymrio and MARIO Input-Output Analysis.
The collaborative tools visualize economic inflow-outflow using economy-wide input-output models of industries, households, and local environments.

Available reporting tools and directories include Georgia Farmers Markets, Georgia Recycling Directories, Georgia Supply Chain Inflow-Outflow, Georgia County Selection, Georgia Industry Annual Payrolls, and Georgia Land Use Inflow-Outflow.

The platform supports creating economic development pages to weigh outcomes during community decision-making regarding environmental aspects, quality job creation, land and water use, health impacts, and value-added contributions to the state and county tax base.
Users can evaluate prospective industries and their potential direct and indirect job creation alongside their potential environmental performance.

The tools help determine how technological improvements in local industries, utilities, or consumption patterns could transform local outcomes toward cleaner air, water, land conservation, and biodiversity.
Community carbon cycling resources explore how manufacturers and municipalities are reusing material to capture carbon and generate clean energy.
Lifecycle impact visualization tools explore topics including biodiversity, energy innovation, farm fresh produce, waste-to-energy, bioproducts, and local product footprints.

Collaborating community participants include local government, educational institutions, development authorities, regional commissions, religious organizations, local industry groups, and local non-profits.
Teaching tools and student research frameworks allow users to use and create applications to learn about the connections between industries, households, and the environment in local communities.
Economist Mariana Mazzucato discusses entrepreneurial ecosystems in relation to the innovation economy.

links.context: Embedding input-output maps and charts sample script

source_title: Georgia.org - Inflow-Outflow Models

entity_name: Advanced Mining and Mineral Production Technologies Office
canonical_name: Advanced Mining and Mineral Processing Technologies (AMMPT) Office
facts: The mission of the Advanced Mining and Mineral Processing Technologies (AMMPT) Office is to expand American mining and mineral production by developing and deploying advanced technologies.
AMMPT funds research and development (R&D) for mineral resource characterization, extraction, and extractive metallurgical processing.
AMMPT also funds advanced R&D collaborations, such as METALLIC and HERMES, in coordination with the U.S. National Laboratories.
These initiatives address high-priority challenges related to minerals production, including characterization and prospecting, mining and extraction, and production and processing.
AMMPT focuses on identifying new resources through faster, more precise resource characterization, AI-driven decision-making and prioritization, and improved data collection and resource estimation.
AMMPT focuses on increasing technical recoverability by advancing conventional and unconventional extraction technologies and making previously inaccessible resources mineable.
AMMPT aims to strengthen the mineral production ecosystem through market and workforce development, interagency and international collaboration, and tools and data to support resilient supply chains.

AMMPT advances critical materials across industry sectors including energy production (hydrocarbon & nuclear fuels), electricity generation, energy storage, electronics (data centers, power transmission, quantum), and advanced mobility (land, sea, air).
The office is part of the Office of the Assistant Secretary of Energy under Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation.
Contact information includes Mail Stop CM-1 at the Department of Energy in Washington, DC 20585.
source_title: Advanced Mining and Mineral Production Technologies Office | Department of Energy

facts: The EcoCAR Mobility Challenge is a 4-year competition in which eleven North American college teams were challenged to reduce the environmental impact of a Chevrolet Blazer SUV while retaining or enhancing its utility, safety, and performance.
After a year of modeling and simulation, teams received their vehicles for year two to implement their designs.
Students had to consider real-world packaging for component fit and finish, drivability, and consumer acceptance.
Headline sponsor General Motors provided the vehicles and encouraged teams to mimic GM's global vehicle design process for prototype development.
General Motors hosted all eleven schools for the finals at its proving grounds in Yuma, Arizona.
The competition showcased finished projects in motion at Phoenix Raceway to media, sponsors, and local students.
One team implemented a one-pedal like driving regenerative mode found in hybrid vehicles, noting that calibration was difficult and they were only allowed one mode of operation.
The US Department of Energy provided expertise and organizational support, with engineers from Argonne National Lab managing the competition.
Judging each year measures progress in emissions, economy, component integration, and pure performance.
The Department of Energy has sponsored these student competitions for more than 30 years to ensure teams work on alternative fuels or advanced technologies like connected and automated vehicles.
DOE aims to train future engineers who will eventually work for car companies, national labs, and the DOE through hands-on training with an OEM vehicle.
Georgia Tech claimed top team honors for 2022.
The Georgia Tech EcoCAR team converted its originally six-cylinder Blazer into a four-cylinder hybrid vehicle equipped with adaptive cruise control.
Georgia Tech's vehicle features vehicle-to-infrastructure communication technology that allows it to communicate with stoplights and adjust speed for optimization.
Ohio State placed 2nd and the University of Alabama placed 3rd in the competition.
Teams utilized varying engines, battery pack sizes, motors, and wheels, with one team using 18-inch wheels and maintaining the most cargo space.

Another team equipped its vehicle with six radars (three front, one on each side, one rear) and a Mobileye windshield camera to provide a 360-degree field of view for driving decisions, reporting that all systems passed testing.
All eleven teams produced drivable, nearly-production-ready vehicles imagined, engineered, and built solely by college students.
The competition provides invaluable knowledge and experience for the next generation of automotive innovators.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: College Students Engineer Next-Generation Vehicles in EcoCAR Mobility Challenge

facts: Nearly half of the projects announced in 2019 were from internationally owned companies.

Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development announced in August that international partnerships accounted for nearly $2 billion in foreign direct investment (FDI) in fiscal year 2021.
Foreign direct investments created twenty percent more jobs than during the previous fiscal year.
Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport provides nonstop service to more than 70 international destinations in 42 countries across Asia, Europe, Africa, and Latin America.

Georgia Ports Authority reported record volumes of 3.9 million 20-foot equivalent container units (TEUs) for the fiscal year-to-date (July–March), a 15% increase, with plans to add more than 1.4 million additional TEUs of annual capacity.
The state hosts 70 international consulates, 42 bi-national chambers of commerce, and 25 international trade offices.
Georgia provides access to a labor pool of more than 120,000 graduates annually from over 80 colleges, technical schools, and universities.
South Korea represents the highest job creation from a single country with nineteen projects, bringing more than $1.3 billion in investment and more than 2,700 jobs during 2020 alone.
Swiss-based projects led in total foreign direct investment.
German businesses created the second-highest number of jobs during fiscal year 2021.
SK Innovation, South Korea’s largest energy company, is building two EV battery plants approximately 70 miles northeast of Atlanta.
The project is part of a $2.6 billion investment in SK Innovation’s U.S. battery business, directly creating more than 2,600 permanent jobs in Jackson County by 2024.
The first SK Battery America plant is scheduled to begin initial operations in 2021 with mass production in 2022.
A second plant at the same site is expected to begin mass production in 2023.

In May, Duckyang, an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America, announced a $10 million investment to open its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
The new Duckyang facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, Georgia.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea, and supplies cockpit modules to customers including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.

Dong-in Son, CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd., stated that entering the U.S. EV battery market with SKBA allows cooperation with automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors, with expected business expansion to supply EV parts and interior parts (cockpit module, door trim panel module) to global automakers based in the U.S.
Netherlands-based Vanderlande Industries will invest $59 million to expand its North American headquarters in Cobb County.
The Vanderlande expansion creates 500 new jobs in Marietta, bringing the company’s total Georgia employment to more than 1,250 people.
Vanderlande is the global market leader for value-added logistics automation in airport, parcel, and warehouse markets, with systems active in 600 airports including 14 of the world’s top 20.

Nick Porter, President of Vanderlande’s Airports & Parcel Solutions, North America, stated the company is moving into a new North American head office in Marietta to expand local teams and support North American customers.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport serves 150 U.S. destinations and more than 75 international destinations in 50 countries.
Georgia ranked among the Top 10 exporting states in 2020 for the first time, with merchandise exports reaching 215 unique countries and territories.
The state exported $38.8 billion in goods last year, with total trade reaching $137.7 billion, ranking 7th in the U.S.
Georgia’s top five export markets were Canada, Germany, China, Mexico, and Singapore.
Exports to Germany increased nearly 50%, followed by China at 45% and Hong Kong at 41%.
Increased exports to China reflect new purchases under a January 2020 U.S.-China trade agreement and the return of market access for U.S. poultry.
Approximately two-thirds of Georgia trade involves 12 strategic markets: Brazil, Canada, Chile, China, Colombia, Europe, Israel, Japan, Korea, Mexico, Peru, and the United Kingdom.
Approximately 90% of Georgia merchandise exports are manufactured goods, with manufacturing exports growing by more than 30% over the last 10 years.
Aerospace products remain Georgia’s No. 1 export industry, totaling $9.98 billion in 2020 and ranking 5th in the U.S. for aerospace exports.
Sales to Hong Kong surged more than 140%, while Germany remains the top customer for Georgia aviation products.
A 13% surge in automotive exports over 2019 was driven by deep water ports and an ideal Southeast location for original equipment manufacturers.
The state’s automotive industry increased exports nearly 600% to Germany and more than 800% to China.
Georgia’s agriculture industry delivered $4.25 billion in global exports in 2020, maintaining 2019 totals, with the state remaining a leader in poultry, cotton, peanuts, and forest products.
source_title: Foreign Investment Travels Nonstop to Georgia – Site Selection Magazine

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated mass-production Electrified Vehicle plant.
The all-new manufacturing facility is located just outside of Savannah, Georgia.
The facility features a highly-connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production processes are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system is designed to create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
HMGMA is designed to build more than 500,000 vehicles per year.
Production at the facility includes Hyundai’s luxury brand Genesis.
HMGMA and Kia America celebrated the production of the Kia Sportage Hybrid in Ellabell, Georgia on June 2, 2026.
HMGMA is home to the award-winning IONIQ 5 and the IONIQ 9 three-row SUV.
HMGMA offers training opportunities to workers.
HMGMA is located approximately 25 miles from downtown Savannah in Bryan County, Georgia.
HMGMA welcomed a new fire truck with a traditional wet-down ceremony to strengthen on-site emergency response.
Savannah Technical College and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America commemorated a landmark apprenticeship partnership with an official signing ceremony.
The facility promotes a "Meta Pro" career path for interested workers.

links.context: Careers website for Meta Pro positions and job alerts

source_title: HMGMA - Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America

facts: Duckyang Ind. Co., Ltd. was established in 1977.
Its headquarters and main plant are located in Ulsan, which is considered to be the main industrial city of Korea.
Its second/third plants are located in Gyeongju and Yesan.
Duckyang’s sales amount is approximately $11 billion per year.
The company started its business by supplying crash pads, a plastic item covering the front automotive part on the dashboard for Hyundai Motor Company’s first-ever car, the Pony.
The Pony was the original model leading up to Hyundai Motor’s latest electric car, IONIQ 5.
Duckyang is manufacturing mainly cockpit modules, door trims, and crash pads, which are automotive interior components.
Among these, the cockpit module is the company’s best sales item.
The cockpit module is assembled to accommodate audio, air conditioner, airbag, etc., for a total of approximately 130 items in the front automotive parts around the driver’s seat.
This assembly process is known as ‘Modulization.’
Duckyang is supplying these items for most Hyundai Motor’s models manufactured in Ulsan Plant, Korea.
Duckyang contributes to Korea automotive export industry by supplying interior parts to exported Hyundai Motor’s models, known as indirect export.
Duckyang sought new business opportunities due to paradigm trend changes in the auto industrial from ICE cars to EVs.
Duckyang succeeded in developing components for EVs, including BMA (Battery Module Assembly) and ESS (Energy Storage System).
Regarding BMA, Duckyang was awarded as an R&D national organization hosted by KIAT (Korea Institute for Advancement of Technology).
Duckyang’s 2020 year exports increased by 4% compared with the 2019 year export amount despite challenging conditions caused by COVID-19.
With increasing demand for EVs, Duckyang expects export amounts to increase further.
Duckyang has a separate R&D department, and its R&D facilities are located in Suwon and Gyeongju.
The company successfully focused on decreasing the weight of the cockpit module through R&D.
Duckyang constantly seeks to ensure top-class quality.
Duckyang received the 2021 National Quality Competitiveness Excellent Corporation award from the Ministry of Trade, Industry and energy for eight consecutive years.
Duckyang is establishing Duckyang America in the state of Georgia, USA, with SK Innovation for BMA and ESS business.
source_title: Winner of the $ 700 Million Export Tower Award – Korean-Machinery.com

facts: A Korean auto supplier will invest $10 million and hire 285 people northeast of Atlanta to make parts that will accompany electric vehicle batteries that SK Innovation will produce nearby.
Duckyang Industrial Co. said Thursday that the plant in Braselton will make battery modules and energy storage systems for SK Innovation's $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker plant in Commerce.
Duckyang didn't say when it would start production.
It said it would occupy a 230,000-square foot (21,000-square meter) building in an industrial park near an Amazon warehouse.
The company already supplies cockpit modules, a dashboard assembly of more than 130 parts that includes the steering wheel, instruments and electronics, to automakers including Kia and Hyundai.
Kia has a factory in West Point and Hyundai has a factory in Montgomery, Alabama.

By entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA, Duckyang will have the opportunity to cooperate with many other automotive companies, including Hyundai and Kia Motors, that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S., according to CEO Dong-in Son.
The company hopes to supply interior parts and electrical vehicle parts from Braselton.
Duckyang could claim various tax breaks, including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $1,250 per job from state income taxes, up 50% of its tax liability.
That could save up to $1.8 million over five years, as long as workers make at least $28,000 per year.
Georgia will provide job training, but will not provide any discretionary incentives, according to Gordon.
Braselton and Jackson County could give property tax breaks.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said the plant shows how a flagship company like SK Innovation can bring other suppliers in its wake.
"In Georgia, we're focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain," Wilson said.
SK Innovation announced Thursday that it would form a joint venture with Ford Motor Co. to make batteries and related equipment starting in 2025 to meet Ford's battery needs.
The joint venture, called BlueOval SK, would make enough batteries to require about two jointly owned plants.
Ford said that it will need the capacity of 10 factories worldwide by 2030.
SK said it would spend about $5.3 billion to build the plants, on top of the $2.6 billion it said is spending on the plant in Commerce.

The U.S. International Trade Commission decided in February that SK stole 22 trade secrets from LG Energy, and that SK should be barred from importing, making or selling batteries in the United States for 10 years.
The dispute was settled in April with SK agreeing to pay $1.8 billion and an undisclosed royalty, allowing it to move ahead with production in Commerce.
source_title: EV Battery: Korean firm to invest $10M, hire 285, to make battery parts, ETAuto

facts: Enchem America Inc. will invest $152.5 million to establish electrolyte manufacturing operations in Brownsville, Tennessee.
The new facility will be located at the I-40 Advantage Industrial Park, a Select Tennessee Certified Site.
The project will create 190 new jobs in Tier 4, At-Risk Haywood County.
The Brownsville facility will operate similarly to Enchem’s U.S. headquarters in Commerce, Georgia.
The facility will manufacture electrolytes for electric vehicle (EV) batteries as well as other EV battery-related services.
Enchem America Inc. is a subsidiary of Enchem Co., Ltd., which is based in South Korea.
The company specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes and other additives for rechargeable batteries.
With the additional Tennessee operations, Enchem will be able to further support its EV partners across the Southeast.
Since 2019, TNECD has supported more than 50 economic development projects in the Southwest Tennessee region, resulting in nearly 9,300 job commitments and $7.2 billion in capital investment.

Gov. Bill Lee stated that Enchem’s decision to invest more than $150 million in Tennessee is a testament to the state's unmatched business climate, highly skilled workforce, and position as a leader in the automotive industry.
Gov. Bill Lee thanked Enchem for choosing Tennessee to establish its second U.S. facility and for creating 190 new jobs to provide greater opportunity to Tennesseans across Haywood County.
TNECD Commissioner Stuart McWhorter noted that South Korea is one of Tennessee’s top 10 foreign direct investment partners.
McWhorter stated Enchem is investing in one of Tennessee’s At-Risk counties and boosting the EV supply chain across Tennessee.
Jung Kang Oh, CEO and founder of Enchem Co. Ltd., expressed gratitude to Haywood County, the City of Brownsville, and professionals who assisted Enchem in expanding its North American presence.
Jung Kang Oh highlighted that the growth of the EV market has been remarkable and will continue at an accelerated pace over the coming decade.
Jung Kang Oh stated confidence that the company is well positioned to capitalize on EV market growth and explore new innovation and partnership opportunities with Haywood County and Brownsville.
Haywood County Mayor David Livingston welcomed Enchem, stating the association signifies a commitment to making the EV revolution a reality in Haywood County.
Livingston noted the economic opportunity will transform Haywood County and West Tennessee with great, new high-paying jobs.

Brownsville Mayor Bill Rawls welcomed Enchem America to the city, noting the announcement fulfills years of work and investment in I-40 Advantage Industrial Park to bring high-quality job opportunities.
Rawls expressed looking forward to working with Enchem to build the advanced manufacturing workforce in Brownsville, Tennessee.
TVA and Brownsville Energy Authority congratulated Enchem America on establishing operations in Haywood County.
John Bradley, TVA senior vice president of economic development, stated helping companies like Enchem create jobs and investment is fundamental to TVA’s mission of service.
Bradley noted pride in partnering with HTL Advantage, Brownsville-Haywood County Industrial Development Corporation, City of Brownsville, and TNECD for the announcement.

Sen. Page Walley (R-Savannah) welcomed Enchem America to Haywood County, stating the investment will bring jobs, boost the regional economy, and establish the community as an innovation hub in the rapidly growing industry.
Rep. Johnny Shaw (D-Bolivar) noted the prospect of a more than $152 million investment is an example of unprecedented growth in Brownsville, Haywood County, and West Tennessee.
Shaw stated BlueOval City lit the fuse for what continues to be an economic explosion for the area.
Shaw thanked Enchem America and local/state officials for keeping momentum going in Haywood County.

TNECD's mission is to develop strategies that make Tennessee the No. 1 location in the Southeast for high-quality jobs, attract new corporate investment, and facilitate expansion and economic growth for Tennessee companies.
source_title: Governor Lee, Commissioner McWhorter Announce Enchem America Inc. to Locate US Manufacturing Operations in Brownsville - TNECD

facts: Since 2012, Enchem has surpassed its domestic competitors to compete against global companies.
In 2013, Enchem became the first company in the world to manufacture electrolytes for second generation electric vehicles.
Enchem achieved the “double 10” and the best growth rate in the past decade.
Enchem represents Korea as the best electrolyte manufacturing company.
Enchem is the only electrolyte company with factories in 3 continents and 4 countries.
Enchem has been challenging itself to globalize and localize its operations.
Enchem expects growth that no other electrolyte company has experienced.
Enchem will work towards providing the best electrolyte solution and service.
Enchem aims to go beyond having the most market share nationally to achieve the highest market share internationally.
Enchem aims to become a Top-Tier electrolyte company.
source_title: Enchem

facts: On May 28, 2026, GrowHub Limited signed a legally binding summary term sheet with EnChem America for a reverse triangular merger.
Under the deal terms, EnChem will merge into a newly formed GrowHub subsidiary and become the surviving entity.
EnChem’s shareholders are slated to receive 85% of the combined company’s equity on a fully diluted basis.
Existing GrowHub holders will retain 15% of the equity, subject to customary regulatory and shareholder approvals, including Nasdaq rules on new share issuance.
The deal values EnChem at a minimum of $400 million and treats it as the accounting acquirer.
The transaction represents a transformative reverse takeover that will effectively pivot GrowHub into the battery electrolyte materials sector.
The deal structure includes a valuation floor that allows EnChem to walk away if GrowHub’s trading price falls below $4 before closing.
The merger is designed to support substantial post-merger capital raising via a large shelf registration and follow-on financings once the combined company is in place.
GrowHub Limited is a Cayman Islands–incorporated holding company listed on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol TGHL and reporting as a foreign private issuer.
GrowHub serves as a public vehicle for corporate combinations and capital-raising transactions.
GrowHub is positioning itself as the listed platform for EnChem America, a U.S. subsidiary of Korea’s EnChem Co.
EnChem America focuses on battery electrolyte materials.
source_title: GrowHub Signs Binding Reverse Merger Deal With EnChem America - TipRanks.com

Production capacity at the Jecheon site is 5,000 tons in 2025 and 7,000 tons expected in 2026.
Enchem aims to open the future electrolyte, the future of lithium-ion battery and the future of electric vehicle.
The company has established a stable mid-to long-term supply system by the internalization of core raw materials.

facts: Enchem America is a South Korean battery component maker.
The company backed out of plans to build an electrolyte manufacturing facility.
The planned facility had a projected investment value of $152.5 million.
The project would have brought 190 jobs to Haywood County.
Enchem America announced its plan to build the facility in 2023.
The proposed site was located on the 'shovel ready' I-40 Advantage Industrial Park in Brownsville.
The site is roughly 16 miles from BlueOval.
source_title: Enchem America Archives • Tennessee Lookout

facts: Enchem is a global leader in electrolyte production for lithium-ion batteries.
The company established its electrolyte production facility in Georgia as early as 2019.
The Georgia plant is the only facility in the U.S. capable of producing 105,000 tons of electrolyte annually.
The Georgia plant supplies major global battery makers including Tesla, SK On, Ultium Cells (a joint venture between LGES and GM), Panasonic, and AESC.
Enchem plans to expand the Georgia plant’s capacity to 200,000 tons annually by 2026.
Additional facilities are planned in Tennessee and Ontario, Canada.
Trump’s second term is likely to focus on aggressive economic measures including tax cuts, universal tariffs, strengthened protectionism, and countering China’s influence.
Proposals for tariffs of up to 60% on Chinese non-consumer goods are reportedly under discussion.
These tariff proposals are expected to signal stricter regulations than those imposed under Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
The measures are expected to weaken the competitiveness of Chinese-made electrolyte products while creating significant opportunities for U.S.-based manufacturers like Enchem.
Trump’s proposed tariff hikes on Chinese products are expected to prompt U.S. battery manufacturers to shift their supply chains toward local companies like Enchem.

Competitors may need to invest in costly new facilities to maintain their foothold in the U.S., while Enchem’s existing infrastructure and proven supply track record create a significant barrier to entry.
Tesla is one of Enchem’s key customers.

Tesla is expected to benefit greatly from Trump’s policies, such as relaxed regulations on autonomous vehicles and further “America First” incentives, potentially reducing production costs and driving market expansion.
Tesla’s stock price surged by approximately 70% following the announcement of the U.S. election results.
Enchem is the only Korean company directly supplying electrolytes to Tesla.

An Enchem representative stated that expanding supply capabilities in North America and enhancing production capacity remain top priorities as they aim to establish themselves as a global leader in the electrolyte industry.
Enchem plans to expand its presence in Europe and Asia by operating large-scale production facilities in key regions worldwide.
Enchem continues to strengthen partnerships with global battery manufacturers to drive rapid growth.
source_title: Trump's Second Term, Enchem’s ‘Tesla Boom’: Spotlight on the Biggest Beneficiaries in the U.S. Electrolyte Market.

facts: Enchem America Inc. is located in Commerce, GA.
The facility operates in the Chemicals sector and is classified under NAICS 325998 (Chemicals).
Enchem America Inc. files annual Form R disclosures to the EPA Toxics Release Inventory under EPCRA Section 313.
Based on annual facility disclosures from 2019 through 2023, the site has reported toxic releases to the EPA Toxic Release Inventory.
Across 2 years of records spanning 2022–2023, the site disclosed a cumulative total of 1.9K lbs in toxic releases across 2 distinct chemicals.
One of the reported chemicals is N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone, which accounts for 1.9K lbs of the total releases.
The second reported chemical is 1,3-Propane sultone, which accounts for 0 lbs and carries a carcinogenic classification.
Of the chemicals reported, 1 is classified as a carcinogen, representing 50% of the facility's chemical profile.
The primary release pathway is air releases, accounting for 61% of total releases.
Air releases specifically account for 1.2K lbs (60.5% of total volume), followed by water discharges at 39.5%.
Between 2022 and 2023, reported toxic release totals increased by 1067%, rising from 150 lbs to 1.8K lbs.
In 2022, the facility reported 150 lbs of releases across 1 chemical.
In 2023, the facility reported 1.8K lbs of releases across 2 chemicals.
TRI Form R values are self-reported estimates rather than measured stack emissions and treat every pound of reported chemical equally regardless of toxicity.
Inclusion in the TRI database does not establish a health risk to nearby residents but documents annual reporting under federal community right-to-know requirements.

Comparison data indicates the facility's reported pounds and chemical counts are 100% and 92% less than respective comparison benchmarks, reflecting reported pounds and chemical counts rather than toxicity or health risk.
Data is current as of the 2023 reporting year.
Data is sourced from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Toxic Release Inventory program.
Facilities that manufacture, process, or otherwise use listed toxic chemicals above threshold amounts are required to report annually to the EPA under the TRI program.

links.context: EPA Toxics Release Inventory Basic Data Files
EPA TRI Program chemical and reporting reference
EPA Enforcement and Compliance History Online

source_title: Enchem America Inc. EPA Record - Commerce, GA | Toxic Releases & Compliance

facts: Korean companies have invested $23 billion in Georgia over the past decade, amounting to about 32 trillion won.
This investment accounts for nearly 15% of the total U.S. investment of $156 billion during the same period.
Hyundai Motor plans to invest $5.5 billion by 2025 in Georgia’s first dedicated electric vehicle manufacturing facility.
SK On will invest 6.5 trillion won in a joint battery manufacturing plant with Hyundai Motor.
LG Energy Solution will invest 2.65 trillion won in a joint battery manufacturing plant with Hyundai Motor.
The relationship between Georgia and Korean companies began in 1999 when SKC built a videotape factory (now a PET film factory) in Georgia.
SKC is currently constructing a semiconductor glass substrate factory in Georgia, scheduled to start operations within the year.
SKC and its subsidiary Absolics have invested $240 million (370 billion won) in Georgia.
Korean companies began entering Georgia in earnest after Kia Motors established a plant in 2009.
Kumho Tire established a production facility in Georgia in 2016.
Hyundai Mobis established a production facility in Georgia in 2022.
About 140 Korean companies have currently entered the Georgia market.
SK On has a battery plant in Georgia.
Battery material company Enchem has electrolyte facilities in Georgia.
Deokyang Industry has battery module and energy storage system facilities in Georgia.
Sungil Hightech is investing in used battery recycling facilities in Georgia.
Korean companies are building a supply chain covering electric vehicles and batteries in Georgia.
Georgia is expected to become a hub for the U.S. electric vehicle industry once the supply chain is completed.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development received the Van Fleet Award jointly with the Korea International Trade Association at the Korea Society Annual Dinner in New York on September 20.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Korean companies have created more jobs in Georgia than any other foreign direct investment over the past three years.
GDEcD announced on the 3rd that Georgia has ranked first overall for 10 consecutive years since 2014 in Area Development's "Best State for Business" rankings.

Georgia received the highest scores compared to other regions in seven categories, including business costs, competitive labor environment, workforce development programs, available real estate, and state government cooperation.
Georgia has become the third-largest region in the U.S. for Fortune 500 company headquarters, with 18 of these companies headquartered in Atlanta.
The Georgia government offers investment attraction packages that include site selection, business permits, connections with infrastructure companies, and investment incentives.
The Georgia government offers the "Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD)" program to help with rapid site selection.
The GRAD program provides information on industrial sites for factories or offices based on a company's desired schedule.

The GRAD program conducts essential preliminary investigations (environmental assessments, cultural asset and endangered species surveys) in advance and approves sites via an expert committee to eliminate additional investigations.
Georgia is divided into 12 grid-like zones, each with a regional project manager assigned to closely support incoming companies.
Tax incentives for attracting investment include job creation tax benefits such as employment tax credits and superior employment tax credits.
Companies using Georgia ports or developing new products and services in Georgia receive separate tax credits related to regional economic development.
Over the past decade (2010-2020), Georgia’s average population growth rate was 10.6%, surpassing the U.S. average of 7.4%.
As of 2021, 460,000 people moved to Georgia in the past five years.
Georgia's average population age is 36.4 years, which is 4% younger than the U.S. average.
source_title: Korean Companies Invested 32 Trillion Won Over 10 Years... How Did Georgia Become a Hub for 'K-Manufacturing'? - The Asia Business Daily

facts: Enchem (348370) is a specialist in secondary battery electrolytes.
Enchem's US subsidiary secured large-scale tariff refunds and Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (AMPC) benefits by leveraging its local production base in the United States.
On the 15th, Enchem announced that its US subsidiary is undergoing a tariff refund process based on the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).
The total amount filed for the tariff refund is $5.45 million (~8 billion won).
Of the filed amount, 63%, or $3.44 million (~5 billion won), has already been refunded.
The company expects the remaining balance to be returned sequentially according to US government administrative procedures.
The refund follows a ruling by the US Court of International Trade (CIT).
Resolving the tariff burden incurred during local distribution and import stages is expected to significantly improve the financial health and cash flow of the US subsidiary.
The cumulative AMPC accumulated by the US subsidiary from 2023 to 2025 amounts to approximately $29.78 million (~45 billion won).
An additional receipt of about $15 million (~23 billion won) is virtually confirmed for this year.
The total cumulative benefit over four years is projected to reach $45 million (~68 billion won).

An Enchem official stated that the tariff refunds and AMPC benefits are not simply a policy windfall, but "the fruit of a global supply chain strategy that took risks to preemptively establish a local electrolyte production system in Georgia, USA."

The same official added that as the US battery industry prioritizes transparency in raw material procurement and supply chain stability centered on allied nations, "the value of Enchem's local production base will only increase day by day."
Industry observers note these results mark the point where the financial outcomes of years of North American investment are beginning to materialize.
The North American battery market is rapidly expanding beyond electric vehicles (EVs) into the energy storage system (ESS) sector.
Demand for 'long-life, high-safety electrolytes'—which determine battery lifespan and safety—is exploding due to a surge in AI data center construction and power infrastructure investment.
Enchem's strategy is to rapidly transfer its quality control capabilities and global operational experience, proven in the existing EV electrolyte market, to the ESS sector.

A company official expressed confidence in the local production base's competitiveness, stating that "the number of companies capable of supplying high-quality electrolytes in a timely manner within North America is limited."
Enchem was founded in 2012 and listed on the KOSDAQ market in 2021.
The company's main business is the manufacture and sale of electrolytes and additives for secondary batteries and EDLCs.
Enchem is pursuing a waste NMP (recycling) business generated after secondary battery manufacturing as a new growth engine.
Industry analysts suggest this performance could be a structural turning point that strengthens Enchem's position within the North American supply chain.
Potential changes in the US administration's trade policies or the possibility of AMPC system reform are cited as variables requiring continuous monitoring.
source_title: Enchem Secures approximately $50 Million via US Tariff Refunds, Tax Credits as North American Bet Pays Off — BigGo Finance

facts: POH+W Architects designed an expansion for F&P Georgia Manufacturing’s existing facility in Rome, Georgia.
The facility expansion was delivered in a Design/Build team approach.
The expansion comprised a 25,500-SF warehouse area.
The expansion comprised a 33,160-SF stamping facility.
The expansion comprised a 3,200-SF office addition.
The total size of the expansion is 61,860-SF.
The expansion design improved material handling and overall production workflow for an efficient facility.
Each expansion was located contiguous to their existing facilities.
Each expansion was designed with similar exterior prefabricated insulated metal panels to complement their existing facilities.
Project features include loading docks.
Project features include shipping and receiving areas.
Project features include weigh scales.
Project features include coil storage.
Project features include high bay storage areas.
Project features include overhead cranes.
Project features include conveyors.
Project features include catwalks.
Project features include mezzanines.
The facility includes provisions for a 3,000-Ton press.
The facility includes provisions for a 1,000-Ton press.
The facility includes deep structural foundations for each of the presses.
The facility includes recessed pits.
The facility includes a scrap room.
source_title: F&P Georgia Manufacturing - POH+W Architects

facts: Rivian Automotive Inc. announced it will increase initial production capacity at its planned Georgia manufacturing facility to 300,000 vehicles annually.
The increased capacity is up from the previously planned 200,000 units.
Rivian is valued at a market cap of $19.93 billion as it works to scale production and improve unit economics.
The expanded capacity at the Stanton Springs North facility represents a 50 percent increase aimed at reducing per-unit costs.
The design allows for additional capacity expansion in later phases.
Rivian has updated its loan agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing.
The total DOE loan stands at up to $4.5 billion, comprising $4,006 million in principal and $494 million in capitalized interest for the initial phase.
The company expects to begin drawing on the loan in early 2027 as vertical construction commences in 2026.
Vehicle production at the facility remains scheduled to begin in late 2028.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe said, "We’re very excited to partner with the U.S. Department of Energy to grow our manufacturing footprint in Georgia."
RJ Scaringe added, "R2 dramatically expands our market opportunity."
Vertical construction is expected to start this spring, with preparations underway for the stamping press area.
Construction progress is expected to accelerate during the summer months as primary buildings take shape.
Rivian previously announced a partnership with Uber to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at the Georgia plant beginning in late 2028.
source_title: Rivian increases Georgia plant capacity to 300,000 vehicles By Investing.com

facts: Auto parts manufacturer PHA will spend $67 million on a new facility in Georgia.
The facility is intended to supply Hyundai Motor Group’s upcoming EV campus, known as the Metaplant.
The governor’s office announced the investment last week.
The Savannah-area facility will produce door modules.
The facility will also produce tailgate and hood latches.
These components are designed for direct use in automakers’ assembly lines.
PHA selected the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center to house its new facility.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2024.
The facility is expected to create 402 jobs.
South Korea-based PHA joins a growing number of parts manufacturers streaming to Georgia to support Hyundai’s future EV campus.
Hyundai’s future EV campus broke ground in October.
Georgia officials originally predicted offsite suppliers would invest nearly $1 billion in locations around the state.
Investments from offsite suppliers to date have exceeded that prediction by $800 million.
PHA will be the second supplier to settle at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
Seoyon E-Hwa announced plans in early February to invest almost $76 million in a facility for interior auto parts at the same location.
The industrial site is part of the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) program.
The GRAD program has fast-tracked up to 60 locations for construction through advance due diligence by the state.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that projects like Hyundai and its suppliers underscore the importance of site preparation to attract jobs and opportunities to a community.
Pat Wilson noted that companies are prioritizing locations that have already completed GRAD certification to meet demand and come online quickly.
source_title: Hyundai’s Georgia EV campus attracts another supplier | Manufacturing Dive

facts: The Rivian Plant in Georgia will be built on 2,000 acres of land.
The plant will employ around 7,500 workers.
The facility is more than five times as large as the Rivian plant in Normal, Illinois.
Company plans to begin producing vehicles at the Georgia site in 2024.
Production capacity will be 400,000 vehicles per year.
Opponents cite concerns including groundwater contamination.
Opponents disapprove of the lucrative public incentives offered by the company.
Opponents worry that the huge facility will change the bucolic character of the area.
Opponents point to increased traffic and light pollution as potential problems.
The plant is a source of contention in Georgia’s Republican primary for governor.
Republican candidate David Perdue has been critical of Governor Brian Kemp’s acceptance of the plant’s location.
New plant will include a test track, adventure trail, 144 electric vehicle charging stations, training center, waste and recycling facilities, and outdoor amenity spaces.
Rivian Motors vice president of public policy stated the company plans to build a battery factory in Georgia.
Company plans to produce 400,000 electric trucks annually and employ 7,500 people.
Rivian is building an EV assembly plant in Georgia and offers competitive pay, benefits, stock, and ownership to employees.
Plant will create spinoff jobs from its suppliers.
Local officials and residents say the project will create jobs, but concerns remain about traffic, groundwater, and urban sprawl.
Rivian says the factory will adhere to all local ordinances.
Opponents fear the factory will destroy the rural landscape and disrupt the ecology.
Opponents are concerned about the destruction of a 200-year-old house and the displacement of residents.
Future of the Georgia electric truck industry depends on availability of skilled labor.
Significant labor shortage in Georgia is a challenge for Rivian, requiring influx of IT specialists, software engineers, and technically skilled assembly line workers.
Governor Brian Kemp endorsed the Rivian factory, making him a target for opponents.
Former senator David Perdue is also opposed to the plant.
Rivian has committed to investing in Georgia’s economy despite criticism over alleged pollution and startup financial losses.
Company plans to deliver 300,000 vehicles a year.
Ford F-150 Lightning is currently available for purchase in Georgia at 45 charging stations.
source_title: How Electric Truck Lightning Georgia? [Answered 2026] | Prettymotors

facts: Georgia is currently home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities contributing $3bn (£2.1bn) annually to the state’s economy.
These facilities include companies involved in the production, recycling, and development of EV batteries.
The state has a fleet of around 30,000 EVs and 930 public charging stations.
More than 116 million EVs on the road are expected by 2030.
Nearly 60% of all passenger vehicle miles around the world will be in an electric vehicle by 2030.
Georgia Power is dedicated to partnering with customers, businesses and government entities across the state to ensure they have the latest in charging capabilities and infrastructure.
OEMs in the state and throughout the south-eastern US are already shifting their product lines to keep up with the increasing production of EVs.
Georgia Power is working with state authorities to support the EV needs of OEMs including Mercedes-Benz, BMW, Kia and Volkswagen.
Working with the Georgia Public Service Commission, Georgia Power has installed 51 direct current fast chargers in the state.
Georgia Power will invest nearly $6m by 2022 in fast-charging infrastructure across Georgia.
Ford and South Korea’s SK Innovation (SKI) announced they will manufacture next-generation battery cells and arrays in the US as part of a new joint venture – BlueOvalSK.
SKI subsidiary, SK Battery America, is set to start mass production of lithium-ion EV batteries at its new plant in Georgia in 2022.
SK Battery America will follow up with a second EV battery facility in 2023.
Nicole Faulk serves as senior VP of customer strategy and solutions at Georgia Power.
source_title: EV manufacturing supply chain 'powering investments in Georgia'

facts: F & P America Manufacturing's revenues are gauged from an analysis of company filings.
F & P America Manufacturing's Income Statement is based on industry averages.

The page outlines a standard P&L structure including Revenue (Sales), Cost of Goods Sold, Gross Profit, Operating Expenses, Advertising, Salaries and wages, Other Operating Expenses, Total Operating Expenses, Operating Income, EBITDA, EBIT, and Net Profit.
A competitive analysis shows other companies are in the same general field as F & P America Manufacturing, though they may not compete head-to-head.
The largest companies by revenue in the industry may not have the largest market share if they have diversified into other business lines.
Competitive advantage is described as coming from offering better pricing or superior products/service.
Some companies compete with neighboring businesses for customers, while others compete to attract skilled employees.
Future competition includes F & P America Manufacturing's fastest growing competitors, which may grow organically or through acquisition.
In some cases apparently high growth rates may be caused by data that weren't available in previous years.
source_title: Annual Report on F & P America Manufacturing's Revenue, Growth, SWOT Analysis & Competitor Intelligence - IncFact

facts: The Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership (GaMEP) is a program of the Enterprise Innovation Institute at Georgia Tech.
GaMEP received recognition from Gov. Brian P. Kemp at the Georgia State Capitol for 65 years of service to the manufacturing industry.
GaMEP supports an industry that adds $82 billion to the state economy and employs 425,000 residents, according to the National Association of Manufacturers.
By 1960, more than 4,500 manufacturers had established operations across Georgia due to strategic efforts by state leaders with economic development assistance from Georgia Tech.
The Georgia General Assembly voted to establish the Georgia Tech Industrial Extension Service (now GaMEP) to provide technical support to manufacturers facing new challenges.
GaMEP created statewide field offices that deliver on-site technical expertise to link industry and innovation.
Tim Israel serves as GaMEP director and Enterprise Innovation Institute associate vice president for corporate engagement/firm-based programs.
In 2024, GaMEP generated $294 in return for every state dollar allocated to manufacturing projects.
Over the past decade, GaMEP provided assistance and education to more than 3,900 manufacturers across 144 counties.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped create or retain 14,500 jobs.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped manufacturers invest $1 billion in capital improvements.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped realize $3.5 billion in sales.
Over the past decade, GaMEP helped save nearly $450 million in costs.
GaMEP primarily serves small- to medium-sized manufacturers, with 75% employing fewer than 250 workers.
GaMEP's top-served industries include fabricated metal products, food, machinery, and chemical and transportation equipment manufacturing.
David Bridges serves as Enterprise Innovation Institute vice president.
GaMEP's reach spans 10 regions across the state, each led by a dedicated region manager who lives and works locally.
Lloyd Avram is the CEO and president of the Georgia Association of Manufacturers.

In the Coastal Region (counties: Bryan, Bulloch, Camden, Chatham, Effingham, Glynn, Liberty, Long, McIntosh, Screven), GaMEP served 269 manufacturers, created/retained 283 jobs, facilitated $23,171,292 in capital improvements, saved $1,645,061 in costs, and realized $13,965,000 in sales, with Roger Wood Foods as a success story.

In the East Region (counties: Burke, Columbia, Glascock, Hancock, Jefferson, Jenkins, Lincoln, McDuffie, Richmond, Taliaferro, Warren, Washington, Wilkes), GaMEP served 169 manufacturers, created/retained 3,899 jobs, facilitated $127,754,280 in capital improvements, saved $14,771,582 in costs, and realized $975,465,000 in sales, with Nutritional Resources as a success story.

In the East Central Region (counties: Barrow, Clarke, Elbert, Greene, Jackson, Jasper, Madison, Morgan, Newton, Oconee, Oglethorpe, Walton), GaMEP served 209 manufacturers, created/retained 621 jobs, facilitated $19,703,035 in capital improvements, saved $2,535,494 in costs, and realized $29,486,000 in sales, with Creature Comforts Brewing Company and Poly Tech Industries as success stories.

In the Metro Atlanta Region (counties: Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, DeKalb, Douglas, Fayette, Fulton, Gwinnett, Henry, Rockdale), GaMEP served 1,601 manufacturers, created/retained 2,928 jobs, facilitated $235,763,480 in capital improvements, saved $112,083,262 in costs, and realized $844,679,890 in sales, with Compass Technology Group, Construction Specialties, and Highland Forge as success stories.

In the Middle Region (counties: Baldwin, Bibb, Crawford, Houston, Jones, Monroe, Peach, Pulaski, Putnam, Twiggs, Wilkinson), GaMEP served 170 manufacturers, created/retained 972 jobs, facilitated $121,814,846 in capital improvements, saved $8,810,950 in costs, and realized $300,213,400 in sales, with Unified Defense as a success story.

In the Northeast Region (counties: Banks, Dawson, Forsyth, Franklin, Habersham, Hall, Hart, Lumpkin, Rabun, Stephens, Towns, Union, White), GaMEP served 280 manufacturers, created/retained 1,029 jobs, facilitated $88,443,395 in capital improvements, saved $63,999,228 in costs, and realized $259,453,900 in sales, with Freudenberg Sealing Technologies as a success story.

In the Northwest Region (counties: Bartow, Catoosa, Chattooga, Dade, Fannin, Floyd, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Walker, Whitfield), GaMEP served 387 manufacturers, created/retained 1,090 jobs, facilitated $92,948,931 in capital improvements, saved $141,460,651 in costs, and realized $326,366,408 in sales, with Trenton Pressing as a success story.

In the South Region (counties: Appling, Bleckley, Candler, Dodge, Emanuel, Evans, Jeff Davis, Johnson, Laurens, Montgomery, Tattnall, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, Wayne, Wheeler, Wilcox), GaMEP served 176 manufacturers, created/retained 969 jobs, facilitated $219,300,221 in capital improvements, saved $6,596,254 in costs, and realized $39,632,275 in sales, with Healthy Pet as a success story.

In the Southeast Region (counties: Atkinson, Bacon, Ben Hill, Berrien, Brantley, Brooks, Charlton, Clinch, Coffee, Cook, Echols, Irwin, Lanier, Lowndes, Pierce, Tift, Turner, Ware), GaMEP served 166 manufacturers, created/retained 281 jobs, facilitated $20,684,800 in capital improvements, saved $3,098,700 in costs, and realized $77,724,500 in sales, with CJB Industries and South Georgia Pecan as success stories.

In the Southwest Region (counties: Baker, Calhoun, Colquitt, Decatur, Dougherty, Early, Grady, Lee, Miller, Mitchell, Seminole, Terrell, Thomas, Worth), GaMEP served 130 manufacturers, created/retained 130 jobs, facilitated $12,266,730 in capital improvements, saved $1,682,790 in costs, and realized $5,002,020 in sales, with Sweet Grass Dairy as a success story.

In the West Region (counties: Chattahoochee, Clay, Crisp, Dooly, Harris, Macon, Marion, Muscogee, Quitman, Randolph, Schley, Stewart, Sumter, Talbot, Taylor, Webster), GaMEP served 111 manufacturers, created/retained 713 jobs, facilitated $37,948,131 in capital improvements, saved $3,197,600 in costs, and realized $69,588,348 in sales, with Oneda Corporation as a success story.

In the West Central Region (counties: Butts, Carroll, Coweta, Heard, Lamar, Meriwether, Pike, Spalding, Troup, Upson), GaMEP served 234 manufacturers, created/retained 1,658 jobs, facilitated $90,750,763 in capital improvements, saved $89,931,074 in costs, and realized $615,900,002 in sales, with Complete Truck Bodies and Mountville Mills as success stories.
GaMEP is a member of the U.S. Department of Commerce National Institute of Standards and Technology Manufacturing Extension Partnership.
GaMEP has advanced manufacturing and economic prosperity in Georgia since 1960.
source_title: Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership at Georgia Tech Celebrates 65 Years of Service

facts: Manufacturing facilities in Georgia generated more than $79 billion and employed more than 430,000 people in 2023.
Manufacturers accounted for 8.27% of the state’s workforce as of the second quarter of 2024.
The Georgia legislature created the Georgia Made branding program to recognize the impact of manufacturing.
Lindsay Martin oversees the Georgia Made program as director of small business, Georgia Made and GRAD Certification for the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
There are an estimated more than 7,000 manufacturing facilities statewide, making up nearly 10% of the state’s gross domestic product.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America became Georgia Made certified in February 2025.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is dedicated to building electrified vehicles in Georgia.
Brent Stubbs serves as chief administrative officer at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hurst Boiler & Welding Company in Thomas County was one of the first manufacturers to receive Georgia Made certification in 2020.
Chad Fletcher is director of purchasing and aftermarket sales at Hurst Boiler & Welding Company.
Hurst Boiler & Welding Company has been an American manufacturer of industrial boilers since 1967.
In 2007, Hurst completed a large expansion that doubled its manufacturing space to 350,000 square feet on a campus of more than 48 acres in Coolidge.
Hurst Boiler & Welding Company employs 250 people in Coolidge, which has a population of just 529.
Hurst recruits employees from nearby universities like Southern Regional Technical College, which has a campus less than 20 minutes away in Thomasville.
EssilorLuxottica manufactures lenses and distributes prescription eyeglasses and sunglasses at its 1,700-employee manufacturing plant in McDonough.
Rob Harris is senior director of optical manufacturing for EssilorLuxottica.
EssilorLuxottica uses the Georgia Made designation as a selling point to attract potential suppliers and employees.
Companies like EssilorLuxottica benefit from alignment with the Technical College System of Georgia and workforce training initiatives including Georgia Quick Start.
Kubota Manufacturing of America Corp. uses multiple programs associated with the Technical College System of Georgia to bolster its workforce.
Ryan Owen is vice president of human resources, facilities, quality, safety and environmental at Kubota Manufacturing of America Corp.
Kubota has been building tractors, mowers and utility vehicles on a 180-acre campus in Gainesville since 1988.
Kubota expanded four times since 2003 and now employs 3,100 workers across three campuses in Hall County.
Kubota's research and development facility is located in Gainesville.
Kubota Industrial Equipment subsidiary has operated large manufacturing and distribution facilities in Jackson County since 2005.
Georgia Quick Start is a company-specific workforce development program that helps train employees for high-tech jobs.
GDEcD Chief Operating Officer Sydne Smith notes that many manufacturers have benefited from the Georgia Quick Start program.
GEORGIA MATCH is a program that informs high school seniors which schools would admit them if they applied to encourage upskilling.
Technical colleges tailor their program offerings to reflect the local industry mix and work with local industry to place graduates.
In 2018, Kubota's Gainesville manufacturing plant and Jefferson industrial equipment facility donated $1 million to the Lanier Technical College Foundation.
The Kubota donation funded the purchase of state-of-the-art equipment in Lanier Technical College's technical and industrial building, located about one mile from the Gainesville plant.
TenCate Protective Fabrics is celebrating its 100-year anniversary as a Georgia manufacturer in 2025.
TenCate Protective Fabrics produces flame-resistant textiles used in clothing and gear for firefighters, military personnel and industrial safety professionals.
The company was originally founded in 1925 as Southern Mills in Union City and acquired by TenCate Protective Fabrics in 2004.
TenCate Protective Fabrics received Georgia Made certification in 2024.
TenCate Protective Fabrics employs 381 workers at four separate manufacturing facilities in Senoia, Zebulon and Molena and its corporate office and lab in Union City.
CEO Maria Gallahue-Worl states that the Georgia Made certification is very important for the economic vitality of the areas where TenCate operates.
Fabrics manufactured by TenCate are used by local fire departments in Macon, Cobb County, Gwinnett County and Coweta County, as well as the Fire Department of New York.
Roper Corp. has held Georgia Made certification since 2020.
Roper Corp. is a wholly owned subsidiary of GE Appliances (GEA).
Roper manufactures cooktops, freestanding ranges and wall ovens at its 2,500-employee facility in LaFayette.
Since 2016, GE Appliances invested $161 million and created 700 new jobs in Walker County for the Roper plant.
In 2023, Roper showcased upgrades from a $118 million investment to expand its manufacturing capabilities.
The Roper expansion unveiled assembly lines featuring advanced manufacturing technology to build new models of freestanding ranges available in 2024.
The Roper expansion more than tripled the robotics used in the factory, improving efficiency and ergonomics for employees.
The state of Georgia gave grant money to the Roper plant to fund an existing apprenticeship program with Georgia Northwestern Technical College that trained 45 employees in robotics.
The Roper-GNTC apprenticeship program started in 2017 with workers training in GNTC’s mechatronics program at its Catoosa County campus.
Luther Ingram is president and executive director at Roper Corp.
More than 50% of all homes in the U.S. have at least one GE appliance, many of which are made at the LaFayette manufacturing plant.
source_title: Made in Georgia: Manufacturing Excellence - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) investment broke a new record in Fiscal Year 2025.
Between July 1, 2024, and June 30, 2025, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 423 facility expansions and new locations.
These commitments represent more than $26.3 billion in investment, a new record.
The projects will create 23,200 new private-sector jobs over the next few years.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia has been the No. 1 state for business for over a decade due to its unmatched ability to attract new companies and foster growth for existing employers.
Expansions at existing Georgia companies accounted for 74% of the FY25 projects.
These expansions build on an estimated 50,000 jobs already employing Georgians.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones highlighted investments in workforce programs with partners including the University System of Georgia, the Technical College System of Georgia, and College Career Academies to ensure a steady talent pipeline.
77% of all FY25 expansions and new locations occurred in communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
Metro Atlanta remains one of the Southeast’s top business hubs.
Companies like TriNet, A.I.G., CRH, Duracell, and Mercedes-Benz established or expanded key corporate functions in the 10-county Atlanta region.
Speaker Jon Burns cited Georgia’s ports at Savannah and Brunswick, extensive rail network, vast energy infrastructure, and world-class roads as key business advantages.
Georgia attracted more than 6,500 new jobs from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S.
The Republic of Korea, Japan, and Canada were the top sources of international investments.
International companies committed to more than $3 billion in investment, with 64% slated for expansions within Georgia.

facts: General Motors Co. announced an investment of USD 491 Million in September 2022 to increase the manufacturing of steel and aluminum stamped parts for future products, including electric vehicles, at its Marion metal stamping facility in Indiana.

Automotive manufacturers are shifting towards electric and hybrid vehicles, creating a growing need for specialized metal components for battery enclosures, powertrain components, and charging infrastructure.

Carmakers are pushing for design innovations that bolster the requirement for advanced stamping technologies like progressive die stamping and deep drawing to meet EV demands for lighter materials such as aluminum.

The automotive industry is a major driver of metal stamping demand, utilizing stamped parts for body panels, engine parts, transmission systems, chassis components, brackets, and structural reinforcements.

Steel accounted for the largest market share in 2025 due to demand for stamped components made from steel materials, which are crucial for automotive body panels and chassis components due to high strength and cost-effectiveness.

Mechanical presses led the market in 2025 and are widely used in automotive manufacturing for producing complex and precision components such as car body parts, engine components, and transmission parts.
The U.S. metal stamping market is growing due to a high defense budget, industrial demand, civilian manufacturing stability, and significant demand for automobile component production.
Increasing drives for reshoring manufacturing coupled with reducing reliance on imports are strengthening domestic U.S. markets.
Federal policies and increasing awareness regarding sustainability are spurring innovation in the U.S. metal stamping industry.
