facts: Honda led the nation’s largest segment as Toyota navigates a disruptive model changeover.
Shareholders reappointed CEO Toshihiro Mibe after his EV bet drove the first losses in nearly seven decades.
Some say he deserves more time amid promises of profit rebound.
Production of the new Element is slated to begin in the second quarter of 2029.
CEO of Honda Canada warned about a possible loss of manufacturing and related jobs as a result of U.S. tariffs and shifting policies.
source_title: Honda News - Automotive News

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp touted that the establishment of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County opens up new jobs and breathes life into the local economy.
The opening ceremony for HMGMA took place on Wednesday in Ellabell, Georgia.
HMGMA represents about 8,500 jobs and over $7.5 billion in investment.
Both the job and investment figures for HMGMA are records for projects with the state economic development team.
Direct or outside suppliers have already committed another 6,900 jobs and over $25 billion in investment.
Supplier investments are happening in over a dozen different counties.
Hyundai plans to increase the new plant's annual production capacity from the current 300,000 to 500,000 vehicles.
The planned capacity increase is equivalent to building an additional manufacturing plant.
Gov. Brian Kemp described the project as a historic investment and a testament to Hyundai’s belief in Georgia, its people, and the benefit of their partnership.
Kia, Hyundai Motor’s sister affiliate, has been operating a manufacturing plant in West Point, Georgia since 2009.
Kia's Georgia plant has an annual production capacity of about 350,000 vehicles.
Kia has invested around $2 billion in the state.
More than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs are linked to Kia's operations in the region.
US Rep. Buddy Carter, a Republican representing the local district, welcomed the grand opening of the megasized vehicle production plant.
Rep. Carter stated that Hyundai made a wise decision and praised the area's people as dedicated, hard-working, and prideful.
Rep. Carter extended a welcome to Hyundai leadership on behalf of the state.
Gov. Kemp noted that organic growth for local businesses will contribute to a new level of economic success and prosperity for decades to come.
source_title: With Hyundai’s arrival, Georgia becomes hub for next-gen manufacturing - The Korea Herald

facts: Hyundai officially opened its $7.6 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, Bryan County on Wednesday.
The plant spans 11.76 million-square-meter (126.58 million-square-foot).
The facility opening will likely allow Hyundai EV buyers to qualify for up to $7,500 in U.S. government subsidies.
The plant aims to propel Hyundai's position in the U.S. EV market, which is currently dominated by Tesla.
The facility includes an automobile factory and a battery facility.
The plant is key to raising Hyundai's total U.S. production to 1.2 million units.
The auto facility's current capacity stands at 300,000 units.
Hyundai aims to increase the facility's capacity to 500,000 units in the future.
This planned capacity will exceed Hyundai's Alabama plant capacity of 360,000 units and Kia's Georgia plant capacity of 340,000 units.
Hyundai's Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 SUVs are currently being manufactured at the plant.
Kia’s hybrid vehicles and Genesis vehicles are slated to roll off the assembly line in the future.

Euisun Chung, executive chair of Hyundai Motor Group, spoke during the plant's opening ceremony alongside Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, Hyundai Motor Group Vice Chairman Chang Jae-hoon, and CEO José Muñoz.

President Trump signed an executive order on his first day directing agencies to immediately suspend disbursement of funds allocated under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), including for EV charger implementation and tax credit system re-examination.
Hyundai and Kia's combined U.S. sales of eco-friendly cars stood at 346,441 units last year, which the plant's 300,000-unit capacity can cover.
Combined EV sales in the U.S. were 6,962 units in February, representing a 10.4 percent decline from the same month the previous year.

Jae Kim, a leading accountant for the Korea desk at Atlanta-based Aprio, stated that entirely killing subsidies is unrealistic due to Republican lawmaker opposition, but subsidies will likely be slashed by some margin.
GlobalData cut its outlook for the U.S. EV market share in 2030 to 28 percent from 33 percent following Trump’s election victory.
BGR Group reported that potential rollback of tax credits and regulatory support has led to delays or cancellations of projects in hydrogen, electric vehicles, and wind power sectors.

The Savannah city government stopped treating the Georgia plant's wastewater in September due to violations of government parameters after finding the water could be harmful to the environment and humans.
Hyundai’s wastewater contained more than six times the acceptable limit of copper and more than twice the allowable zinc concentration.
The city halted treatment immediately after discovering Hyundai failed to continuously observe its wastewater conductivity.
Since September, Hyundai has been transporting millions of gallons of wastewater via tanker trucks to external treatment facilities in states like Florida and North Carolina.
Hyundai stated it will resolve the wastewater issue once Bryan County completes its $129 million North Bryan County Water Reclamation Facility.
Construction of the water reclamation facility is expected to be completed no earlier than the end of 2025.
The carmaker's factory began mass production on Wednesday, the same day as the official opening ceremony.
A Hyundai Motor spokesperson confirmed that after receiving notice of violations, the company immediately stopped discharging wastewater and began a root cause analysis to determine the source.
Hyundai contracted a specialized company to pump wastewater into tanker trucks and verified that all receiving treatment facilities are properly classified, permitted, and authorized.
source_title: Hyundai's $8B Georgia plant faces critical test in Trump's U.S.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America deployed 21 XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell electric trucks for logistics operations at its Bryan County, Ga., facility.
The deployment was announced on Wednesday.
Oscar Kwon serves as CEO of HMGMA.
HMGMA is committed to creating sustainable transportation solutions.
The Hyundai XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell trucks that support the Metaplant release zero emissions.
The trucks offer a cleaner alternative to traditional freight logistics methods used at other manufacturing facilities.
The trucks will transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the plant.
During the initial rollout, XCIENT trucks will transport parts between suppliers and the on-site Consolidation Center.
The truck operations are planned to expand later to a broader network.
Earlier this year, Hyundai Motor Group announced HTWO, a new hydrogen value chain business brand.
HTWO encompasses Hyundai's businesses and affiliates, enabling each stage of the entire hydrogen value chain from production to storage to transportation to utilization.
HTWO represents hydrogen and humanity, two main pillars of Hyundai's hydrogen business.
Jim Park serves as senior vice president of commercial vehicle and hydrogen business development for Hyundai Motor North America.
HMGMA's deployment of XCIENT trucks for logistics and operation is one of the main initiatives of HTWO.
The deployment delivers on Hyundai's strategy and vision to showcase a closed-loop hydrogen logistics ecosystem.
The initiative aims to improve the environmental sustainability of manufacturing operations.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group rolls out hydrogen trucks at Georgia plant | Trucks, Parts, Service

facts: Hyundai Motor Group confirmed it is going forward with previously announced plans to expand its Georgia plant.
The expansion comes just weeks after an immigration raid delayed the startup of an electric vehicle battery plant at the site.
Hyundai will spend $2.7 billion to increase production capacity at the Ellabell site by 200,000 over the next three years.
The total production capacity at the Ellabell site will reach 500,000 vehicles a year.
The company first announced the expansion in March at the grand opening of the plant west of Savannah.
Hyundai had said in August that it would invest an additional $5 billion in the United States overall.
The immigration raid included arrests of more than 300 South Korean citizens.
The raid led to questions about the wisdom of South Korea investing in the U.S.
Hyundai plans to produce 10 models of electric and hybrid gas-electric vehicles in Georgia, up from the current two the plant has been assembling as it ramps up production.
Hyundai is on track to expand production worldwide to 5.6 million vehicles a year by 2030.
The automaker pledged that 60% of those vehicles will be electric or hybrid powered.
The company is targeting sales in South Korea, North America and Europe.
Hyundai plans to make more than 80% of vehicles sold in the United States domestically by 2030.
Total domestic content will increase from 60% to 80%.
For the first time, the Georgia plant's expanded production will include a mid-sized pickup truck.
Hyundai already makes the Santa Cruz model, a four-door compact pickup, that it started selling in 2021.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz has said the immigration raid will delay opening the battery plant by at least two to three months.
Spokesperson Michael Stewart said Thursday that the facility will open in the first half of 2026.
Both Hyundai executives and Georgia officials have been trying to calm the situation since the raid.
The raid mushroomed into a diplomatic dispute between South Korea and the United States.

Republican Gov. Brian Kemp told reporters Tuesday at a ceremony marking the groundbreaking of a Rivian Automotive electric vehicle plant that he remains confident Georgia's business advantages would “win out” in terms of foreign investment.
Kemp said the raid may even wind up smoothing the way for South Korean employees to more easily obtain legal permission to help build and operate facilities in the United States.
Kemp said he has had good conversations with companies that are here doing business in Georgia, companies that are looking to do business here.
Kemp said he has had good conversations with people in the White House about the visa issue.
Brent Stubbs, the chief administrative officer of the Ellabell site, wrote in an opinion piece published Wednesday in The Atlanta Journal-Constitution that the company is still committed to Georgia.
Stubbs wrote that the situation doesn’t change their plans to continue expanding and localizing in the United States.
Stubbs wrote that their investments in America are part of a long-term strategic plan.
The $2.7 billion investment confirmed Thursday will go to expanding capacity at the plant and group affiliates.
That would bring total investment in the auto plant to $10.3 billion.
This total does not count the $4.3 billion that Hyundai and its joint-venture partner, LG Energy Solution, are putting into the battery plant.
Hyundai and its onsite affiliates currently have 3,129 employees in Ellabell.
Hyundai pledges to hire at least 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
State and local governments have promised $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives.
The boost at the Ellabell site is the biggest part of Hyundai's plan to raise production by 1.2 million vehicles a year worldwide.
That includes another 250,000 vehicles out of Pune, India.
That includes 200,000 more at Hyundai's electric vehicle plant in Ulsan, South Korea.
The automaker said it would also deliver parts for assembly into an additional 250,000 vehicles at plants in Saudi Arabia, Vietnam and North Africa.
Hyundai underlined a previous announcement to deepen its investment in robotics.
By 2027, Hyundai will launch extended-range electric vehicles with gasoline motors to extend the range of its electric batteries to more than 600 miles (960 kilometers).
source_title: Hyundai Says It Will Spend $2.7 Billion Expanding Part of the Georgia Complex Raided by ICE

facts: Hyundai Motor Co. unveiled an ambitious growth plan to accelerate its electrified vehicle offerings in North America.
The company's strategy hinges on rapid expansion in Georgia.
Hyundai detailed a $2.7 billion expansion to its electric vehicle factory near Savannah.
The factory only completed its first construction phase 11 months ago.
Hyundai plans to hire 3,000 more workers in Georgia.
Hyundai plans to increase production to 500,000 units annually at the Metaplant by 2028.
The Metaplant is a 16 million-square-foot factory.
The 3,000 new jobs are part of the 8,500 total jobs Hyundai previously said it will eventually employ at the campus.
The campus is located along I-16 in Bryan County.
By the end of the decade, Hyundai aims for 60% of all vehicle sales to have electrified powertrains, which includes fully electric vehicles and hybrids.
The Metaplant is slated to eventually assemble 10 different EV and hybrid models.
Thursday’s announcement comes on the heels of a Sept. 4 immigration raid at the campus’ battery factory, currently under construction.
The immigration raid triggered an international firestorm.
Hyundai has said it remains committed to the project despite the raid.
The raid has caused anger in Korea.
Construction of the site will temporarily pause amid discussions about visa issues.
source_title: Hyundai announces Georgia Metaplant to soon undergo $2.7B expansion

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has begun operations at its third U.S. plant in Georgia.
The Georgia facility broke ground two and a half years prior to operations.
The plant incorporates artificial intelligence, information systems, and robots to dramatically improve productivity.
The automation rate at the Georgia plant is 40%, which is four times higher than the average 10% rate at other Hyundai factories.
Hyundai has concentrated advanced systems and equipment in the Georgia facility, backed by a $8 billion investment.
The Georgia plant is Hyundai’s largest single-plant project to date.
More than 950 robots have been deployed across production lines at the facility.
The production lines are capable of producing around 100,000 vehicles.
The plant employs roughly 880 production workers, meaning robots outnumber human workers on the line.
Hyundai employs around 8,500 local workers at the Georgia facility.
Robots at the plant are primarily deployed for tasks that are physically demanding or difficult to standardize.
Human workers take on more advanced responsibilities, such as programming and managing digitalized production systems.
Hyundai and Kia are ramping up new plant construction and announcing major investments in the United States.
Labor unions in South Korea have voiced concerns over job security due to Hyundai's overseas expansion.
South Korean labor unions are demanding more domestic investment from Hyundai.
The only new Hyundai plant under construction in South Korea is an electric vehicle-dedicated facility in Ulsan.
The Ulsan EV plant is slated for completion next year.
The Ulsan plant will be Hyundai's first new plant since the Asan plant opened in 1996, nearly three decades ago.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to invest $8.5 billion to build a Hyundai Steel plant in the United States.
The Hyundai Steel union is on strike, demanding bonuses equivalent to those at Hyundai Motor.
Due to a slump in the steel industry, Hyundai Steel’s Incheon plant is set to suspend operations for a month in April.
The Hyundai Steel union has questioned whether the company has money to build a U.S. steel plant but not to pay out performance bonuses.
Corporate investments like the U.S. steel plant are funded through means such as corporate bonds or the sale of idle assets, with expectations of future revenue and profit.
Performance bonuses require immediate profitability, unlike long-term capital investments.
Union strikes and demands for higher pay are driving companies to shift investment overseas instead of staying in South Korea.
source_title: Editorial: As Hyundai looks abroad, unions at home push it away

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Project
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced an additional $2 billion investment from Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution for their battery cell manufacturing collaborative project at the Metaplant site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The additional investment brings the venture's total investment to $4.3 billion.
The project promises to generate 400 new employment opportunities in the region.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that within just a year, they inaugurated the grandest project the state has ever witnessed.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that numerous suppliers were attracted statewide for Hyundai’s Metaplant and LGES was ushered into Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is being positioned as the nucleus of e-mobility in the country.
Governor Brian Kemp remarked that massive investments transform into wages for Georgians, facilitating the improvement of educational institutions and infrastructure.
José Muñoz, who holds significant positions at Hyundai Motor Company, vocalized commitment to fostering a greener future facilitated by the American workforce.
José Muñoz acknowledged relentless support from Governor Kemp and Georgia communities, aiming to become pioneers in the electric mobility sector globally.
Earlier in May 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution formulated a memorandum of understanding.
The memorandum of understanding established LGES as Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s (HMGMA) integral partner for on-site battery cell manufacturing.
The present declaration symbolizes a monumental $7.59 billion venture.
The venture aims to create 8,500 job opportunities in the coming eight years.
Dong-Myung Kim leads the Advanced Automotive Battery Division at LG Energy Solution.
Dong-Myung Kim emphasized dedication towards accelerating America’s transition to electric vehicles and fortifying the local economy with substantial employment opportunities.
Dong-Myung Kim expressed enthusiasm to integrate premium products and established operational expertise within Georgia’s communities.
The facility will have a production capacity of 30 GWh.
The facility will substantiate manufacturing of 300,000 electric vehicle units annually at its peak.
Hyundai Mobis is slated to assemble battery packs utilizing cells manufactured at this plant.
The assembled battery packs will cater to Hyundai Motor Group’s American factories producing Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis electric vehicle models.

Carter Infinger, Chairman of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, anticipates the additional investment will revolutionize the Savannah region by generating 400 more lucrative job positions.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson commended the strategic efforts and collaboration of multiple state and community partners.
Pat Wilson emphasized Georgia’s sustained industry leadership and success in fostering job opportunities across the state.
Georgia remains steadfast in cultivating job prospects encompassing the entire EV supply chain as the market gains momentum.
Since 2018, Georgia has secured over $25 billion in investments and generated more than 30,000 jobs related to the EV supply chain.
source_title: Governor Kemp Announces Further $2B Investment from Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution in Georgia | Midsouth Mechanical Contractors

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is making a $7.6 billion investment in its Metaplant in Georgia.
The project is described as Georgia’s largest investment ever.
The Megaproject is creating thousands of American jobs.
Hyundai's arrival has transformed Georgia into a hub for advanced production, high-skilled employment, and supply-chain resilience.
The project is catalyzing workforce pipelines across the state.
The project is driving new supplier facilities in Georgia.
The project is building critical EV supply-chain infrastructure.
The investment reduces private-sector risk.
The investment ensures the benefits of global trade reach local communities.
Long-term foreign direct investment is essential to U.S. industrial strategy as Washington works to secure supply chains and expand advanced manufacturing.
The analysis explores how cities, counties, and states across the country are bringing Korean investment in to advance American competitiveness, boost jobs, and strengthen economic security.
source_title: Georgia’s Largest Investment Ever: Inside Hyundai’s $7.6B Megaproject - Korea Economic Institute of America

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to invest approximately $5.54 billion (£4.43 B / €5.25 B) into the state of Georgia.
The investment will be used to construct dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities on a 2,923 acre site in Bryan County.
The site is located less than 31 miles (50 km) from the Port of Savannah.
Hyundai noted the Port of Savannah is the “single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S.”
Construction on the plant is slated to begin early next year.
The plant should begin producing vehicles in the first half of 2025.
The plant will have an annual production capacity of 300,000 units.
The facility will build a “wide range of full electric vehicles for U.S. customers.”
Hyundai declined to say which specific vehicles will be produced at the new facility.
Previous reports suggested the facility could be tasked with making the Hyundai Ioniq 7 and Kia EV9.
Both models are large crossovers that will appeal to American consumers.
The vehicles are slated to ride on the E-GMP platform.
The vehicles will offer three rows of seating as well as ranges of at least 300 miles (483 km).
The battery plant will be established through a strategic partnership with details being released at a later date.
The project is expected to create approximately 8,100 new jobs.
The project is designed to support Hyundai Motor Group’s goal of “becoming a leader in electric mobility in the U.S.”
Hyundai Motor Group wants to become one of the top three EV providers in America by 2026.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated the company is incredibly proud to share its plan to open its first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.

Euisun Chung added that the U.S. has always held an important place in the Group’s global strategy, and expressed excitement to partner with the State of Georgia to achieve shared goals of electrified mobility and sustainability in the U.S.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group To Build New EV And Battery Plant In Georgia Creating 8,100 Jobs | Carscoops

facts: José Muñoz serves as Hyundai Motor president.
Hyundai Motor emphasized investment plans for the U.S. state of Georgia, where about 300 Koreans were detained.
In an interview published on the 15th (local time) with the Atlanta Business Chronicle, Muñoz said he would invest $26 billion (about 38 trillion won) in the United States over four years.
Muñoz stated that the investment policy remains unchanged.
Hyundai will maximize the productivity of the Georgia plant through robotic automation and artificial intelligence (AI).
Hyundai will localize automobile production and the logistics supply chain to reduce expense and increase competitiveness.
Hyundai will continue hiring until it reaches maximum production.
Hyundai will create 40,000 direct and indirect jobs in Georgia.
On tariffs, Muñoz stated that localization is a simple solution.
Hyundai has discussed this localization solution for some time and is implementing it now.
In September, U.S. immigration authorities arrested about 300 Korean workers at the construction site of the Hyundai Motor Group–LG Energy Solution battery joint-venture plant in Georgia.
The detained workers were released following South Korea–U.S. diplomatic talks.
Local media reported that factors such as insufficient local hiring may have provided grounds for the crackdown.
source_title: Muñoz says Hyundai will create 40,000 jobs in Georgia - CHOSUNBIZ

facts: LG Energy Solution (LGES) is a South Korean battery maker that supplies Tesla, General Motors, and Hyundai Motor.
LGES has a conservative view of revenue growth for 2025.
LGES will significantly reduce capital expenditure in 2025 due to slowing electric vehicle demand.
LGES booked a 39% drop in third-quarter profit.
CFO Lee Chang-Sil expects the result of the upcoming U.S. presidential election to have a significant impact on EV market direction.

Lee Chang-Sil cited continuing macro uncertainty, geopolitical risk, increased battery exports by Chinese rivals, and automaker customer plans to manufacture their own batteries as factors that would intensify competition.
LGES expects capital expenditure to be significantly reduced in 2025 compared to 2024, with the exception of some essential and necessary investments.
In April, LGES announced plans to reduce capital expenditure for 2024 due to slowing growth in EVs.
Earlier this year, LGES stated that its 2024 capital expenditure would be similar to the previous year's 10.9 trillion won.

Several automakers are scaling down electrification targets due to slowing EV demand caused by a lack of affordable models, slow proliferation of charging points, trade tension, and increased competition from cheaper Chinese rivals.

A senior LGES executive told Reuters in July that EV demand will likely recover in about 18 months in Europe and two to three years in the United States, depending on climate policies and other regulation.
Analyst Kang Dong-jin at Hyundai Motor Securities stated that the pace of EV demand growth could be slower if Donald Trump is elected to a second term due to his suggestion to cut EV tax credits.
LGES reported an operating profit of 448 billion won ($322.84 million) for July-September, which was in line with its earlier forecast but down from 731 billion won in the same period a year earlier.
Improved demand from some European and North American automakers helped LGES beat the LSEG SmartEstimate of 374 billion won for the quarter.
LGES stated it would have booked an 18 billion won operating loss in the quarter without a tax credit received under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.
LGES revenue fell 16% to 6.9 trillion won.
LGES share price rose 1.2% after the results, outpacing a 0.9% rise in the benchmark KOSPI.
source_title: Battery maker LGES offers measured 2025 outlook after slow EV demand drags down Q3 profit - AOL

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is scheduled to produce 500,000 units by 2028.
The plant expansion involves a $2.7 billion investment and will create 3,000 more jobs.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz said the company wants to produce more than 80% of its cars sold in the U.S. domestically by 2030.
Several automakers are following similar steps after President Donald Trump announced steep auto tariffs to encourage domestic production.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp congratulated Hyundai on the project and wrote, “We look forward to working with them on their continued growth and success for years to come!”
On Sept. 5, ICE conducted a raid on the southeast Georgia manufacturing site, detaining about 475 people.
More than 300 South Korean workers were flown back to their home country in a charter plane last week.
Homeland Security Investigations officials said the raid resulted from a months-long investigation into accusations of illegal hiring at the site.
According to the workers’ lawyers, the detained workers were in the country to help install parts of the factory that American workers don’t have the training to do.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has called for a rehaul of the U.S. visa system following the incident.
source_title: Hyundai plans $2.7B expansion of Ga. plant hit by immigration raid

facts: Hyundai Motor Group officially completed its acquisition of robotics company Boston Dynamics on Tuesday.
The deal was announced in December and valued Boston Dynamics at $1.1 billion.
The purchase gives Hyundai an 80 percent controlling stake in the company.
Previous owner Softbank retains a 20 percent ownership stake.
Boston Dynamics was spun off from MIT in 1992.
The company survived for most of its history on DARPA research grants.
Google acquired Boston Dynamics in 2013 as part of an Android co-founder Andy Rubin-led robotics initiative.
Google freed the company from surviving on military contracts.
Executive interest in robotics left Google when Andy Rubin departed a year after the acquisition.
Google sold Boston Dynamics to Softbank in 2017 for a reported $165 million.
By December 2020, Softbank faced financial difficulties after investments like Uber, WeWork, and OneWeb failed.
Softbank began selling off major assets like Arm during its financial struggles.
Hyundai wanted to invest in more "mobility solutions."
Softbank was able to sell Boston Dynamics at a profit.
Prior to the acquisition, Boston Dynamics had conducted only pure research.
Boston Dynamics began selling its first product, the $74,500 robotic dog "Spot," one year prior to the article's publication.
Reducing costs is essential for Boston Dynamics to achieve profitability through robot sales.
Few companies specialize in the mass production of robotics parts and components.
A car manufacturer like Hyundai is considered a good fit for scaling robotics manufacturing.
source_title: Boston Dynamics is officially part of Hyundai Motor Group - Ars Technica

facts: United States immigration authorities arrested 475 people in a raid on a Hyundai manufacturing site in Georgia.
Steve Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations Atlanta, announced the arrests at a press conference on Friday.
The Hyundai facility is located in Ellabell, Georgia, approximately 30 miles west of Savannah.
The raid was conducted as part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.
The raid was authorized by a judge-issued search warrant following a monthslong investigation of the facility.
The investigation of the worksite remains active, but no charges of wrongdoing have been filed.
The arrested individuals worked for several contractors as well as the parent company, Hyundai.
Steve Schrank stated that this was the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of Homeland Security Investigations.
Hyundai released a statement saying it was aware of the incident and closely monitoring the situation while working to understand the specific circumstances.
Hyundai stated that none of those detained were directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
South Korea's foreign ministry conveyed concerns and regret to the United States over the raid on a Hyundai-LG battery plant.
A spokesperson for South Korea's foreign ministry urged special attention to ensure that the legitimate rights and interests of Korean citizens are not violated.
The spokesperson emphasized that the economic activities of Korean companies investing in the U.S. and the rights and interests of their nationals must not be unfairly violated.
Consular officials were sent to the plant, and local diplomatic missions were instructed to set up a task force to address issues related to the raid.
A DHS spokesperson stated that the investigation is focused on ensuring accountability for those who violate the law and upholding the rule of law.
source_title: 475 people taken into ICE custody at Hyundai plant in Georgia - ABC News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group set out to build a nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant in Ellabell, Georgia.
The facility is designed for producing electric and hybrid vehicles.
Moses Grass Company served as the primary erosion control contractor for the project.
Moses Grass Company has over 75 years of expertise supporting infrastructure growth throughout Georgia.
Construction crews implemented comprehensive erosion control strategies throughout the site.
Crews installed miles of silt fencing to protect nearby waterways and wetlands.
Crews carried out wetland mitigation activities on the site.
Hydroseeding was utilized to rapidly stabilize disturbed areas.
Agricultural drones were introduced to plant temporary grass efficiently in challenging locations like materials stockpiles.
The erosion control efforts ensured continuous environmental protection and regulatory compliance.
The project is described as enhancing infrastructure, protecting the environment, and strengthening local economies across Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Metaplant America - Ellabell, GA

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced it has entered into an agreement with the State of Georgia to build its first dedicated full electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.

Governor Brian Kemp stated the future of transportation is in the Peach State, calling it the largest project in the state’s history delivering high-quality jobs on the leading edge of mobility to hardworking Georgians.
Governor Brian Kemp noted they are welcoming Hyundai to Georgia’s coastal region within a year of Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite.
The Group’s U.S. investment decision supports its goal of becoming a leader in electric mobility in the U.S. market.
The decision highlights the Group’s commitment to sustainability through electrification and supporting the economies where it operates.

The official signing ceremony was held in Bryan County, attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, other Georgia officials, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jaehoon Chang, and Hyundai Motor President and Chief Operating Officer José Muñoz.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung joined the signing ceremony virtually.

Executive Chair Euisun Chung expressed pride in sharing plans to open their first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S., noting the U.S.'s importance to global strategy and excitement to partner with Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability.
The new EV plant and battery manufacturing facilities represent an investment of approximately USD 5.54 billion.
The new facility will break ground in early 2023.
Commercial production is expected to begin in the first half of 2025 with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
The battery manufacturing facility will be established through a strategic partnership, with details to be disclosed at a later stage.
The Group plans to produce a wide range of full electric vehicles for U.S. customers at the new Georgia EV plant.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Local EV production aims to increase U.S. consumer accessibility to the Group’s innovative EVs.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain and build a healthy EV ecosystem in the U.S.
The EV and battery manufacturing plant will be located on a dedicated 2,923-acre site in Bryan County Georgia.
The site has immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways which creates easy access to 250 major metro areas.
The site is less than 50 kilometers from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S.
Two Class I rail facilities on-site are provided by Georgia Central Railway.
West Point, Georgia is already home to the Group’s Kia manufacturing hub in the US.

Hyundai Motor Group selected Georgia as the EV and battery production site due to favorable business conditions including speed-to-market, talented workforce, and existing network of affiliates and suppliers.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registration per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia constantly develops access to publicly available EV charging stations and already offers more outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.
The Group’s new investment will help accelerate the state’s sustainable shift to electrification.
The project is expected to create about 8,100 new jobs.

The EV plant investment comes as part of the Group’s 2021 announcement to invest USD 7.4 billion by 2025 to foster future mobility in the U.S., including production of EVs and offering smart mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group will be a leader in the U.S. auto market’s EV transition with the new facility.
The Group is accelerating its electrification efforts with a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network that will ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.
Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor, stated they decided to build their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S. because America embraces change and drives innovation.
Chang noted the new EV plant is the future of their business and will help meet growing demands of US customers who want leading edge design, safe, zero-emissions vehicles now and in the future.
With additional EV and battery production capabilities in the U.S., the Group aims to become one of the top three EV providers in the U.S. by 2026.
The Group plans to lead the EV market not just in sales, but also in terms of design, technologies, and mobility solutions optimized for electric mobility era.
The investment is in line with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification as global OEMs announce plans to expand their local EV production in the U.S.
Last year, the U.S. government announced a new initiative under which sales of zero-emission vehicles should account for at least half of OEM’s new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
The Group plans to implement many of its advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center in Singapore (HMGICS).

The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where all production processes including order collection, procurement, logistics and production will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
As part of the Group’s commitment to sustainability, the plant will mainly rely on renewable energy sources to power the facility and use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
source_title: Atlanta Trend - Connecting People. Processing News

facts: Humans won’t be the only ones building cars at Hyundai Motor Group’s massive electric vehicle factory in Georgia.
Thousands of humanoid robots are slated in a few years to join their human counterparts working on the assembly lines.

Korean automaker Hyundai and its subsidiary, Boston Dynamics, unveiled a sweeping artificial intelligence robotics strategy at the CES trade show Monday in Las Vegas, a presentation that featured doglike robots dancing to K-pop songs.
The pageantry was to highlight a new Atlas robot, a humanoid AI-powered machine designed to help with repetitive tasks in Hyundai’s factories and warehouses.
Hyundai has not announced where the Atlas robots will be manufactured.
Boston Dynamics CEO Jacky Kim stated that a new generation of robots in the near future will be more intelligent than humans.
The announcement did not include where the robots will be built.
Hyundai officials said jobs are critical to the state’s economic recovery efforts and the plant's success.
Hyundai plans to expand the plant's production capacity to reach 500,000 vehicles by 2028.

facts: The facility is Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC located in Bryan County.
The facility discharged industrial wastewater to a publicly owned treatment works without an industrial pretreatment permit for such discharge.
The facility utilized a sewage holding tank at the Facility for purposes other than construction trailers at a construction site.

The order requires submission of all required documents to EPD Industrial Permitting Program for final action by the Director regarding an industrial pretreatment permit for discharge to the North Bryan WRF.
The order requires compliance with requirements for pumping and hauling Non-Process Wastewater.
The order requires submission of a revised Corrective Action Plan for review and approval.

facts: South Korean officials negotiated the release of more than 300 South Koreans detained during a US immigration raid at a Hyundai EV plant in Georgia.
The workplace raid was conducted as part of the Trump administration’s mass deportation agenda.
Kang Hoon-sik, chief of staff for President Lee Jae Myung, confirmed that South Korea and the US finalized negotiations on the workers’ release.
South Korea plans to send a charter plane to bring the detained workers home as soon as remaining administrative steps are completed.
Foreign Minister Cho Hyun is scheduled to leave for the US on Monday for talks related to the workers’ releases.
US immigration authorities detained 475 people, mostly South Korean nationals, during a raid on Hyundai’s manufacturing site in Georgia on Friday.
The targeted plant is still under construction.
Hyundai has partnered with LG Energy Solution at this site to produce batteries that power EVs.
Foreign Minister Cho stated that more than 300 of the detained individuals were South Korean nationals.
State officials have long referred to the targeted site as Georgia’s largest economic development project.
South Korea agreed in July to purchase $100 billion in U.S. energy and make a $350 billion investment in the US in return for the U.S lowering tariff rates.
US President Donald Trump and President Lee Jae Myung held their first meeting in Washington about two weeks prior to the raid.
President Lee stated that the rights of South Korean nationals and economic activities of South Korean companies must not be unfairly infringed upon during US law enforcement procedures.
South Korea’s Foreign Ministry issued a statement expressing “concern and regret” over the case and sent diplomats to the site.
ICE released video showing federal agents directing workers to line up outside, frisking them, and shackling their hands, ankles, and waists.
Most detained individuals were taken to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
Steven Schrank, lead Georgia agent of Homeland Security Investigations, stated that none of the detained have been charged with crimes yet and the investigation is ongoing.
Some detained workers had illegally crossed the US border, while others entered legally but had expired visas or entered on visa waivers that prohibited them from working.
Kang Hoon-sik said South Korea will push to review and improve visa systems for those traveling to the US on business trips for investment projects.
source_title: Seoul secures return of 300 South Korean workers detained at US Hyundai plant - France 24

facts: Georgia Tech has entered into a multiyear partnership with Hyundai Motor Group to advance hydrogen mobility solutions on campus.
The agreement expands research, education, and real-world application of zero-emissions transportation.
The partnership builds on a century-long relationship between two Atlanta mainstays.

facts: In May 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LGES signed a memorandum of understanding establishing LGES as the partner for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s (HMGMA) onsite battery cell manufacturing JV.
The additional investment announced on Aug. 31, 2023 is $2 billion.
The EV manufacturing facility and the battery JV represent an estimated $7.59 billion in total investment.
The project will create 8,500 new jobs for the region over the next eight years.

Dong-Myung Kim, president and head of the Advanced Automotive Battery Division of LG Energy Solution, stated the investment underscores dedication to driving America’s EV transition while bolstering the local economy through the creation of quality jobs.
Dong-Myung Kim noted thanks to support from the State of Georgia, they are excited to bring top-quality products and storied operational experience to grow with local communities.
The 30 GWh facility will be able to support the production of 300,000 units of EVs annually at full operations.

Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant, then supply them to the Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.

Carter Infinger, Chairman of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, stated the announcement will bring 400 additional well-paying jobs for those in the Savannah region.
Carter Infinger noted the continued investment by Hyundai Motor Group and announced suppliers like LG Energy Solution is truly remarkable and will be transformative for the region.

Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, Georgia Power, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Governor Kemp set a goal of Georgia becoming the top location for EV manufacturing in the United States, and the state is in a great position to claim that distinction with this announcement.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated through focus on building an ecosystem supporting the entire EV supply chain, Georgia continues adding to industry-leading success and bringing future jobs to Georgians in every corner of the state.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that state and community partners like the Savannah-area JDA, utility providers, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start create a collaborative, business-friendly environment that sets Georgia up for success.
Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire supply chain, resulting in more than $25 billion in investments and the creation of over 30,000 jobs since 2018.
Battery related projects have accounted for at least $11 billion in investment in Georgia.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.

facts: South Korea’s top automaker Hyundai Motor Group has been granted $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives from the state of Georgia and local governments for building its first U.S plant fully dedicated to electric vehicles in the southeastern state, the largest subsidy package for a vehicle plant in Georgia.
The state of Georgia announced Friday, local time, that it has decided to offer $1.8 billion in incentives for Hyundai Motor’s EV project.
The amount is larger than the $1.5 billion incentive package promised to electric carmaker Rivian Automotive Inc. last year.
Under the signed agreement, Georgia will give Hyundai Motor $472 million in property tax breaks over a 26-year period starting in 2023.
Georgia will reward $212 million in state income tax credits over five years for creating jobs.

Hyundai Motor unveiled plans in May to build an EV and battery manufacturing facility at a cost of around $5.54 billion near Savannah in Georgia by 2025 with annual capacity of 300,000 units and create 8,100 new jobs.
Construction for the new facility will begin next year with mass production expected to start in 2025.
Georgia kept corporate tax rate at 6 percent for more than 30 years and further cut it to 5.75 percent in 2019 to draw investment.
Companies are provided with prior information of sites for industrial locations to help decision-making.
The state of Georgia offers information related to environment, cultural properties, and endangered species linked to the sites.
The benefits went to companies like Adidas, Kumho Tire, and Starbucks.
The state of Georgia also offers a “Quick Start” program that provides workforce training for companies creating jobs.
About 3,000 U.S. and foreign companies have nestled in the region, including Korea’s Kia Corp., SK on.
More than 100 Korean automotive-related companies are in business in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Motor granted $1.8 bn in tax incentives for $5.54 bn EV project in Georgia - 매일경제 영문뉴스 펄스(Pulse)

facts: Georgia offers $1.8 billion in incentives for an 8,100-job Hyundai plant coming to Bryan County.

The state of Georgia and local governments are giving $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai in exchange for the automaker building its first U.S. plant dedicated to electric vehicles.
The deal calls for Hyundai to invest $5.5 billion in its Georgia plant and hire 8,100 workers.
It is the largest economic development deal in the state’s history.
The deal comes just months after Georgia closed another major deal with electric vehicle maker Rivian to build a factory in the state.

Pat Wilson, the state’s economic development commissioner, said the generational projects solidify Georgia's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition and ensure thousands of Georgians will benefit from future jobs.
Hyundai executives and Gov. Brian Kemp announced the deal in May with a champagne toast at the project’s 2,900 acre (1,170 hectare) site in Bryan County, west of Savannah.
Hyundai plans to start construction on the plant next year.
Hyundai plans to begin producing up to 300,000 vehicles per year in 2025.
The new factory will also produce vehicle batteries.
Officials declined to reveal what incentives the automaker had been promised until after the agreement was signed.
The package disclosed Friday is worth roughly $300 million more than incentives promised to Rivian.
The deal amounts to Georgia and four counties in the Savannah area giving Hyundai about $228,000 per job created.
Wilson said Hyundai’s payroll at the new plant is expected to reach $4.7 billion over 10 years.
Parts suppliers are expected to create thousands of additional jobs in the state.
The $1.8 billion in incentives is easily the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised for an automotive plant, according to Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First.
Greg LeRoy called the deal "inherently super-risky" because it bets a huge amount on one company and one facility.
Local governments are giving Hyundai more than $472 million in property tax breaks.
Hyundai will pay more than $357 million in lieu of taxes over a 26-year period starting in 2023.
The company will also receive more than $212 million in state income tax credits, at $5,250 per job over five years.
If Hyundai didn’t owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state and local governments spent $86 million to purchase the plant site.
The state will spend $200 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend $50 million more to help fund construction, machinery and equipment.
Sales tax exemptions on construction materials and machinery expenses are estimated to cost $396 million.
Georgia officials say the deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Kia, another subsidiary of the Hyundai Motor Group, got more than $450 million in incentives for its plant in West Point, southwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has promised SK Innovation $300 million in incentives for a $2.6 billion, 2,600-worker battery plant that the Korean company is building northeast of Atlanta.
source_title: Georgia leaders offer $1.8 billion in incentives for Hyundai jobs - WJCL

facts: Georgia state and local leaders officially welcomed South-Korean automaker Hyundai Motor Group to Bryan County on Friday.
The company will construct a $5.5 billion manufacturing facility.
The facility will be located on the nearly 2,300 acre Bryan County mega site, which sits adjacent to Interstate 16 near the U.S. 280 interchange.
The site will employ 8,100 workers between an electric vehicle assembly plant and an adjacent EV battery factory.
The project makes it the largest single economic development project in Georgia history.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp said the announcement marked a historic day in the state's ongoing success story.
Gov. Kemp cited Georgia's 3.1% unemployment rate, record breaking number of Georgians working, and record breaking trade and commerce numbers as metrics of the state's success.
Gov. Kemp stated, "And today, we are doing it yet again."
In 2006, former Georgia Gov. Sonny Perdue inked a deal with Hyundai officials for a new Kia assembly plant in West Point, which helped lay the groundwork for this week's announcement.
Gov. Kemp said, "It is clear companies recognize that in Georgia, we do not rest on our laurels, and we will always continue to work towards our future."

Gov. Kemp stated, "And after today, no one can doubt that Georgia is not only leading the great recovery as a whole, but we're also the unrivaled leader in the nation's emerging electric mobility industry."
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung said Friday was a momentous occasion in the history and the future of Hyundai Motor Group.
Chung announced the company's plan to open its first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facility in the U.S.
Chung added that the U.S. has always held an important place in the group's global strategy.

Chung stated, "We are proud to partner with the state of Georgia to achieve a shared goal towards electrified mobility and sustainability. We are looking forward to continuing our success story here. Let us enjoy this electrifying ride together."
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jaehoon "Jay" Chang told the crowd that the manufacturing facility represents a critical next step in the evolution of the Ho Motor Group.
Chang said, "When he decided to build our first fully dedicated EV plant outside of Korea, we chose the United States because America embraces changes and drives innovation."
As temperatures hovered around 90 degrees, Hyundai Motor President and COO José Muñoz proposed a toast to the new development.
During the event, attendees chanted "Hyundai! Hyundai! Hyundai!" in response to a question from Muñoz about the best global auto group in the world.
State Sen. Ben Watson, who represents Bryan County, said the impact of the development stretched far beyond county lines.
Sen. Watson noted that the project is a joint effort with Bulloch County, Effingham County, Chatham County, and especially Bryan County.
Sen. Watson said he heard from several South Korean officials that they chose the area because it was "such a well oiled machine and a great package that they had put together."
Savannah native U.S. Senator Raphael Warnock shared on Twitter that the deal would "help bring more clean jobs to our state."
U.S. Congressman Buddy Carter said the thousands of jobs created by the facility is a huge win not only for area residents, but Hyundai, too.
Rep. Carter stated, "The people who work here, they are going to just love this company. It's a great company. And this company is going to love the people of this area."

Rep. Carter added, "They have made a very wise decision, they will find that the people here are dedicated hard working people, salt of the earth people. So it's just a win-win situation for everyone."
U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff issued a statement crediting months of behind-the-scenes work in getting the deal done for Georgians.
Friday's announcement will help build an already strong partnership with the Port of Savannah.

Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch noted that Kia, located up in west central Georgia, has been an amazing partner for many years and just celebrated 10 years in Georgia and a million cars.

Lynch stated, "to have the Hyundai Group invest again, in Georgia speaks volumes about the relationship they have with the state, the ports and everyone else and so we're just so excited to continue to grow that partnership."
source_title: Hyundai! Hyundai! Hyundai! Savannah, Chatham leaders celebrate new auto plant in Bryan County

facts: Hyundai Motor Group officially broke ground Tuesday on its planned $5.5 billion electric vehicle factory in Bryan County.
The project is poised to become the largest economic development project in Georgia history.
It will be the South Korean automaker's first fully dedicated facility for EV manufacturing.

Hyundai global president and chief operating officer José Muñoz stated, “We heard the clarion call of this administration to hasten the adoption of new electric vehicles and reduce carbon emissions — so, we took action.”
The factory is dubbed by Hyundai as the “Metaplant.”
The company says it will create more than 8,100 jobs in Bryan County.
The site is located just west of Savannah.
Dozens of business leaders and government officials attended the groundbreaking ceremony.
U.S. Sen. Raphael Warnock and Gov. Brian Kemp attended the event.
Gov. Brian Kemp remarked, “This project is just incredibly monumental in our state,” citing low unemployment and record job creation and investment in Georgia.
Warnock was not included in the list of speakers at the ceremony.
South Korea's ambassador to the U.S., Taeyong Cho, spoke at the event.
Ambassador Cho noted that when President Biden visited South Korea in May, Hyundai Chairman Chong promised to build electric vehicle and battery plants in the United States.
Cho stated, “Today, Hyundai is delivering on that commitment.”
Cho expressed support for President Biden's efforts to address climate change through the Inflation Reduction Act.
Cho criticized a provision of the Inflation Reduction Act that limits electric vehicle consumer tax credits to only EV models made in North America.
Cho warned that Korean companies are at risk of being disadvantaged by this tax credit provision.
Cho stated, “I believe it is not good for [the] Korea-U.S. partnership, for the state of Georgia, and not even for the common cause of climate change by limiting the consumer choices.”
Cho said South Korea and the U.S. are working very hard to find a workable solution regarding the tax credits.
The two governments will exert maximum efforts in the next few months to address the issue.
Hyundai plans to manufacture about 300,000 vehicles per year at its Metaplant in Bryan County.
The automaker does not currently create any EVs in North America.
Warnock voted for the Inflation Reduction Act in August.
Warnock has since introduced legislation that would effectively extend the tax credits to Hyundai.
source_title: Hyundai breaks ground on $5.5 billion, 8,100-job factory in Bryan County | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Hyundai Transys is implementing its in-house-developed AI system, 'Transys Advanced Data Analytics' (TADA), at its production sites to boost quality competitiveness.
TADA essentially is a digital platform driving innovation across Hyundai Transys’ business operations.

TADA encompasses developing AI systems to implement smart manufacturing technology, a big data platform to support data-driven decision-making, and training programs to enhance employee’s digital transformation capabilities.

The TADA Smart Solution and TADA Edge Solution are self-developed programs that utilise AI deep learning technology to enhance production efficiency and defect inspection accuracy on the manufacturing floor.
TADA Smart Solution is a vision inspection system where deep learning AI detects defects during the scanning process, identifying small bubbles and damage in parts during manufacturing and assembly.
The TADA Smart Solution, trained with high-quality component X-ray images, has improved inspection accuracy from 93% to 99.9%, even detecting defects that human inspectors might miss.

As of July 2024, Hyundai Transys has implemented the TADA Smart Solution in seven factories in Seosan, including Jigok and Seongyeon, as well as in 54 processes at the Georgia Powertrain Plant in the United States.
The TADA Edge Solution is a small AI inspection system developed to easily create and apply customized deep learning models on-site, even by nonexperts.

Typically, at the production site, production process images such as the mounting status and quantity check of the parts to inspect are collected through a small camera and then work proceeds with the labelling work.

The TADA Edge Solution will conduct defect inspection after learning and is used to check the number of bolts assembled, mounting status, and joint lifting inspection at four Hyundai Transys Seosan Plant locations.
The TADA Edge Solution can be installed at one-tenth the cost of traditional AI deep learning inspection equipment (vision systems).

The TADA Edge Solution does not require network construction work or specialized maintenance technicians, allowing small and medium-sized partners to enhance production efficiency and reduce defect rates effortlessly.
The TADA Edge Solution is currently installed and operational at the partner company responsible for manufacturing sheet parts.

Hyundai Transys has installed AI-based responsive CCTV in four areas of the Jigok Plant, where logistics vehicles and forklifts frequently pass through to prevent accidents by alerting workers when vehicles approach.
Around 30 responsive CCTVs detect equipment damage and immediately send alerts and messages to facility managers in case of oil or wastewater leaks, enabling swift action.
In the R&D field, TADA AI has been utilised to improve work efficiency within Hyundai Transys’ Electrification R&D Division.
The Electrification R&D Division at Hyundai Transys has incorporated AI technology into the durability testing process for powertrain gear development.

A deep learning AI measurement machine can now analyze the damage range and complete the report within an hour, whereas this task previously took researchers up to 24 hours to determine the damaged areas in gear durability tests.
The collected test data from the AI measurement machine can assist in root cause analysis and the design process.
source_title: Hyundai Transys targets zero production defects with AI tech | Autocar Professional

facts: HYUNDAI TRANSYS ranked first in the Small/Compact Car Seats Category for Mass Market Car Brands in the “2024 J.D. Power Seat Quality and Satisfaction Study”.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS has been consistently ranked among the top 3 in Passenger Car and SUV Seat Satisfaction in the United States for five consecutive years.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS is the sole Korean company to achieve five consecutive years of top-3 rankings in US seat satisfaction surveys.
The company clinched the top spot in the Small/Compact Car Seat category for Mass-Market Brands in August 2024 specifically for seats in the Kia Forte.
This marks the first time HYUNDAI TRANSYS has claimed the top spot in the seat quality and satisfaction survey.
The 2024 US Seat Quality and Satisfaction Survey measures complaints and satisfaction per 100 vehicles, targeting US purchasers and lessees who have owned a 2024 model for at least 90 days.
The comprehensive survey was conducted from July 2023 to May 2024, encompassing 99,144 purchasers and lessees.
A lower score in the survey indicates fewer complaints and reflects high satisfaction with quality.
Since its integrated launch in 2019, HYUNDAI TRANSYS has consistently ranked among the top three in seat quality satisfaction surveys from 2020 to 2024.
In 2020, Hyundai ACCENT and Kia Rio secured second and third positions, respectively, in the Small/Compact Car category for Mass-Market Brands.
In 2021, Genesis G90 seats clinched third place in the Premium Car category.

In 2022, HYUNDAI TRANSYS achieved second place for the Small/Compact Car category (Kia Forte), second place in the Small/Compact SUV category (Kia Soul), and third place for the Midsize/Large SUV category (Kia Sorento).
In 2023, HYUNDAI TRANSYS secured second place with the Kia Rio in the Small/Compact Car category and the Kia Sorento in the Midsize/Large SUV category under Mass-Market segments.

Seo Seung-woo, Executive Director of the Seat Division at HYUNDAI TRANSYS, stated that for the past 20 years, the company has prioritized passenger safety and comfort while incorporating advanced technology and differentiated design.

Seo Seung-woo added that the company is committed to establishing itself as a top-tier seat manufacturer delivering high-quality products to domestic and global customers, including those in North America.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS operates 33 global facilities across 11 countries to meet demands of global automakers and ensure continuous revenue growth.
The company conducts research, development, and production of power trains and seats in various locations across Georgia, Illinois, and Alabama in the United States, as well as in Monterrey, Mexico.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS’s Savannah facility is located at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Savannah, Georgia.
HMGMA is an electric vehicle–dedicated factory of Hyundai Motor Group.
The Savannah facility is set to manufacture seats for electric vehicles, supporting Hyundai Motor Group’s global electrification efforts.
source_title: News | HYUNDAI TRANSYS

facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis will invest $926 million in a new Electric Vehicle Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The new plant is expected to hire at least 1,500 people.

Governor Kemp stated that the groundbreaking of Hyundai's new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County was expected to unleash transformational job creation and investment in the region.
This announcement marks the second supplier to locate in the area within just two weeks.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing factory in West Point since 2009 with almost 1,200 employees.
The West Point factory is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies car manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies as part of the Hyundai Mobis family.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated that the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia's auto industry.
H.S. Oh noted that Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market and will trigger more growth within the sector.
The new facility will be a 1,200,000-square-foot building.
The plant is expected to produce over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually.
The plant is expected to produce 450,000 Integrated Charging Control units annually.
Production will be supplied to several factories in Georgia and one factory in Alabama.
Construction is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
The site for the new plant is the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Construction officials predict production will begin sometime in 2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia's growing EV ecosystem.
In May, GDEcD predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia in connection with the project.
That $1 billion investment prediction was blown past in about two weeks.
The project involves support from partners including Georgia Quick Start, sister agencies, Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and the Georgia Ports Authority.
source_title: New Hyundai Metaplant to bring 1,500 jobs to Bryan Co., Gov. Kemp says – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta
