facts: Federal agents carried out a large-scale operation at the Hyundai electric-vehicle Metaplant on September 4, 2025.
The raid led to the detention of approximately 475 workers.
The detained workers included nationals from South Korea, Mexico, Japan, China, Indonesia, and several Latin American countries.
The facility is located in Ellabell, Georgia, outside Savannah.
Witnesses reported that workers hid for hours as federal teams moved through the site.
Federal agents confiscated cellphones and grouped workers into lines for questioning.
Many households lost income overnight due to the detention of wage earners.
Some parents kept children home from activities to avoid potential police checkpoints near schools or workplaces.
Most detained South Korean nationals were quickly sent home, according to community leaders.
Many non-Korean workers remain in legal limbo, either still detained, recently released with reporting requirements, or hard to reach.
Immigration attorneys noted that several detained workers held valid visas or U.S. work permits.
Nonprofits including the Grow Initiative and Migrant Equity Southeast have been flooded with calls for legal aid, rent support, and mental health services.
Community advocates report a chilling effect within Asian American networks and other immigrant communities across Georgia.
People with no direct link to the raid are scaling back daily activities due to fear of future workplace sweeps.
Small business owners worry about losing customers as people limit outings.
Workers with Temporary Protected Status or pending employment authorization documents are second-guessing commutes and carpool arrangements.
Civil rights groups and immigration lawyers are gathering accounts raising due process concerns, including reports of rushed screenings and inadequate language access.
Advocates argue that similar past sweeps mixed workers with viable legal claims alongside those with clear violations, causing broad harm without improving compliance.
The raid will delay the battery facility’s opening by at least two to three months, according to local officials and people familiar with plant operations.
The delay disrupts supply timelines and hiring plans for the plant itself and nearby suppliers.
Local restaurants and landlords are affected because they expected steady paychecks circulating through the community.
Business groups warn that fear among immigrant communities can slow consumer spending and reduce labor supply during existing staffing gaps.
Protests have followed in several cities, with participants criticizing the militarized approach and treatment of workers holding valid work permits.
Some foreign investors are questioning whether large U.S. projects could face similar interruptions.
Economic development officials are concerned about reputational damage to Georgia, where international companies have invested heavily in EVs and advanced manufacturing.
State officials are working to reassure partners that day-to-day operations can proceed safely and employee rights will be respected.
The operation has stirred diplomatic tension with South Korea, whose officials are reviewing possible human rights issues linked to the raid.
The Biden administration defends the action as part of enforcing federal law.
Trade watchers warn such disputes can complicate broader talks on supply chains and clean energy cooperation between the U.S. and South Korea regarding battery and EV investment.
Long-term questions include whether high-profile enforcement deters future investment and whether companies will demand stronger assurances on worker screenings and legal support.

Legal experts argue for smarter future planning, including clear language access protocols, rapid verification for lawful status workers, coordination with social service groups to prevent family separation, and consistent federal communication.
Attorneys expect more bond hearings and case reviews for those still in custody in the coming weeks.
Community groups are preparing for longer recovery efforts including rent relief and trauma counseling.
Business leaders are measuring what the delay means for hiring and production targets.

links.context: Official guidance on worksite enforcement policy and employer obligations

source_title: Opinion: Georgia Immigrants Live in Fear After Hyundai Metaplant Raid

canonical_name: United States Electric Vehicle Battery Supply Chain
facts: President-elect Donald Trump is known for attacking electric vehicle mandates, but his impact on the EV supply chain from mining to recycling remains unclear.
Trump is expected to speed up permitting for new mines.
One analyst predicted a Trump battery boom for the industry.
The battery supply chain is a continuum from mining, to making films and powders, to synthesizing battery cells and packs, to vehicle assembly.
The U.S. has invested $200 billion in the effort to build the EV supply sector.

Nearly every part of the EV battery manufacturing process is in its earliest stages and highly reliant on government tax credits, grants, and loans for survival, according to Nick Nigro, founder of Atlas Public Policy.
Ongoing activity from mining to the construction and planning of EV battery plants is largely occurring in Republican congressional districts.
Simon Moores, CEO of Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, stated that $110 billion has already been invested in EV supply chains, mostly downstream.
About 60 percent of individual gigafactories and 80 percent of the investments are moving forward in red states.

Jay Turner, a professor at Wellesley College, noted that of the 15 EV battery factories announced or expanded after the Inflation Reduction Act passed in 2022, eight are in planning and seven are under construction.
Thirteen of those EV battery factories are located in Republican congressional districts.

Demand for EV batteries could fall as much as 20 percent by 2030 compared to previous estimates if Trump follows through on campaign promises, according to Iola Hughes, head of research at Rho Motion.
Trump is expected to alter the regulatory landscape for EVs, starting with EPA tailpipe emissions standards for cars built in model year 2027.
The former president could impose tariffs on imported materials and target tax breaks for EVs and other Inflation Reduction Act provisions.
Industry consensus suggests negative impacts on the EV battery supply chain likely would not be fully felt until 2027, even if Trump acts immediately upon taking office.
Mining could be the part of the EV supply chain that gets the biggest boon from the change in presidential power.
Peter Bryant, a strategist with Clareo, stated there will undoubtedly be a more pro-mining administration in the White House.
Trump's actions on critical minerals date back to a 2017 executive order aimed at reducing the nation's reliance on other countries for them.

Mining boosters are already in talks with Trump's transition team pushing for faster permitting, rollbacks of rules hindering mines, more access to public lands, and changes to the Inflation Reduction Act to ensure domestic mining companies benefit from production tax credits.

Adam Webb, head of battery raw materials at Benchmark, said demand for raw materials used in lithium-ion batteries is expected to be lower in the U.S. than previously forecast due to expected lower EV sales under the incoming administration.
Rho Motion's scenario analysis showed a 20 percent drop in demand for EV batteries by 2030 compared to the current outlook.
The U.S. EV market currently accounts for about 8 percent of global lithium demand, 3 percent of global nickel demand, 7 percent of global cobalt demand, and 6 percent of natural graphite demand.

Of the $83 billion the private sector has committed to the battery supply chain since the Inflation Reduction Act passed, $70 billion is going to Republican-led districts, with the remaining $13.1 billion going to Democratic-controlled areas.
The Inflation Reduction Act's advanced manufacturing tax credit, known as 45X, will provide the biggest pot of money for battery companies.
The $7,500 tax credit that car buyers can get for purchasing an EV is considered one of the most vulnerable incentives to elimination or rule changes.
To qualify for the consumer EV tax credit, automakers must make battery cells in the U.S. and source an ever-escalating amount of crucial materials from the U.S. or its allies.
Loren McDonald, chief analyst at Paren, noted that Musk has previously suggested ending the consumer tax credit would only minimally harm Tesla while sapping the EV efforts of traditional automakers.
source_title: Trump could oversee an EV ‘battery boom’ — or bust - E&E News by POLITICO

facts: The Georgia Chamber is hosting a series of events known collectively as the 2024 New Georgia Economy Tour.
The tour will gather input and data from every state region to prepare a comprehensive planning strategy for 2025 and beyond.
The Savannah stop of the tour will be Tomorrow, June 5, from 7:30 – 9:15 a.m. at the Georgia Southern University’s Armstrong Campus Ballroom, located at 11935 Abercorn Street, Savannah.

Georgia Chamber President and CEO Chris Clark stated the goal with the Tour is to listen and collect information from hardworking Georgians that will help create a strategic plan for the future of the state’s economy.

Since 2020, the Georgia Chamber's strategic initiatives have focused on determining issues that will most impact competitiveness and affect Georgia’s ability to attract and maintain continued economic development success.
The Georgia Chamber is shifting its focus to 2025 and beyond to garner input from local chamber partners to develop solutions and recommendations securing the future prosperity of Georgia.
Savannah Chamber President and CEO Bert Brantley added that the New Georgia Economy tour provides valuable information to assist the Savannah business community in planning for the future.
The Savannah Chamber looks forward to working with the Georgia Chamber to host this event and offer feedback from the community on how Georgia can best move forward.
source_title: Tomorrow in Savannah: Georgia Chamber’s New Georgia Economy Tour Focuses on Future Growth - Savannah Chamber

facts: GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson was named the 2023 “Georgian of the Year” by Georgia Trend on Friday, January 20th, 2023.
The award recognizes Commissioner Wilson as the top salesperson for the state and commends his work promoting the advantages of Georgia to attract investments and create jobs.

A cover story credits the state’s success to a team approach when conducting business and highlights Wilson’s focus on keeping relationships strong with partners and businesses across the state and globe.
Wilson was appointed economic development Commissioner by former Governor Nathan Deal six years prior to the article's publication.
Georgia maintained its place as the No. 1 state in the nation to do business for the ninth consecutive year in 2022.

Former Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue credited Wilson with helping make Georgia one of the best places to grow a business by communicating all the great things that the Georgia governor and legislature have done during those years.
In August 2022, GDEcD announced that in FY22, investments in expansions and new locations totaled more than $21.2 billion.
Those FY22 investments would create 51,132 direct new jobs in the state, marking both new records for Georgia.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s work providing solutions for companies and keeping communication open were two key factors that helped Georgia land two of its largest-ever investments in FY22.
Wilson stated he gets emotional when hearing how jobs have changed people's lives and expressed gratitude to the governors who gave him the chance to do his job.

GDEcD teammates Lee Thomas, Director of the Georgia Film Office, and David Nuckolls, Executive Director of the Center of Innovation, were also recognized in Georgia Trend’s lists for their contributions to the state.
GDEcD is described as the “quarterback” that brings partners across the state together to make business happen.

facts: Georgia offers one of the more aggressive packages of business tax incentives in the Southeast, anchored by credits for job creation, capital investment, and industry-specific spending.
The Georgia Department of Revenue administers the tax side of these programs, while the Georgia Department of Economic Development handles economic certification for many of them.
Georgia’s corporate income tax rate sits at 5.19 percent, and these credits directly reduce that liability, sometimes to zero.

The Job Tax Credit under O.C.G.A. § 48-7-40 serves as the primary incentive system, with every county placed into one of four tiers based on unemployment, per capita income, and similar economic indicators.
The county tier determines both the credit value per job and the minimum number of positions a company must create to qualify.
Tier 1 counties have a two-job minimum threshold for qualifying businesses.
A business creating ten jobs in a Tier 1 county qualifies for $175,000 in total credits over five years.

The Job Tax Credit applies to qualifying industries including manufacturing, warehousing and distribution, processing, telecommunications, tourism, and research and development, while excluding retail businesses.
In Tier 1 counties and less developed census tracts, excess job tax credits can be applied against quarterly or monthly payroll withholding payments if they exceed income tax liability.
Businesses in Tier 2 through Tier 4 counties can access the payroll withholding offset only if certified as “competitive” by the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Unused job tax credits can be carried forward for five years from the close of the tax year in which the qualifying jobs were created.

The Quality Jobs Tax Credit under O.C.G.A. § 48-7-40.17 targets companies creating higher-paying positions, requiring at least 50 new jobs (with a reduced threshold for rural counties) paying at least 110 percent of the county’s average wage.
The Quality Jobs Tax Credit can offset 100 percent of Georgia income tax liability in a given year, with any excess applied against withholding.
Companies cannot claim both the standard Job Tax Credit and the Quality Jobs Tax Credit for the same positions or project.
Georgia designates economically distressed areas as Opportunity Zones, offering an enhanced Job Tax Credit of $3,500 per new job with a minimum threshold of two positions.
Any lawful business can qualify for the Opportunity Zone credit, regardless of industry restrictions that apply to the standard Job Tax Credit.

Businesses must obtain local jurisdiction certification confirming their location falls within a designated zone and meets all local ordinances and licensing standards before claiming Opportunity Zone credits.
Businesses cannot claim credits under both the county tier program and the Opportunity Zone program for the same jobs.
Businesses shipping through Georgia’s ports can access enhanced job or investment credits by increasing port traffic by more than 10 percent over their base year.
The base year for port traffic calculations is the 12-month period two years prior to the claim year.
Base year port traffic must meet minimum thresholds of at least 75 net tons, five containers, or 10 twenty-foot equivalent units.
The Port Activity Tax Credit acts as a multiplier that boosts the value of existing job or investment tax credits rather than functioning as a standalone credit.
Under O.C.G.A. § 48-7-40.12, businesses can claim a Research Tax Credit equal to 10 percent of qualified research spending in Georgia that exceeds a calculated base amount.

The research credit base amount is calculated using Georgia gross receipts, and the business must also claim the federal research credit under Section 41 of the Internal Revenue Code for the same tax year.
All wages and purchases of services and supplies counted toward the Research Tax Credit must be for research conducted within Georgia.

The Research Tax Credit is limited to the same qualifying industries as the Job Tax Credit: manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, processing, telecommunications, broadcasting, tourism, and R&D, excluding retail.
Established manufacturing and telecommunications companies can offset capital costs through the Investment Tax Credit across three statutes tied to county tiers.
To qualify for the Investment Tax Credit, a business must have operated in Georgia for at least three consecutive years.

Qualifying property under the Investment Tax Credit must be reasonably related to the manufacturing process or telecommunications services and result in an expansion of the company’s asset base, excluding equipment used solely to repair existing property.
Employers providing or sponsoring state-approved retraining programs can claim a credit equal to half the cost of retraining per employee, up to $500 per employee per program.
If an employee completes more than one approved retraining program in a year, the total credit for that employee caps at $1,250.
Retraining programs must be state-approved before the credit applies and typically cover new technologies, equipment, or processes to keep employees current.
Georgia’s job tax credits include an enforcement mechanism where net employment falling below the minimum required for a county tier in any year results in losing the credit for that year.
Credits already claimed in prior years are not clawed back if headcount drops; companies simply stop earning new credits until headcount recovers.
If the Department of Revenue determines credits were overclaimed due to negligence, the underpayment penalty is 5 percent of the tax shortfall, while fraud penalties jump to 50 percent.
Interest accrues on any underpayment at the Federal Reserve prime rate plus 3 percent, reviewed annually each January.
Most Georgia tax credits are claimed through the Georgia Tax Center, with specific forms varying by credit type.
Many credits require certification from the Georgia Department of Economic Development before the Department of Revenue will process them.

Required supporting documentation includes payroll records showing new hire dates and wages, equipment invoices for investment credits, itemized expense ledgers separating Georgia spending from out-of-state costs, and property records for capital purchases.
The Department of Revenue retains the right to review any claimed credit, and poor recordkeeping can result in losing the credit.
source_title: Georgia Tax Incentives: Business Credits and Exemptions - LegalClarity

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp signed H.B. 463, which makes many taxpayer-favorable changes to the state’s income tax code.
H.B. 463 reduces Georgia’s personal and corporate income tax rates from 5.19% to 4.99% for 2026.
The bill allows for potential further annual rate reductions of 0.125% down to a floor of 3.99%, pending the state meeting revenue collection targets.
H.B. 463 increases the standard deduction and dependent exemptions for individual income tax purposes for tax years beginning on or after January 1, 2026.
The bill provides a temporary reduced exclusion of overtime pay and tips subject to state tax compared to the amounts exempt from federal income tax under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.
Taxpayers subject to ASC 740 should account for the currently effective 2026 tax rate change in their income tax provisions under the accounting standard.
For deferred income tax purposes, taxpayers must measure deferred income taxes using enacted rates expected to apply to taxable income in the periods when deferred tax items reverse.
The contingent nature of subsequent years’ tax rate reductions leaves open whether and when such reduced rates will go into effect.
Taxpayers should be cognizant of deferred income tax implications associated with tax rates that have yet to be enacted and therefore have uncertain effective dates.

Due to the tie-in with state revenue collection targets, taxpayers should not automatically expect additional decreases to the individual and corporate income tax rates to be immediately effective for tax years beginning January 1, 2027.
Taxpayers should consider the contingent nature of any future rate reductions in the context of deferred income tax accounting.
H.B. 463 is one of several developments during Georgia’s 2026 legislative cycle.
source_title: Georgia Enacts Income Tax Reduction Bill | BDO

facts: More than 300 South Korean workers detained in an immigration raid at a Georgia battery plant departed Atlanta on Thursday on a flight home.
The workers went by bus from southeast Georgia to Atlanta earlier in the day and are expected to land in South Korea on Friday afternoon, according to South Korea’s Foreign Ministry.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung called for improvements in the U.S. visa system as he spoke about the Sept. 4 immigration raid that resulted in the arrest of more than 300 South Korean workers at Hyundai’s sprawling auto plant west of Savannah.
U.S. authorities have released the 330 detainees — 316 of them Koreans — and that they were being transported by buses to Atlanta’s Hartsfield-Jackson airport.
The group includes 10 Chinese nationals, three Japanese nationals and one Indonesian.
The flight is back on track after an earlier departure had been canceled for an unspecified reason.
A Korean Air Boeing 747-8i departed from Seoul for the U.S. to bring back the detained Korean workers and landed in Atlanta.
The workers were being held at an immigration detention center in Folkston, in southeast Georgia, near the state line with Florida.
It’s a 285-mile (460-kilometer) drive from Folkston to Atlanta.

South Korean officials said they were negotiating with the U.S. to win “voluntary” departures for the workers, rather than deportations, which could make them ineligible to return to the U.S. for up to 10 years.

During a visit to Washington, South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun met with U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio and told him that his people were left with “big pains and shocks” because the video of the workers’ arrests was publicly disclosed, the ministry said in a statement.

Cho called for the U.S. administration to help the workers leave as soon as possible — without being handcuffed — and to ensure they do not face problems in future reentry to the U.S., the statement said.

During his meeting with Rubio, Cho also proposed the creation of a joint South Korea-U.S. working group to introduce a new visa category for workers from the Asian nation, according to Cho’s ministry.
South Korean TV showed Cho Ki-joong, consul general at the embassy in Washington, speaking outside the detention center.
Cho Ki-joong said some administrative steps remained but things were going smoothly.
The Foreign Ministry declined to comment on media reports that he and other diplomats met with the detained workers.

South Korea’s president said in a speech marking 100 days in office Thursday that Korean companies would likely hesitate to further invest in the U.S. unless Washington improves its visa system for their workers.
Lee said that whether the U.S. establishes a visa system allowing South Korean companies to send skilled workers to industrial sites will have a “major impact” on future investments.

“It’s not like these are long-term workers. When you build a factory or install equipment at a factory, you need technicians, but the United States doesn’t have that workforce and yet they won’t issue visas to let our people stay and do the work,” he said.

“If that’s not possible, then establishing a local factory in the United States will either come with severe disadvantages or become very difficult for our companies. They will wonder whether they should even do it,” Lee added.
U.S. authorities have said that those detained during the raid were “unlawfully working” at the plant.

Charles Kuck, a lawyer representing several of the detained South Koreans, said the “vast majority” of the workers from South Korea were doing work that is authorized under the B-1 business visitor visa program.
A B-1 visitor for business visa allows foreign workers to stay for up to six months, getting reimbursed for expenses while collecting a paycheck back home.

There are limits — for example, they can supervise construction projects but can’t build anything themselves — but if it’s spelled out in a contract, they can install equipment, Los Angeles immigration lawyer Angelo Paparelli said.

South Korea is one of 41 countries whose citizens can use the U.S. Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA), which provides visa waivers to those who can provide “a legitimate reason’’ for their visit.
This basically gives them B-1 visa status for up to 90 days, according to Los Angeles immigration attorney Rita Sostrin.
The raid targeted one of the Georgia’s largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, touted by the governor and other officials as the largest economic development project in state history.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing EVs a year ago at the $7.6 billion plant, which employs about 1,200 people.

In a statement Wednesday, the governor’s office stressed its “strong relationship with the Republic of Korea and Korean partners like Hyundai, stretching back 40 years to the establishment of Georgia’s trad office in Seoul.”
source_title: What to know: Flight to bring detained Korean workers home from Georgia | AP News

facts: A major immigration raid at the Hyundai Electric Vehicle Plant near Pooler led to the arrest of more than 450 individuals, primarily South Korean nationals.
Hyundai says those who were arrested weren’t directly employed by the South Korean car company.
The operation, conducted by Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Homeland Security, involved around 400 agents.
The government says the raid was prompted by allegations of unlawful employment of immigrants here illegally.
The majority of those arrested are currently detained at an ICE facility in Folkston, Georgia.
The White House has expressed support for the raid, emphasizing the importance of legal entry for foreign workers involved in U.S. projects.
Democratic critics have labeled the raid as politically motivated.
South Korea is responding to the incident by sending diplomats from its embassy in Washington and its consulate to Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Company released an updated statement Friday night, saying the company believes none of the people arrested works directly for the company.

Hyundai stated it is committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations in every market where it operates, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws, and expects the same commitment from all partners, suppliers, contractors, and subcontractors.
At a news conference in Savannah, Homeland Security officials clarified that this was not a routine ICE raid but was specifically targeted due to federal law violations.
U.S. Attorney Margaret E. Heap stated the goal of the operation is to reduce illegal employment and prevent employers from gaining an unfair advantage by hiring unauthorized workers.
U.S. Attorney Margaret E. Heap also stated another goal is to protect unauthorized workers from exploitation.
Steven Schrank, Special Agent in Charge, Homeland Security Investigations, said at a news briefing Friday that the majority of the people detained were from South Korea.
Steven Schrank stated the operation underscores our commitment to jobs for Georgians and Americans.
Steven Schrank noted that this was in fact the largest single site enforcement operation in the history of Homeland Security Investigations.
A spokesperson for Gov. Brian Kemp said Friday that the Department of Public Safety coordinated with federal agencies to provide support for the operation.

The spokesperson stated that Georgia will always enforce the law, including all state and federal immigration laws, and that all companies operating within the State must follow the laws of Georgia and our nation.
The investigation has been ongoing for several months, with authorities receiving leads from community members and former workers.

The operation targeted one of Georgia’s largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, touted by the governor and other officials as the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing EVs a year ago at the plant.
The plant represents a $7.6 billion investment.
The plant employs about 1,200 people.
ATF Atlanta joined HSI, FBI, DEA, ICE, GSP and other agencies in a major immigration enforcement operation at the Hyundai mega site battery plant in Bryan County, GA.

facts: Kia Motors Corporation (KMC) carried out the groundbreaking ceremony for its first-ever U.S. manufacturing facility in West Point, Troup County, Georgia.
The automotive assembly and manufacturing plant represents a US$1 billion investment.
The facility is central to Kia’s plans to increase North American sales (US and Canada) to around 650,000 units by 2010.

The groundbreaking ceremony was attended by over 500 VIPs from Korea and the U.S., including Mong-Koo Chung (Chairman of Hyundai-Kia Automotive Group), Euisun Chung (President & CEO, Kia Motors Corporation), Sonny Perdue (Governor of Georgia), and high-ranking Korean government and State of Georgia officials.
Mong-Koo Chung stated that the establishment of the U.S. plant will serve as a springboard to achieve Kia's ultimate goal of firmly establishing itself as a truly world-class global automaker.

Chung added that Kia Motors will secure jobs and sustain financial growth in the area through transparent management and world-class quality assurance, striving to become a responsible corporate citizen contributing to the local community and preservation of the environment.

Governor Sonny Perdue stated that the ceremony marks the beginning of a long-term partnership between Kia Motors and Georgia, anticipating years of cooperation as the new plant creates thousands of jobs and sets the standard for the automotive industry.
The Georgia facility is named KMMG (Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia) and is scheduled to begin production in 2009.
At full capacity, the plant is expected to produce 300,000 vehicles per year.
The 224,000-square meter plant will be situated on a site covering 2.6 million square meters.
The plant will employ approximately 2,500 local workers.
Five to six suppliers have plans to set up operations in Troup County and its environs, creating an additional 2,000 jobs.
A total of around 10 suppliers are eventually expected to locate in the vicinity of the plant.

Alongside a second manufacturing plant in China scheduled for completion next year and the start of production at its first European facility in Slovakia this November, the U.S. plant will play a critical role in boosting Kia’s sales and brand development worldwide.
The U.S. plant will bring Kia’s total annual overseas production capacity to around 1 million units.
Kia Motors, along with Hyundai Motor, has invested more than US$300 million in the North American region since 2002.
Past North American investments include an R&D center in Michigan, a custom-built vehicle proving ground in the Mojave desert, and the largest automotive design studio in California.
Kia Motors America is currently constructing a new US$85 million, 22,000 square meter corporate headquarters building in Irvine, California.
The Irvine corporate headquarters is scheduled for completion in December 2006 and occupancy in January 2007.
The new California facility will also house a US$35 million, 9,400 square meter Kia-exclusive design center to be completed later in 2007.
Following construction of the KMMG facility, Kia will have the capability to localize all areas of its business in the U.S., from R&D and production to marketing, sales, and after service.
Since entering the North American market, Kia has expanded to a full product line of vehicles and enjoyed 12 years of consecutive sales increases.
Kia sold 304,137 vehicles in 2005.
For September 2006, Kia posted a year-on-year sales increase of 6.4% in the U.S. and Canada.
source_title: New manufacturing plant to be the cornerstone of Kia's plans to more than double sales in North Amer - AI Online -

facts: Korean car maker Kia Motors announced a leadership transition at its Georgia manufacturing arm, Kia Georgia Inc., in mid-December.
Jangsoo (Jason) Shin led Kia Georgia starting in 2017.
Under Mr. Shin’s watch, the company introduced two new models: the Telluride SUV and the K5 sedan.
The Georgia factory began producing the latest generation of the Sorento mid-size SUV under Mr. Shin’s watch.
Starting Jan. 1, Jangsoo (Jason) Shin was replaced as CEO by Stuart Countess.
Stuart Countess is the former chief operating officer who will also hold the role of president and already played a more visible role in the state’s manufacturing community.
Mr. Countess joined the company in 2008, after Kia had announced the factory in Georgia but before it had broken ground.
After production began in 2009, Mr. Countess moved steadily up the ranks, working in manufacturing and quality and taking up the chief administrative officer position before becoming COO.
Stuart Countess said: “I am honored to have the opportunity to lead Kia Georgia’s world class team in my new capacity.”

Stuart Countess said: “Kia is looking to the future with great confidence in our contribution to the automotive industry, and Kia Georgia intends to be an integral part of our North American strategy.”
In response to industry trends, Kia is looking to electrify more of its lineup.
Officials hope Kia's electrification efforts will lead to more investment at the Georgia plant.
Where Kia’s localized EV production will be placed is an open question.
Kia Georgia announced recently that it would add a fourth model to its Georgia-made lineup: the Kia Sportage crossover.

Mr. Countess assumes leadership during a tough time for the auto sector, with rising wages and inflation and snarled supply chains forcing closures at many plants built on the just-in-time model of inventory management.
Kia Georgia reportedly has weathered a few brief closures due to shortages of semiconductors.
The investment that Kia pioneered as the state’s only vehicle assembly plant has continued to pay dividends through suppliers and more recently, other OEMs following Kia into the state.
Georgia announced in December that Rivian, an electric truck maker, would put a $5 billion plant in Covington.
source_title: Kia Promotes Plant Veteran as New CEO for Georgia Operations  - Global Atlanta

facts: On Feb. 24, 2026, Kia Georgia team members marked another historic day at its West Point facility with the start of production of the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride.
The ceremonial vehicle represented two other major milestones: the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in the state of Georgia and the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.

A ceremony for the milestone was attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, team members and state/community leaders, with the single Telluride symbolizing Kia’s continued growth, innovation and investment in advanced manufacturing in Georgia.

Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that as the only plant in the world that builds Telluride, the launch reflects the strength of the West Point team, customer trust, and Kia’s commitment to building the future of mobility in Georgia.

Yoon added that reaching five million vehicles and introducing, not only the first HEV assembled in Georgia, but Kia’s first US-assembled HEV, is a testament to both Kia’s storied history and direction going forward.
Since beginning production in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s largest employers and a cornerstone of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride is one of the industry’s most awarded vehicles, has become a flagship model for the brand, and serves as a symbol of quality built in West Point.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that since its introduction in 2019, the Telluride has been exclusively Georgia-made and has become one of the most successful vehicles in Kia’s lineup.
Governor Kemp attributed this success to a proven partnership approach creating long-lasting relationships between Kia Georgia, the local community, and the entire state.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s President and CEO, emphasized that the day’s milestones were made possible by the dedication of the team members at Kia Georgia.
Countess stated that five million vehicles do not happen by chance but are the result of famous teamwork, versatility, and talent in producing world-class gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs and now HEVs.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
The facility has an annual capacity of 350,000 units and is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
The plant produces the Telluride, Sorento (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage (excludes HEV and PHEV models) SUVs, and the all-electric EV9 and EV6.

Ceremony attendees included Consul General Joon-ho Lee; State Rep. Vance Smith; Technical College System of Georgia Commissioner Greg Dozier; Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Chris Nunn; Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson; Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess; Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp; Georgia First Lady Marty Kemp; Kia North America and Kia America President and CEO Sean Yoon; HATCI President John Robb; Georgia Chamber of Commerce President Chris Clark; and Hyundai Motor Group Washington Office Senior Vice President Drew Ferguson.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebrates trio of major milestones, including Start of Production for 2027 Kia Telluride - Kia Georgia

facts: The Inaugural Battery Belt Charging Forward Forum was held on Tuesday, August 26 in Atlanta, GA.

The forum was hosted by the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM) at the University of Georgia with guest experts from the American EV Jobs Alliance, Kia Georgia, MAGNET, and the Salata Institute for Climate and Sustainability.

This closed-door session was one of the first regional convenings in the Southeast dedicated specifically to the link between charging policy, infrastructure deployment, and consumer adoption challenges.
The expert roundtable brought together representatives from utilities, academia, OEMs, industry, and policymakers from Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, and South Carolina.
Michael E. Murphy, Executive Director of the American EV Jobs Alliance, shared voter survey data showing that charging reliability is the top concern for EV adoption.
Michael E. Murphy emphasized that framing electric vehicles around jobs and competitiveness resonates most strongly with the public.
Mike Oatridge, CEO of MAGNET, outlined MAGNET’s tri-state tech hub and research partnership.
Southern Company and the University of Georgia/GNEM are founding members of MAGNET.
Mike Oatridge highlighted how charging access and supply chain resilience directly support U.S. competitiveness and national security.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, Inc., joined a panel to discuss OEM priorities for scaling charging access.
Kevin Neal, Director of External Affairs for EV Sales at Ford Motor Company, joined the same panel.
The panel was moderated by Gabrielle Pierre, GNEM Director of Strategy and Partnerships.
The panel discussed ensuring that infrastructure keeps pace with manufacturing investment.

Dr. Elaine Buckberg, Senior Fellow at the Salata Institute for Climate and Sustainability at Harvard University and former Chief Economist at General Motors, presented data-driven insights on charging reliability and maintenance.
Dr. Omar Isaac Asensio, Associate Professor at the Georgia Institute of Technology's School of Public Policy and Director of the Data Science & Policy Lab, presented alongside Dr. Buckberg.
The speakers discussed opportunities for model legislation to accelerate infrastructure deployment through mandating data availability on reliability, maintenance, and uptime.
Stephanie Valdez Streaty, Director of Industry Insights at Cox Automotive Inc., delivered an EV market update.
Stephanie Valdez Streaty underscored how charging infrastructure impacts both new and used EV adoption and long-term consumer confidence.
Roundtable themes included cross-state collaboration to reinforce policy alignment across state lines for sustained EV adoption, advanced automotive manufacturing, and regional economic development.
Roundtable themes recognized charging as a key linchpin for consumer uptake and continued manufacturing competitiveness.
Roundtable themes focused on leveraging academic, voter, and industry insights to guide policy and improve reliability.
Roundtable themes emphasized ensuring OEMs, utilities, and policymakers are aligned in building public confidence and scaling infrastructure.
Gabrielle Pierre stated that charging infrastructure will likely form the backbone of the Southeast’s role as the nation’s leading hub for electric and future mobility manufacturing and innovation.
Gabrielle Pierre expressed excitement about leading in seeding regional partnerships and collaboration opportunities focused on key unlocks for the industry.
Georgia public service commissioner Tim Echols attended the meeting.
Tim Echols stated that batteries have become the new generator providing back-up in case of storm outage, and with more vehicles able to provide power to the home, it is important.
Tim Echols noted he has had eight EVs over the last 13 years and the products continue to get better.

The forum marked a critical step in aligning public and private sector leaders to meet electric vehicle drivers’ needs while reinforcing the Southeast’s leadership in electric and broader advanced mobility innovation and manufacturing.
Elaine Buckberg stated that making public charging seamless for EV drivers is a crucial enabler for EV adoption, which means jobs in the Southeast.
Elaine Buckberg noted that conversations at the forum position the Southeast Battery Belt region to lead in charging, as well as EV and battery production.
GNEM will continue convening regional and national expert roundtables in the months and years ahead to strengthen collaboration across the region and beyond.
The Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM) is a statewide strategy engine, public-private partnership network, industry consortium, and think tank embedded at the University of Georgia.
GNEM's founding partners include Georgia Power, Cox Automotive, Kia Georgia, and the Georgia Department of Transportation.
GNEM works across research, workforce development, community engagement, and industry partnerships to advance electric and future mobility in Georgia and across the Battery Belt.
source_title: Inaugural Battery Belt Forum brings together policymakers, industry, and utilities to advance EV infrastructure across the Southeast - College of Engineering

facts: Together with local suppliers, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is responsible for the creation of more than 10,000 jobs in West Point and surrounding areas.
Powertech America, the on-site transmission supplier for KMMG, announced plans to add 143 new positions at its West Point facility.
Powertech America announced plans to add a third shift at its West Point facility.
KMMG added a third shift in 2011 as part of a $100 million expansion.
KMMG's production contributed to Kia's string of 21 consecutive monthly sales records.
KMMG produces the award-winning and top-selling Kia Sorento CUV and Kia Optima mid-size sedan.
On-site and local suppliers have created or announced nearly 8,000 jobs.
KMMG has directly created more than 3,000 jobs.
Byung Mo Ahn, Group President and CEO for Kia Motors America and KMMG, stated that the success of KMMG has led to tremendous growth at the plant and with suppliers who continue to expand with them.
Byung Mo Ahn expressed pride in the work of team members at KMMG and its suppliers who contributed to continued regional success.
Powertech held job fairs beginning the week of June 11.
The separate third shift at Powertech is taking place by January.
This latest expansion will increase total employment at Powertech to 454 team members.
KMMG is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Motors Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
KMMG is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
KMMG began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Beginning in 2012, the plant has the ability to build 360,000 vehicles annually from U.S. and globally-sourced parts.
source_title: Strong demand for vehicles built at Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia leads to additional hiring for on-site supplier - Kia Georgia

facts: Kia is investing $200m into an expansion of its Georgia West Point plant to accommodate the assembly of its new EV9 SUV in the second quarter of 2024.
The EV9 model will be Kia’s first EV to be assembled in North America.
The expansion to produce the fifth vehicle assembled at the plant will create almost 200 additional jobs in Troup County, according to the OEM.
Currently, the Georgia West Point plant assembles the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs as well as the K5 mid-size sedan.
Sean Yoon, president and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that like the Telluride, the EV9 has the potential to be another game changer for Kia.
Sean Yoon also noted that the EV9 will be the most sophisticated vehicle Kia has ever built and a standout in the EV market and on the road.
Earlier in the year, Kia’s sister plant, the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, added EV production to produce the first Genesis model made in the US.
The South Korean carmaker invested $300m to bring about production of the Genesis Electrified GV70 SUV in the Montgomery plant, resulting in the creation of 200 jobs.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution officially announced a plan last month to establish a 50/50 EV battery joint venture in Savannah, Georgia.
The Hyundai-LG Energy Solution joint venture signed a memorandum of understanding to build a 30GWh battery factory worth more than $4.3 billion.
In April, the carmaker announced plans to invest $5 billion, along with SK On, to build a new EV battery cell plant in Georgia.
Production at the new Kia-SK On EV battery cell plant is slated to start in the second half of 2025.
SK On has been a strategic partner for Kia in its work on the Genesis GV60 and other models.
The Hyundai-LG Energy Solution joint venture will support the group’s electrification efforts and provide a stable supply of batteries for EV production in the US, according to Hyundai.
The new battery plant is set to be based in Bartow County, Georgia.

The Bartow County plant location is close to Hyundai’s US production facilities including the Kia Georgia plant, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing plant in Alabama, and Hyundai’s Motor Group Metaplant America.
source_title: Kia plans $200m expansion for EV assembly at Georgia plant | Automotive Manufacturing Solutions

facts: Autos Drive America hosted its first Board meeting of 2023 in Washington, D.C.
The association’s leadership met with key Congressional leaders and Biden administration officials to discuss policy opportunities and challenges facing international automakers operating in the U.S.
Autos Drive America announced Pablo Di Si of Volkswagen Group of America, Inc. as its new Board Chair.
Jennifer Safavian serves as President & CEO of Autos Drive America.
Autos Drive America members are committed to boosting the economy and creating high-skilled career opportunities through continued investments in communities across the U.S.
The association will advocate for policies that promote free and fair trade, strengthen global supply chains, and foster investment in careers and communities across the U.S. this year.
Members discussed international automakers’ sprawling footprint in communities nationwide.
International automakers account for 2.3 million direct and indirect jobs in the U.S.
International automakers operate 500 facilities in the U.S.
International automakers have made investments totaling more than $100 billion in the U.S.

Pablo Di Si stated that international automakers are critical to the U.S. economy and are investing billions of dollars to strengthen domestic assembly and supply chains for electric vehicles, creating thousands of new jobs for American people.
Autos Drive America announced its 2023 Board of Directors to guide the association’s policy agenda and advocacy over the year.
Scott Brand, Executive Vice President of Subaru of Indiana Automotive, Inc., was named Vice Chair.
David Johnson, Senior Vice President of Manufacturing and Supply Chain Management at Nissan North America, Inc., was named Secretary.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, Inc., was named Treasurer.
source_title: Autos Drive America Hosts First Board Meeting of 2023, Announces New Board Leadership - Autos Drive America

facts: Officials with Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG), Inc. celebrated the official grand opening of Kia’s first-ever U.S. automobile manufacturing plant in West Point, Georgia on Feb. 26, 2010.
The plant is capable of producing 300,000 cars annually for the North American and global markets.

The ceremony was attended by approximately 500 VIPs from Korea and the U.S., including Chairman of the Hyundai-Kia Automotive Group Mong-Koo Chung; Group President and CEO of KMMG and Kia Motors America (KMA) Byung-Mo Ahn; Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue; and other high-ranking Korean government and State of Georgia officials.
Group President and CEO of KMMG and Kia Motors America (KMA) Byung-Mo Ahn stated that Kia is proud to call West Point, Georgia home and looks forward to many years of growing together.
Continued diligent work has helped elevate Kia to one of the fastest growing automobile brands around the globe, according to Byung-Mo Ahn.

The successful launch of the 2011 Kia Sorento CUV and establishment of Kia’s first manufacturing plant in the United States position the Kia brand for further growth and to become a worldwide leader in quality and innovation, according to Byung-Mo Ahn.
The project represents a $1 billion investment by Kia Motors Corporation.
The sprawling, new automobile manufacturing plant is 2.2 million square feet and situated on more than 2,200 acres of land between Exit 2 and Exit 6 along Interstate 85 in West Point.
KMMG consists of four main shops: stamping, welding, paint and assembly.
The site also includes a transmission shop, module shop and a two-mile test track where every vehicle produced by KMMG will be thoroughly tested.

KMMG’s general assembly area is outfitted with more than a half mile of height-adjustable conveyors to achieve the most favorable installation position for each team member and reduce physical fatigue.
The general assembly area is outfitted with wood flooring, which provides a more comfortable and forgiving work environment for team members.

Georgia Governor Sonny Perdue noted that today’s event meant the continuation of a mutually beneficial partnership between the State of Georgia and Kia that will mean many great things for the State in years to come.
Kia Motors has brought thousands of new jobs to the West Point area and is truly delivering as a tremendous corporate citizen, according to Governor Sonny Perdue.

The State of Georgia pledges to continue providing specialized training through the Kia Georgia Training Center that will guarantee a quality workforce and the future success of this plant in Georgia, according to Governor Sonny Perdue.
The massive Kia automobile manufacturing plant and its supplier companies have already provided thousands of jobs.
KMMG currently employs more than 1,200 team members and is expected to nearly double that number by the end of the year (relative to Feb. 26, 2010).
Onsite and nearby suppliers will create up to 7,500 additional jobs in the region.
Randy Jackson, director of human resources and administration at KMMG, said he was glad that KMMG could help give back to the local community through job creation, community development and tourism.
The Grand Opening marks a new milestone for Kia, according to Randy Jackson.

Beginning this spring (relative to Feb. 26, 2010), KMMG will open its doors to the community to show firsthand the quality and craftsmanship that is built into every Kia vehicle, according to Randy Jackson.
KMMG hopes every Kia owner, and anyone thinking about purchasing a Kia, will visit them, according to Randy Jackson.
Mass production of the all-new 2011 Kia Sorento CUV began on Nov. 16, 2009 with the product officially going on sale in the U.S. in January of this year (2010).
The 2011 Sorento sold 7,398 units in its first month, making it Kia’s best-selling vehicle in January (2010).
The outstanding early performance of the first vehicle produced from the West Point, Georgia plant is a significant achievement for KMMG, KMA and the Kia brand as a whole, according to Byung-Mo Ahn.
As the company grows, it will continue to expand its line of vehicles here and abroad while staying true to the Kia brand tenets of value, quality, safety and technology, according to Byung-Mo Ahn.
KMMG will play a prominent role in achieving these goals, according to Byung-Mo Ahn.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Motors Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
At full capacity, the plant will have the ability to build 300,000 vehicles annually from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
source_title: KIA CELEBRATES GRAND OPENING OF $1 BILLION STATE-OF-THE-ART AUTOMOBILE MANUFACTURING PLANT IN GEORGIA

facts: Kia Georgia officially began production of the all-new 2023 Sportage subcompact SUV on Tuesday, February 1, 2022.
The plant facility is located in West Point, Georgia.
The 2023 Sportage becomes the fourth model produced by Kia Georgia, joining the Telluride, Sorento, and K5 models.
The celebratory launch vehicle was jungle green in color with the X-Pro Prestige trim package.
The all-new Sportage is Kia's longest-running nameplate and has undergone its most significant changes to date as part of Kia’s “Movement that inspires” transformation.
Sean Yoon, president and CEO for Kia North America, introduced the vehicle at the launch event.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp drove the celebratory Sportage off the assembly line alongside James Watson, Kia Georgia’s vice president of production.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “It was an honor to be here today for this milestone achievement by the Kia Georgia team. The Sportage is yet another great product built in Georgia, and its launch here in West Point is a proud moment for our state. I congratulate all of the great Kia Georgia team members for their continued commitment to quality and growth, and thank them for bringing more opportunity to the No. 1 state for business.”

Sean Yoon stated: “The launch of Sportage in Georgia is a further testament to Kia’s longstanding dedication to the U.S. market and the local economy. Joining our tremendous fleet of SUVs, the all-new Sportage is poised to take the industry by storm with its cutting-edge design, adventurous capability and desirable in-car technology. Coming off a record-setting sales year in 2021, today’s launch adds further momentum to the Kia brand’s fast start in 2022.”
Stuart Countess, president and CEO of Kia Georgia, credited the team's long history of success and reputation for quality as key factors in adding a fourth model to the plant.

Stuart Countess stated: “The Kia Georgia team as a group is second to none and has been producing award-winning vehicles for more than 12 years. Today’s ceremony is another phenomenal accomplishment by the Kia Georgia team and I hope that each team member will take a lot of pride in being a part of this special day.”
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation, which is based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 340,000 units.
The Kia Georgia facility is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
Kia Georgia began mass production on November 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia's current production lineup includes the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and K5 mid-size sedan.
source_title: Kia Georgia begins production of the all-new 2023 Sportage

facts: Kia Georgia's revenues are gauged from an analysis of company filings.
Kia Georgia's Income Statement is based on industry averages.

Financial metrics tracked for Kia Georgia include Revenue (Sales), Cost of Goods Sold, Gross Profit, Operating Expenses, Advertising, Salaries and wages, Other Operating Expenses, Total Operating Expenses, Operating Income, EBITDA, EBIT, and Net Profit.
A competitive analysis shows companies in the same general field as Kia Georgia may not compete head-to-head.
The largest companies by revenue in this industry may not have the largest market share if they have diversified into other business lines.
Competitive advantage comes from offering better pricing or superior products/service.
Companies similar in business line and location to Kia Georgia compete with neighboring businesses for customers and skilled employees.

Kia Georgia faces future competition from rapidly expanding companies that may grow organically or through acquisition, where high growth rates may be caused by data that weren't available in previous years.
source_title: Annual Report on Kia Georgia's Revenue, Growth, SWOT Analysis & Competitor Intelligence - IncFact

facts: The 2022 UGA Electric Mobility Summit was held in Athens, GA.
Georgia is fast becoming a hub of investment and activity in electric mobility.
The state has a vibrant multi-modal transportation network that includes Atlanta-Hartsfield Jackson International Airport, the Port of Savannah, and an extensive road and rail network.
The state is poised to be a leader in the adoption of electric mobility technologies for the benefit of the health and resilience of its communities.

This inaugural meeting focused on how the integration of electric mobility in communities will change Georgia, the educational programs needed to support growth, and the future of electric mobility in Georgia and beyond its borders.
The event took place on Thursday, April 28 and Friday, April 29.
An E-Mobility Showcase and Reception was held from 6:00 to 7:30 p.m. at the Delta Innovation Hub, located at 210 Spring Street, Athens, GA.
Commissioner Tim Echols of the Georgia Public Service Commission delivered opening remarks.
The main sessions were held at Mahler Hall – Georgia Center, located at 1197 S Lumpkin Street, Athens, GA.
A session on Opportunities for E-Mobility Technologies was scheduled for 8:30 a.m.
Stephanie Gossman from Electric Transportation at Georgia Power Company spoke during the morning sessions.
Russell McMurry, Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Transportation, spoke during the morning sessions.
Roundtable 1 focused on Perspectives from the E-Mobility Industry and began at 9:30 a.m.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, Inc., participated in Roundtable 1.
Joe George, President of Cox Automotive Mobility Solutions, participated in Roundtable 1.
Steven Jahng, Director of External Affairs and Social Value at SK Battery America, participated in Roundtable 1.
Lincoln Wood, Electrification Policy Manager at Southern Company, participated in Roundtable 1.
A session on Education Pathways for E-Mobility: Existing Programs and Future Needs was scheduled for 11:00 a.m.
Steven Ferguson, Chief Information Officer of the Technical College System of Georgia, spoke during the education pathways session.
Art Recesso, Chief Innovation Officer of the University System of Georgia, spoke during the education pathways session.
Roundtable 3, titled A New Mobility Era, began at 1:30 p.m.
Anne Blair, Director of EV Policy at the Electrification Coalition, participated in Roundtable 3.
Elyse Hart, Assistant Manager of EV Training and Integration at GM's EV Ecosystem, participated in Roundtable 3.
Roger Lin, Vice President of Marketing & Government Relations at Ascend Elements, Inc., participated in Roundtable 3.
Andrew Sunderman, Senior Manager of Product Management for Global Farming Solutions at AGCO Corporation, participated in Roundtable 3.
source_title: 2022 UGA Electric Mobility Summit - Georgia Economic Developers Association

facts: Georgia’s economic development efforts are continuing to feature a rural focus.
The state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team reported more than $20.3 billion in investment across Georgia during the last fiscal year.
The last fiscal year included 429 facility expansions and new locations that created 26,900 new private-sector jobs.
More than 18,300 of those jobs, representing $12.5 billion in investment, came outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
Investments outside the metro Atlanta area accounted for 83% of all facility expansions and new locations during the fiscal year.
Prioritizing economic growth in rural Georgia has been a priority of Gov. Brian Kemp since he first ran for governor back in 2018.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that this strategy ensures regional opportunities for rural Georgians to take advantage of without having too long a commute.
Gov. Brian Kemp cited the huge “Metaplant” Hyundai Motor Group is building in Bryan County west of Savannah as a prime example of job opportunities in the pipeline for areas outside metro Atlanta.
The electric-vehicle manufacturing plant is now under construction.
The Metaplant site will be within easy reach not only for residents of Savannah but for job seekers living in rural counties west of Bryan County along Interstate 16.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that engineering graduates from Georgia Southern University can stay home and have a great-paying job with great benefits instead of moving to Atlanta.
Manufacturing accounted for more than 50% of the expansions or new locations that took place across Georgia during fiscal 2024.
Notable expansions included Kia Georgia’s addition of the EV9 to its lineup in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corp. established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero expanded or established operations in Augusta.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer completed its most recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Metaplant suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens counties.
Gov. Brian Kemp expressed confidence that Georgia's industry will eventually become a major success despite sluggish sales, given the state is all-in on electric vehicles manufacturing.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the Biden administration with the IRA tried to push the market before it was ready.
Gov. Brian Kemp predicted that once charging stations are built out, there’s going to be a big market for these vehicles.
The governor and Georgia First Lady Marty Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland during the last fiscal year.
These missions focused on strengthening existing relationships with key investors including Hyundai and its supplier network.
Those trips and similar overseas missions the previous year helped land more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that a lot of the ground plowed internationally is starting to pay off.
source_title: Rural jobs, investment dominate Georgia economic growth - Rough Draft Atlanta

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Kia will invest more than $200 million at its West Point plant.
The investment will create nearly 200 new jobs.
The investment is to accommodate assembly of the company’s all-electric 2024 EV9 three-row SUV.
The production line transformation will make the EV9 the fifth model assembled at Kia Georgia.
The EV9 joins the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, and the K5 mid-size sedan in Kia Georgia's lineup.
EV9 assembly is expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
Governor Kemp noted that this project will bring more opportunity to Georgians and help propel the state toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Currently, more than 40 percent of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
The addition of the EV9 will further increase the facility’s output.
The EV9 is Kia’s first three-row EV SUV.
The EV9 is the first Kia EV to be assembled in North America.
The EV9 is the flagship vehicle for Kia’s global Plan S strategy focused on sustainable mobility leadership.
Sean Yoon, President & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated that like Telluride, the EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia.
Sean Yoon stated that the EV9 will be the most innovative vehicle Kia has ever built and a standout in the EV market and on the road.
Kia Georgia is a certified Georgia Made manufacturer.
Kia Georgia will continue to utilize the services of Georgia Quick Start, the nation’s top-ranked workforce development program.
Georgia Quick Start operates the Kia Georgia Training Center.
Quick Start’s comprehensive, customized training services will prepare new employees with the skills required to perform successfully on the new assembly line.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, stated that the Kia Georgia team looks forward to continuing its tradition as the plant begins production of its first EV.
Stuart Countess noted that partnerships developed statewide with the Governor’s Office, the Department of Economic Development, and Georgia QuickStart have been tremendous.
Stuart Countess noted that local partnerships with the City of West Point and Troup County have also been tremendous.
City of West Point Mayor Steve Tramell stated that Kia has been an outstanding corporate citizen and community partner for seventeen years.
Mayor Tramell noted that by providing employment opportunities and fostering economic growth, Kia has helped to improve the livelihoods of many people in West Point.
Senior Regional Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, the City of West Point, Troup County, Georgia Center of Innovation’s Manufacturing and Energy teams, and Georgia Quick Start.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that since Georgia recruited Kia in 2006 to create long-lasting opportunities for West Georgia, Kia has exceeded expectations as a partner by giving back to its adopted community.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in the state of Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.
Earlier this year, Governor Kemp and state lawmakers recognized January 31 as “Kia Day.”

The recognition commended Kia’s vehicle manufacturing and its impact to the state since making one of the biggest single foreign investments in Georgia’s history with the 2006 announcement that a state-of-the-art vehicle assembly facility would be established in West Point.
From Georgia, Kia ships vehicles to sales markets around the globe, including the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East and Pacific nations.
Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire supply chain as the emerging electric vehicle market continues to grow.
This pursuit has resulted in more than $22.7 billion in investment and the creation of over 28,400 jobs since 2020.
source_title: Kia to Invest Over $200 Million in EV9 Production Expansion - Georgia_CEO

facts: Two of Kia's most popular EV models now qualify for federal tax credits that can save American consumers thousands of dollars.
A new U.S. presidential administration is creating an uncertain future for incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Kia has begun manufacturing its EV6 and EV9 models at a facility in Georgia.
Kia is using qualifying components for its EV6 and EV9 models.
Both the EV6 and EV9 are eligible for the IRA's full $7,500 tax credit for new EVs in 2025.

The U.S. government determines IRA rebate eligibility based on manufacturers' suggested retail prices, battery components and mineral sourcing, assembly location, and individual modified adjusted gross income.
Kia sold more than 70,000 vehicles in November for the first time ever.
Kia's EV division grew by 45% year-over-year.
Demand for the all-electric EV6 soared by as much as 46%, setting another sales record.
This marks the first time Kia's EV6 and EV9 have been added to the IRA eligibility list.
According to the Environmental Protection Agency, a typical passenger car releases more than 10,000 pounds of carbon pollution each year.
The bulk of Kia's sales still come from its gas-powered and hybrid vehicle lineup.
Kia aims to have 15 EV models in circulation by 2027.
Kia aims to sell 1.6 million EVs internationally by 2030.
Kia intends to expand its charging infrastructure.
Kia has a seven-year partnership with The Ocean Cleanup to tackle plastic pollution.
Kia aims to operate its business sites entirely on renewable energy by 2040.
Last year, Kia introduced 6 megawatts of solar generation to plants across South Korea and India.
source_title: Kia's strategic manufacturing refresh could save consumers thousands on EVs — here's what you need to know

facts: Patriot One Technologies Inc. has announced that Kia Georgia, Inc. has implemented the company’s Multi-Sensor Gateway screening technology.
The screening technology is designed to detect guns, knives and other prohibited items on people at large workplaces.
This deployment will be part of a larger service that includes Patriot One’s artificial intelligence (AI)-powered platform.
The implementation aims to further enhance the current guard service for the entire manufacturing facility.

facts: Production of the hybrid version of Kia’s top-selling model, the Sportage crossover, is shifting from South Korea to Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to see its $5 billion tariff bill ease due to this production shift.
The Georgia plant location is in Ellaville, Ga.
source_title: Kia shifts Sportage Hybrid production to Georgia plant - Automotive News

facts: Kia announced it will invest more than $200 million in its assembly plant in West Point, Georgia.
The investment is to begin production of Kia's electric EV9 SUV next year (2024).
Kia will add about 200 jobs to build the three-row SUV alongside four other models at the West Point, Georgia, plant.
The EV9 will be the first Kia electric vehicle assembled in North America.

Assembly in North America is a requirement for vehicles to be eligible for up to $7,500 in U.S. consumer electric vehicle tax credits under the $430 billion U.S. Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) signed in August 2022.
Electric carmaker Rivian Automotive is building a $5 billion plant in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On said in December they will build a new battery manufacturing plant in Georgia to supply Kia's U.S. assembly plants.
Hyundai and SK aim to begin operations at their joint facility in 2025.
Stakeholders estimate the Hyundai-SK project will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Georgia's Bartow County.
Hyundai separately broke ground in October on a $5.54 billion EV and battery plant in Georgia's Bryan County.
SK Innovation opened a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022 that is producing batteries for the Ford F-150 EV.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp said more than $22.7 billion have been invested in Georgia with more than 28,400 jobs created since 2020 in the EV supply chain.
source_title: Kia to invest $200 million in US plant, build EV9 SUV in 2024 By Reuters

facts: The Georgia Manufacturing Alliance partners with The Greener Manufacturing Show in Atlanta June 8-9, 2022.

The Greener Manufacturing Show brings together more than 400 sustainability leaders from a wide range of industrial sectors to explore industry innovations, market trends, regulatory updates, optimizing manufacturing processes, and R&D in green materials, green chemicals and greener manufacturing facilities.

Three dedicated conference tracks will target sustainability priority areas for manufacturing companies, including Innovations in sustainable materials, New greener chemicals, Targeting net-zero operations, and Tackling waste plastic and other valuable resources.
Delegates will have access to all 3 conferences as well as post-conference proceedings.
Several Georgia Manufacturing Alliance members will be exhibiting at this event.
Stuart Countess, President & Chief Executive Officer of Kia Georgia, Inc., will be speaking on June 8th at 9:25 am.

The Greener Manufacturing Show North America is the must-attend event for any sustainability-focused company looking to design and manufacture their products from more sustainable materials, limit and eliminate the use of toxic chemicals, and reduce the impact of industrial and manufacturing processes on the environment and global climate change.

Jason Moss, CEO of the Georgia Manufacturing Alliance, stated they are excited to be co-hosting this event to showcase some of the sustainability success stories in the Georgia manufacturing community and that industry leaders will have a chance to network with other thought leaders and learn best practices.
Guests can visit the exhibit hall free of charge, but registration is required.
Conference tickets are required to attend the educational sessions.
Conference attendees will get access to more than 80 speakers, all 3 conference sessions taking part alongside the co-located Plastic Waste Free World conference.
GMA members are eligible for special conference pricing.
The Georgia Manufacturing Alliance is a membership-based industry organization founded in 2008 to support Georgia's manufacturing community.
GMA provides monthly plant tours, educational sessions, tradeshows, and unique networking opportunities designed to help make profitable business connections for its members.
