facts: Shiroki North America is hiring for a Quality Engineer position in Smithville, TN 37166.
The work location is in person.
The role involves reviewing and disposing of customer returns daily to ensure timely resolution and documentation.
Responsibilities include developing, implementing, and sustaining quality assurance procedures across manufacturing and assembly operations.
The engineer leads root cause analysis and corrective/preventive actions (CAPA) for internal and external quality issues, including returned parts.
Monthly PPM reports for both customer and internal metrics must be prepared and presented.
Regular audits of production processes are conducted to ensure compliance with company, customer, and regulatory standards.
The position requires conducting monthly audits of MQC (Control Plans), Inspection Standards (IS), and FMEAs to ensure accuracy and effectiveness.
Direct customer support is provided through meetings, planning sessions, and documentation management.
The engineer collaborates with production teams to support part manufacturing and resolve quality-related issues.
Warranty parts are analyzed upon request to support the Warranty Quality Engineer in issue resolution.
Inspection standards are created and SQPR issues for service parts are addressed.
Daily Kaizen activities are reported and continuous improvement initiatives on the plant floor are supported.
The role leads daily Strike Team meetings to drive resolution of open quality concerns.
Partnerships with engineering and production teams focus on improving product design, manufacturability, and defect prevention.
Lab testing activities are supported, including assisting the Lab Technician as needed.
The engineer leads Scrap Reduction Team efforts to identify and resolve top scrap drivers in collaboration with production.
Continuous improvement ideas are proactively submitted and implemented to enhance quality and efficiency.
Qualifications require a Bachelor’s degree in a related discipline plus one year of experience, an Associate’s degree plus three years, or non-degreed status with five plus years in a related field.
Experience working with Tier 1 OEMs and launching new vehicle models is highly preferred.
Experience with gauges and gauge makers is required.
Strong knowledge of quality systems, standards, and methodologies including ISO 9001, IATF 16949, Six Sigma, and SPC is needed.
Proficiency with root cause analysis tools such as 8D, Fishbone, and FMEA is required.
The candidate must be able to coordinate multiple projects and programs.
Proficiency in Microsoft Office and quality management software is expected, with ERP system experience preferred.
Effective interpersonal communication, group presentation, and professional writing skills are necessary for creating reports, presentations, and emails.
Problem-solving and root cause analysis abilities are essential.
The ability to read 2-D prints and Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T) symbols is required.
Computer skills in Excel, PowerPoint, Word, etc., are needed along with time management capabilities.
Proper communication techniques are required to effectively deal with change and diverse people.
The role demands emotional resilience and the ability to withstand ongoing pressure.
Candidates must deal with difficult situations while maintaining performance and complete work in a timely and consistent manner.
The job type is full-time.
Benefits include 401(k), dental insurance, health insurance, life insurance, paid time off, vision insurance, 401(k) matching, employee assistance program, employee discount, and referral program.
Required experience includes three years in quality control and one year in quality audits.
source_title: 2 shiroki north america jobs in Smithville, TN, July 2026 | Glassdoor

facts: Shiroki North America, Inc. is a premier manufacturer of automobile seat tracks and adjusters, window regulators, doorframes and door sash sold to automotive OEMs and their top tier suppliers.
Key customers include Toyota Engineering and Manufacturing, Toyota Boshoku America, Nissan, Mitsubishi, Subaru-Indiana, Total Interior Systems-America, Johnson Controls, and NHK Spring Co., Ltd.
The firm's parent company is Shiroki Corporation of Japan, which has been a global leader in automotive manufacturing for half a century.

Shiroki North America initiated an in-house machine design/build engineering process based on standardization of tooling and die parts used in production jigs for mass manufacture of customer products.
The company orders fixed and configurable factory automation and motion control components directly from Illinois-based MISUMI USA to achieve its in-house design/build goals.

Brandon Johnson, Assistant General Manager of Engineering and Maintenance for Shiroki North America, notes that MISUMI components are utilized in virtually all new production jigs the company designs.
The largest quantity of MISUMI components purchased is used as spare and replacement parts for existing production jigs as part of ongoing quality control and maintenance operations.
Once engineers design a part to precise dimensions, it is standardized to save time and control costs, with MISUMI assigning a unique product number to facilitate re-ordering.
Due to MISUMI's very short lead and delivery times, Shiroki North America does not need to maintain an abundance of inventory for spare and replacement parts, which helps hold down costs.
The company's engineering team uses SolidWorks as the design platform when designing new production jigs for customer products.
MISUMI's ability to configure components to exacting dimensions reduces the need for time- and labor-intensive fabrication of custom parts, whether in-house or by an outside supplier.
MISUMI's CAD support offers automatic download of native CAD files, meaning engineers do not have to spend time drawing components or pay an outside supplier to draw them.
Electrical panels play an integral role in Shiroki North America's products, including window regulators and seat adjustment mechanisms.
The company uses MISUMI's print catalog and Web ordering system for electrical panel building components such as wire duct, terminals, and overall enclosures.
Shiroki North America's partnership with MISUMI enabled the company to build a sizable machine development group and manufacture machines in-house rather than outsourcing.
This initiative led to the largest cost savings in Shiroki North America's history.
Shiroki North America entered the U.S. market in 1988 to provide a convenient supply source of high quality auto parts and assemblies for U.S.-manufactured vehicles.
The company currently employs about 500 people.
Shiroki North America operates manufacturing plants with a total of 238,000 sq ft of space in Dalton, Georgia, and Smithville and Gordonsville, Tennessee.
The company continues to support operations in Japan, China, and Thailand.
source_title: Case Study: Shiroki North America eCatalog | MISUMI USA: Industrial Configurable Components Supply

facts: SHIROKI North America has an estimated annual revenue of $209.6M.
SHIROKI North America has an estimated revenue per employee of $412,500.
SHIROKI North America currently employs 508 people.
SHIROKI North America increased its employee count by 11% in the last year.
The company originated in 1988 as SW Manufacturing, Inc., serving as the first international manufacturing facility for Shiroki Corporation.
In 1995, the company expanded from one plant to two plants by adding a facility in Dalton, GA.
In late 2006, the company expanded to three plants by adding a facility in Gordonsville, TN.
In 2007, SW Manufacturing was renamed SHIROKI North America, Inc., and the Smithville, TN site was designated as the Headquarters for all North American business.
At the end of 2014, it was announced that AISIN SEIKI (Japan) would subsume Shiroki Corporation.
The acquisition by AISIN SEIKI was concluded in April 2016, making Shiroki Corporation a subsidiary of AISIN SEIKI.
SHIROKI North America operates manufacturing facilities in Smithville, TN; Dalton, GA; and Gordonsville, TN.

The company's executive structure includes positions such as EVP, General Manager, Human Resources Manager, Senior General Manager, PC Support Manager, Corporate Project Manager, Assistant General Manager Quality, Plant Manager, Manager of Production Control and Shipping & Receiving, and Corporate General Manager Human Resources.
SHIROKI North America conducts business across North America (United States, Canada, and Mexico), Europe, India, China, and Japan.
Key competitors in the market include Brose, Magna, Antolin, American Mitsuba, Technico Industries Ltd., Hi-Lex, Grupo Antolin, and Aisin Tianjin.
source_title: SHIROKI North America: Revenue, Competitors, Alternatives

facts: A $2.6 billion battery plant is under construction in Commerce, Ga.
The facility is located in Jackson County.
SK Battery America expects the plant to eventually create more than 2000 jobs building batteries for electric cars.
State and local governments agreed to provide $300 million in tax breaks and free land for the project.
Contractors for the plant have been flying in overseas workers rather than using Americans for basic construction tasks.
An SK Battery America contractor recruited workers from Micronesia, which is more than 7800 miles from Commerce, GA.
Residents on Glenn Fuller Circle in Commerce complained that construction workers are sleeping in single-family dwellings, with neighbors counting up to 14 men at one house.
Jackson County ordinances limit the number of unrelated people living in a home to four.
One five-bedroom, three-bath home on Glenn Fuller Circle was reported to have housed 14 men, though workers claimed only five lived there and others were just dropping by for lunch.
Workers from the Federated States of Micronesia signed six-month work contracts starting in July and perform labor duties at the site.
Residents of the Federated States of Micronesia do not need a visa to work in the United States.
SK Battery America stated it has already created more than 1000 American jobs during the construction phase.
The company told its contractors from the start to place a priority on hiring local American workers and believes they are appropriately doing so.
Neighbors have called the sheriff’s office multiple times due to loud drunken parties, loud music, and work vans driving across private lawns.
A renewed effort is underway to get Immigration and Customs Enforcement to launch a comprehensive investigation into the worker housing situation.
The FOX 5 I-Team observed that most construction workers at the site appeared to be Korean, with some sharing rental homes in neighborhoods around the plant.
Five Korean workers were found living in a Braselton home; they did not speak English and moved out the day after the news team's visit.
Congressman Doug Collins asked ICE and Customs and Border Protection to investigate SK Battery and its contractors, citing more than 30 houses rented to support the workforce.
Rep. Collins highlighted a separate situation in neighboring Banks County involving an abandoned chicken farm five miles from the construction site.
Homeowner Chris Phagan reported noticing a steady stream of work trucks heading to the abandoned farm starting in July.
Workers at the Banks County site told Phagan they were conducting secret welding training.
Banks County code enforcement officer Paul Ruark filed an incident report stating a manager said testing sites were set up to qualify welders.
Rep. Collins described the Banks County operation as an effort to replace American workers with Korean foreign nationals.
SK Battery stated it has found no information that such an off-site operation existed and clarified that any off-site workshops perform welding for the site, not training programs.
Work at the Banks County site ceased after local authorities checked on it.

Hart County resident and Plumbers, Pipefitters & HVACR Technicians Local Union 72 representative David Cagle insisted there are plenty of local union and non-union workers available for the construction jobs.
Cagle stated that contractors appear to be set up to work Koreans and emphasized that Americans can man the job.
In May, Customs and Border Protection stopped 33 Koreans at Hartsfield-Jackson airport for allegedly trying to use fake employment letters to get construction jobs at the plant.
The contractor involved in the May CBP incident was not named.
SK Battery announced it will now require all workers to provide daily documentation proving they are lawfully allowed to work in the country, with a policy of 'no documentation, no work'.
The company stated that no illegal workers are permitted on site and that contractors in violation will face severe sanctions, including possible termination.
SK Battery plans to conduct ongoing inspections to ensure contractor compliance with applicable laws.
Rep. Collins asked Customs to suspend visas for any SK employee traveling to Georgia from anywhere in the world until the illegal worker issue is fully investigated.
Neither CBP nor ICE commented on Rep. Collins' requests.
The mega-plant is connected to an International Trade Commission probe and is scheduled to be up and running by 2022.
source_title: SK Battery announces major change as homeowners complain some workers have become troublesome neighbors | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: The company promised 2,600 jobs once its factory was up and running.
No one made sure that at least some of the people actually building the plant would be from Georgia.
Each morning vans drop off Korean construction workers at the SK Battery America plant in Jackson County.
A FOX 5 I-Team investigation raised questions over who is currently benefiting from a $2.6 billion factory under construction in Jackson County.
Korean-owned SK Innovation chose Georgia after receiving one of the biggest tax relief packages the state has ever awarded: around $300 million in tax abatements and grants.
SK Battery America is building what will eventually be a $2.6 billion factory that will employ 2,600 workers.
Jackson County donated the land for the plant.
SK Battery America is a subsidiary of SKI.
When it is finished in 2022, SK Battery America promised 2,600 people will eventually be hired to build batteries for electric cars.
David Cagle with the Georgia Local Union 72 of plumbers, pipefitters, and HVACR technicians complained that Georgians have gotten the shaft over the SKI battery plant.
Cagle said when his people tried applying for construction jobs to build the plant itself, they were told nothing was available.
Cagle stated there are about 500 people out of work right now who could come up and be on this job and make a living, but instead Koreans are making a living.
David Cagle works for local Union 72.
He says it has been difficult for both union and non-union pipefitters, plumbers, electricians, or HVAC workers to get jobs at SK Battery.
On multiple visits to the job site, FOX 5 saw what bothered Cagle: Korean labor was everywhere.
When asked if SK Battery America needed Koreans to build something specific, Cagle answered no, stating electrical work, ductwork, pipework, pipe fitting, and pipe welding are all generic stuff.
The only legal way to bring in a foreign construction worker is with a special visa indicating no American can do the job or no American wants the job.
In May, Customs and Border Protection caught 33 Koreans trying to sneak into the country with fraudulent employment letters to work at a battery factory under construction.
The fraudulent letters claimed they had a 'specific skill set for specialized wo'.

In May, CBP announced 33 Koreans were caught at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport trying to sneak into the country for the sole purpose of working illegally at a construction company and battery plant in Georgia.
An SK Battery America spokesman confirmed the plant was theirs but pointed out they do not do the construction hiring.
SK Battery America stated it requires all contractors to ensure their workers at the site meet proper state and federal regulations.

After becoming aware of the issue in June, SK Battery America reiterated with its contractors that these guidelines must be followed and is cooperating with authorities to provide support in resolving any issues.
SK Battery America said more than 1,000 Americans have been hired since construction began in 2019.
Georgia congressman Doug Collins wrote a letter urging ICE to determine whether and to what extent there are foreign workers illegally employed at the battery plant.
Congressman Doug Collins still supports SKI but wrote a letter asking Immigration and Customs Enforcement to fully investigate complaints raised by Cagle, who lives in Collins' district.

Collins wrote that his understanding is that CBP determined this was not an isolated incident and that these Korean nationals were part of a larger scheme to illegally bring foreign workers into the United States.
Customs said the Koreans caught at Hartsfield were using fraudulent employment papers and were immediately sent home.
Congressman Doug Collins told the FOX 5 I-Team that this is not a matter of whether you are a good company or a bad company, but a matter of following the rules that apply to workers in the country.
FOX 5 watched the job site and saw many Korean construction workers getting bused to and from work.
Korean construction workers were seen sharing houses in various neighborhoods surrounding SK Battery.
Some shared rental homes in a Braselton neighborhood under construction, piling out of vans at the end of the workday still wearing reflective work vests.
In similar neighborhoods in Pendergrass and Jefferson, local union leaders recorded video of multiple Korean workers living in the same house.
FOX 5 found five Korean men sharing one house in Braselton.
When FOX 5 tried to ask them whether they were American or had a visa to work at the plant, none said they spoke English.
The day after the visit, neighbors say all five men quickly moved out.
Cagle complained that Georgia gave them a huge break and stated this is not a union-non-union issue but a Georgia issue about what is right for Georgians getting treated right.
The plant is still under construction.
The next phase, and another 430,000 square feet of space, is due to start later this year.
source_title: Calls for ICE to investigate who's building Korean battery plant in Jackson County | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Since the passage of the clean energy plan, 40 new clean energy projects have created 29,472 jobs and invested $23.12 billion in Georgia.
32 of Georgia’s 40 clean energy projects are located in Republican-held districts.
These 32 projects represent 82% of all clean energy projects in the state.
The projects in Republican-held districts account for $20.4 billion in investment and over 19,105 new jobs.
Despite these benefits going to communities across Georgia, state Republican politicians have voted against, voted to repeal, and consistently attacked the clean energy plan.
Governor Brian Kemp has welcomed 40 new clean energy projects to Georgia since the plan's passage, generating 29,472 new jobs and $23.12 billion in investments.
Georgia ranks first in the nation for clean energy jobs and is fast becoming a hub for EV manufacturing.
Governor Kemp has touted Georgia as the “electric mobility capital of America” and sought to make it the “EV capital of the nation.”
Kemp has repeatedly criticized the Inflation Reduction Act, stating it “hurt us in a lot of different ways” and “hurt Georgia more than it’s helping.”
Kemp has been “honored” and “excited” to welcome clean energy manufacturing operations, electric vehicle production, and manufacturing investments in the state.
Rep. Earl “Buddy” Carter represents Georgia’s 1st Congressional District, which has attracted eight new clean energy projects generating 7,222 new jobs and $7.81 billion in investments.
Carter voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and claimed it was “a bad deal for Georgia and threatens key economic investments that employ thousands of Georgians.”
Carter co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Carter celebrated Hyundai’s EV and battery plant in his district, saying it would “diversify and expand our economy.”
Rep. Drew Ferguson represents Georgia’s 3rd Congressional District, which has attracted four new clean energy projects generating 1,163 new jobs and $2.9 billion in investments.

Ferguson voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, falsely claiming it will raise taxes on hardworking Americans, result in fewer jobs, reduce lifesaving health care innovation, squeeze out American energy producers, spend over $350 billion on radical “Green New Deal” initiatives, and further the economic recession.
Ferguson co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Ferguson celebrated Daesol Ausys’ investment in a manufacturing facility for EV components and accessories.
Ferguson celebrated Kia’s investments to expand its electric SUV plant in his district, touting both projects’ job creation.
Rep. Richard McCormick represents Georgia’s 6th Congressional District, which has attracted one new clean energy project generating 200 new jobs and $19 million in investments.

While campaigning for his seat, McCormick criticized the clean energy plan, wrongly claiming it will not reduce inflation, re-ignite the economy, or make the country safer, stronger, or more prosperous, and that it raises taxes and boosts energy prices.
Rep. Andrew Clyde represents Georgia’s 9th Congressional District, which has attracted three new clean energy projects generating 304 new jobs and $67 million in investments.

Clyde voted against the clean energy plan and argued Democrats are doubling down on failed policies, wreaking havoc on Biden’s broken economy, burning through billions on liberal projects, contributing to national debt, and burdening Americans with tax hikes amidst economic crises.
Clyde co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Clyde said he was “proud” of SK Battery America’s investments in EV battery production in his district, touting the project’s job creation.
Rep. Mike Collins represents Georgia’s 10th Congressional District, which has attracted four new clean energy projects generating 410 new jobs and $141 million in investments.
Despite these benefits to his district, Rep. Collins voted to repeal the clean energy plan.
Rep. Barry Loudermilk represents Georgia’s 11th Congressional District, which has attracted three new clean energy projects generating 5,560 new jobs and $7.48 billion in investments.

Loudermilk voted against the clean energy plan and co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Rep. Rick Allen represents Georgia’s 12th Congressional District, which has attracted seven new clean energy projects generating 2,736 new jobs and $1.46 billion in investments.

Allen voted against the clean energy plan and argued it is a bloated Democrat spending bill that will raise taxes during a recession, while Biden and congressional Democrats focus on socialist Green New Deal initiatives instead of addressing the economic crisis.
Allen co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene represents Georgia’s 14th Congressional District, which has attracted two new clean energy projects generating 1,110 new jobs and $515 million in investment.

Despite voting against the clean energy plan and continuing to deny climate change, Greene told POLITICO that “we’re excited to have jobs” in response to QCells’ announcement to invest $2.5 billion in Georgia to expand its solar panel manufacturing plant in Dalton and build a new facility in Bartow County.
Greene co-wrote an op-ed with seven other Republican representatives claiming the law is “full of empty promises that harm some of Georgia’s flagship companies.”
Greene celebrated Qcell’s investment in a solar module factory in her district, saying “we’re excited to have jobs.”
source_title: Georgia’s Republican Politicians Said No, But Took The Dough | Climate Power

entity_name: Southern Battery Belt / Georgia EV Supply Chain
facts: Construction is humming at the Toyota Battery mega-site in Liberty, North Carolina as of early June.
Production at the Toyota Battery site is slated to begin in 2025.
By 2030, the 7 million-square-foot Toyota Battery complex will be fully operational with 14 production lines (10 for EVs and plug-in hybrids, 4 for hybrid electric vehicles).
The Toyota Battery site will operate 5,100 workers.
The total population of Liberty, North Carolina is 2,655.
The Toyota Battery mega-site carries a $13.9 billion price tag.
In Kings Mountain, North Carolina, two companies backed by a $90 million Department of Defense grant plan to drain a lake that was once an open-pit lithium mine and resume mining.
The Kings Mountain lithium project is expected to yield enough lithium for 1.2 million electric vehicles per year.
The mined lithium will be refined at a soon-to-open $1.3 billion facility in Chester County, South Carolina.
Much of the EV supply chain expansion is driven by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) passed in 2022.
The IRA provided grants and tax credits to encourage cleaner energy production.
State governments are adding record-breaking incentive deals including tax breaks, infrastructure development, and workforce development funding.
Georgia will give Hyundai up to $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other state assistance for its $7.9 billion capital investment in an EV and battery production complex outside Savannah.
The Georgia Hyundai EV and battery complex is forecast to employ up to 8,500 workers.
The IRA aims to compete with China, which currently produces the most EVs at the lowest prices.
Stephen Roach, former chair of Morgan Stanley Asia, told Axios in July: “We want to contain China, even if it comes at the expense of addressing the world’s climate change crisis.”
The Biden administration recently imposed massive tariffs on imports from China related to various aspects of EV production.
Since the IRA passage, companies have announced $110 billion in capital investments in new EV manufacturing.
Roughly half of the $110 billion investment and corresponding jobs are destined for the U.S. South according to the EV Jobs Hub.
Eighty-five percent of the EV investments are concentrated in right-to-work states.
S&P Global Market Intelligence estimates that as many as two-thirds of EV jobs may eventually be located in the South.
Michigan tops the list in EV capital investments, followed closely by Georgia ($23 billion), North Carolina ($20 billion), and Tennessee ($17 billion).
A joint venture between Ford and SK On is investing $5.6 billion to develop BlueOval City outside of Memphis, Tennessee.
BlueOval City is a six-square-mile complex where 6,000 workers will produce EVs and batteries.
The company boasts BlueOval City will be “among the largest auto manufacturing facilities in U.S. history.”
The BlueOval City site will receive batteries from BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky, a $5.8 billion project that will employ another 5,000 people.
Scout Motors, owned and operated by Volkswagen, recently broke ground on a $2 billion investment site outside Columbia, South Carolina.
Once operational in 2026, the Scout Motors site will employ 4,000 workers and produce 200,000 vehicles a year.
South Carolina is the country’s number one exporter of tires and home to BMW’s massive 11,000-worker plant in Spartanburg.
The BMW Spartanburg plant will soon begin receiving batteries from a nearby facility operated by AESC.
In April, AESC announced an expansion adding a second battery production facility for a total investment of $3.1 billion.
Auto and manufacturing industries are drawn to the South due to a pro-business climate with the lowest union density, lowest wages, and most stringent anti-labor laws in the country.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson told the Financial Times last October that massive labor stoppages in traditional union states will benefit Southern states as companies look to grow.
In March, Georgia passed a law forbidding companies that receive state incentives from recognizing a union by any method other than secret ballot elections.
South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster pledged in his State of the State address to “fight [unions] to the gates of hell.”

McMaster and five other Southern governors issued a joint statement claiming the UAW drive at Chattanooga Volkswagen “threatens the values we live by” and would “certainly put our states’ jobs in jeopardy.”
Nearly every new foreign-owned auto plant that has opened in the U.S. since the 1990s has chosen the South for its base of operations.
The Midwest’s share of automotive jobs fell from around 60 percent to around 45 percent, while the South’s share grew from 15 percent in 1990 to 30 percent today.
The South is home to 60 percent of Black workers along with a growing population of immigrant workers.
Nearly two-thirds of plant construction investments in Southern states are for manufacturing beyond EVs.
The UAW committed $40 million over the next two years to organize 13 non-union automakers largely concentrated in the South.
Workers at Ultium Cells in Lordstown, Ohio, which manufactures batteries for General Motors, recently won a strong agreement providing the UAW’s initial step into the EV industry.
Chattanooga Volkswagen workers won their union vote overwhelmingly in April.
Daimler Truck North America workers in North Carolina, Georgia, and Tennessee won a record contract several weeks after the VW vote.
Workers at Mercedes in Alabama came up short in their May vote on unionizing but continue organizing through worker committees and shop floor militancy.
Last year, workers in Georgia at Blue Bird, the country’s second-largest bus manufacturer which produces electric buses, won a victory to join the Steel Workers (USW).
Workers at electrified bus manufacturer New Flyer in Alabama voted yes this year for the Industrial Division of the Communications Workers (IUE-CWA).
The Southern Workers Assembly, a cross-sector rank-and-file network, recently launched a program aimed at getting workers jobs in strategic sectors to organize.
source_title: New ‘Battery Belt’ Opens Organizing Front in the South | Labor Notes

facts: SK Battery America (SKBA), a US subsidiary of SK On, is poised to start mass-producing electric vehicle batteries for Hyundai Motor from October at its Georgia, US, plant.
SKBA is transitioning its Georgia plant's production focus from Ford to Hyundai Motor.
The strategic shift aligns with recent US policy changes and Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) benefits.
source_title: SK Battery America gears up for EV battery production for Hyundai - Automotive Technology Insight | Forecasts | Industry News | Supply Chain

facts: BlueOval SK Battery Park is a $5.8 billion joint venture project between Ford Motor Co. and South Korea-based SK On.
The 1,500-acre site is located just off Interstate 65 south of Elizabethtown in Hardin County, Kentucky, specifically in the rural village of Glendale.
The land was originally purchased and gifted to the county from the state in 2001 to become an industrial manufacturing hub.
Former Kentucky Governor Paul Patton previously hoped the site would become home to Hyundai, but that deal fell through.
Former Kentucky Governor Steve Beshear also had hopes for the site that never materialized.
The project was announced in 2021 after more than two decades of local preparation.
Construction is on pace with operations expected to start next year relative to the article's March 3, 2023 publication date.
U.S. Secretary of Transportation Pete Buttigieg visited the BlueOval SK Battery Park facility in Glendale, Kentucky on March 3, 2023.
The facility is expected to create 5,000 jobs for Kentuckians and generate a direct payroll of $265 million in 2026.
In October, Ford announced it would delay production at the second battery plant on the site because EV market demand was not rising as quickly as initially anticipated.
Andy Games, president and COO of the Elizabethtown-Hardin County Industrial Foundation, stated that the production delay gave them time to take a deep breath.
The facility is described as the 10th largest manufacturing facility in the world and will house twin battery plants.
Production at the site is expected to gear up in 2025.
Within 18 months of the battery park announcement, 90% of the remaining available industrial park land managed by the foundation was sold to Korean supplier companies.
Hardin County anticipates a population increase of more than 22,000 residents by 2030, with the majority moving to Elizabethtown and pushing it to become the fifth largest city in Kentucky.
A study indicates the county will need more than 8,800 new housing units to support the growing population.
To support construction workers on the battery park project, including more than 2,800 traveling workers, the county built several RV parks for temporary housing.
In the areas immediately surrounding Glendale, six residential duplexes and 64 new single-family dwellings were permitted in the year of the article.
There are seven new subdivisions in development across the county, including the county's largest with 419 units.
In a typical year, the county sees less than 200 new residential developments.
The city of Elizabethtown receives requests for roughly 150 housing units annually on average.
From 2022 through the present, the city has seen around 2,000 new housing units permitted.
Local officials are working to protect Historic Downtown Glendale, which is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The county and state have developed better transportation routes for the twin battery plants, including roundabouts, additional funding, and projects to reduce commuter traffic effects on local roads.
BlueOval SK Battery Park opened its job training center on Wednesday, May 8, 2024.

Joe Reverman, director of planning and development for the city of Elizabethtown, stated that locals knew something was going on at the site for over 20 years and that the city had pieces in place to handle growth.
Andy Games noted that developers from Michigan to Florida to South Korea are calling about ground due to the large influx of people expected.
The city has entered a new phase of development with more commercial developments, restaurants, and grocery store interest.
Andy Games remarked that "The United States of America has figured out where [Hardin County] is."
source_title: BlueOval SK Battery Park spurs massive growth in Hardin County – Here’s what it means.

facts: Nearly 1,000 workers were laid off at SK Battery America’s manufacturing plant in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs mark one of the most significant workforce reductions yet in the U.S. electric vehicle supply chain.
According to a WARN notice, 958 employees — about 37% of the facility’s workforce — were let go.
Their final working day was in early March.
The plant will continue operating with roughly 1,600 remaining workers.
The $2.6 billion facility opened in January 2022.
It quickly became a cornerstone of Georgia’s push to become an EV manufacturing hub.
It notably supplied batteries for Ford’s F-150 Lightning.
SK said the cuts were meant to “align operations to market conditions,” reflecting a broader recalibration happening across the auto industry.
Automakers are pulling back from aggressive EV targets they pledged only a few years ago.
In December, Ford announced it would cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning and pivot to an extended-range version instead.
That decision rippled across suppliers like SK.
Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis have reneged on factory expansions, delayed new EV launches, and shifted investment toward hybrids and plug-in hybrids.
Hybrids are seen as more palatable to mainstream consumers worried about range and charging access.
Industry analysts describe the past year as a period of retrenchment.
North America has seen EV plans delayed or walked back, even as global markets continue to electrify.
While EVs remain part of long-term strategies, automakers are adjusting to slower-than-expected adoption at home.
A key turning point came when the $7,500 federal EV tax credit expired.
EVs made up nearly 12% of the U.S. market last September — a record high.
That share fell to 6% by January, with sales plunging 20% month over month.
The drop caught many companies off guard.
Congress has since eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers’ purchases of new or used EVs.
The administration has moved to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas standards, reducing regulatory pressure on automakers to accelerate electrification.
Analysts say the EV market had been partially propped up by mandates and incentives, and without them, demand is now settling at a lower “natural” level.
For suppliers like SK, that translates directly into excess capacity and layoffs.
Georgia was one of the biggest beneficiaries of the EV manufacturing boom during the Biden administration, attracting tens of billions of dollars in announced projects.
SK and Ford had previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S., though that joint venture ended in December.
The state is still home to major projects, including SK and Hyundai’s $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville.
The nearly 1,000 layoffs signal that the pace of growth may not match earlier projections.
Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff criticized the cuts, arguing they show how policy changes are affecting local jobs.
For workers and local communities, the EV slowdown isn’t abstract — it’s economic.
While the U.S. market cools, global EV adoption continues to accelerate.
In 2025, global EV registrations rose 20% to 20.7 million vehicles.
China alone reached 12.9 million registrations, and Europe recorded 4.3 million.
By contrast, U.S. EV sales growth was essentially flat.
In 39 countries, EVs now exceed 10% of new car sales, including emerging markets such as Vietnam and Thailand.
The U.S., however, remains below that threshold.
Analysts warn that slower domestic production can delay improvements in battery technology and increase the risk that the next generation of automotive value creation happens elsewhere.
China has heavily subsidized its EV industry, to the tune of an estimated $230 billion from 2009 to 2021, and now dominates many global supply chains.
As American automakers slow investment, they risk ceding ground in a sector widely seen as the future of mobility.
Despite the layoffs and retrenchment, many analysts caution against declaring the EV era over.
Even amid the slowdown, EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025.
Industry observers describe the current moment as a recalibration after an unusually fast burst of investment and optimism.
Consumers still report high satisfaction with EV ownership, and upcoming lower-cost models, including new entries around $30,000, could expand the market in coming years.
The nearly 1,000 layoffs at SK’s Georgia plant stand as a stark reminder that the U.S. EV transition is no longer a straight-line ascent.
As automakers scale back plans and policymakers reshape incentives, the future of American battery manufacturing, and the jobs tied to it, hangs in the balance.
source_title: 1,000 Workers Laid Off at Leading Battery Plant as Automakers Scale Back EV Plans – Blusher

facts: Auto manufacturing is in flux, and Michigan is in a fierce fight with southern states to keep its signature industry.
Ultium Cells Delta Township will be GM’s third battery plant, in addition to ones in Lordstown, Ohio, and Spring Hill, Tennessee.
Ford announced in 2021 it was building $11.4 billion in EV campuses in Kentucky and Tennessee.
Other states, such as Tennessee and Georgia, have been developing land and offering it to companies among big subsidy deals for 20 years.
source_title: Michigan has spent $1B on EV, battery plants. So far, they’ve created 200 jobs - Bridge Michigan

facts: More than 30 Korean employees on business trips to the United States were detained by immigration authorities at a joint battery factory operated by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution in Georgia.

U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) conducted a raid Thursday morning at the HL-GA Battery Company construction site in Ellabell, near Savannah, Georgia.
Most of those detained were team leaders or higher-level employees from LG Energy Solution's headquarters in Korea.

The detained workers reportedly entered the United States under B-1 business visas or through the Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA) and were officially registered as business travelers.
Authorities suspect they were directly involved in installing factory equipment and preparing for production, which are activities not permitted under those visa categories.

ICE and HSI were initially investigating the presence of undocumented foreign workers near the construction site before discovering the Korean nationals engaging in work that allegedly violated their visa terms.
The Korean Embassy in Washington confirmed the detainment of more than 30 Korean nationals.
LG Energy Solution spokesperson stated: “Construction has been suspended to cooperate with the investigation, and we plan to fully cooperate with authorities.”
LG Energy Solution added: “We are still working to confirm the exact number of detainees and whether they remain in custody.”
In September 2020, 13 Korean workers employed at an SK Battery America plant were detained by ICE for illegal employment after entering on ESTA.
In May 2020, another 33 Koreans were denied entry and deported at the airport.

Rep. Doug Collins (Republican from Georgia) criticized Korean companies for illegally hiring their own nationals instead of local residents and called for a full investigation by ICE and Customs and Border Protection.
SK Battery America responded to past incidents by tightening worker identification procedures and increasing local hiring efforts.

A business community source noted: “Obtaining employment visas such as H-1B can take several months in the United States. Some companies resort to sending staff on short-term business or ESTA visas to fill urgent labor shortages.”
The same source stated: “Whether the workers’ activities align with their visa status has become a key issue in crackdowns. Coordination between governments and systemic reform is needed.”
The Trump administration has ramped up immigration enforcement following its return to power.
ICE reported that in the first 200 days of the new administration, approximately 359,000 undocumented immigrants were arrested, and about 332,000 were deported.
source_title: U.S. detains over 30 Korean workers tied to Hyundai–LG battery facility in Georgia

facts: American managers John Brueshaber, Desmond Salmon, and Russell Bratcher filed a federal lawsuit against SK Battery America, Inc. on March 19, 2026.
The lawsuit was filed in the United States District Court for the Northern District of Georgia.
SK Battery America is a Delaware corporation operating a manufacturing facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The plaintiffs allege intentional discrimination based on race and Korean ethnic ancestry regarding pay and promotions at the Commerce facility.
The complaint claims violations of 42 U.S.C. § 1981, which prohibits racial discrimination in contracts.

Plaintiffs assert that Korean managers of Korean ethnic ancestry occupy a disproportionate share of high-compensation managerial positions despite performing substantially similar managerial functions as American employees.
The complaint states that compensation levels are determined by centralized executive management rather than individual supervisors.

Both Korean and American managers operate within the same grade band system, report through the same chain of command, are evaluated under identical performance metrics, and are subject to the same centralized compensation approval authority.
Despite identical structures, plaintiffs allege that Korean managers receive substantially higher base salaries and supplemental compensation than their American counterparts.

Specific examples in the suit note that lower-level Korean workers supervised by the plaintiffs receive base salaries exceeding those of their direct supervisors by amounts ranging from $80,000 to more than $150,000 annually.

The suit alleges additional remuneration forms such as company-provided housing, vehicle allowances, tax equalization payments, guaranteed bonuses, and other financial benefits are systematically provided to Korean employees but denied to American employees performing comparable work.
Plaintiffs argue these compensation disparities cannot be explained by job function, tenure, performance qualifications, or legitimate expatriate assignment status.

The plaintiffs claim that upon information and belief, compensation disparities were based on Korean ancestry rather than job function or any legitimate expatriate assignment or temporary relocation status.

Beyond pay differences, the complaint alleges retaliation against Brueshaber and Salmon after they raised concerns about discriminatory practices in writing to executive leadership around December 24, 2025.
Within weeks of their complaints, both men reportedly experienced exclusion from management meetings and communications as well as removal from supervisory authority.

The lawsuit outlines counts including race and ethnicity discrimination in compensation, discrimination in terms and conditions of employment, discrimination in job assignments, pattern or practice of race/ethnicity discrimination, and retaliation for protected activity.
Plaintiffs claim they have suffered economic losses as well as emotional distress due to these alleged violations.

The plaintiffs seek compensatory damages for lost wages and emotional harm, punitive damages against SK Battery America for willful conduct with reckless indifference to their rights, and injunctive relief requiring changes to company policies or practices deemed discriminatory.

The complaint notes that charges have been filed with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) related to these issues but clarifies that administrative exhaustion is not required under Section 1981.
Plaintiffs reserve the right to amend their complaint should they receive Notices of Right to Sue from the EEOC.
While the initial filing does not formally seek class certification at this stage, it reserves such rights pending further discovery.
The document asserts that the defendant's discriminatory compensation practices are systemic across departments at its Commerce facility.
Legal representation for the plaintiffs is provided by Larry A. Pankey of Pankey & Horlock LLC in Atlanta, Georgia (Georgia Bar No. 560725).
The case is identified as Civil Action File No. 1:26-cv-01516-JPB-LRS.
source_title: American managers accuse SK Battery America of race and ethnicity discrimination in pay and promotions - Georgia Courts Daily

facts: Ford has officially broken ground on its $5.8 billion Blue Oval SK Battery Park in Glendale, Kentucky.
The project is a joint venture between Ford and SK (SK On).
The facility consists of two massive battery manufacturing plants capable of more than 80-gigawatt hours of production annually.
Production at the park is scheduled to begin in 2025.
The project will create 5,000 new jobs in the region upon commencement of production.
New workers will receive training at a new institution at Elizabethtown Community and Technical College before starting operations.
The plant is expected to help increase Ford’s electric vehicle production output to two million units per year.
SK On President and CEO Jee Dong-seob stated that the park will be at the core of the electrification of the North American auto market.
SK On President and CEO Jee Dong-seob added that the company expects its leadership in the global electric vehicle market to be solidified through BlueOval SK alongside Ford.
Construction is currently on schedule with significant progress being made at the site.
Site preparation has involved moving 4.3 million cubic yards of soil.
Workers have laid 283,000 tons of stone at the construction site.
Construction crews have poured 66,000 cubic yards of concrete for the facility.
The project has installed 3,300 tons of rebar ties to reinforce the concrete structure.
Workers have installed 1,300 deep foundations at the site.
Construction crews have erected 7,900 tons of structural steel for the buildings.
Ford is also developing a separate $5.6 billion battery manufacturing plant in West Tennessee known as BlueOval City.
The BlueOval City project in West Tennessee will also produce a future electric pickup truck model.
Ford broke ground on the BlueOval City project in late September.
source_title: Ford breaks ground on $5.8B Blue Oval SK Battery Park in Kentucky

facts: Ford’s BlueOval SK electric vehicle (EV) battery plant in Tennessee is ready to start trial operations next month.
The trial run will ensure that Ford’s TN EV battery plant can produce high-quality cells efficiently and consistently.
The BlueOval SK plant in Tennessee is part of a $11.4 billion joint venture between legacy automaker Ford and South Korea-based SK On.
Besides Ford, SK On is also working with Hyundai on a battery factory in Georgia.
The South Korean company aims to build a battery facility in Georgia with a production capacity of 35 GWh.
SK On reportedly plans to send production personnel to Tennessee to oversee BlueOval SK’s trial operations.
The South Korean battery supplier’s production personnel will primarily focus on the plant’s electrode-related processes, including assembly, activation, and battery packs.
Ford and SK On have three BlueOval SK battery plants under construction in the United States.
One BlueOval SK battery plant in Kentucky and the one in TN are expected to start commercial operations in 2025.
Ford and SK On’s second plant in Kentucky is slated to begin operations in 2026.
The three battery plants are expected to generate a total production capacity of 127 GWh.
The BlueOval SK plant in Tennessee has a total production capacity of 45 GWh.
Ford’s TN battery plant will be part of BlueOval City, which is comprised of an electric vehicle facility and a parts production factory.
State officials recently suggested that Ford was considering Shelbyville for a $400 million car parts factory with a focus on EV components.
source_title: Ford’s EV battery plant in TN ready to start trial operations next month

entity_name: SK On and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Battery Plant
canonical_name: SK On-Hyundai Motor Joint Battery Plant
facts: The Georgia Economic Developers Association announced on November 13 that the joint battery plant project of SK On and Hyundai Motor Group won the "Deal of the Year" award in the Large Community category.
The Georgia Economic Developers Association initiated its "Deal of the Year" award in 2008.
The association annually selects award nominees based on their economic impact on Georgia, contract finalization complexity, and required expertise.
SK On and Hyundai Motor Group disclosed a collaborative investment of USD 5 billion (approximately KRW 6.5 trillion).
The investment is for constructing a 35 GWh battery production plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The joint plant has a targeted operational commencement date in the second half of 2025.
SK On previously won the "Deal of the Year" award in the Mid-size Community category in 2019.
The 2019 award was for SK On's battery production plant, "SK Battery America," located in Commerce, Georgia.
SK On plays a crucial role in establishing the EV industry belt in the southeastern U.S.
Commissioner Pat Wilson of the Georgia Department of Economic Development highlighted SK On's pivotal role in the EV industry belt.
Pat Wilson stated that SK Battery America was the first mover in creating an American battery facility.
Pat Wilson emphasized that SK's facility opening marked the beginning of an evolving ecosystem for battery production in the U.S.
SK On secured the top position on Fortune magazine's "Change the World" list of innovative companies for its leadership in North American electrification.
SK On currently operates two battery production plants in Georgia.
SK On is actively constructing a joint battery plant with Hyundai Motor Group.
SK On is simultaneously building three additional battery plants: two in Kentucky and one in Tennessee.
The three additional battery plants are being built in collaboration with Ford Motor Company.
The completion of the Kentucky and Tennessee plants in 2025 is expected to bring SK On's total U.S. battery production capacity to over 180 GWh.
source_title: SK On and Hyundai Motor Joint Battery Plant Clinches 'Deal of the Year' Award - Korea IT Times

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On EV Battery Facility
canonical_name: SK On / Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture EV Battery Plant
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On selected a site in Bartow County for a new electric vehicle (EV) battery manufacturing facility.
The new facility will supply Hyundai Motor Group's plants in the U.S.
Stakeholders estimate the project will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4-5 billion of investment in Bartow County.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S., with details still in development.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia's ever-growing automotive industry.
SK On was established in 2021 as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary branch of SK Innovation.
SK On currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce.
In July, SK Battery America announced it would partner with the Work for Warriors Georgia program to hire veterans, servicemembers, and their families at their Georgia operations.
The new facility is subject to execution of relevant agreements and HMG and SK On's final board decisions.
The facility will be located at Bartow Centre, a zoned manufacturing and industrial site located on Highway 411.
The project is aiming to begin operations in 2025.
Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision of Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
Taylor thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre Industrial property and noted the project will establish Bartow in Georgia's EV ecosystem in a significant way.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was pursued in partnership with Georgia EMC, Development Authority of Bartow County, the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that HMG and SK have been pioneering partners for Georgia for decades as major drivers for Georgia's automotive renaissance and the first Korean manufacturer to locate in the state, respectively.

Wilson noted that supporting cooperation across the growing EV ecosystem is creating a fully integrated supply chain for automotive OEMs while connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers to close the loop on battery manufacturing.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric transportation industry due to its prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects announced in the state since 2020 total approximately $17 billion in investment and more than 22,800 new jobs in Georgia.
In May, Hyundai Motor Group separately announced plans to invest $5.54 billion in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, an EV manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on the Metaplant America facility in October with Governor Kemp and other state, local, and federal officials.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with about 250,000 employees worldwide and mobility brands including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
SK On was launched as an independent company in 2021 after SK Innovation split off its battery business.
SK On aims to become a world leader in the clean energy industry by leveraging its global production base and R&D capabilities.
SK On is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea, and has battery plants currently operating or in construction across the United States, Europe, and Asia.
source_title: EV's Are Good For American Workers:Hyundai Motor Group and SK On To Build EV Battery Facility in Bartow County

facts: Hyundai announced that its popular Ioniq 5 SUV will be the first electric vehicle to roll off the production line of its new $7.5 billion manufacturing campus in Georgia.
The Ioniq series has been a hit in the US and globally, pulling the rug out from under EV market naysayers despite some glitches last year.
EV sales began to pick up in January 2024 and momentum has continued.
Hyundai stated in a June 18 press release that “The Hyundai IONIQ 5’s sales numbers prove its popularity.”
The Ioniq 5 is Hyundai’s highest volume EV, with sales up 43% so far in 2024.
Ioniq 5 sales increased by 82% in May alone according to the June 18 press release.
In a June 18 announcement, Hyundai affirmed that its new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will host production of the 2025 Ioniq 5.
The 2025 Ioniq 5 model has not yet had an official unveiling in North America.
HMGMA is currently training and preparing to begin production in the fourth quarter of this year (2024).
The new plant will produce all trim lines except IONIQ 5 N.
The plant will eventually be the sole facility building IONIQ 5 models for the U.S. market.
The Metaplant came to attention in 2022 as the nexus for Hyundai’s growing EV supply chain web in Georgia.
Hyundai is investing $7.59 billion to develop the new campus located in Ellabell.
The investment expects to create 8,500 new direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia that will employ 3,500 workers.
A Center for Automotive Research study calculated Hyundai’s electric vehicle investments in Georgia will create almost 40,000 jobs.
The same study estimated an annual figure for individual earnings of $4.6 billion.
The state of Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
This follows an announcement that Hyundai will invest an additional $2 billion at its EV complex in Georgia.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the originally announced $1.8 billion deal last year.
Only $2.75 million of the incentive increase represents additional cash from the state; the rest comes from increases in tax breaks.
Georgia has a longstanding school-to-factory worker training and hiring program attractive to manufacturers.
Georgia is a “right to work” state under regulations passed in 1947, making it difficult for workers to unionize.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) states the state is rapidly developing as the capital of EV manufacturing for the nation.
GDEcD notes Kia’s facility in West Point will begin making the company’s new EV9 model this year.
Rivian announced plans for a $5 billion factory in Georgia back in 2021 but switched focus to Illinois after site preparation, though characterized as temporary.

GDEcD is targeting the whole EV ecosystem, noting expansion across cathode and anode manufacturing, chemical and mineral solutions, battery manufacturing, metals and aluminum manufacturing, and battery recycling.
Energy storage startup Freyr Battery Inc. announced relocating its home base back to the US from Europe earlier this year.
Freyr cited global tax developments and U.S. incentive programs for battery manufacturers as a key advantage of the move.
The redomiciling will help facilitate construction of Freyr’s new Giga America factory in Coweta County, Georgia.
Freyr announced the site selection for Giga America in 2022.
The initial phase of Giga America is planned to be a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh based on the next-generation of 24M’s U.S.-based SemiSolidTM production platform.
Initial projected capital cost for the Giga America phase is $1.7 billion.
Freyr name-checked the Inflation Reduction Act as a key element supporting the project.
Freyr expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade due to the IRA.
The total financial incentive package for Giga America includes assistance from the State of Georgia and Coweta County plus expected production tax credits from the IRA.
Freyr indicated in a corporate update earlier this year that plans for the Giga America battery factory are moving forward.
source_title: Money Talks: The Electric Vehicle Revolution Trumps Red State Politics - Infralog

facts: Georgia officials want the state to be a leader in manufacturing electric vehicles, which could very well be the next big thing.
When it comes to seeing EVs on the state's highways and backroads, though, Georgia's got a long way to go.
source_title: Georgia tries to become leader in an industry that's no sure thing | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: Solvay SA is seeking U.S. government protection and import limits for its new polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) plastics materials plant in Georgia.

The U.S. International Trade Commission (USITC) announced on Feb. 14 that it is investigating a patent infringement complaint brought by Solvay against several Chinese competitors for allegedly exporting PVDF resin to the U.S. in violation of Solvay patents.
Several Chinese firms, including Sinochem Lantian Co. Ltd., have disputed Solvay's patent infringement allegations.

Solvay and its spinoff company Syensqo argued in a petition to the U.S. ITC that import restrictions are needed to protect a PVDF thermoplastic resin plant being built in Augusta, Ga., which is using a grant of nearly $180 million from the U.S. Department of Energy.
Solvay stated that with the DOE's help, Syensqo has invested substantial resources for the domestic production and distribution of its patent-protected, battery-grade PVDF called Solef.

Solvay noted these significant investments in Solef's development and the domestic industry were made with the understanding that the patent protecting the technology would prevent competitors from unfairly importing infringing products.

Solvay said the planned PVDF resin plant in Alpharetta is part of $5 billion in U.S. government grants to develop the battery supply chain and electrical grid under the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, signed by President Joe Biden in 2021.
Solvay stated that protecting the complainant's investment in the Georgia plant will promote the substantial domestic production planned by the complainants and their federal and local partners.

The USITC investigation is a Section 337 review examining whether the Chinese firms violated a Solvay patent with their materials and seeks to limit imports of the allegedly infringing material in the U.S.

Besides Sinochem Lantian in Hangzhou, the U.S. ITC complaint lists Inner Mongolia 3F Wanhao Fluorochemical Industry Co. Ltd. in Fengzhen; Zhejiang Juhua Co. Ltd. in Quzhou; Zhejiang Fluorine Chemical New Material Co. Ltd. in Shaoxing; and China Hubei Fluorine New Materials Co. Ltd. in Qianjiang.
The U.S. ITC accepted Solvay's complaint but stated it has not made any rulings on the merits of the case.
Sinochem Lantian argued that Solvay was venue shopping its intellectual property complaint after related patent infringement claims by Solvay and its business units in China were not going well.
Sinochem wrote in a U.S. ITC filing that the Chinese litigations are struggling and ending badly for the complainants.

Sinochem noted that in recognition of the struggling Chinese cases, complainants have started shopping their claims to the ITC with patent infringement claims on a related patent having the same claim limitations as the one unsuccessfully litigated in China.
Solvay stated that the patents at issue in the U.S. and China are different.
Sinochem argued that Solvay is trying to protect a non-existent domestic U.S. industry, since the Georgia plant is not yet open and may not finish before 2028.
Zhejiang Juhua told the trade commission that restricting PVDF imports would harm U.S. consumers by exacerbating shortages and raising prices.
Juhua stated there had been a persistent shortage of PVDF in the U.S. in recent years, which is projected to continue in the future.
Juhua warned that the requested remedial orders would distort prices and competition, negatively impacting U.S. consumers.
Juhua stated that limits on PVDF resin would make other U.S. industries less competitive, including electric car manufacturing.
Juhua noted that higher battery prices would drive up EV costs, further deceasing the competitiveness of EVs manufactured in the U.S.
Juhua added that a continued shortage or persistently high cost of PVDF would drive up costs for medical devices and water purification plants, negatively impacting U.S. consumers.
Solvay countered that PVDF shortages are not a real concern because there are other large makers of PVDF in the U.S. and globally who can supply materials to EV manufacturers and other companies.
Solvay warned that if the U.S. government does not act, its Chinese rivals could gain an unfair advantage in the U.S. market.
Solvay stated it is expected that the Chinese firms, if allowed to continue their infringement, could gain an unfair competitive advantage in the domestic market in the near term.
The original complaint was brought to the U.S. ITC by Solvay Specialty Polymers, USA LLC in Alpharetta, Syensqo SA of Brussels, and Solvay Specialty Polymers Italy SpA.
Executives held a groundbreaking for the Georgia plant making PVDF resins in 2024.
source_title: Solvay wants US protection for Georgia EV plastics materials plant > Notice | 우진플라임

facts: Stryten Energy is a leading U.S.-based provider of advanced energy storage solutions.
The company delivers innovative battery technologies for transportation, motive, essential power, and military sectors.
Stryten Energy is actively expanding its battery energy storage offerings.
Expansion efforts focus on grid resilience, renewable integration, and advanced manufacturing to meet rising demand for sustainable power solutions.
The company launched modular Lead and Lithium BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems) for commercial and utility-scale applications.
Stryten Energy is making strategic efforts to expand market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.
Stryten Energy delivers comprehensive energy storage solutions tailored for commercial, industrial, utility, and government applications.
Their product portfolio includes modular Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), intelligent chargers, and energy management software.
Their solutions support grid resilience, backup power, and integration with renewables.
Stryten maintains vertically integrated U.S. manufacturing to ensure secure sourcing and sustainability for its customers.
The company partners with leading organizations to power vehicles, microgrids, warehouses, and critical infrastructure worldwide.
Stryten Energy is located in Alpharetta, Georgia, United States.
The company was founded in 2020.
Stryten Energy operates as a private company.
source_title: Stryten Energy | Advanced Energy Storage Solutions for In...

facts: Stryten Energy is based in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company generates over $757.4 million in revenue.
Stryten operates as a national energy partner focused on more than just hardware.
Its mission is to deliver sustainable, efficient, and resilient energy storage solutions that support America’s power transformation.
The company offers a portfolio of energy storage technologies critical for transportation systems, military equipment, telecommunications, and factories.
Stryten specializes in lead batteries, which are long-lasting and recyclable, ideal for backup systems and transportation.
Stryten specializes in lithium batteries, known for excellent energy density, perfect for fast-paced and mobile applications.
Stryten specializes in flow batteries, an emerging technology for long-duration energy storage and grid energy storage capacities.
The company deploys energy storage systems to stabilize network loads and balance renewable inputs for the manufacturing industry.
Stryten provides high-capacity battery rooms for auto factories and efficient mobile power for robotic systems.
The company engineers advanced battery designs for EV start-stop batteries, charging station energy support, and mobile backup systems for service vehicles.
Stryten supports vehicle electrification and charging infrastructure to enable widespread EV adoption while enhancing grid reliability.
The company is investing in utility-scale and distributed grid storage to collect excess energy during peak generation times and release it when demand surges or supply drops off.
Stryten provides energy banks for traffic signals, communications towers, and subway systems to ensure essential services stay active during storms, surges, or brownouts.
The company operates closed-loop recycling programs that recover metals and materials from old lead batteries to reuse them in new ones.
Stryten participates heavily in the lead-acid battery recycling ecosystem, which has a recycling rate over 99% according to the EPA.
Stryten has headquarters in Alpharetta, Georgia, and several plants across the U.S.
The company employs thousands of Americans and supports skilled labor and high-tech manufacturing jobs.
Stryten is creating job pathways in welding, engineering, robotics, and recycling.
Projected revenue for Stryten Energy is $950 million (Est.) for 2025 and $1.2 billion (Est.) for 2030.
The global battery market is expected to grow to $310 billion by 2030.
Stryten supplies strategic battery systems to defense agencies, including keeping military bases running and arming mobile defense vehicles.
Reliable domestic battery suppliers like Stryten reduce dependency on foreign technologies for national security and strategic resilience.
source_title: Powering America’s Energy Future With Stryten | Distrilist

facts: Battery manufacturer Stryten Energy announced plans to add 10 GW of domestic energy storage capacity across its 11 U.S. manufacturing and battery component plants.
The expansion brings Stryten’s total domestic manufacturing capacity to 24 GW.
The expanded capacity will increase the domestic supply of batteries for long-duration energy storage applications.
The expanded capacity will increase the domestic supply of batteries for military applications.
The expanded capacity will increase the domestic supply of batteries for transportation applications.
The expanded capacity will increase the domestic supply of batteries for material-handling applications.
Existing advanced manufacturing tax credits supported Stryten's new investment.
The Georgia-based company’s investment will fund its facilities in Georgia, Arkansas, Kansas, Iowa, Ontario, Indiana, Missouri, Pennsylvania, Wisconsim and New York.

Mike Judd, president and CEO of Stryten Energy, stated that the path to American energy security must rely upon domestic companies like Stryten that manufacture batteries to keep supply chains running and critical infrastructure supplied with backup power.
Stryten operates a domestic, vertically integrated supply chain that provides battery components to its factories and the broader U.S. battery manufacturing industry.
Stryten’s energy solutions are built in the U.S. mostly using domestic components, though it uses some imported components.
Stryten has a suite of domestically manufactured battery solutions that use advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow battery technology.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC announced a new plan expected to expand its domestic manufacturing capacity to 24 Gigawatts.
The expansion focuses on increasing production across the company's 11 U.S. manufacturing and battery component plants.
More than 2,500 people currently work at these U.S. facilities producing batteries for military and government, data centers, grid storage, automotive, and material handling applications.
The new investment is supported by existing advanced manufacturing production tax credits to accelerate capacity growth while supporting the domestic industrial base.
Mike Judd, President and Chief Executive Officer of Stryten Energy, stated that the company is committed to growing its domestic energy storage capacity to serve U.S. customers and partners.
The expansion will increase the domestic supply of batteries for military, long-duration energy storage, transportation, and material-handling applications.
Stryten advances energy storage in the U.S. with a suite of domestically manufactured battery solutions using advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow battery technology.
The company operates plants in Kansas, Georgia, Iowa, Virginia, and Arkansas.
Plans to increase gigawatt production capacity will bring hundreds of high-paying manufacturing jobs and deliver a positive economic impact to surrounding communities.
Stryten operates a domestic, vertically integrated supply chain that provides battery components to its factories and the broader U.S. battery manufacturing industry.
The company's plants in Pennsylvania, New York, Indiana, Missouri, and Wisconsin ensure a reliable U.S. source for critical materials needed to manufacture batteries.
The investment plan includes increasing domestic processing capacity of recycled plastic from spent batteries to produce new battery components for automobiles, forklifts, and locomotives.
Stryten recently opened a lithium battery assembly plant in Georgia that supports military and industrial battery needs using proprietary lithium module technology.
The company is partnering with Dragonfly Energy, headquartered in Reno, Nevada, to bring advanced lithium batteries to its automotive aftermarket and battery distributor partners.
Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC, an affiliate of Stryten Energy, recently announced the launch of Storion Energy LLC jointly with a subsidiary of Largo Inc. and Largo Clean Energy Corp.

Storion Energy is focused on removing barriers for battery manufacturers to domestically source price-competitive electrolytes used in vanadium redox flow batteries for long-duration energy storage applications in the U.S.

facts: Stryten Energy LLC announced the formation of its Motive Power and Essential Power divisions to provide advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium energy storage solutions to help customers transition to clean energy sources.

The company's GNB Industrial Power division was rebranded to Stryten Energy to unify go-to-market efforts under the Stryten Energy brand both directly and through its dealer network and service partners.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.

The Motive Power division provides a comprehensive portfolio of advanced lead and lithium batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software for material handling, mining, and railway applications.

The Motive Power service team offers planned and predictive maintenance, repair services, installation and de-installation, recycling, application audits, power studies, training, and inspections to ensure customer fleet efficiency and sustainability.

The Essential Power division provides a suite of advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium battery technologies and services, including chargers and racking systems for backup power in military, government, telecommunications, UPS, railway, utility, microgrids, and long-duration renewable energy storage applications such as solar and wind.

The Essential Power research and development team will identify, develop, and commercialize innovative lithium and flow battery technologies to support emerging sustainable power and distribution markets.

CEO Tim Vargo stated that the creation of the Motive Power and Essential Power divisions enables the company to focus on developing and manufacturing high-performing, top-quality energy storage solutions across multiple chemistries to meet growing U.S. clean power demand and decarbonization goals.

The company supports customer sustainability goals by enabling transitions from internal combustion to battery-powered electric forklifts in distribution centers and providing environmentally friendly energy storage for on-demand power needs.
President and COO Mike Judd noted the announcement follows significant investments in battery technology innovations, vertical integration, and continuous improvement at the company's plants.

The rebrand of GNB Industrial Power to Stryten Energy was expected to be complete by the end of 2022, with significant support provided to dealer networks and service partners for a successful transition.
Batteries and chargers previously carrying the GNB brand label are now offered as part of the Stryten Energy product portfolio.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
The company's stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.

Stryten Energy is backed by more than a century of industry expertise in delivering energy solutions for applications ranging from submarines and subcompacts to microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
