facts: Stryten Energy is a US-based battery technology company.
The company recently installed a pilot-sized version of its vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) at a facility operated by Snapping Shoals EMC, an electricity cooperative in Georgia, United States.
The installed battery is a 20 kW/120 kWh VRFB with a recharge time of 7.5 hours and connected to the grid at 480V.
The system consists of several modules with built-in safety features, each operating independently at 6.67 kW/40 kWh per cycle.

Scott Childers, Vice President of Essential Power at Stryten Energy, stated the system was specifically designed and sized for an initial evaluation phase with Snapping Shoals to conduct long-duration testing of six hours or more.
The vanadium electrolyte aqueous solution is a proprietary combination of sulfuric acid and vanadium oxides.
The electrolyte is stable up to 50 C, but the system can be designed to mitigate external temperature effects beyond this range.
The VRFB system is supported by remote management software that tracks and stores performance data.
As product commercialization matures, Stryten Energy’s VRFB will be highly scalable to serve kW to MW needs for commercial, industrial customers, and full-scale utilities.
The VRFB technology was originally developed with Storion Energy, a US-based startup that Stryten acquired last year.
A prototype VRFB system is installed at a PV plant in the Colorado test facility known as Solar Technology Acceleration Center (SolarTAC).
The SolarTAC prototype has already dispatched 30 MWh of energy over 1,100 cycles, demonstrating capability to store solar energy for on-demand deployment.
Stryten Energy anticipates the VRFB will be commercially available in January 2025.
The company plans a complete US-based supply chain and manufacturing of all components of the VRFB system, including the vanadium electrolyte.
A manufacturing location has not yet been selected.
source_title: Stryten Energy vanadium redox flow battery on path to commercialization - pv magazine Global

facts: Stryten Energy LLC, a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider, has received a Phase 2 award from the U.S. Department of Energy through the MAKE IT Prize Facilities Track to build a domestic vanadium electrolyte manufacturing plant in Georgia.

The Manufacture of Advanced Key Energy Infrastructure Technologies (MAKE IT) Prize Facilities Track was developed by the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Technology Transitions, Office of Clean Energy Demonstrations, and Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, and is administered by DOE’s National Renewable Energy Laboratory.

The prize is part of DOE’s American-Made program, where competitors demonstrated their readiness for the construction of a manufacturing facility including proof of location, permits, financing, and community engagement.
Phase 2 winning teams were awarded $4.5 million each, bringing their total prize winnings to $5 million per team.
The Securing America’s Vanadium Electrolyte Supply (SAVES) project will deploy Stryten’s proprietary reactor design to provide domestically produced, low-cost vanadium electrolyte.
Vanadium electrolyte typically constitutes 40 to 60 percent of the overall system cost for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB).

Building a cost-effective domestic supply chain for vanadium electrolyte presents a transformational opportunity to rapidly grow adoption of this technology and secure leadership for the U.S. in this critical infrastructure application.

Scott Childers, Vice President of Essential Power for Stryten Energy, stated that the award will enable them to accelerate the manufacturing of vanadium electrolyte in Georgia and establish a vertically integrated domestic supply chain needed to meet the growing demand for VRFBs in long-duration energy storage applications.

VRFB technology is ideally suited to provide long-duration energy storage greater than 4 hours to help ensure grid stability and facilitate increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers across the U.S.
With proper maintenance, VRFBs can last more than 20 years without its electrolyte losing storage capacity.
The vanadium electrolyte itself is infinitely recyclable, and the battery offers a near-limitless cycle life.
These systems are independently scalable in power and capacity, with potential for substantial cost reduction when discharge time goes up.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC., will locate its first U.S. recycling facility in Georgia.

facts: SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC, a lithium-ion battery recycler and raw materials provider, will locate its first U.S. recycling facility in Stephens County.
The new facility will be located at the Hayestone Brady Business Park, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site, in Toccoa.
The recycling process will use an electric-powered dryer instead of an incinerator or natural gas combustion.
The facility will operate entirely without combustion.
The process involves receiving batteries, dismantling modules, heating cells to dry them, and shredding the dried material to produce battery powder.
The produced battery powder will be shipped to Korea.
All materials generated during the recycling process will be recycled for beneficial reuse, with none sent to a landfill.
No waste from the facility will enter the city's wastewater system in a way that causes processing issues.
The recycling process has been in use since 2008, though the facility's equipment will be upgraded.
Additional details regarding local or state incentives for the project will be made public at a later date.
SungEel plans to invest $37 million in the new facility.
The company plans to utilize North Georgia Technical College's services for employee training and hiring.
SungEel intends to act as a community partner through potential corporate sponsorships and donations.
The recycling process will be fully compliant with federal and state permitting regulations regarding air pollutants.
An air permit will be secured before operations begin, and the permit application will be publicly available once submitted.
SungEel is actively working with Georgia's Environmental Protection Division (EPD) and Department of Natural Resources (DNR) throughout the permitting process.
The company expects to start production in December 2023.
Unlike the previous proposal in New York which included a natural gas-fired dryer, the Georgia facility will exclusively use an electric-powered dryer.
The Georgia facility will recover more electrolyte material compared to the previous New York proposal.
The recycling process will not release any PFAs (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances).
Emissions from the facility will not exceed state or federal air quality regulations.
The facility will not serve as a battery storage site, but will include separate designated areas for raw materials and short-term finished goods storage.
SungEel met with local fire personnel, the EMA director, and the county's commercial building inspector to address safety questions.
The facility will not require special equipment from local fire departments to extinguish fires.
SungEel will install state-of-the-art fire suppressant systems that satisfy all building codes and insurance requirements.
Local fire personnel will conduct six-monthly training sessions with employees on fire precaution procedures, a requirement at all SungEel facilities.
The facility will create 104 jobs.
The average pay range for these positions will be above the county's current average hourly wage of $22.85.
SungEel expects to hire locally qualified individuals from the Toccoa-Stephens County area to fill the new positions.

Textron is repositioning some of its R&D funding into the supply chain and focusing on making factories much more effective.
Management intends to invest in enabling the workforce with better tools and capabilities to improve production output and yield.
Kautex revenues were $486 million, up $36 million or 8% from a year ago, primarily due to a favorable impact of $20 million from foreign exchange rate fluctuations and higher volume and mix.
Industrial segment revenues were $786 million, down $6 million from last year's first quarter on a reported basis, but up 4% organically once accounting for the powersports divestiture.
Textron is reallocating some of its R&D investment into supply chains and factories to drive growth and enhance profitability across its business.
source_title: Textron (TXT) Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript | The Motley Fool

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles and Jacobsen, both Textron Incorporated companies, have announced they will combine their operations in Augusta by the end of 2017.
Jacobsen’s operation in Charlotte will relocate to the Textron Specialized Vehicles facilities in early 2017 with their first phase of manufacturing.
Approximately 150 new jobs will be created as part of the combined operations.

Augusta's Economic Development Authority Chairman Henry Ingram stated that the relocation of the Jacobsen operation is another prime example of an outstanding existing industry that is continuing to grow in our region.
Jacobsen is a leading manufacturer of professional turf-care equipment under the Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper® and Ransomes® brands.
source_title: Textron and Jacobsen to combine bringing about 150 jobs to Augusta in 2017

facts: Kautex Textron has received a significant business award for its Pentatonic battery enclosure system targeted at electric passenger vehicles.
The Pentatonic system includes a top cover, bottom tray and integrated thermal management, and is designed for a multi-vehicle platform focused on future hybrid-electric models.
The award expands Kautex Textron’s role in the electric vehicle battery systems market and underscores the company’s focus on advanced EV-related technology.
This award points to growing traction for Textron’s technology in the electric and hybrid vehicle supply chain, particularly in critical components like battery enclosures and thermal management.

Textron Inc. announced its intent to separate its Industrial segment from the Company’s core aerospace and defense businesses to enhance its strategic and operational focus and drive long-term value for stakeholders.

Textron intends to explore multiple paths to effect the planned separation of its Industrial segment, including but not limited to a sale of the Industrial businesses or a tax-free separation into a standalone, publicly traded company.

Industrial, with over $3 billion in expected 2026 revenues, is composed of Kautex – a leader in plastic fuel systems, battery enclosures, and clear-vision systems for the automotive industry – and Textron Specialized Vehicles, a manufacturer of specialized vehicles and equipment with globally recognized brands such as E-Z-GO, PACE Technologies, Jacobsen, and TUG Technologies.

The Company is targeting completion of the separation within 12 to 18 months, subject to the satisfaction of certain conditions customary for such a proposed separation, including receipt of any required regulatory approvals and final approval of the Company’s Board of Directors.

As the Company pursues this separation, Textron will continue to operate its Industrial businesses in alignment with its current strategy, including continued investments in growth, margin improvement, and innovation.
source_title: Textron (NYSE:TXT) - Stock Analysis - Simply Wall St

facts: E-Z-GO partners with Rollick to enhance online purchasing experiences for consumers interested in E-Z-GO vehicles.
E-Z-GO will utilize Rollick's digital engagement module, RollickDR, to enable consumers to complete more of their transaction online.
E-Z-GO vehicles are designed and manufactured by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company.
The EliTE series of electric vehicles is powered by Samsung SDI lithium-ion technology.

Consumers can view available incentives and special offers, prequalify for financing, estimate trade-in values, reserve units with a down payment, and schedule appointments with local dealers via the new system.

A study by The Center for Generational Kinetics for WPEngine found that 65 percent of all generations rely more on technology now than ever before, and over 60 percent of GenZ and Millennials plan to continue using digital channels adopted during the pandemic.
Gunnar Kleveland, President and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles, stated that crafting a straightforward and simple online experience for customers is at the forefront of E-Z-GO's mission.
Meagan Kusek, Managing Editor of Motorcycle Powersports News, noted that consumers expect the internet to remember their entered information and preferences as technology advances.

A PwC study found that great customer experiences can yield up to a 16 percent price premium on products and services plus increased loyalty, with 63 percent of U.S. consumers willing to share more personal information for great experiences.
Bernie Brenner, CEO of Rollick, said E-Z-GO is leading in helping dealers serve customers in a richer, more personalized way that aligns with buyer expectations.
E-Z-GO rolled out RollickDR in early December and added Rollick's Inventory Tools to its Aimbase lead management solution powered by Rollick last year.
A study found that two-thirds of consumers say a frustrating website experience hurts their opinion of the brand.
E-Z-GO was founded in Augusta, Ga. in 1954 and is a globally renowned leader in the design and manufacture of golf cars and personal transport vehicles.
E-Z-GO models include RXV and TXT fleet golf cars; Liberty, Freedom and Valor personal golf cars; and E-Z-GO Express personal utility vehicles.

The employees of Textron Specialized Vehicles are committed to a culture of compliance and pollution prevention, aiming to sustain premier status through integrating environmental performance into business processes.
E-Z-GO became part of Textron Inc. in 1960 and operates as part of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc.
On the day this news was published, TXT stock declined 0.33%.

Rollick's outdoor recreational vehicle buying marketplace

source_title: E-Z-GO Partners with Rollick to Build Deeper Connection with Consumers | TXT Stock News

facts: Teledyne Brown Engineering announced April 6 that it is leading a team with Sierra Space, Nissan North America and Textron Arctic Cat to design the crewed Lunar Terrain Vehicle (LTV).
Teledyne Brown Engineering built the first lunar rover prototype for Dr. Wernher von Braun in the 1960s and has subsequently supported almost every major U.S. human-rated space mission.
Sierra Space will provide flight software, space qualified mechanisms, communications, pointing, navigation, and timing for the LTV.
Former astronaut Steve Lindsey serves as Sierra Space’s chief strategy officer.
Nissan North America brings long-running experience in mass-market automotive design and production to the team.
Nissan's U.S.-based arm operates four manufacturing facilities capable of producing 1 million vehicles, 1.4 million engines, 1.4 million forgings and 456,000 castings annually.
For the LTV project, Nissan will focus on autonomy and teleoperations, power management systems, vehicle connectivity, and human-machine interface.
Maarten Sierhuis is director of Nissan’s Alliance Innovation Laboratory in Silicon Valley.
Textron Specialized Vehicles will contribute Arctic Cat off-road capabilities for chassis design, vehicle dynamics and suspension handling.
Textron Specialized Vehicles operates separately from Textron Aviation and Textron Systems.
Venturi Astrolab unveiled its Flex rover family last month; the startup comprises NASA and SpaceX alumni based in Hawthorne, California.
Northrop Grumman announced in November that it is leading an industry team pitching an LTV, partnering with AVL, Intuitive Machines, Lunar Outpost and Michelin.
Lockheed Martin announced in May 2021 that it was partnering with General Motors to develop an LTV.
In 2020, the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) said it was working with Toyota on a pressurized rover utilizing fuel-cell electric vehicle technology.
source_title: Nissan, Sierra Space, Teledyne Brown, Textron Team For Moon Buggy | Aviation Week

facts: Augusta, Ga.-based Textron Specialized Vehicles’ corporate parent said in a federal regulatory filing that it will lay off about 400 employees — roughly 10% of its workforce — because of “significant changes” it has undergone since acquiring Arctic Cat in 2017.

A Textron Specialized Vehicles spokesman would not say how many, if any, of the positions would be at the company’s local operations, which manufacture E-Z-Go brand golf cars and other small vehicles.

The Dec. 4 SEC filing reiterated parent company Textron Inc.’s third-quarter earnings report, which said operating results from the Arctic Cat and Textron Off Road brands “were significantly below our expectations” because of problems introducing new products through its dealership network.
In addition to the layoffs, the company said it would close several factory-direct turf-care branch locations and a “manufacturing facility.”

Last month, the company notified the Indiana Department of Workforce Development that it would close its Coatesville, Ind., factory, which produced its Dixie Chopper brand of residential, commercial and industrial lawnmowers.
The closure will result in 107 lost jobs, according to its notification.
Textron estimated the downsizing could cost up to $85 million in severance payments, contract termination fees and impairment charges related to acquired intangible assets.
“We anticipate that this plan will be substantially completed by the end of 2018,” the SEC filing said.
In 2016, the company invested $40 million to purchase the former Procter & Gamble plant on Mike Padgett Highway near its existing Augusta plant on Marvin Griffin Road.
The expansion boosted the company’s local employment to approximately 1,400.
source_title: Textron to Lay Off 400 Workers

facts: ADVICS North America, Inc. is a member of the ADVICS Group, which is part of the AISIN Group.
The company is a premier supplier of advanced brake and stability control systems to automotive manufacturers in North America.

ADVICS North America focuses on the engineering, manufacturing, and sales of disc brakes, anti-lock braking systems (ABS), electronic stability control (ESC) systems, and regenerative braking systems.
It serves as the central hub for sales, engineering, research and development, and corporate administration for ADVICS operations in North America.
The company operates a modern office and engineering facility equipped with advanced testing and validation capabilities to support automotive OEMs.
ADVICS North America is strategically located in the Detroit metropolitan area, placing it in close proximity to major automotive manufacturers and suppliers.
The Plymouth, Michigan headquarters address is 15000 Centennial Drive, Plymouth, Michigan, USA.
ADVICS Manufacturing Ohio, Inc. operates at 1650 Kingsview Drive, Lebanon, OH 45036.
A facility is located at 1000 Unisia Drive, Athens, GA 30601.
The Athens, Georgia location is strategically positioned to serve automotive manufacturers in the Southeastern United States, enhancing regional supply capabilities and logistics.
ADVICS North America supports the just-in-time supply chain for automotive assembly plants in the Midwest and Southern USA, focusing on high-volume, quality production.
The company collaborates closely with OEMs to deliver tailored solutions that emphasize vehicle safety, performance, and environmental responsibility.

ADVICS North America is an integral part of a global network with research and development, manufacturing, and sales sites across Japan, Asia (China, Thailand, Indonesia, India), Europe, and the Americas.
The company works with its global entities for technology sharing, product development, and servicing global automotive platforms to ensure consistent quality and innovation.
ADVICS Manufacturing Ohio was honored by American Honda Motor Co., Inc. for Excellence in Value during the 39th annual Honda Supplier Conference on April 28, 2023.
ADVICS Manufacturing Ohio was recognized as a Top Workplace in the Cincinnati region for 2023 by The Enquirer Media on June 11, 2023.
Fumiko Fukunishi was appointed President of ADVICS North America, Inc., effective January 1, 2023, succeeding Masayuki Yamashita, with the executive change announced on December 21, 2022.
The company uses the @advics-na.com email domain with common formats based on employee names.

facts: The Company is a subsidiary of Aisin, a company which is partly owned by the Toyota Group.
It is a specialized brake system manufacturer in the Aisin Group, which is led by Aisin Seiki.
The Company was established jointly by Aisin Seiki Co., Ltd., Denso Corporation, Sumitomo Electric Industries, Ltd., and Toyota Motor Corporation in 2001.
As of Mar. 31, 2025, Aisin Corporation holds a 51.1% shareholding and Denso Corporation holds a 34.0% shareholding.
The Company manufactures brake systems categorized under Safety, Comfort, Environment, Components, Actuations, Foundations, and Parking brakes.

Safety products include ABS (Anti-lock Brake Systems), BA (Brake Assist), ESC (Electronic Stability Control), AEBS (Autonomous Emergency Brake System), lane keep assist system, and autonomous emergency brake.
Comfort products include ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control), A-TRC (Active Traction Control), HAC (Hill-start Assist Control), DAC (Downhill Assist Control), electric parking brake, and Crawl control.
Environment products include regenerative brake systems.
Components include ABS & ESC modulators, high-performance ESC modulator, and hydraulic booster with built-in ESC.
Actuations include brake boosters & Master cylinders with brake assist, hydraulic booster, and load sensing proportioning valves.
Foundations include disc brakes and drum brakes.
Parking brakes include electric parking brakes, caliper integrated parking brake, integrated drum parking brake, and foot release parking brake pedals.
In Jul. 2001, the company was established with registered capital of 1 billion yen.
Commercial operations started in Oct. 2001.
ADVICS North America, Inc. was established in USA in Jan. 2002.
The company won a contract from GM to supply full brake systems for the Hummer H3 in Feb. 2002.
In Apr. 2003, the company acquired two U.S. facilities from Sumitomo Electric Industries, Ltd. and established both ADVICS Manufacturing Ohio, Inc and SAFA L.L.C.
ADVICS Asia Pacific Co., Ltd. was established in Thailand in May 2003.
PT. ADVICS Indonesia was established in Indonesia in Sep. 2003.
ADVICS Tianjin Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. was established in China in Feb. 2004.
ADVICS Guangzhou Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. was established in China in Nov. 2004.
The company acquired ISO14001 certification in Jan. 2005.
ADVICS Taiwan Automotive Parts Co., Ltd. was established in Taiwan in Dec. 2005.
Mass production of the world's first electric parking brake (EPB) system with a shift linked function started in Sep. 2006, featured on the Lexus LS460.
Mass production of the next-generation ABS ADS-A2 and ESC ADS-V2G for the new Toyota Corolla started in Oct. 2006.
Mass production of the world's first Crawl Control Systems for the new Toyota Land Cruiser started in Sep. 2007.
ADVICS Europe Office was established in Belgium in Apr. 2008.
Mass production of ESC systems in the U.S.A. started in May 2008.
ADVICS Tianjin Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. merged with Tianjin AISIN Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. in July, 2008.
Mass-production of the world's smallest and lightest ESC modulator (ADS-V2P) for the new Toyota iQ started in Oct. 2008.
Mass-production of cars with regenerative brake system started in May 2009.
Delivery of control brake to Nissan Motor (ADS-V2G) started in Nov. 2009.
The company acquired Kariya plant and AS Brake Systems Inc. from Aisin Seiki Co., Ltd. in Apr. 2010.
The company acquired AISIN Brake & Chassis Inc. from Aisin Seiki Co., Ltd. in July 2010 and altered its company name to ADVICS Manufacturing Indiana, L.L.C.
ADVICS Changzhou Administration Co., Ltd. was established in China in June 2011.
ADVICS Fuzhou Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. was established in China in July 2011.
ADVICS Europe GmbH was established in Germany in Aug. 2011.
ADVICS Manufacturing (Thailand) Co., Ltd. was established in Thailand in Dec. 2011.
ADVICS Yunfu Automobile Parts Co., Ltd. was established in China in Apr. 2012.
ADVICS North India Private Limited and ADVICS South India Private Limited were established in India in Aug. 2012.
The company acquired ISO9001, ISO/TS16949 certification in Sep. 2012.
The company was awarded the Deming Prize in Nov. 2013.
ADVICS Manufacturing Czech s.r.o. was established in Czech Republic in Mar. 2014.
ADVICS Automotiva Latin America LTDA was established in Brazil in 2014.
ADVICS South Africa (Pty) Ltd. was established in South Africa in 2014.
The building of Technical center was constructed in Jun. 2015.
The Handa Plant was constructed in Jan. 2016.
The company turned into a subsidiary as the result of an increase in a third-party allotment of shares of Hosei Brake Industry Co., Ltd. in Feb. 2018.
Hosei Brake Industry Co., Ltd. acquired Nissinbo Brake Company's drum-brake business (AH Brake Co., Ltd.) in Apr. 2018.
ADVICS North India Private Limited and ADVICS South India Private Limited merged and became ADVICS India Private Limited in Apr. 2021.
Hosei Brake Industry Co., Ltd. merged with AH Brake in Apr. 2022.
BIADS India Private Limited was established in India in Jun. 2024, a joint venture with Brakes India.

Ferguson, Georgia's third congressional district representative, feels pressure to create new, lasting jobs in the community.
The LaGrange area and Troup County continue to face staffing shortages in various fields.
Rep. Drew Ferguson visited Troup County in September 2021 to encourage economic recovery at a job fair.

facts: About 285 invited guests – including many South Koreans and Americans – attended the grand opening celebration luncheon Thursday at AJIN Georgia’s automotive metal structural parts factory in Bulloch County’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.

Many hundreds of robots and a few hundred employees inside the 853,000-square foot facility have been making parts for the Ioniq 5, an all-electric Hyundai Motor Group crossover SUV, on a trial basis for about a month.
The parts produced include the main metal portions of a vehicle’s doors, dash, fenders, and floorboard.
The facility represents a $317-million corporate investment with a Register, Georgia address.
When production begins in earnest, tractor-trailer trucks will haul parts from the AJIN plant to Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant America in northern Bryan County about 29 miles down Interstate 16.
Hyundai announced in June that it would begin production at the Metaplant with the 2025 IONIQ 5 in the fourth quarter of 2024.
So far, with no market-ready assembly of vehicles yet underway at the Metaplant, AJIN has undertaken “control production” of parts used for calibration and testing.

Human Resources General Manager Jamie Calloway explained that during Hyundai's calibration and assembly process, AJIN sends them parts, and they also build components that are destroyed for testing and certification purposes.

Guests observed hundreds of one-armed robots ranging from a couple of feet tall to 20 feet or more in blue, yellow, and orange colors swiveling, flexing, lifting and lowering in robotic sub-assembly and welding areas.
The plant features massive stamping presses that exert up to 1,500 tons of force and rolls of steel that feed them.
Cavernous empty areas remain in the plant with only safety lines painted on the floor and some bases for equipment yet to be installed.
These empty areas will soon be filled with equipment for production of a second vehicle line, referred to simply as “the SUV.”
AJIN Georgia currently employs about 200 people and plans to hire more in August and September.
The company expects about 350 employees on the job by the end of the year.
This hiring trajectory leads toward the plant’s eventual full-capacity employment of about 630 people, as predicted in the late-November 2022 plant announcement.
The AJIN plant's headcount is far fewer than the 8,500 jobs expected to be created eventually on-site at Hyundai's Metaplant.
AJIN is the first tier-one supplier to get up and running, meaning it sends parts directly to the Metaplant.

Supplier plants in a number of counties, along with a battery factory also at the Bryan County Mega Site, should boost the total number of jobs associated with the Metaplant to more than 15,000 over the next few years, according to state officials.
Two other tier-one suppliers are building plants in Bulloch County and are expected to be completed soon.
Hanon Systems is a maker of "automotive thermal and energy management" equipment, which includes air-conditioning components.
Ecoplastic America Corporation will manufacture injection-molded plastic automotive body parts for Hyundai Motor Group vehicles.
AJIN USA Chief Executive Officer Jungho Sea welcomed guests in Korean while another staff member translated his remarks to English.

Sea stated: “As long as I continue operating my humble organization and factory here, I will always take the local community into my considerations. I will always take my employees as my top priority, as well.”

Local community guests included Bulloch County commissioners and staff, officials of Statesboro’s city government, state officials, representatives of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, the Statesboro-Bulloch Chamber of Commerce, and media organizations.
Some Korean attendees were AJIN executives and plant employees in specialized roles, such as technicians and electricians.
AJIN USA already operates two plants in Alabama, including one that makes the same kinds of parts now to be made at the Register plant.
The Republic of Korea’s Consul General Sangpyo Suh from the Atlanta Consulate attended and spoke during the ceremony.
Suh congratulated Sea and other AJIN corporate officials on a successful site selection, noting the plant’s proximity to the interstate highway and direct path to the Metaplant.

Billy Allen, chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, thanked DABC staff, county commissioners, and others for making the commerce park presentable to attract companies like AJIN Georgia.
Sea and Development Authority CEO Benjy Thompson exchanged plaques, with both AJIN and DABC expressing continuing appreciation to each other.
Sydne Smith, chief operating officer of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, noted that next year the state will celebrate the 40th anniversary of its trade office in South Korea.
Smith reported that for the last three years, Korea has been the top international source of investment for Georgia.
Georgia District 4 State Sen. Billy Hickman, R-Statesboro, cited a recent Georgia Southern University study valuing the annual economic impact of manufacturing in Bulloch County at $1.17 billion.
Hickman, a certified public accountant, appreciated the economic impact number but might argue with the multiplier economists used, thinking it should actually be higher.
Hickman stated: “This is economic activity that helps other businesses in the community be successful, helps employ thousands of our citizens and enhances our quality of life.”
Georgia Southern, Ogeechee Technical College, Bulloch County Schools, and some neighboring school systems were represented by their top officials at the celebration luncheon.
Sizeable groups of students from Seoul National University and Daegu High School, both in South Korea, also attended.

A group of educators from Bulloch County, Candler County, and Evans County school systems who took part in a 12-day visit to South Korea in June as part of the company’s “Korean Corner” program gathered around Sea to publicly thank him for the opportunity.
The educators had visited schools and cultural sites, as well as AJIN worksites during their trip.
Bethany Gilliam, CTAE director for Bulloch County Schools, stated that the program helps community members understand Korean culture when they have new neighbors or work in Korean-owned businesses.
source_title: Opening celebration guests see AJIN Georgia factory in action - Statesboro Herald

facts: The company is hiring for an SAP Operations Manager role responsible for the overall stability, performance, and day-to-day management of its SAP landscape.
The position requires overseeing the daily operations and ongoing maintenance of SAP systems (e.g., S/4HANA) and databases (e.g., HANA, SQL) to ensure high availability and optimal performance.
The role involves providing technical support to end-users, resolving incidents, and addressing requests related to SAP systems.
The incumbent will conduct training sessions for new features and processes as needed.
The position requires participating in system capacity planning and proposing continuous improvement activities to enhance operational efficiency.
The role acts as a liaison between the SAP team and other IT departments (e.g., infrastructure, security).
The position involves working with external vendors and service providers to resolve issues and manage technical support.
Candidates must have 5+ years of experience in ERP operations or Basis administration or programming development.
In-depth knowledge of SAP systems (ERP, S/4HANA, etc.) and database management (HANA, etc.) is required.
Understanding of ITSM (IT Service Management) and ITIL processes is necessary.
Experience with cloud-based SAP environments (AWS, Azure) is preferred.
Strong analytical and problem-solving skills with the ability to troubleshoot complex technical issues are required.
Excellent communication and interpersonal skills to effectively collaborate with various teams and stakeholders are essential.
A mindset for IT services in the manufacturing industry is essential.
The candidate must be able to communicate in both Korean and English with people.
Ability to adapt to a fast-paced environment and manage multiple priorities effectively is required.
Experience in program development and database processing is needed.
Experience with ABAP with SAP, MS-SQL, WEB is preferred.
A Bachelors degree in Computer Science, Information Technology, or a related field is required.
source_title: Joon Georgia Inc. (Ajin Georgia) Archives - Charge Forward Georgia

facts: Ajin Georgia is a metal auto body parts manufacturer in Bulloch County’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park supplying Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The company presented a total of $40,000 in donations to 11 local community service, public safety and educational organizations during a luncheon on Thursday.
The company is also known as Joon Georgia – a part of the South Korea -based Ajin Corporation.
One year ago, the company distributed an identical amount of money to a similar set of organizations while the plant was nearing completion but had not opened yet.
After starting production last spring, the plant held a grand opening celebration in July.
Ajin Georgia Human Resources General Manager Jamie Calloway served as master of ceremonies at the luncheon.
The plant now employs about 300 people.
The plant makes parts for the Ioniq 5 all-electric SUV that is the first vehicle to be produced at HMG Metaplant America, which is located in northern Bryan County.
As first announced in 2022, the 853,000-square-foot plant involved a $317 million investment.
The project included a commitment to eventually create more than 600 jobs.
The second production line may open in first quarter 2025.
Public safety organizations receiving donations from Ajin Georgia were the Bulloch County Fire Department, Bulloch County Sheriff’s Office and Bulloch County Emergency Medical Service.

Nonprofit community service organizations and local government hosted programs that received checks included Safe Haven (Citizens Against Violence shelter), the Boys & Girls Club of Bulloch County, the Bulloch County Recreation and Parks Department and a new recipient this year, Bulloch County Special Olympics.

Education organizations that received donations were Bulloch County Schools, Evans Region College and Career Academy (part of the Evans County Charter School System in Claxton), Metter College and Career Academy (part of the Candler County School District) and the Ogeechee Technical College Foundation.

Ajin Group earlier this year also sponsored a 12-day “Korea Corner” trip for 15 educators from the Bulloch, Candler and Evans County school districts to South Korea, where they visited schools and cultural sites.

Representatives of two economic development agencies – Regional Industrial Support Enterprise, or RISE, and the Development Authority of Bulloch County – attended the event as guests of Ajin Georgia and to show support.
source_title: Ajin Georgia donates $40K to community, school and public safety agencies, second year running - Statesboro Herald

facts: ALBAform provides manufacturing solutions mastering sheet metal bending techniques, CNC wire forming, welding, laser profiling, metal fabrication and sheet metal assembly.
The company performs forming, welding and assembly of metal components for the automotive, agriculture, and e‑mobility sectors, as well as heavy industries.
ALBAform is a 100% women-owned business.
The company operates in 3 countries – EUROPE (Czech Republic), USA, MEXICO.
ALBAform has over 30 years of wire bending, metal fabrication and laser profiling experience.
The firm provides state of the art laser profiles alongside metal bending, metal welding, sheet metal forming, and wire assembly for automotive and heavy equipment components.

facts: A "Solar Roof photovoltaic energy storage charging station" combines photovoltaic power generation (solar panels converting light to electricity), an energy storage system (battery modules storing electricity), and EV charging piles for renewable energy applications.
Global EV car growth is expected to reach more than 30% in 2030, creating a huge gap in EV charging infrastructure.
Some regions in China provide 30%~50% investment subsidies for photovoltaic energy storage charging projects.
In Europe, carbon emission reduction trading (ETS) and green electricity certification (such as RECs) increase revenue for these stations.
The typical ratio of photovoltaic installed capacity to energy storage capacity is 1:0.2 to 1:0.5.
A 1:0.2 configuration ratio equates to 1MW photovoltaic plus 200kWh energy storage.
The ratio of energy storage capacity to charging pile power usually ranges from 1:0.5 to 1:5, depending on the system's charge/discharge rate and charger power.
A 1:1 storage-to-charger ratio means 200 kWh of energy storage supports a 200 kW EV charging pile for continuous 1-hour charging.
For an example system with 1MW Solar PV, 200kWh energy storage, and 10 fast charging piles, the total investment is $275,000.

In the example system, the PV system costs $110,000 (35%~50%), the energy storage system costs $83,000 (30%~40%), EV charging costs $41,000 (10%~15%), and construction/operation/maintenance costs $41,000 (5%~10%).
The cost share of EV charging in the entire solar EV charging station is only about 15%.
Split EV charging piles consist of a power cabinet and a charging terminal, suitable for large-scale stations to improve efficiency via intelligent distribution.
Bluesky offers split liquid-cooled EV charging models BSEC-960K100S (960kW), BSEC-720K100S (720kW), and BSEC-4800K100S (480kW).
Bluesky's power cabinet dimensions are 2400 mm × 1025 mm × 1830 mm, floor-mounted, with 95% efficiency at full load, air cooling, IP55 protection, and supports 4G/CAN/RS485 communication.
The power cabinet input voltage is 380Vac±10%/620-800Vdc, frequency 50/60Hz, power factor ≥0.99, harmonic ≤5%, with input current ranging from 770A to 1920A and output voltage 200-1000Vdc.

The power cabinet supports a maximum of 6 terminals with dual connectors, steady flow accuracy ≤ ±1%, voltage stabilization accuracy ≤ ±0.5%, operating temperature -40℃~+75℃, storage temperature -40℃~+70℃, altitude ≤2000m, and humidity 5%RH～95%RH.

The power cabinet includes over-current, under-voltage/over-voltage, residual current, lightning, short circuit, and over-temperature protection, plus 220Vac auxiliary power for the terminal controller.
Bluesky's liquid-cooling super charging terminals output a maximum of 500A per gun, while natural cooling fast charging terminals output 250A per gun.

Terminal models BSEC-480K1000S and BSEC-1000K1000S have dimensions around 990x345x2140 mm or 962x664x1835 mm, floor-mounted, IP55 protection, operating temp -40℃~+75℃, noise ≤55dB/≤50dB, and storage temp -40℃~+70℃.
Terminals support output voltage 200~1000Vdc, OCPP1.6J communication (LAN/WiFi/4G), authentication via RFID/APP/QR Code/POS, and connectors CCS2/CCS1/GBT/CHAdeMO.
Integrated DC EV charging combines the power module, controller, and charging gun into one independent body.

Integrated models CDZ-YL and CDZ-WY offer output powers of 60kW/120kW/160kW/180kW/240kW, dimensions around 1110x1260x2110 mm or 1110x600x2200 mm, and share similar environmental, communication, authentication, connector, and security specs with the terminals.
The system uses matrix connection for intelligent power distribution, allowing each gun to call any module group, with power expansion possible by connecting two charging power sources in parallel.
Tesla Kettleman City Supercharging Station configuration includes Solar Roof (2MW), Megapack energy storage (15MWh), and 50 units V3 supercharging piles.
The Tesla station achieves a daily average power generation of 8 MWh, with energy storage covering 30% of charging needs and annual carbon reduction exceeding 5,000 tons.

CATL's Photovoltaic Storage Charging and Inspection Intelligent Supercharging Station configuration includes Photovoltaic (150kW), Energy Storage (200kWh), 12 units 30kW DC piles, and a Rapid Detection System.
The CATL station innovation integrates battery safety detection, allowing car owners to simultaneously detect battery health status while charging.

Future trends include declining battery costs increasing the proportion of energy storage, and integrated photovoltaic storage and charging equipment from companies like Huawei and CATL reducing deployment costs for larger scale adoption.

Innovative business models include virtual power plants (VPPs) participating in the electricity market for ancillary service revenue, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) interaction using EV batteries as distributed energy storage units.
Bluesky focuses on the R&D and manufacturing of EV charging station equipment, providing ODM/OEM services worldwide.
source_title: Solar Roof+Energy Storage+EV Charging Station Solution - Fuel Dispenser,EV charging,CNG/LNG/LPG,Hydrogen Equipment and systems manufacturers

facts: Bainbridge/Decatur County provides excellent training opportunities at the Technical College.
The region has a quality workforce and a can do leadership group that works in concert to make things happen.
The area features a great transportation network with US 27 & US 84 highway access.

facts: Anovion Technologies will invest $800 million in a plant in Southwest Georgia that will create more than 400 jobs, according to Gov. Brian Kemp.
Chicago-based Anovion Technologies supplies premium synthetic graphite, the largest battery material used in EVs by mass, exceeding copper, nickel, manganese, cobalt and lithium.
The new plant will be 1.5 million square feet and located in Bainbridge.
The Bainbridge facility will be the first of its size and scale for synthetic graphite manufacturing in North America.

Anovion CEO Eric Stopka stated that Georgia's strong technical workforce and top-tier schools will help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline for the company's innovation and climate technology jobs.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation, noting that job creators are recognizing every corner of the state has the resources and workforce needed for success.
The two largest economic development projects in Georgia history are electric vehicle manufacturing plants.
Hyundai is investing $5.5 billion in a plant near Savannah in Bryan County expected to generate 8,100 jobs when fully built out.
Rivian's $5 billion plant east of Atlanta will create 7,500 jobs.
Suppliers of electric vehicle batteries are springing up across Georgia to serve the two major EV manufacturing plants.
Anovion will be hiring for technical, production, and engineering jobs, as well as administrative and management positions.
The Bainbridge plant is expected to begin operations in late 2025.
source_title: Maker of EV battery materials Anovion Technologies is coming to Bainbridge

facts: Birla Carbon is moving forward with a $1 billion plant in South Carolina to produce synthetic graphite.
Synthetic graphite is an anode material for lithium-ion batteries.
The Indian firm received a $150 million grant for the project from the US Department of Energy.
The grant was awarded in September.
Birla Carbon will use a new manufacturing process that consumes less energy than conventional graphite production methods.
Firms in China produce nearly 70% of the world’s synthetic graphite, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
Chinese firms process virtually all natural graphite materials, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The Chinese government imposed restrictions on graphite exports last year.
Companies in the US produce about 5% of the world’s synthetic graphite.
US companies produce no natural graphite.
Several other firms are trying to increase US graphite production.
Anovion Technologies began building an $800 million synthetic graphite plant in Georgia last year.
Epsilon Advanced Materials is planning a $650 million synthetic graphite plant in North Carolina.
Firms are investing in natural graphite processing plants in Alabama, Louisiana, and other locations.
source_title: Birla Carbon plans $1 billion synthetic graphite plant in South Carolina

facts: Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) plays a crucial role for automakers, suppliers, manufacturers, and logistics operations considering job expansion in Georgia.
Griff Lynch serves as the CEO and president of Georgia Ports Authority.
Through collaboration between GPA and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), the state revealed 350 projects from potential port users in Fiscal Year 2023.
These announcements are expected to generate over 33,000 jobs in manufacturing and logistics.
These announcements are expected to attract investments totalling US$21 billion.
The economic benefits will extend to 96 counties statewide.
GPA, GDEcD, and the Georgia Department of Transportation are collaborating to foster responsible growth that delivers good jobs while preserving quality of life.
Georgia Ports is investing $29 million in an overpass that will route trucks leaving its Ocean Terminal in Savannah directly onto U.S. 17, avoiding community streets.
The expanding electric vehicle ecosystem in Georgia has made an impact throughout the entire state.
This EV growth is primarily driven by announcements related to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America has established operations in over 10 counties across Georgia.
Companies in e-mobility are prioritizing speed-to-market solutions.
Pat Wilson serves as a commissioner at the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Communities can prepare for new opportunities by investing in site development and obtaining “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” certification.
Anovion Technologies, a synthetic graphite manufacturer, is creating 400 job opportunities in Southwest Georgia’s Decatur County.
Chip Dunn serves as the Executive Chairman of Anovion Technologies.
State recruitment efforts, including meetings with Gov. Brian Kemp, influenced Anovion’s decision to establish its presence in rural Georgia.
The GPA’s inland port system comprises the Appalachian Regional Port in Crandall, Ga., and the ongoing construction of the Blue Ridge Connector rail yard near Gainesville, Ga.
This inland port system facilitates the transportation of large, heavy products from centrally located communities to the state’s deepwater ports.
In the Savannah region alone, the industrial property inventory now exceeds 11.5 million square meters.
Over 1.3 million square meters of industrial property in the Savannah region was under construction as of the end of Calendar Year 2023.
Stephen Ezelle serves as an industrial real estate broker at Cushman & Wakefield.
Despite a slowing economy across most U.S. markets, the Savannah regional economy continues to expand and demonstrate robust absorption in the industrial sector.
The outlook for Savannah’s industrial market remains favorable due to continued growth at Georgia Ports and conservative projections to eclipse 8 million TEUs of throughput by 2030.
Operational efficiency offered by GPA and a shift in import cargo from the West Coast and NY/NJ are key drivers in the growth of Savannah’s industrial market.
Savannah is poised for sustained, steady growth, largely attributed to the Hyundai announcement and GPA’s increasing national market presence.
Savannah’s industrial sector concluded 2023 with a 7% overall vacancy rate.
Stacy Watson serves as the director of economic and industrial development at Georgia Ports Authority.

Properties located 40 or 50 miles away from Savannah remain competitive for drayage, lease rates, and overall cost of doing business due to the port’s location upriver of the Savannah population center and DOT efforts reducing truck route congestion.
Efficient cargo movement to and from the marine terminal at the Port of Savannah is facilitated by direct truck routes connecting to Interstates 95 and 16.
On-terminal service at the Port of Savannah is provided by two Class I railroads.
Properties in Bryan, Liberty, and McIntosh counties, as well as those further west, offer access to interstate and rail connections linking to the port and vibrant inland markets.
source_title: Georgia Ports growth plans and collaborations fuel job expansion and economic opportunities statewide - Container News

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Anovion Technologies will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
Anovion Technologies is a supplier of premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
The project involves a $1 billion investment.
source_title: Breaking News – Sowegalive

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced Pratt & Whitney's expansion at the 2023 Paris Air Show in France.
Pratt & Whitney is an RTX business specializing in aircraft and helicopter engines.
The expansion project will create nearly 400 new jobs.
The project brings $206 million in investment to Muscogee County.
Georgia exported $9.2 billion worth of aerospace products in 2022, ranking as a top five exporter in the U.S.
The Pratt & Whitney Columbus facility opened in 1984 and includes the Columbus Engine Center and Columbus Forge on the same campus.
The Columbus Engine Center is located at 8987 Macon Road in Columbus.
Columbus Forge is located at 8801 Midland Road in Columbus.
The Columbus Engine Center maintains Pratt & Whitney GTF™ engines for the Embraer E-Jets E2, Airbus A220, and Airbus A320neo aircraft families, along with F117 and F100 military engines.
Columbus Forge produces compressor airfoils and other critical components for Pratt & Whitney’s commercial and military engines.
Pratt & Whitney has operated in Georgia for nearly 40 years, growing from a small manufacturing facility to a large overhaul center.
The Columbus business currently employs approximately 2,000 people.
Shane Eddy serves as President of Pratt & Whitney.
The expansion aligns with Pratt & Whitney's Industry 4.0 strategy to expand workforce and company capabilities in Columbus.
The expansion will add 81,000 square feet to the Columbus Engine Center.
The expansion will increase overhaul capacity to 400 GTF engines per year.
The expansion is expected to be completed by 2028.
The company will hire for engineers, mechanics, and operators roles.
Columbus Mayor Skip Henderson stated that economic vitality for residents and area businesses remains a top priority.
Heath Schondelmayer serves as Chair of the Board for the Development Authority of Columbus.
Christy Bozeman is the Regional Project Manager representing the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this project.
The project involved partnerships with the Greater Columbus Chamber of Commerce, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted Pratt & Whitney is a long-time partner for the state and other industry leaders, including Atlanta-based Delta Airlines.
In February 2023, Delta TechOps announced plans to open a new engine repair shop at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport to support Pratt & Whitney GTF engines.
The Delta TechOps after-market facility will service PW1100G and PW1500G engines used in Delta’s Airbus A321neo and A220 airliners.
source_title: GA: Pratt & Whitney Georgia Expansion Takes Off at Paris Air Show, With 400 New Jobs in Muscogee Co. | Trade and Industry Development

facts: CJ Foodville Corporation, a subsidiary of CJ Group, announced it will invest more than $47 million to build a new bakery and food processing facility in Gainesville.
The investment is expected to create an estimated 285 jobs in Hall County.
The new facility will be located at the Gainesville Business Park northeast of Atlanta.
The facility is expected to become operational in 2025, according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
Once operational, the facility is expected to produce more than 19,000 tons of products per year at full capacity.
CJ Foodville made its first overseas expansion and entered the U.S. market in 2004 with its TOUS les JOURS bakery café brand.
The company currently operates four storefronts in Georgia under the TOUS les JOURS brand.
Tony Hunsoo Ahn, CEO of CJ Foodville USA, stated the factory establishment is based on strong profitability to enhance production and supply capabilities for the expanding TOUS les JOURS business.
The company aims to establish the TOUS les JOURS brand as a globally cherished bakery brand that transcends borders.
South Korea was one of the top three international sources of job creation in Georgia during Fiscal Year 2023.

Two other South Korean companies recently announced investment projects in Georgia: Duckshin Housing will invest more than $15 million for its first U.S. manufacturing presence in Athens, and Daesol Ausy will invest $72 million for a new manufacturing facility in Harris County.
Half of the United States’ top 100 food processing companies operate in Georgia.

New and expanding food processing companies ranked among the greatest number of new industrial projects during fiscal year 2022, resulting in more than $923 million in investment in fiscal year 2023, according to GDEcD.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the CJ Foodville investment on September 5, 2023.

links.context: Georgia Economic Development tweet announcing the investment

source_title: South Korean Food Processing Company Announces $47 Million Investment in Georgia - Tennessee Star

facts: Ascend Elements, a US-based company, has announced the opening of its $50 million commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Georgia.
The Georgia facility is the largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility in North America to date.
The facility already began partial operation in August 2022.
The facility can process up to 30,000 tonnes of batteries and manufacturing scrap per year.
The company claims it can recover up to 98% of critical metals used in lithium-ion batteries.
The recovery process is more efficient and environmentally friendly compared to other recycling processes.

The company uses a unique approach called the ‘Hydro-to-Cathode’ process, which involves engineering the composition and microstructure of lithium-ion cathode particles during the direct precursor synthesis phase.
This process delivers custom-made battery materials to precise customer specifications.
Ascend Elements states that the process recovers 98% of critical battery metals in spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The process produces new cathode material at half the cost compared to similar cathodes made with newly mined and refined metals.
The process produces new cathode material with 90% lower greenhouse gas emissions compared to similar cathodes made with newly mined and refined metals.
The company is planning to produce sustainable battery materials for electric vehicles in Kentucky.
The Kentucky facility should be completed towards the end of the year.
The Kentucky facility will manufacture enough sustainable cathode material and precursors for up to 250,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Patrick Gruber stated that the facility demonstrates that sustainable battery production is possible and that a circular economy for lithium-ion batteries can be created.
Patrick Gruber expressed excitement about leading the way in recycling and sustainable production.
Patrick Gruber stated the company is committed to developing new technologies and processes that support a greener future.
source_title: Ascend Elements Launches Largest Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling Facility in North America, Investing $50 Million in Georgia

facts: The Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PAIH) named Ascend Elements’ Project Apex 2 its Manufacturing Investment of the Year.
Project Apex 2 is focused on developing the EU’s first fully integrated battery materials refining center.
The facility will turn spent lithium-ion batteries into new, low-carbon cathode materials using Hydro-to-Cathode® technology.
The Republic of Poland granted Ascend Elements USD 320 million (approximately 1.22 billion PLN) to advance construction of its first European pCAM and Lithium manufacturing facility.
This grant is one of the largest ever made by Poland’s Ministry of Economic Development and Technology.
The award was announced at the PAIH 2026 Investor’s Gala in Warsaw.
Linh Austin serves as President and CEO of Ascend Elements.
"Project Apex 2 is about more than a single facility; it is about proving that Europe can lead the world in a truly circular, low‑carbon battery economy," said Linh Austin.
Paweł Pudłowski is Deputy Chairman of the Board at the Polish Investment and Trade Agency.

"The most innovative companies in the global clean‑technology and battery industries are choosing Poland as a location for their investments, and Ascend Elements is further proof of this trend," said Paweł Pudłowski.

Project Apex 2 will refine separated key cathode raw materials recovered from spent lithium-ion batteries, known as "black mass," into lithium carbonate battery-grade materials and precursor cathode active material (pCAM).
These products are essential for the production of cathode active material (CAM), a core component of lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles and stationary energy storage systems.
The facility will commercialize Ascend Elements’ Hydro-to-Cathode® hydrometallurgical technology in Europe to produce sustainable lithium and pCAM materials from recycled battery materials.
The investment will help create an integrated, low-emission supply chain for lithium-ion batteries inside the EU, reducing dependence on imported metals and cathode materials from high-risk markets.
Project Apex 2 aligns with the EU Critical Raw Materials Act (CRMA) and circular-economy objectives to ensure strategic metals are recovered and processed within the European Union.
Michał Jaros is Secretary of State at the Polish Ministry of Economic Development and Technology.
"Project Apex 2 is exactly the kind of strategic, high‑value investment Poland needs to solidify its position at the heart of Europe’s clean‑energy economy," said Michał Jaros.
Ascend Elements already operates commercial-scale hydrometallurgical recycling and lithium recovery facilities in the United States.
In 2025, Ascend Elements produced approximately 2,500 tonnes per year of battery-grade lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃).
The company plans to increase global lithium carbonate capacity to more than 15,000 tonnes per year by 2027.
Project Apex 2 will transfer proven technologies to Poland to close Europe’s growing gap in cathode precursor supply and reinforce the EU’s strategic autonomy in critical battery materials.

Ascend Elements is a North America- and Europe-based battery materials leader that converts spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into premium precursor cathode material (pCAM) and battery-grade lithium carbonate through its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® process.
source_title: Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PAIH) Names Ascend Elements’ Project Apex 2 Manufacturing Investment of the Year

facts: Ascend Elements, a Westborough, Mass.-based provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions, has raised an additional $162 million in equity investments.
The new financing brings the firm’s 12-month funding to $704 million.
The funding accelerates construction of its $1 billion Kentucky manufacturing facility that will produce sustainable lithium-ion battery cathode materials for electric vehicles.
Major investors in the new funding round included Just Climate, Clearvision Ventures and IRONGREY.
In September, Ascend Elements raised $542 million led by investments from Decarbonization Partners, Temasek and Qatar Investment Authority.
Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC acted as sole placement agent for the Series D funding round.
The project will be the first facility of its kind in North America.
Ascend Elements is building a $1 billion Apex 1 facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
When it opens in early 2025, the facility will be North America’s first sustainable cathode precursor (pCAM) manufacturing facility.
Upon completion, the 1 million-square-foot facility will produce sustainable pCAM and cathode active materials (CAM) for up to 750,000 electric vehicles per year.
CAM and pCAM are engineered materials made to precise microstructure specifications for use in EV batteries.
Most of the world’s pCAM and CAM are currently manufactured in China from mined metals.
Ascend Elements is commercializing a method to produce sustainable pCAM and CAM from black mass, which is the output of lithium-ion battery recycling facilities.
At Apex 1, Ascend Elements will deploy its patented Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology.
The Hydro-to-Cathode technology will eliminate several steps in the traditional cathode manufacturing process and provide economic and carbon-reduction benefits.
Ascend Elements broke ground on the Apex 1 plant in October 2022.
Late last month, the construction team placed the final piece of structural steel on the 315,000-square-foot pCAM leaching building.
The pCAM building is one of many large structures under construction at the 140-acre campus.
Construction of the pCAM operations will continue into 2025.
CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that investments from a diverse group of leading climate investors and industry partners underscore the market's confidence in the company's business.

O’Kronley said the company is accelerating the global transition to zero carbon emissions through lithium-ion battery recycling and producing new engineered battery materials with lower carbon emissions.

Aruna Ramsamy, managing director at Just Climate, stated that Ascend Elements’ first pCAM facility in the U.S. has the potential to unlock the supply of critical battery materials to accelerate electric vehicle rollout.
Ramsamy noted the Hydro-to-Cathode technology provides a sustainable production option for battery materials and will champion circularity in an industry poised to scale significantly.
The project is supported in part by the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (BIL) and the U.S. Department of Energy, including at least two DOE grants totaling $480 million.
The project is expected to bring hundreds of full-time jobs and billions of dollars in positive economic impact to Kentucky.

A recent manufacturing report from Savills stated that the current U.S. manufacturing revival is being driven by significant public financing, including federal allocations from the BIL, the Inflation Reduction Act, and the CHIPs and Science Act.
Savills stated those federal allocations supported at least $628 billion in capital investments in manufacturing since the start of 2021.
source_title: $1B EV Battery Plant Gets Key Capital Infusion - Commercial Property Executive

facts: Ascend Elements announced the appointment of Linh Austin as its new president and chief executive officer on March 20, replacing Mike O’Kronley at the post.
Austin is considered a “seasoned energy industry executive” with three decades of experience.
Austin has been on the Ascend Elements Board of Directors since September 2024 after previous stops at McDermott, BP and ARCO.
Austin stated that Ascend Elements is “poised to become Europe and North America’s leading commercial-scale manufacturer of domestic, critical battery minerals.”
The company utilizes a hydro-to-cathode direct cathode precursor synthesis process, which Austin described as the “pinnacle of technology” in the battery materials and recycling industry.
Ascend Elements currently operates one of North America’s largest lithium-ion battery recycling facilities located in Covington, Georgia.
The company is still expected to begin commercial-scale production of up to 3,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate in 2025.
Ascend Elements continues to build North America’s only commercial scale pCAM manufacturing facility, Apex 1, in Christian County, Kentucky.
The Kentucky facility remains partially funded by a $316 million U.S. Department of Energy grant.
The company's operations and expansion “squarely align” with U.S. energy policy related to increasing domestic production of critical minerals and enhancing U.S. energy independence.
Former CEO Mike O’Kronley announced his departure via LinkedIn, stating it was a difficult decision but that he has “full confidence” in the company’s continued growth and success.
source_title: Ascend Elements Names New CEO | WKDZ

facts: Ascend Elements manufactures advanced battery materials using valuable elements reclaimed from discarded lithium-ion batteries.
The company is focused on creating a domestic supply of critical minerals through urban mining and innovative recycling processes.
In 2024, Ascend Elements's revenue reached $169.4M.
Since its launch in 2015, Ascend Elements has shown consistent revenue growth.
Ascend Elements hit $169.4m revenue in December 2024.
The company launched with $0 revenue in 2015.
Ascend Elements reached a $1.7B valuation in 2023, set during its None round.
Ascend Elements has raised $1B in total funding across 4 rounds, most recently a $162M None round in 2023.
In 2023, Ascend Elements completed a $542M funding round at a $1.7B valuation, selling 32% of the company.
In 2023, Ascend Elements also completed a $300M funding round.
In 2023, Ascend Elements completed a $25M funding round.
Ascend Elements employs approximately 172 people as of 2026.
The company reached 172 employees in November 2025.
Ascend Elements was founded by Linh Austin.
Linh Austin serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.
Ascend Elements is headquartered in Westborough, Massachusetts, United States.
source_title: Ascend Elements Revenue, Valuation & Funding History (2024)

facts: US company Ascend Elements has announced plans to invest up to one billion US dollars in a factory to produce sustainable battery materials for electric vehicles in southwestern Kentucky.
Production shall start at the end of 2023.
Ascend Elements expects construction at Hopkinsville this year, with operations to begin by late 2023.
The facility will use the company’s “hydro-to-cathode” technology, which directly processes black mass from recycled EV batteries into new cathode materials.
The usual intermediate step of hydrometallurgically splitting the black mass into individual materials to produce new cathode materials is redundant.
An investment of 310 million US dollars (currently 304 million euros) is to be made in the first stage of the plant, which is called Apex 1.
The production capacity for the first stage is unclear from the announcement.
The total investment of one billion dollars or 980 million euros will not come together until the last of “several possible” phases.
After full expansion, the plant should be able to produce enough lithium-ion cathode material for up to 250,000 electric vehicles.
It is not known by when this final expansion is to be achieved.
Up to 400 jobs will be created in the last development, from engineers and chemists to production and warehouse employees.
Ascend Elements describes its self-developed process technology as a closed manufacturing system.
Compared to conventional processes for cathode production, this should produce only “minimal waste and CO2 emissions”.
Apex 1 will also have its chemical recycling facility and a waste-water treatment plant.
According to CEO Michael O’Kronley, the company considered more than 50 sites for the plant – Commerce Industrial Park II in Hopkinsville was the only site that met all the requirements.
The company cites easy access to transportation, clean energy and a potential workforce as key site selection factors.
Proximity to electric vehicle manufacturers and lithium-ion battery factories in the southeastern United States was another vital requirement.
Relative accessibility to the company’s Covington, Georgia, facility (350 miles) was also a factor.
In Covington, Ascend Elements operates a battery recycling facility called ‘Base 1’.
The black mass produced at Base 1 is an essential feedstock for Kentucky’s new Apex 1 plant.

CEO Michael O’Kronley stated: “We’re in the middle of a global energy transformation, and we must produce lithium-ion battery material in the United States. Our future energy independence and national security depend on it.”

He added: “This new facility in Hopkinsville will produce sustainable, active battery material for approximately 250,000 EVs per year. That will go a long way toward making electric vehicle batteries cleaner.”
SK Ecoplant, an SK company, is investing $50 million to boost the Kentucky battery materials factory.

With their investment, Ascend Elements aims to commercialise its “hydro-to-cathode” process technology faster, which converts black mass from recycled EV batteries directly into new cathode materials.
The investment demonstrates SK Ecoplant’s commitment to the North American EV battery market and strengthens the relationship between the two organisations.
More news or funds has come in, so the company has begun constructing its announced factory in southwestern Kentucky.
When completed in late 2023, the Hopkinsville factory shall produce enough sustainable cathode material and precursors for up to 250,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
The announcement coincided with the US Department of Energy selecting Ascend Elements as one of 20 companies to receive funding for its activities along the US battery supply chain.
The company will receive just over $316 million in funding from the US Department of Energy.

The funding is for Ascend Elements to “establish industrial-scale US production capacity of sustainable, low-cost precursor cathode materials by integrating the separation of critical cathode materials from spent lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) with the production of both precursor cathode active materials and metal salts to support domestic production of cathode active material.”

links.context: Press release source for September 2022 update
Press release source for October 2022 update

source_title: Ascend Elements to make battery materials in Kentucky - electrive.com

facts: Honda has partnered with Ascend Elements to recycle lithium-ion batteries and recuperate the elements into new cells.
Honda is working towards selling its first electric vehicle, the Honda Prologue, in 2024.
Honda's EV production and manufacturing capabilities are taking form, including battery and drivetrain production facilities and battery cell manufacturing plants.
The new battery recycling and recuperation deal is with a recycler in Massachusetts.
Honda has been working with Ascend Elements to recycle lithium-ion batteries since 2021.
Honda is hoping to start using the materials the recycler takes from used batteries.
Honda is focused on using recycled lithium, nickel, and cobalt in new battery cells.
The deal was announced today to help Honda achieve a perfectly circular supply chain and achieve “zero environmental impact” by 2050.
Honda did not specify the amount of material it would be receiving from the recycler.
Honda did not specify when it would be able to start using the recycled materials.
Honda has established a series of battery production deals, the first with LG Energy Solutions.
Honda and LG Energy Solutions are launching a new production facility in Ohio.
Honda's second supply deal is with GS Yuasa.
The second supply deal will likely focus on meeting demand in Asia.
Honda continues to develop its electric drivetrain capabilities at its production facilities scattered throughout America.
Honda currently produces electric drivetrains for use in hybrid applications.
Honda is working with various manufacturers to develop new electric vehicles.
The Honda Prologue comes from Honda’s partnership with GM.
Honda and GM have stated they will likely continue to cooperate “to produce affordable EVs.”
Honda is working with Sony in its Afeela joint venture.
The Afeela joint venture is set to bring its first EV to market in 2026.
Honda’s motorcycle division plans to introduce three new electric motorcycles to the United States and Europe.
Honda’s motorcycle division plans to introduce a larger number of smaller models and scooters to Asia.
source_title: Honda EV production begins to take form with new battery deal

facts: Lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered materials startup Ascend Elements (formerly Battery Resourcers) is investing $43 million to open its first commercial-scale battery recycling plant in Newton County.
The Ascend Elements facility will create 150 jobs.
When fully operational in August, the 154,000-square-foot facility will be the largest of its kind in North America.
Roper Corp., a subsidiary of GE Appliances, is investing $118 million to expand its cooking products manufacturing operations in LaFayette.
The Roper Corp. expansion will create 600 new jobs.
Roper Corp. has been in Georgia since 1973 and employs 2,000 people in the region.
The Georgia Institute of Technology is expanding its biosciences and health innovation district, Technology Enterprise Park.
The five phases of development would add more than 365,000 square feet of life sciences-focused laboratory and office space and 280 apartments.
The Georgia Tech project has the potential to create 3,500 prominent jobs for the area, generating upwards of $8 billion annually.
The nation’s largest vertical farming company Bowery Farming is opening an indoor vertical farm in Locust Grove, creating more than 100 jobs.
Bowery’s farms are powered by 100% renewable energy and grow pesticide-free greens and herbs for more than 800 U.S. grocery stores and e-commerce sites.
Titan Steel Door is investing more than $11 million to expand operations in Hart County.
The 145,000-square-foot Titan Steel Door expansion will increase capacity of its existing lines and add a new product line of detention walls and ceilings, creating 90 new jobs.
Atlanta-based T5 Data Centers is building a 140-acre government and enterprise cloud data center campus in Augusta across from Fort Gordon.
In 2021, the Port of Savannah moved a record 5.6 million twenty-foot equivalent container units (TEUs), 20% more than in 2020.
Colonel’s Island Terminal in Brunswick saw an increase of 10.6% in volume of cars, trucks and machinery.
