facts: AWS pledged $11 billion for data centers in the state of Georgia.
The announcement follows a similar $10 billion funding pledge for data centers in Ohio at the end of last year.
Microsoft announced it will spend half of an $80 billion tranche of data center funding for 2025 in the US alone.

Roger Wehner, vice president of economic development at AWS, stated that ongoing infrastructure investments across the United States demonstrate a commitment to powering customers' digital innovation through cloud and AI technologies.
The growth in data centers has been spurred by the rise in generative AI.
AWS said the new infrastructure will be designed to be flexible, working for traditional loads as well as meeting the demands of massive AI models.
AWS data centers are flexible enough to efficiently run GPUs for traditional workloads or AI and machine learning models.
AWS has invested $18.5 billion in Georgia data centers since 2010.
Wehner noted that state and local leaders have cultivated an environment that enables companies like AWS to make bold, forward-looking investments.
AWS predicted the initiative would create at least 550 new high-skilled jobs in the region.
The initiative will also create thousands of non-technical roles such as construction.
The investment is expected to focus on Butts and Douglas counties.
Russ Crumbley, chairman of the Butts County Board of Commissioners, said the investment would be the "most significant in Butts County history."
AWS did not detail how the new infrastructure would be powered.
AWS noted it had previously made "significant investments" in renewable energy in the state, including eight solar farms.
Once fully operational, these projects will generate enough carbon-free energy to power an estimated 123,000 US homes each year.
source_title: AWS eyes ‘flexible’ data center expansion with $11bn Georgia investment | IT Pro

facts: Flambeau, Inc. laid off 298 workers across 3 WARN notices from Jan 2014 to Jul 2024 in Georgia and North Carolina.
A WARN layoff notice for Flambeau, Inc. in Georgia covered 89 workers with a notice date of 2024-06-22 and a layoff date of 2024-07-31 at 1330 Atlanta Hwy, Madison, Georgia.
A second WARN layoff notice for Flambeau, Inc. in Georgia covered 89 workers with a notice date of 2024-06-17 and a layoff date of 2024-07-31 at 1330 Atlanta Hwy, Madison, Georgia.
A WARN layoff notice for Flambeau, Inc. in North Carolina covered 120 workers with a notice date of 2014-01-20 and a layoff date of 2014-06-30 in Weldon.

facts: Norway-based battery manufacturer Freyr Battery has withdrawn its plans to establish a $2.5 billion factory in Coweta County, Georgia.
The proposed facility would have been Freyr Battery’s inaugural American plant.
The project was expected to generate over 720 jobs and represent a significant investment in the region’s economy.
A spokesperson from the Georgia Department of Economic Development stated that the state has been made aware of Freyr’s decision to discontinue its plans in Coweta County.
Georgia’s incentives process protects taxpayers, ensuring discretionary incentives are repaid when a company’s plans change.
The company and state are working together to ensure the state grant used for project site acquisition is repaid expeditiously.
Freyr Battery expressed gratitude for the support received from Coweta County.
The company stated it is focusing at the moment on a solar module manufacturing facility in Texas, marking a strategic realignment within the United States.
The planned factory was announced in 2022.
The facility was to occupy a 368-acre site in the Bridgeport Industrial Park off I-85.
Governor Brian Kemp highlighted the project’s potential impact, stating the plant would have significantly bolstered Georgia’s sustainable technology ecosystem.
The facility was designed to produce lithium-ion battery cells, which are essential components for electric vehicles and energy storage solutions.
Local officials in Coweta County conveyed disappointment over the cancellation but acknowledged the efforts of various stakeholders involved in the project.

A county representative expressed sincere appreciation to the County staff, Coweta County Development Authority, and the Georgia Department of Economic Development for their assistance, hard work, and due diligence throughout the process.
Local officials emphasized that the incentives in place are structured to protect taxpayer interests.
The property remains a viable opportunity for future investment and job creation.
The termination of Freyr Battery’s project is a developing story with ongoing discussions between the company and state officials to address financial implications.
The community and local authorities remain hopeful for new opportunities that could arise from the available property and existing infrastructure.
source_title: Freyr Battery Cancels $2.5B Georgia Factory Plans - Battery-Tech Network

facts: Freyr Battery has canceled plans to construct a $2.6-billion battery manufacturing facility in Coweta County, Ga.
The company is shifting its focus to solar production in Texas.
Freyr announced plans in November 2022 to construct the Giga America project plant in the Atlanta suburb of Newnan along Interstate 85.
The project was set to invest funds by 2029.
The project was expected to create 720 jobs.
In a Jan. 21 letter to the county development authority, Freyr stated the cancellation decision was made reluctantly and claimed it resulted from a realignment of its near-term strategic goals.
The plant cancellation follows Freyr's December-announced acquisition of Trina Solar's U.S.-based solar manufacturing assets.
The acquisition included a 5-GW solar module plant in Wilmer, Texas.
The Wilmer, Texas plant began production in November.
The Wilmer, Texas plant is expected to ramp up to full production in 2025.
Amy Jaick, Freyr senior vice president of communications, said the firm is focusing at the moment on the solar module manufacturing facility in Texas.
Freyr announced it has selected Austin as its global headquarters to be close to its Wilmer operations.
In a Feb. 10 release, Freyr stated the move to Texas was done to geographically align the company’s workforce with its operations and strategy to provide solar modules made in the U.S.
In the Feb. 10 announcement, Freyr said it has an agreement to sell its 368-acre site in Coweta County to an undisclosed party for $50 million.
The sale of the Coweta County site is expected to close on Feb. 15.
Freyr expects to net $22.5 million from the sale after repaying state and local grants it has received for the project.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported in early 2023 that the firm was set to receive up to $358 million in total incentives for the project.
The incentives included $228 million in local tax breaks over 20 years.
The county offered a $20 million grant for the project.
The state offered $90 million in sales tax exemptions.
The state offered almost $11 million in tax savings via credits.
A $7-million grant was designated to offset land acquisition costs.
It was not clear if the project received federal tax credits or other incentives, nor their status.
The 368-acre parcel in an industrial park has room for future expansion.

Freyr previously stated its plans followed strong tailwinds in U.S. renewable energy development, an intensifying focus on grid stability initiatives, and tax incentives associated with the Inflation Reduction Act.
Plans called for an initial investment of $1.7-billion.
The cell production module was planned to have a capacity of roughly 34 GWh.
At the time of the 2022 announcement, the project was expected to be one of the largest battery cell manufacturing developments globally.
source_title: Battery Maker Cancels $2.6B Plant Near Atlanta | Engineering News-Record

facts: Freyr Battery is two steps closer to Giga America, its US-based battery factory, as it leaves Europe behind.
Freyr received shareholder approval for all proposals at its extraordinary general meeting (EGM) on December 15, 2023.
Freyr expects to complete the process to redomicile from Luxembourg to the U.S. by the end of 2023.
Tom Einar Jensen is Freyr's Executive Chairperson.
Giga America is currently undergoing initial development in Coweta County, Georgia.
The U.S. Department of Energy (DoE) invited Freyr to submit the Part II loan application under the DoE Title 17 Clean Energy Financing Program for the Giga America project in Georgia.
Birger Steen is Freyr's Chief Executive Officer.
Freyr will utilize the U.S.-based 24M Technologies SemiSolidTM platform for the manufacture of utility scale energy storage LFP battery systems.
The DoE loan application process is a key element of Freyr’s capital formation strategy to fund construction of the Giga America project.

Freyr continues to engage with potential investors in project-level equity to finance the equity component of anticipated capital expenditures and organizational development associated with the project.
The company plans to continue advancing the DoE Title 17 process during 2024.
In November, Freyr announced plans to cut costs at its battery venture in Mo i Rana, Northern Norway, in 2024.
The Giga factory had been referred to as potentially one of the largest investments in mainland Norway in recent times.
Freyr chose to minimize Giga Arctic spending for 2024 due to the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
The company stated that Norway and Europe have yet to offer a competitive response to the US IRA or similar incentives from Canada.

Freyr plans to minimize spending on the Norwegian project in 2024, secure the asset with remaining committed capital spending, and work with stakeholders in Norway and Europe to develop a mutually attractive policy solution.
Almost 80 employees were laid off from the company shortly after the IRA-related announcements.
source_title: Freyr Battery Receives Shareholder Approval For Redomicile to USA

facts: FREYR Battery plans to open a new assembly plant in Coweta County, GA, about 40 miles south of Atlanta.
The factory will make batteries for electric vehicles.
The project represents a total capital investment of approximately $2.6 billion.
The exact location of the manufacturing facility was not disclosed.
The manufacturing facility will be known as Giga America.
The project is expected to create more than 700 new jobs over the next seven years.
FREYR considered more than 150 sites across 25 states before settling on this specific location.
Georgia has recently established itself as a hub for electric vehicle production.
Last December, California-based electric truck manufacturer Rivian Inc. announced plans to build a $5 billion facility in Rutledge, about 50 miles east of Atlanta.
In May of this year, Hyundai Motor Group revealed plans to develop a $5.5 billion manufacturing hub for its electric vehicle division in Bryan County, located near Savannah in coastal Georgia.
source_title: FREYR Battery to Open $2.6 Billion Assembly Plant in Atlanta, Create 700 Jobs | ASSEMBLY

facts: Freyr Battery is a Norway-based manufacturer establishing the first battery supply chain in the country with contributions from Asian partners.
The company aims to leverage cheap energy prices and optimized production processes to make cost-competitive products.
According to an International Energy Agency report, demand for EV batteries will surge from 340GWh to 3500GWh by 2030.
Freyr Battery was established in 2018.
The company has been listed on the New York Stock Exchange since July 2021.
Freyr is constructing a production base in northern Norway.
The project's first phase includes a customer qualification plant (CQP) and two gigafactories, Giga Arctic, costing around US$1.7 billion.
The CQP will host one industrial-scale production line instead of a pilot.
Production at the CQP is scheduled to start in the first quarter of 2023.
CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated the CQP will produce sample cells, test raw materials, and train operators to help achieve high production in the gigafactories later.
Giga Arctic will have eight production lines with an annual capacity of 29GWh.
Operations at Giga Arctic will start in the first half of 2024.
To secure material supply, Freyr partnered with Aleees of Taiwan to build an LFP cathode plant in the Nordic region.
The joint cathode plant will be the largest outside China, with an initial capacity of 10,000 tonnes per year by 2024.
CEO Tom Einar Jensen emphasized that scale is essential for driving down production costs and staying competitive.
Freyr will leverage economical renewable energy, noting that 100% of industrial electricity onshore in Norway comes from hydro or other green power sources.
The company claims it probably pays the lowest power prices in the world compared with other battery makers.
Producing batteries with green energy can cut down the carbon footprint by 25 to 30%.
If cathode materials are also made with renewable energy, it would reduce more than 50% of carbon emissions during the whole battery production process compared to many China-based manufacturers.
Freyr has licensed the 24M SemiSolid™ production platform from MIT startup 24M Technologies.
The platform enables an optimized production process with smaller and simpler facilities while making much larger and thicker electrodes.
The platform requires less than half of the operators that a conventional battery plant needs and allows for three times more batteries per employee.
CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated that half the CapEx, 20 to 25% lower OpEx, and a localized supply chain enable them to be very competitive while having the cleanest battery solutions on the planet.
Freyr formed a joint venture with Koch Strategic Platforms to build a gigafactory in the US.
The joint venture duo has looked into more than 150 sites in the country and narrowed the list down to five.
Freyr will also expand to Finland in the City of Vaasa.
The company cited Finland's abundant renewable energy, minerals and metals, and workforce competency as advantages for its planned battery factory.
The startup recently opened a technology resources campus and business unit in Japan.
The new Japanese office enables Freyr to access battery engineering proficiency, next-generation solutions, and local partnerships to scale up rapidly.
Freyr has secured initial customer agreements with companies focusing on energy storage systems (ESS).
On August 30, the company announced a battery cell sales agreement with Nidec Corporation of Japan.
The technology is particularly suitable for ESS, but the company has also received significant interest from EV and commercial mobility segments, with two times the contract volume for ESS.
Freyr aims to reach 50GWh of annual production capacity by 2025, 100GWh by 2028, and 200GWh by 2030.
The company is considering building anode manufacturing facilities besides producing cathode materials with Aleees.
Freyr plans to diversify its technology platform beyond 24M to offer a broader set of battery technologies.
CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated the company has no restrictions on who they partner with and welcomes any companies that see them as a relevant partner.
source_title: Freyr Battery poised to scale up clean and cost-competitive battery production

facts: Georgia state and local governments offered more than $358 million in grants, tax breaks, and other inducements to Freyr Battery to build its planned Coweta County factory.
The incentives offered to Freyr Battery are detailed in documents obtained by The Atlanta Journal-Constitution through the Georgia Open Records Act.
Freyr Battery announced plans in November for a $2.57 billion factory in Coweta County.
The planned factory will employ 724 workers.
The factory will produce giant energy storage systems for utilities and other clients.
The factory will produce lithium-ion batteries.

Greg LeRoy, executive director of left-leaning incentives watchdog Good Jobs First, said he worries Coweta County residents will have to cover local government costs incurred by Freyr’s new factory until the tax abatement ends.
Greg LeRoy stated, “You’re going to have to hire more teachers, pick up more trash, widen more lanes and answer more 911 calls.”
Greg LeRoy added, “If this company isn’t paying for a share of those induced costs, then everybody else gets stuck with the bill.”
source_title: In bid for big battery plant, Georgia anted up $358M in incentives - Good Jobs First

facts: Freyr Battery abandoned its plan to build a giant electric battery factory in Atlanta's suburbs after shifting focus to buy a solar panel plant in Texas.
The company informed officials in Newnan on Thursday that it would not proceed with a $2.6 billion plant planned to hire more than 700 people.
Freyr sent a Jan. 21 letter to the Coweta County Development Authority announcing its plans to end the project.
The planned factory was designed to build batteries to store electricity produced by renewable sources and release it later.
When announced in 2023, the facility would have been the second-largest battery factory worldwide.
Freyr, a startup founded in 2018, never began construction on the 368-acre (149-hectare) site.
The company moved its corporate headquarters from Norway to Newnan in part to maximize eligibility for U.S. tax benefits under President Joe Biden's climate law.
Freyr shifted its focus to a newly opened solar panel factory that it bought last year for $340 million from top Chinese solar panel maker Trina Solar.
Freyr spokesperson Amy Jaick stated the company is grateful for support and partnership in Coweta County and throughout Georgia.
The company noted in a December release that it is currently focusing on its solar module manufacturing facility in Texas.
Freyr senior vice president of business development Jason Peace met with local officials on Thursday.
Freyr cited rising interest rates, falling battery prices, a change in company leadership, and a shift in goals as drivers for the decision.
Coweta County Development Authority President Sarah Jacobs reported the meeting and reasons in an email on Friday.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development conveyed a $7 million grant to purchase a site for Freyr in Newnan, located about 35 miles (55 kilometers) southwest of Atlanta.
Georgia Department of Economic Development spokesperson Jessica Atwell stated the state and company are working together to ensure the grant money is repaid expeditiously.
Freyr may also owe money to Coweta County.
Atwell stated that Georgia's incentives process protects taxpayers and ensures discretionary incentives are repaid when a company's plans change.
Coweta County Development Authority President Sarah Jacobs said planning for the project made the county a stronger candidate for future projects.
Freyr previously planned to build battery factories in Norway and Finland but announced in November that it would try to sell its European business.
The company terminated its license for battery manufacturing technology, paying $3 million to the licensing company.
Then-CEO Tom Einar Jensen told investors in August that raising money for battery production had become difficult due to a surplus of lower-cost Chinese batteries.
Freyr switched its strategy to businesses allowing it to raise cash, including solar panel manufacturing.
The company's cash on hand fell from $253 million at the end of 2023 to $182 million on Sept. 30.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp has targeted the recruitment of the electric vehicle industry.
Korean firm SK Innovation built a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta, hired 3,000 workers, and later laid off or furloughed some workers.
Hyundai Motor Group has started production at a $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery plant near Savannah, with plans to hire 8,500 workers.
Electric truck maker Rivian revived plans to build a plant east of Atlanta after receiving a $6.6 billion loan from the Biden administration.
source_title: Battery firm abandons plan for a $2.6 billion plant in Georgia

facts: The project is being led by Stonemont Financial Group.
Stonemont Financial Group is an Atlanta real estate investment firm.
Stonemont Financial Group specializes in build-to-suit and speculative industrial development.
Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. launched three new all-terrain Wrangler tires: Outbound AT, Workhorse AT 2, and ElectricDrive AT.
The tire lineup targets a wide range of vehicles including full-size SUVs, light trucks, work vans, and electric pickups.

Jonathan Thomas, Goodyear’s director of Category and Strategy, stated the lineup reflects customer feedback and aims to deliver solutions for job sites to backcountry trails across the U.S. and Canada.
The Wrangler Outbound AT targets adventure-ready SUVs and light trucks such as the GMC Yukon, Ford F-150, Jeep Grand Cherokee, and Toyota 4Runner.
The Wrangler Outbound AT features DuPont Kevlar for added durability and an all-new tread design and compound to boost grip on dirt and gravel while maintaining wet-weather performance.
Goodyear backs the Wrangler Outbound AT with an up to 65,000-mile (105,000 km) tread life limited warranty.
The Wrangler Workhorse AT 2 is built for light trucks, work vans, and SUVs including the Ford F-Series, Ram ProMaster, and Chevrolet Tahoe.
The Wrangler Workhorse AT 2 delivers enhanced wet traction and carries an improved 55,000-mile (90,000 km) tread life limited warranty.
Every size of the Wrangler Workhorse AT 2 carries the Three-Peak Mountain Snowflake (3PMSF) designation for tested winter capability.
The Wrangler ElectricDrive AT is designed specifically for electric SUVs and pickups such as the Ford F-150 Lightning and Rivian R1T/R1S.
The Wrangler ElectricDrive AT combines rugged all-terrain capability with 3PMSF winter confidence and adds SoundComfort® Technology to reduce in-cabin noise for quieter EV platforms.
Goodyear backs the Wrangler ElectricDrive AT with a 50,000-mile (80,000 km) tread life limited warranty.
The launch expands Goodyear’s consumer portfolio to meet growing demand for rugged, versatile performance on and off the road.
source_title: Goodyear launches three new all-terrain Wrangler tires | Race Auto India

facts: Building on the success of Goodyear's and TNO's integration of tire intelligence into antilock braking systems (ABS) showcased at CES 2024, this project further underscores the role of tire data in advancing automotive safety systems.
The AEB system is designed to help prevent or reduce the severity of collisions by automatically applying the brakes when a crash is imminent.
Existing AEB systems are typically tuned for high-friction surfaces like dry asphalt.

Integrating Goodyear SightLine, the company's suite of tire intelligence solutions, can help the AEB system function effectively across a wider range of driving environments, including low-friction surfaces such as wet roads or ice.
Through the contact patch with the road, tire intelligence provides the AEB with deeper insights into actual tire and road conditions to enable smarter, more precise decision-making.
Chris Helsel, senior vice president and Chief Technology Officer at Goodyear, stated: "Safety is a cornerstone of Goodyear's commitment to innovation."

Providing critical inputs about tire and road conditions into AEB systems helps OEMs and their customers take a leap forward in safety, well ahead of the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration's (NHTSA) 2029 mandate for AEB systems in passenger vehicles.
AEB systems will play a critical role in automated driving, helping to provide a comprehensive safety solution at all times.

Recent wet road testing indicates that an enhanced AEB system incorporating Goodyear SightLine technology has the potential to assist in mitigating impacts at speeds of up to 80 kph (50 mph) by engaging braking earlier.
The integration of AEB with SightLine insights on tire and road condition data helps ensure robust performance across diverse scenarios.
Martijn Stamm, marketing director for Unit Mobility & Built Environment at TNO, stated: "TNO is dedicated to enhancing the safety, efficiency and sustainability of vehicles."

The collaboration with Goodyear aims to predict the braking distance of a vehicle in more conditions, like dry and wet asphalt, and trigger the AEB timely to reduce accidents and ultimately save lives.
This knowledge is a key enabler for a safe introduction of higher levels of automation.
The breakthrough positions Goodyear and its partners as leaders in delivering advanced safety solutions to meet and exceed regulatory requirements.
As major OEMs and tier-1 suppliers prepare for the 2029 AEB mandate, the SightLine integration can offer a competitive edge in the race to build safer, smarter vehicles.
Goodyear is one of the world's largest tire companies.
Goodyear employs about 71,000 people and manufactures its products in 55 facilities in 22 countries around the world.

Goodyear operates two Innovation Centers in Akron, Ohio, and Colmar-Berg, Luxembourg, to develop state-of-the-art products and services that set the technology and performance standard for the industry.
TNO is the largest Dutch independent research and innovation organisation.
TNO connects people and knowledge to create innovations and evidence-based insights that boost the sustainable competitive strength of industry and well-being of society.
TNO focuses in particular on the societal challenges of a safe, healthy, sustainable and digital society.
TNO is driven by 5,000 professionals in its work every day.
source_title: Goodyear and TNO Demonstrate Advanced Vehicle Integration to Reduce Crashes in Adverse Weather - Automotive Era

facts: Great Dane is an American manufacturer of commercial semi-trailers and truck bodies, founded in 1900 as the Savannah Blow Pipe Company in Savannah, Georgia, and headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.

The company produces a range of products including dry van trailers for truckload and less-than-truckload freight, refrigerated vans for temperature-controlled transport, flatbed trailers in steel, aluminum, and combo configurations, and custom truck bodies for foodservice and grocery applications.

With over 125 years of operation, more than 5,000 employees, and approximately 400,000 trailers on the road, Great Dane emphasizes innovations such as FleetPulse telematics for fleet management, Microban-infused liners for antimicrobial protection, and SlipNot safety flooring with a 15-year warranty.

Originally focused on metal fabrication, Great Dane entered the trailer industry in the 1930s amid the expansion of the U.S. highway system, introducing its first factory-finished refrigerated trailer in the 1940s.

During World War II in the 1940s, Great Dane shifted significant resources to military logistics, producing approximately 12,000 specialized heavy-duty trailers under government contracts and receiving the Army-Navy E award five times.

The company adopted the "Great Dane" name in the 1960s, expanded manufacturing plants across the United States in the 1970s, and merged with Pines Trailer in 1997 to form Great Dane Limited Partnership.
In 2011, the company rebranded from Great Dane Trailers to Great Dane to better encompass its expanding portfolio of transportation solutions beyond just trailers.
Great Dane operates as a privately held subsidiary of CC Industries, a Chicago-based investment holding company controlled by the Crown family.

As of 2025, Dean Engelage serves as CEO after assuming the role in January 2023, while Rick Mullininx holds the positions of President and COO with his planned retirement set for December 31, 2026, after 45 years of service.

Leadership at Great Dane is centered in its Chicago headquarters at 222 N. LaSalle Street, established during post-2010s centralization efforts, with additional legacy corporate offices in Savannah, Georgia.
The company operates 11 manufacturing facilities across the United States serving as the core of its production infrastructure for trailers and truck bodies.

The Statesboro, Georgia facility specializes in refrigerated trailer production, features a 450,000-square-foot operation capable of producing over 5,000 units annually, and employs more than 400 workers.
In Brazil, Indiana, the plant focuses on dry van trailers, reefers, containers, and truck bodies, having produced over 485,000 units since its 1975 opening and currently employing nearly 900 people.
The Elysburg, Pennsylvania plant opened in 2016 for dry van trailer production and reached its 25,000th trailer production milestone in May 2025.
Recent expansions in Pennsylvania include a $3.5 million investment at the Elysburg plant in 2021 to add a second production shift and increase output, creating over 200 jobs.
Great Dane initiated truck body production at its Danville, Pennsylvania facility in 2025, which also earned the 2024 Truck Trailer Manufacturers Association Plant Safety Award.

Great Dane maintains a network of 19 company-owned branches across the United States, providing direct sales, service, and support to customers in key logistics hubs such as Atlanta, Georgia, and Dallas, Texas.
The company relies on an extensive dealer network of authorized partners throughout the U.S., Canada, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, and Peru to broaden market reach.
In April 2025, Trudell Trailer Sales rebranded to Midwest Great Dane, deepening integration with the manufacturer's brand and enhancing dealer services in the Midwest region.

The Champion series dry van trailer features a Superior Strength Lightweight (SSL) van with galvalume steel side panels, a puncture-resistant liner six times stronger than traditional plywood, aerodynamic side panels, hot-dipped galvanized components, and an EnduroGuard rear frame.
Great Dane's refrigerated Everest series supports multi-temperature zoning options for simultaneous carriage of fresh, frozen, and chilled items in partitioned compartments.

Refrigerated trailers incorporate advanced refrigeration units from partners like Thermo King or Carrier, enabling precise temperature maintenance from 34°F to 41°F for refrigerated loads or -10°F to -20°F for frozen cargo.
Insulation in refrigerated trailers is achieved through composite panels and ClimaCore technology, an HFO-based system with near-zero global warming potential that enhances thermal efficiency.

Great Dane's flatbed trailers feature decks constructed from high-tensile steel, lightweight aluminum, or combo configurations blending both materials, enabling payload capacities supporting gross vehicle weight ratings up to 80,000 lbs.
Great Dane's truck bodies utilize the AnchorPoint mounting system that eliminates traditional U-bolts and filler strips, enabling seamless integration and transfer between chassis.

The Johnson and Alpine refrigerated truck body series incorporate integral cold plate technology with automatic defrost and all-electric refrigeration systems, achieving high thermal performance through high-pressure panel foaming and ArcticTherm™ liners.
Microban antimicrobial liners eliminate 99.9% of bacteria to meet food safety standards such as the FSMA across Great Dane's temperature-controlled product lines.
FleetPulse telematics is standard on all new trailers since its introduction, enabling real-time GPS tracking, cargo monitoring, and predictive maintenance alerts to optimize fleet operations.
Aerodynamic enhancements such as side skirts and integrated fairings on various trailer lines reduce fuel consumption by up to 5% by minimizing drag, verified through EPA SmartWay certification.
In 2025, Great Dane showcased the Sahara C-Series truck body at NTEA Work Truck Week, featuring an innovative tie-down system that secures mounting without U-bolts.

Facilities received TTMA Plant Safety Awards in 2020 for Elysburg and Statesboro plants, in 2021 for Jonesboro and Huntsville, in 2022 for Statesboro, and in 2024 for the Danville, Pennsylvania plant.
source_title: Great Dane Trailers — Grokipedia

facts: Leslie Doggett Industries acquired the privately-held Great Dane Trailer distributor/dealer for El Paso, Laredo and McAllen/Pharr, Texas with territory stretching to Las Cruces, New Mexico.
The acquisition was purchased from El Paso’s trucking magnate and International Truck dealer, Royal Jones.
The purchase price was undisclosed.
This acquisition follows Doggett’s acquisition of El Paso’s Truck Enterprises two weeks prior.
Doggett previously acquired Freightliner dealerships throughout the State of Arkansas last year.

The addition complements Doggett’s existing Freightliner and Western Star dealerships in El Paso, Laredo and McAllen/Pharr to sell and service flatbeds, dry freight and refrigerated vans with over-the-road truck offerings.

Paul Burk, SVP of the Doggett Truck Group, stated the addition allows partnering with trucking companies and dealers on a measurable basis and expects extraordinary growth with Great Dane products alongside Freightliner, John Deere, Toyota, LinkBelt, and Ford businesses.
Great Dane’s history dates back to 1900, establishing itself as a transportation solutions leader delivering innovative products and services for trucking customers.
Great Dane engineers and builds high-quality trailers and truck bodies.
Great Dane manufactured 49,000 trailers in 2018.
Great Dane’s headquarters are in Savannah, Georgia with manufacturing plants throughout North America.

Doggett is a Houston-based diversified heavy equipment dealer for seven first-tier manufacturers: John Deere construction and forestry equipment (17 dealerships), Toyota Industrial Equipment forklifts & material handling (7 dealerships), Freightliner and Western Star on-highway and vocational trucks (9 dealerships), Link Belt Cranes (3 dealerships), a Ford auto and truck dealership, and Great Dane Trailers (3 dealerships).
Doggett was founded by Leslie Doggett and Brady Carruth in 1993 with 17 employees.

Ranked by the Houston Chronicle in 2019 as Houston’s 12th largest private company with annual sales exceeding US $1 billion and 1400+ full-time employees including 500+ factory trained and certified technicians.
Doggett is a family-owned heavy equipment, highway truck and automotive dealership group without outside investment of any kind.

facts: Bloom Energy Corporation designs, manufactures, sells, and installs solid oxide fuel cell systems for on-site power generation in the United States and internationally.

The company offers the Bloom Energy Server, an energy server platform that converts fuels such as natural gas, biogas, hydrogen, or a blend of these fuels into electricity through a non-combustion electrochemical process.
Bloom Energy provides the Bloom Electrolyzer for producing hydrogen.
The company sells its products through direct and indirect sales channels to utilities, data centers, retail, healthcare, education, telecom, manufacturing, and other industries.
Bloom Energy is experiencing significant momentum with a $25 billion partnership expansion with Brookfield.
The company is navigating rapid growth in AI infrastructure.
Bloom Energy Corporation was formerly known as Ion America Corp. before changing its name to Bloom Energy Corporation in 2006.
The company was incorporated in 2001 and is headquartered in San Jose, California.

facts: Hanon Systems is a South Korea-based provider of automotive thermal management products and HVAC parts like vents, compressors, cooling fans and condensers.
Hanon Systems is putting a new plant in Georgia, investing $40 million and hiring 160 people.
The factory will be located in Bulloch County, where Statesboro is the seat and largest city.
The facility will be located in the county’s Gateway Regional Industrial Park, north of Interstate 16.
The park is home to a Great Dane trailer factory and a large Walmart Distribution Center.
Hanon already has a plant in Alabama near Hyundai’s original factory in the Southeast U.S.
The new Georgia facility is slated to work closely with the so-called Hyundai Meta Plant in Bryan County.
The Hyundai Meta Plant is the Korean auto maker’s first dedicated facility for the production of electric vehicles in the United States.
The $5.5 billion complex has already attracted more than $1 billion in supplier commitments.
Bulloch is one of four counties in the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority that banded together to woo the Hyundai plant.
All four counties have now seen substantial new supplier commitments.
Bulloch has landed the $205 million Ecoplastics plant set to employ 456 people in injection molding and painting for bumpers, consoles, trims, molds and other parts when it opens in 2024.
In November, Ajin became the first Hyundai Meta Plant supplier to announce in Georgia.
Ajin is planning a $317 million Joon Georgia plant that will hire 630 people in Bulloch County.
A release from the governor’s office did not set a timeline for the Hanon facility coming online.
Hanon has a cluster of plants near Detroit.
Hanon works with a variety of auto makers beyond its Korean customers.
Earlier this year, Hanon was named a supplier of the year for General Motors for 2022.
The company was founded as Halla Climate Control Corp. in the 1980s as a joint venture between Ford and Korea’s Mando Corp.
The company produced its first HVAC system for the Hyundai Sonata in 1988.
The Hanon name was adopted in 2015 when a majority stake was acquired by Hahn & Co. Auto Holdings, a Korean private-equity firm.

Gov. Kemp noted, "As our state furthers its position as the EV capital of America, new jobs and opportunities like these are coming to hardworking Georgians all over the state and will continue to do so."

Billy Allen, chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, said, "Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems' decision to locate its new facility here."
Billy Allen noted the company will work closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.

Pat Wilson, commissioner for the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems are able to successfully support larger industry ecosystems through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.

The Hella Group develops and manufactures lighting and electronic components and systems for the automotive industry.
In the aftermarket segment, Hella operates one of the largest trade organizations for automotive parts, accessories, diagnostics, and services within Europe.

facts: The Ministry of Industry, Employment, Innovation and Commerce of the Government of Cantabria granted the Hitachi Astemo Buelna plant a subsidy of one million euros through the competitive call for large projects to improve industrial competitiveness.
The Government of Cantabria will support the San Felices factory in the experimental development of new technological solutions in the field of brake systems.
The project has an execution period of 18 months and represents a total investment of approximately five million euros.

The subsidy was announced by Minister Eduardo Arasti during a visit to Hitachi in San Felices, accompanied by factory director Pablo Fernández and SODERCAN head of Creation, Innovation and Business Consolidation Alfredo Cuesta.
Minister Arasti stressed that the automotive components sector is “strategic” for the economy of Cantabria.
He added that R&D projects are “crucial” in addressing the challenge faced by the sector “in a moment of rapid transformation driven by the need for technological innovation and sustainability”.
Advances in electric and autonomous vehicles, coupled with strict environmental and safety regulations, are redefining the requirements for braking systems.

These changing realities have forced companies to adapt by implementing advanced solutions that not only meet the most exacting technical standards, but also improve efficiency and reduce environmental impact.

The Minister reaffirmed the commitment of the Government of Cantabria to support the automotive sector with several different lines of funding, including those aimed at large projects to improve the industrial competitiveness.

The primary aim of SODERCAN is to encourage innovation by supporting experimental industrial research and development projects that will boost the competitiveness of Cantabrian industry and promote energy efficiency.

Through calls for large projects to improve industrial competitiveness, SODERCAN has provided subsidies of 5.3 million euros to 9 large companies in Cantabria, representing 3,637 jobs and with a total investment of some 23 million euros in R&D+i.

“We want to help promote and encourage R&D projects and energy efficiency in Cantabrian companies, as well as to orient industrial diversification and excellence towards new and cutting edge technologies” Arasti stated.
Hitachi Astemo Buelna aims to develop new advanced technological solutions for the improvement of conventional brakes (hydraulic and electric).
The company aims to create new intelligent braking systems without hydraulic systems.
Hitachi Astemo Buelna plans to implement highly automated and efficient production processes to build out its product portfolio and ensure its competitiveness in the global market.
The Hitachi Astemo Buelna plant is located in the Agüera industrial park in San Felices de Buelna.
The facility extends over a total of 18,500 square metres.
The plant employs a workforce of 346 highly skilled employees, of whom 7.51% are women.
Hitachi Astemo Buelna is one of the largest manufacturers of brakes for the automotive industry worldwide.
Hitachi produces three main product lines: conventional brake systems, brake systems for electrical applications and mechatronic brake systems.
With a client list including the leading car manufacturers, the products of Hitachi Astemo Buelna offer great added-value in terms of quality and innovation, adapted to the needs of each vehicle.
Hitachi brake systems are commercialised through automotive parts distribution channels.
The last financial year (ending March 31, 2023), Hitachi Astemo Buelna saw an increase in turnover of 34.25% compared to the previous year.
Some 80% of its products were exported, principally to other EU countries with 11% exported to America and Asia.
The company is highly automated and has implemented an advanced ‘lean’ manufacturing system.

In recent years, the company has made significant investments to ensure its technological advantage, with a commitment to quality as a strategic asset in competing in a global market and a guarantee of continuity and success in the future.
Hitachi Astemo, headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, is a joint venture of Hitachi, Ltd., Honda Motor Co., and JIC Capital, Ltd.
With 80,000 employees worldwide and operations in the Americas, Asia, China, Europe, and Japan, the company is a global mega-supplier in the automotive industry.

The Electrical Division, the Chassis and Internal Combustion Engines Division and the Motorcycle Division are all engaged in the development, manufacture, sale and servicing of automobile parts, as well as industrial machinery and transportation systems.
Hitachi Astemo is committed to creating a sustainable society and delivering greater corporate value.
source_title: SODERCAN supports R&D+i projects at Hitachi Astemo Buelna with a subsidy of one million euros. - SODERCAN

facts: Hitachi Astemo, Ltd. has developed an autonomous driving technology that helps safely and smoothly pass by oncoming vehicles on narrow roads and other situations by utilizing 3D sensing results of the surrounding vehicle environment.
Hitachi Astemo aims to strengthen its cost competitiveness through the use of 3D sensing data obtained from new stereo cameras with improved cost benefits.

The demand for autonomous driving is increasing to help solve social issues such as alleviating traffic congestion and supporting the transportation of disabled, elderly and others with special needs.

For practical implementation of autonomous driving on public roads, it is necessary to predict risks of complex behavior of pedestrians and other moving objects, as well as sudden emergence from hidden areas, to avoid danger in advance.

A vehicle is required to plan a safe driving trajectory and driving control based on 3D sensing information such as LiDAR and stereo cameras for smooth driving in an environment mixed with non-autonomous vehicles.

Until recently, Astemo's technologies were developed to prevent collisions by predicting the behavioral changes of pedestrians and other road users in autonomous driving on general roads, and by performing safe and natural deceleration.

Astemo has also developed a hazard prediction and avoidance driving technology that predicts areas with potentially high collision risk, such as the behavior of other moving objects and sudden appearances from blind spots, and drives at a speed and trajectory that can avoid risk in advance.
Hitachi Astemo has developed technologies that enable smooth passing automated driving in narrow roads by coordinating with oncoming vehicles.
The technology integrates three-dimensional information obtained from sensing such as LiDAR to recognize the driving environment around the vehicle in three dimensions.
It understands intended movement from detected free space and the behavior of oncoming vehicles, and performs route prediction.
Hitachi Astemo is working towards collaborating with a high-precision, versatile new stereo camera launched in March 2023.
The new stereo camera can detect pedestrians and bicycles with a high degree of accuracy and measure distances by combining long-range detection with a wide viewing angle.
The stereo camera stores identification patterns in advance using machine learning techniques, contributing to collision prevention during right and left turns at intersections.

By utilizing Hitachi Astemo's AI and recognition technology, software processing has been created that enables advanced image recognition and vehicle control on cost-competitive electronic control units.
Collaborating with the new stereo camera will further enhance the cost competitiveness of autonomous driving technology that enables cooperative behavior in narrow roads.

Hitachi Astemo's autonomous driving technology using cooperative behavior in narrow roads and the new stereo camera will be introduced at the "Automotive Engineering Exposition 2023 Yokohama" (booth number: 148) held at Pacifico Yokohama from May 23.

Hitachi Astemo is committed to strengthening its business and delivering technological innovation through a strategic business portfolio, which includes Powertrain & Safety Systems, Chassis, Motorcycle, Software and Aftermarket businesses.

Aiming for growth based on the pillars of "green," "digital," and "innovation," Hitachi Astemo will contribute to a better global environment by developing xEV systems and highly efficient internal combustion engine systems that reduce emissions.
Hitachi Astemo will deliver enhanced safety and comfort through autonomous driving systems, advanced driver assistance systems and advanced chassis systems.
Through such advanced mobility solutions, Hitachi Astemo will contribute to realizing a sustainable society and provide enhanced corporate value for its customers.
An October 11, 2019 announcement covered driving control technology based on risk prediction of driving environment on general roads.
A December 25, 2019 announcement covered stereo cameras that combine distance detection and wide angle view.
source_title: Hitachi Astemo develops autonomous driving technology that enables cooperative behavior on narrow roads

facts: Hitachi Astemo is a joint venture between Hitachi, Ltd. and Honda Motor Co., headquartered in Tokyo, Japan.
Hitachi Astemo installed its ground mounted solar power plant of 3 megawatt (MW) at its Jalgaon manufacturing plant.
The Solar Power Plant is built in an area of 43,301 sqm.
The installation consists of 7,128 ground mounted solar panels and 10 inverters.
This is the first Solar power plant of Hitachi Astemo in India.
The initiative contributes to fulfilling the Prime Minister’s vision of making India carbon neutral by 2070.
The initiative aims towards achieving Hitachi’s mission of becoming carbon neutral by 2050.
The plant will eliminate close to 4,000 tons of carbon dioxide emissions each year.
The annual emission reduction is equal to planting approx.150,000 trees.
The installation will support in reducing electricity consumption charges.
In the first phase, Hitachi Astemo has installed in Jalgaon a 3-megawatt solar power plant.
Hitachi Astemo will commission an additional 1.5 megawatt Solar Plant by March 2023.
Hitachi Astemo is dedicated to the development, manufacture, sale, and service for automotive and transportation components, as well as industrial machinery and systems.
At its Jalgaon plant, the company manufactures brake systems (disc brakes & drum brakes) for 3-wheeler and 4-wheelers.
The Jalgaon plant also includes foundry operations.
source_title: HITACHI ASTEMO INSTALLS 3 MW SOLAR POWER PLANT IN JALGAON | Electrical India Magazine

facts: Laurens County will see nearly 500 new jobs come to the area after an international automotive parts company opens their new manufacturing facility.
Hwashin plans to build a new manufacturing facility in Dublin, which will create around 460 jobs and bring more than $176 million investment to Laurens County.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the goal to become the e-mobility capital of the nation is bringing opportunity to communities all across the state.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that Hwashin is the latest to join Georgia's growing ecosystem of companies bringing good jobs to hardworking Georgians in this industry.
The new facility will be located at the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site.
The facility is scheduled to start production in late 2025.
The site is the first Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Select site in the state, which took years of planning and months of work.
Fred Williams, Chair of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, stated that Hwashin becomes the seventh international company to call Dublin and Laurens County home.
The new facility will mainly focus on manufacturing chassis components for original equipment manufacturers.
Original equipment manufacturer customers include Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Kia Georgia.
Hwashin is based in South Korea and is a Tier-1 supplier that specializes in producing chassis and body parts for automobile makers.
Hwashin has manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Asia, and South America.

Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight stated that the staff and board of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority did an excellent job showing Hwashin why Dublin-Laurens County is a great place to do business.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight noted excitement about beginning work with their new Korean partners.

Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight stated that the company's manufacturing facility in Dublin will become an economic engine for the region, leading to more employment opportunities and quality growth for the town.
source_title: More than 450 jobs are coming to Laurens County as auto parts company opens facility - AOL

facts: Valdosta State University has partnered with Hwashin Georgia to promote entrepreneurship and small business development by supporting the creation of ventures conceived, managed, and owned by students.
Hwashin Georgia recently donated $10,000 to support the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship’s annual Business Plan Competition at VSU.
The Business Plan Competition is open to all VSU students regardless of major or business experience.
Throughout this multi-week event, participating students develop their understanding of a successful business plan and learn how to create one.
Students are provided expert feedback from industry leaders and the opportunity to win seed money to help turn their ideas into reality.

Dr. Byeonghwa Park, assistant professor of management for VSU’s Harley Langdale Jr. College of Business Administration and director of the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship, stated the competition helps students gain real-world experience and enhances their entrepreneurial skills.
The competition provides students with the chance to win financial support for their ventures and promotes engagement with industry leaders and potential future employers like Hwashin.
The J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship will begin accepting applications for this year’s Business Plan Competition in November.
Students have nearly three months to register for the event and submit their initial business ideas.
The final round of the competition takes place in April, when finalists present their full business plans to a panel of judges.
Hwashin is a specialized manufacturer of chassis and body parts for automobiles.
Hwashin was established in Korea in 1975.
Following its successful expansion into Greenville, Alabama, in 2003, the company is now making a significant new investment in Dublin, Georgia.
Hwashin aims to begin mass production of its automotive parts in Dublin, Georgia, by December 2025.

Sungkee Kim, chief executive officer of Hwashin Georgia, stated the company looks forward to a strong partnership with Valdosta State University and anticipates positive outcomes from the supported Business Plan Competition.
Hwashin aspires to be a valuable contributor to the economic growth of the Georgia region.
VSU’s Harley Langdale Jr. College of Business Administration opened the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship in early 2023.
The center is located in Pound Hall on the Rea and Lillian Steel North Campus at VSU.
The center brings emerging student entrepreneurs, faculty, staff, and industry leaders together to ignite a spirit of innovation and foster connections across the university, region, and world.
The center offers a wide range of resources, technology, equipment, and mentorship needed to unlock the potential of the next startup generation.
source_title: VSU, Hwashin Collaboration Helps Innovative Ideas Become Sustainable Businesses - Valdosta CEO

facts: Centrally located in Middle Georgia half-way between Atlanta and Savannah on Interstate 16.
From anywhere in the county, it is a less than 2-hour drive to either location.
Features a 331-acre featured property available as a select site along HWY 257.
The total workforce for the area is 301,204.
Market connectivity includes being less than 2 hours from Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport.
Key industrial sectors in the region include Distribution, Automotive, and Manufacturing.
source_title: Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority - Home

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on October 31, 2023, that Hwashin will establish a new manufacturing facility in Dublin.
The project will create over 460 new jobs in Laurens County.
The investment totals more than $176 million.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated the goal is to become the e-mobility capital of the nation and highlighted Hwashin as part of a growing ecosystem bringing good jobs to Georgians.
Hwashin operates as a Tier-1 supplier specializing in chassis and body parts for Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and international original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company is headquartered in Korea and maintains production facilities across the United States, Asia, and South America.
CEO Seo Jin Jung stated Hwashin aims to shape a future global city focused on electric vehicles using its 50-year expertise and collaboration with Georgia and Laurens County.
The new Georgia facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2025.
The site will be located at the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site, which is the first Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Select site in the state.
A GRAD Certified Select status indicates a site has met or exceeded more rigorous certification requirements.
The facility will primarily manufacture chassis components for OEMs including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Kia Georgia.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight welcomed Hwashin to the community and praised the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority for demonstrating why the area is a great place to do business.
Jeff Davis, Chairman of the Laurens County Board of Commissioners, noted Hwashin is the seventh international company to call Dublin and Laurens County home.

The county chairman highlighted the partnership between Laurens County, the City of Dublin, and the development authority, predicting new career opportunities and increased housing and retail development.
Georgia Ports Authority President and CEO Griff Lynch welcomed Hwashin as a member of the manufacturing community and emphasized the port's mission to connect companies to international markets.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at GDEcD's Global Commerce team, represented the department on the project alongside partners including the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that from the chosen site, Hwashin will have direct interstate access to at least two long-term automotive partners and can pursue an accelerated construction timeline due to the GRAD Select status.
Georgia's fiscal year 2023 saw a 324 percent increase in automotive industry job creation compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility OEMs resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued its entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs.
Hwashin Co., Ltd. was founded in 1975 and has held a prominent position in the Korean auto parts industry for nearly five decades.
The company actively engages in research and development and continuously expands its product offerings to include lightweight aluminum components and eco-friendly solutions.
Hwashin Co., Ltd. employs more than 4,200 personnel worldwide.
source_title: Auto Parts Supplier Hwashin to Bring 460 Jobs to Middle Georgia - Charge Forward Georgia

facts: Georgia Ports Authority leaders presented an update on the port’s $1.9 billion master plan and infrastructure investments at the Savannah State of the Port event on Oct. 12, 2023.
The capacity crowd of 1,200 included state and local political leaders, GPA customers, maritime/logistics industry executives, investors, developers and supply chain partners.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that ports in Savannah and Brunswick are essential to maintaining Georgia’s unprecedented and decade-long status as the No. 1 state for business.
Governor Brian Kemp cited responsible government, top-ranked workforce training program, and world-class infrastructure as key reasons for the state's record trade and commerce.
The U.S. Southeast population has grown by 9 percent since 2012, adding 6.5 million people and increasing consumer demand.
Texas, Florida, North Carolina, Georgia, South Carolina, and Tennessee are the fastest growing states.
Manufacturing is shifting to the Southeast, with major brands establishing a presence.
GPA Board Chairman Kent Fountain stated that Governor Kemp’s leadership and a pro-business approach with a strong Economic Development team continue to attract and win major companies to Georgia.
GPA Board Chairman Kent Fountain emphasized ensuring Georgia Ports grows responsibly to bring new jobs and philanthropic partnerships.
A “China plus one” sourcing trend shifting supply chains from Southeast Asia favors U.S. East Coast delivery.
Southeast Asia shipping routes to the U.S. East Coast via the Suez Canal are five days faster than U.S. West Coast routings.
India's expanding consumer class represents new business opportunities for GPA customers; about a dozen weekly India services currently call GPA.
More global supply chains have shifted from the U.S. West Coast to the U.S. East Coast, with East Coast ports now handling 54 percent of the nation’s global trade.
GPA President and CEO Griff Lynch stated that Savannah’s ocean carrier customers are upsizing their vessels; 80 percent of container ships entering the port are 11,000 TEU or larger.
Georgia Ports provides one of the nation’s best-connected port operations with 35 weekly ocean carrier services and additional terminal capacity and land available to attract new business.
Inland connectivity is a GPA growth strategy, with 75 percent of the U.S. population within a 3- to 4-day rail trip from the Port of Savannah.

GPA will focus on a mid-American arc ranging from Dallas to Chicago and Cincinnati, Ohio, as well as other cities within a 200-mile radius of Savannah such as Charlotte, Rocky Mount, N.C., Tampa, Orlando and Miami.
GPA handled volumes for FY2023 of 5.4 million TEU and forecasts 4-6 percent growth for the coming years.
At the Port of Brunswick, Roll-on/Roll-off cargo hit a record in FY2023, leading GPA to an overall growth of 17.7 percent over the previous year, at 723,515 units.

Georgia Speaker of the House Jon Burns stated that the ecosystem of service providers surrounding the ports plays a vital role in making sure needed products reach store shelves and factory floors across the country.
Mason Mega Rail Terminal provides rail capacity to serve inland markets, reduce road traffic congestion, and lower emissions.
Strong intermodal connections to locations more than 250 miles inland complement Georgia’s motor carrier network.
Mason Mega Rail provides the greatest on-dock rail capacity of any port in the Western Hemisphere.
GPA has seen significant cargo volume growth at the Appalachian Regional Port (ARP) since opening the Northwest Georgia location five years ago, now providing service to ARP in only 36 hours.
A new partnership with CSX provides a Carolina Connector service to Rocky Mount, N.C., creating a faster supply chain and opening Ohio Valley opportunities.

Savannah’s position as a first port of call for ocean carriers transiting the Panama Canal means cargo will be available at Rocky Mount before containers could be unloaded from vessels at ports farther up the coast.
Efforts are underway for a new partnership with Norfolk Southern to serve Northeast Georgia.
The Blue Ridge Connector service to Gainesville, Ga., will open in 2026.
GPA announced an eight-year, $6 million pledge through Housing Savannah, Inc., and the Community Housing Services Agency, Inc.
The Port of Savannah Area Workforce Housing Initiative is designed to improve quality of life and housing for individuals living in near-port communities.
The workforce housing program encompasses the repair, construction, and purchase of affordable and workforce housing to benefit low- and moderate-income homeowners, first time homebuyers and renters.
The initiative will prioritize home repairs for existing neighborhood residents and support eligible applicants with down payment assistance for first time home purchases in the Savannah area.
Garden City Terminal has an annual capacity of 7.5 million TEU as the largest container terminal in the Western Hemisphere and can accommodate vessels up to 20,000 TEU.
Renovations at Container Berth 1 were completed in August, creating another big-ship berth option and adding 1.5 million TEU of capacity.
The terminal can now serve seven ships simultaneously, including four vessels with a capacity of 16,000+ TEUs.
GPA ordered eight new STS cranes in tandem with the expanded berth.
The first four STS cranes arrived in February and are operational.
The next four STS cranes (capable of 22-wide, 24-wide reach) arrived in September and will be operational in January.
At Container Berth 2, four more STS cranes are being erected and will be operational in December.
All efforts are focused on getting Berth 2 operational to handle more ship calls and return to normal operations in two months.
New cranes on Berth 9 will be ready early next year.
A newly built, 100-acre container storage yard is centrally located for longer-term storage needs with fewer drays required.

Phase 1 of the Garden City Terminal West Storage Yard will open in November 2023 and provide one-stop storage location for exporters and importers with a dedicated truck gate to speed cargo flows and avoid traffic on local streets.
Berth and container yard renovations are under way at Savannah’s 200-acre Ocean Terminal to serve two large container ships simultaneously and offer 2 million TEU annual capacity.
Renovations to the first 1,325-foot berth will be completed in January 2025, with the second completed in June 2026, for a total of 2,650 feet of berth.
Berth 13 construction starts in October 2023, with this portion closed for ship operations.
650 feet of new berth will be completed by the time the new STS cranes arrive in June 2024.
Eight additional cranes have been ordered as part of the $1 billion Ocean Terminal refurbishment project.
Entry and exit for truckers at Ocean Terminal will be directly onto I-16/U.S. 17, minimizing neighborhood impact.

The Ocean Terminal facility is transitioning from a container-Ro/Ro-breakbulk terminal into a container-only model so that the Port of Savannah will handle containers, and the Port of Brunswick will handle Ro/Ro and breakbulk.
By February 2024, all non-containerized cargo at Ocean Terminal will transition to Brunswick.
More gates, extended hours and improved turn times are the goals to attract more trucking companies to Georgia.
The South Atlantic Chassis Pool 3.0 was recently acquired by Oaktree and will infuse the Port of Savannah with new and high-quality, refurbished chassis.

Built by GPA and opened in September 2023, the Savannah Transload Facility is designed to complement the local Savannah supply chain ecosystem and offer transload services from international marine containers into domestic 53-foot truck trailers.
The Savannah Transload Facility is managed by NFI, a third-party logistics provider, and adds 10,000 TEU of additional transload capabilities.
Savannah warehouse vacancy rate is currently 4.32 percent according to the most recent figures by CBRE.
The optimal warehouse vacancy range is 6-8 percent, so GPA is looking to attract more investment by developers and investors.
GPA is expanding capacity to serve growing Ro/Ro cargo volumes by investing $260 million in Colonel’s Island at the Port of Brunswick.
The Colonel’s Island investment develops 640,000 square feet of new processing space, 122 acres of additional auto storage and a fourth Ro/Ro berth.
GPA will host the State of the Port for Brunswick on Oct. 30 at the Jekyll Island Convention Center.
Hyundai Metaplant announced a $9.8 billion investment in Georgia for a new electric vehicle manufacturing site that will generate over 14,000 jobs when opened in late 2024.
Burlington announced a new 2 million square foot warehouse operation.
Bradshaw Home, a kitchenware manufacturer, will open a 1.2 million square-foot distribution center in Bloomingdale, Ga.
Kubota is expanding its location in Gainesville, Ga.
Hwashin, an automotive part manufacturer, is creating 460 new jobs in Laurens County, Ga.
DAS Corp, an automotive parts supplier, is creating 300 jobs and generating more than $35 million in Candler County, Ga.
Hanwha Q Cells, LG, and GE have established new locations now operating in the GPA inland port location of Appalachian Regional Port.
Aeronet Worldwide, a logistics services provider, has opened its second location in Georgia, a 180,000 square-foot warehouse eight miles from the Port of Savannah.
In 2023, 38,400 new jobs and $24 billion in new investments were created in Georgia.
The State has been ranked #1 in the nation to do business, for 10 years in a row by Area Development Magazine.

links.context: Brunswick Golden Isles Chamber of Commerce contact

source_title: Lynch: GPA ‘All-in’ On Building Best Gateway for U.S. Supply Chains — Savannah Traffic Club

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Joint Venture EV Battery Plant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will invest an additional $2bn (2.64trn won) in their joint venture electric vehicle battery plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The joint investment will create 400 jobs.
The investment brings the total project value to more than $4.3bn.
The facility has a capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours.
The plant will contribute to the production of 300,000 units of EVs annually.
Hyundai will use cells from this Georgia plant to assemble battery packs for EV models for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis at US manufacturing facilities.
LG became a partner in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s onsite battery cell manufacturing joint venture in May.
Governor Brian P Kemp welcomed the investment expansion.

Governor Kemp stated that in a single year they broke ground on the largest project in state history, landed multiple suppliers across the state for Hyundai’s Metaplant, and welcomed LGES to Bryan County.
Governor Kemp said Georgia is continuing to make itself the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Governor Kemp noted that major investments ultimately go to hardworking Georgians in the form of pay cheques, improved schools and infrastructure, and more.
Governor Kemp thanked Hyundai Motor Group and LGES for again recognising Georgia as the number one state for business.
Bryan County is located south of the coastal city of Savannah.
In 2021, manufacturing companies around Savannah accounted for 16.5% of the region’s economic output, or $4.2bn, according to figures released by the Savannah Area Chamber of Commerce.
The new battery plant in Georgia is part of US plans to boost domestic investment in the EV batteries sector.
In June 2022, US President Joe Biden allowed the Department of Energy to use the Defense Production Act to encourage domestic production of minerals required to make EV batteries.
As of June 2023, the White House reported that private companies had pledged $491bn of investment through the ‘Manufacturing the Future’ programme.
Of the total pledge, $149bn was allocated to EVs and batteries projects, according to Verdict’s report on the changing global map of battery cell manufacturing for the EV market.
Hyundai is an automotive manufacturer based in South Korea.
In June, Hyundai announced it was building a $60m factory in Bekasi, Indonesia to expand its global supply of EV batteries and provide systems for flagship vehicle models in the region.

José Muñoz, president and CEO at Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that the incremental investment in Bryan County reflects continued commitment to create a more sustainable future powered by American workers.
José Muñoz said Hyundai is proud to partner with LGES and is grateful for the support of Governor Kemp and communities throughout Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai and LG expand Georgia battery plant - Investment Monitor

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution have decided to funnel an additional $2 billion into their existing battery cell manufacturing joint venture at the Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
This new influx of capital raises the joint venture’s total investment to over $4.3 billion.
The expansion will also add 400 new jobs to the local economy.

Governor Brian Kemp acknowledged that these large-scale investments not only translate to job creation but also contribute to the overall improvement of Georgia’s infrastructure and educational facilities.
Hyundai and LGES initially established a memorandum of understanding in May 2023 for onsite battery cell manufacturing.
With this new commitment, the estimated total investment in EV manufacturing and battery cell production in the region will reach $7.59 billion.
Over the next eight years, the facility is expected to generate 8,500 new jobs.
The 30 GWh facility will be capable of supporting the production of 300,000 EV units annually.
Battery packs will be assembled at the plant by Hyundai Mobis and supplied to Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis manufacturing facilities in the U.S.

Various stakeholders, including the Georgia Department of Economic Development, the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, and utility providers, played key roles in securing this investment.
The state has garnered more than $25 billion in investments and created over 30,000 jobs in the EV supply chain since 2018.
source_title: Hyundai and LG Energy Amplify Georgia Investment by $2B - The EV Report

facts: In March 2022, LG Energy Solution announced a $1.7 billion investment to quintuple plant capacity in Holland, Mich.
The investment provides a pipeline to produce battery components in Michigan today and into the future as the EV industry grows.
LG Energy Solution promotes sustainability, electrification and innovation.
Bonchul Koo is President of LG Energy Solution Michigan.
Michigan was chosen due to its rich pool of talent, proximity to the geographic epicenter of the automotive industry, and strong support.
LGES aims to achieve carbon neutral by 2050.
LGES was founded in 1947.
LGES was formerly known as LG Chem Michigan and is a division of LG.
The company designs and produces innovative energy solutions for the automotive industry.
LGES manufactures large lithium-ion polymer battery cells and packs for EVs and other energy storage applications.
LGES has had a presence in Holland since 2010.
Michigan was chosen for LGES's first EV battery plant in the United States.
LGES now has 1,495 employees in Michigan.
The company grew its Holland battery factory in 2018, adding 100,000 square feet to its facilities.
GM announced a historic $7 billion investment in the state mere months before this announcement.
GM's investment includes up to $2.5 billion to build Ultium’s third U.S. battery cell plant in Lansing and Delta Township.
GM acted as a key partner in bringing LG to Michigan.
GM's latest Ultium joint venture with LGES is expected to grow the state’s global leadership in EV and advanced battery production.
The facility will include a clean room environment and machinery/equipment for high-volume cell output.
The project is expected to generate a total capital investment of $2.5 billion and create up to 1,700 jobs.
The Ultium battery cell plant project was among the first approved utilizing the new Critical Industry Program and Strategic Site Readiness Program.
These programs were signed into law by Governor Whitmer in December 2021.
The programs aim to ensure Michigan can compete for billions in investment and attract tens of thousands of jobs.
LG has investments statewide in Troy, Holland, and Hazen Park that began under former Michigan Governor Jennifer Granholm (now U.S. Secretary of Energy).
A new Ultium battery cell plant is in Lansing.
Michigan is home to about one-third of U.S. battery production.
Michigan ranks 5th nationally for employment related to electric vehicle battery manufacturing.
There are 2,000 workers employed at various Michigan battery manufacturing and OEM locations including LGES.
West Michigan – dubbed the “Smartcoast” – has become a hub for lithium-ion battery technology found in electric cars, RVs and specialty vehicles.
Holland-based companies Jolt Energy Storage Technologies, Volta Power Systems, and Global Battery Solutions are at the forefront of this entrepreneurial revolution.
Lakeshore Advantage is a nonprofit economic development organization founded in 2003 with key support from MEDC.
Lakeshore Advantage aids companies from startups to grownups and helps nurture entrepreneurship in Ottawa and Allegan counties.

Through its SURGE program located in the Holland SmartZone, Lakeshore Advantage supported Jolt, Volta, and Global Battery Solutions by providing mentorship, resources, and networking opportunities that helped them secure a collective $90,000 to achieve their next milestones.
Lakeshore Advantage was a vital partner in securing the recent LGES investment in Michigan, providing hands-on support and guidance.
Jennifer Owens is Lakeshore Advantage President.
LG choosing West Michigan for expansion cements it as a national competitor for future-focused economic prosperity.
LGES was drawn to Holland for its quality of life, community-centered focus, and proximity to Lake Michigan.
LGES's Holland production facilities are landfill-free, meaning less than 1% of its total waste stream goes to a landfill.
Of the waste produced, 97.4% is repurposed and 2.5% goes to a waste energy facility.
LGES is developing a Battery Academy for onboarding employees from entry level to experienced.
The training module lays out advancement pathways.
Training programs include career path training, apprenticeships, engineering development programs, leadership development, and hydraulic and pneumatic trainings.
Modules will be developed in cross-collaboration with higher ed institutions: Grand Rapids Community College, Muskegon Community College, and Grand Valley State University.
LGES provides tuition reimbursement for job-related degrees and certifications.
LGES debuted on the South Korean stock market in January 2022.
From its initial 2009 investment in Holland to the latest expansion, LGES has poised Michigan to become a battery capital of the world.
South Korea is the third highest global investor in Michigan, investing $1.2 billion over the last five years.
South Korean FDI projects were the largest amongst all countries, with projects averaging $152.7 million in capex.
Projects originating in South Korea were approximately four times larger than the average across all other source countries.
