facts: Opponents trying to derail a $5 billion, 7,500-job electric truck plant in Georgia dominated a state meeting this week that was meant to gather suggestions on how to design the plant to mitigate any impact on the environment.
The state assumed oversight over the Rivian Automotive project after opponents overwhelmed Morgan County planning and zoning officials.
The plant was announced by the company and Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp in December, and is the biggest single industrial project in state history.
The first meeting of one of the oversight committees was Monday in the city of Monroe.

The Irvine, California-based electric vehicle manufacturer announced last year that it would build the facility on a 2,000-acre (809-hectare) site in Morgan and Walton counties about 45 miles (72 kilometers) east of Atlanta along Interstate 20.
It plans to produce up to 400,000 vehicles a year there.
Rivian, which also has a plant in Normal, Illinois, said it hopes to break ground as early as this summer and begin production in 2024.

The state panel, led by John Eunice, deputy director for the state Environmental Protection Division, did not get much cooperation from a hostile crowd that gathered at Athens Technical College in Monroe, news outlets reported.
Opposition to the plant has been heavy from Rutledge-area residents who say the plant will spoil their rural quality of life.
Residents criticized the meeting as a sham, saying it is impossible to make meaningful suggestions when there is not yet a plant design and saying the state is only working to get the plant built.
Pam Jones stated: “I was sitting at home and I saw my governor get on TV and say Rivian, 2,000-acre plant, coming to Rutledge, Georgia and it’s a done deal.”
Many speakers Monday voiced concerns about possible well-water contamination, light pollution, and the disruption of wildlife habitats and farmland for heavy industry.

Edwin Snell of Oconee County stated: “I don’t understand why you are sitting on that side of the table, which is the Rivian side of the table and why you’re not sitting over here asking Rivian and Gov. Kemp to explain this environmental project and how it’s a disaster.”
A Rivian executive was present via video conference but did not speak during the hearing.

A spokesperson for Rivian said the meeting was a valuable opportunity for the company to gather input and that the company is committed to sharing details of their plans for the site once they are complete and “meet our own high design and environmental standards.”

The plant is a subject of contention in Georgia's Republican primary for governor, with former U.S. Sen. David Perdue attacking Kemp for agreeing to the Rivian location without support from neighbors.
Eunice said he does not know when Rivian will file for environmental permits needed to build the facility.
He said the division will take public comment on the permits.
Monday's meeting was the first of four planned for the site design and environmental committee.
The state plans four meetings each with three other committees tasked with examining quality of life, workforce and local business engagement issues.
source_title: Rivian electric car plant blasted by foes at Georgia meeting

facts: President Joe Biden's administration announced a $6.6 billion loan from the U.S. Department of Energy to Rivian Automotive to support the construction of a factory in Georgia.
The Georgia factory project had been stalled as the electric vehicle startup struggled to achieve profitability.
It remains uncertain whether the loan can be finalized before Donald Trump assumes office in less than two months or if the incoming administration might attempt to revoke it.
Donald Trump has previously pledged to eliminate federal electric vehicle tax credits, currently valued at up to $7,500 for new zero-emission vehicles and $4,000 for used ones.
Rivian began producing its R1 series of electric SUVs, pickup trucks, and delivery vans in 2021 at a repurposed Mitsubishi factory in Normal, Illinois.

Shortly after beginning Illinois production, the California-based company announced a $5 billion investment to build a second plant near Social Circle, Georgia, approximately 40 miles east of Atlanta.
The R1 vehicles were priced at $70,000 or more and targeted a premium market.
Rivian intended to use the Georgia facility to produce the R2, a smaller SUV with a lower price point aimed at broader consumer adoption.
The plant’s first phase was projected to produce 200,000 vehicles annually.
A second phase of the Georgia plant was projected to double that capacity.
At full scale, the Georgia plant would create 7,500 jobs.
Rivian's inability to meet production and sales targets led to financial strain.
In March, the company announced a halt to construction at the Georgia site, opting to begin R2 production at its Illinois facility instead.
Rivian’s CEO, RJ Scaringe, cited a need to accelerate R2 availability and reduce capital expenditures by $2.25 billion for the pause decision.
In June, Rivian’s cash flow improved after German automaker Volkswagen AG invested $5 billion in a joint venture with Rivian.
The joint venture with Volkswagen AG includes an agreement to share software and electrical technology.
The partnership with Volkswagen provided crucial financial relief to the startup.
Following the loan announcement, Rivian stated that construction of the R2 and a future R3 model will now proceed at the Georgia site.
Production of the R2 and R3 models at the Georgia facility is slated to begin in 2028.

CEO RJ Scaringe stated that the loan would enable Rivian to more aggressively scale its U.S. manufacturing footprint for competitively priced R2 and R3 vehicles that emphasize capability and affordability.
The Department of Energy emphasized that the loan would significantly bolster domestic electric vehicle production.
The loan aligns with President Biden’s goal for zero-emission vehicles to comprise half of all new U.S. sales by 2030.
source_title: Biden administration to loan $6.6 billion to EV maker Rivian, $RIVN, to build Georgia factory that automaker paused

facts: Saft is a French company involved in the design, development, and manufacturing of batteries used in transport, industry, and defense.
The company was established in 1918 by founder Victor Hérold.
In 1953, the US Naval Air Command requested 2000 batteries of 24 V, with a contract valued at US$12,033,582 in 2025 dollars.
In 1980, together with PSA, Saft conducted a study to evaluate the potential of using batteries to power electric cars.
Following the 1980 study, investment was focused on building a sophisticated robotic assembly line.
It became the leading manufacturer of nickel-cadmium batteries both in the aviation sector and in the railway sector.
In the 1970s, Saft opened a subsidiary in the United States.
In 2001, Saft acquired from Invensys plc the American company Hawker Eternacell, a leading provider of lithium batteries to the American and British armed forces.

In January 2006, Saft and Johnson Controls Inc announced the launch of a joint venture named Johnson Controls-Saft Advanced Power Solutions LLC to develop, produce, and sell advanced technology batteries for hybrid electric and electric vehicles.
In January 2008, Johnson Controls-Saft opened the first production facility for lithium-ion hybrid vehicle batteries in Nersac, France.
In 2009, Johnson Controls-Saft started the construction of a lithium-ion production plant in Michigan.
In 2009, Saft built another lithium-ion production plant for itself in Florida.
In May 2011, Johnson Controls requested the dissolution of the joint venture to the Delaware Court of Chancery due to increasing dissatisfaction with agreement restrictions.
The two companies agreed to the separation, and Johnson Controls paid Saft $145 million for its shares in the joint venture as well as for the right to use certain technology developed by it.
Johnson Controls retained the Michigan facility built by the partnership after the dissolution.
The French joint facility was transferred to Saft following the separation.

In February 2013, Airbus dropped the use of lithium-ion batteries for its A350 due to Boeing's difficulties with the 787 Dreamliner, which hit Saft because it had a supply contract with the aircraft manufacturer.
The company publicly denied a major impact from the Airbus battery decision.
In July 2013, Saft was awarded a $6.5 million contract to supply high power lithium-ion batteries for the Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II.
Saft supplies the battery for the Exagon Furtive-eGT, a four-seat electric sports car produced by Exagon Motors.
The battery supplied to the Exagon Furtive-eGT is capable of withstanding 3,000 charge cycles while conserving 80% of its capacity.
In 2016, Total (later renamed TotalEnergies) acquired all Saft shares and delisted it from the stock exchange in August of that year.
In 2020, the Saft part of TotalEnergies signed a joint venture with the Stellantis Group to develop and produce lithium cells for electric vehicles.
The Automotive Cell Company (AAC) joint venture shall set up a pilot plant at Saft in Nersac.
A gigafactory is planned to follow in Douvrin, Hauts-de-France, and later in Kaiserslauten, Germany.
Saft's Aerospace, Defense, and Performance division produces nickel, lithium-ion, and silver-based batteries for aerospace, defense, and performance applications.

Saft's Industry, Mobility, and Infrastructure division produces nickel technology and lithium-ion batteries for applications including the electrification of industrial vehicles and railway on-board traction systems.
As of 2023, Saft reported revenue of €1.2 billion.
Saft has 4,300 employees.
Cedric Duclos serves as the CEO of Saft.
source_title: Saft (company) - Wikipedia

facts: Saft America Inc. could have more time to create the jobs it promised the city in a 2009 economic development agreement.

City Office of Economic Development Executive Director Kirk Wendland introduced an amendment at the Mayor’s Budget Review Committee granting the lithium-ion battery manufacturer another 18 months to create 279 jobs.
The extension comes after the company missed that job target at year-end 2017.
Wendland told the committee Saft exceeded 279 employees in 2015 but had trouble maintaining those jobs.
Wendland stated that Saft lost a couple of contracts during the 2016 period and had a reduction in headcount, and is working on building back that workforce.
Saft America, a subsidiary of Paris-based Saft Groupe S.A., opened a 235,000-square-foot building in September 2011 on 12 acres at 13575 Waterworks St. in Cecil Commerce Center.
The agreement violation could have cost the company $326,164, according to a letter Wendland sent Feb. 23 to Saft Financial Controller Mohamed Tejan.
Those incentive funds were negotiated by the Jacksonville Economic Development Commission, which the city split into the OED and the Downtown Investment Authority in 2012.

According to the economic development agreement, the company was awarded a Countywide Economic Development Fund Site Work Grant worth up to $1 million as part of a $5.3 million city-backed taxpayer incentives package.
The company also received $14.9 million in state-backed incentives and up to $95.5 million in federal stimulus funds from the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act for building its facility.

The city’s package included a $3.3 million Recaptured Enhanced Value Grant, a 20 percent share of the $2.37 million Qualified Target Industry Tax Refund grant of $474,300, and the Countywide Economic Development Fund Site Work Grant worth up to $1 million.
The city also provided a $550,000 Public Service Tax Exemption.

The city agreed to pay Saft $700,000 of the site work grant upfront to help cover its initial build-out of the facility in anticipation of hitting job numbers by the deadlines outlined in the agreement.

Saft promised to invest more than $122 million in the facility to build lithium-ion batteries for wind- and solar-based power generation plants and hire 279 employees at a full-time average salary of $44,807.

Wendland said the amendment gives the company until year-end 2019 to create the jobs, allows OED to measure the average number of jobs between 2018 and 2019 against the 279-job requirement, and reduces this year’s REV grant incentive by $25,000 as a financial penalty for the extension.
City Council and Mayor Lenny Curry need to approve the changes before they take effect.
source_title: Saft America Inc. may get more time to create required jobs | Jax Daily Record

facts: In August 2009, Saft was awarded a $95 million grant from the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act.
The funding is designated for a high-volume manufacturing plant to build advanced lithium-ion cells and batteries.
The manufactured batteries will serve military hybrid vehicles, aviation, smart grid support, broadband backup power, and energy storage for renewable energy applications.
Alternative energy is a designated high-impact sector in Florida.
The grant provided an opportunity to leverage federal funding with state and local resources to encourage growth and diversification of Florida’s alternative energy sector.
Florida and Jacksonville officials worked with Saft long before the DOE grant was awarded and faced significant competition for the project.
State and local partners secured the project by creating a package containing cash grants, tax incentives (credits, abatements, refunds and exemptions), and workforce training grants.
Company officials identified Jacksonville's transportation infrastructure and workforce as well-suited for a major battery manufacturing plant.
The facility is located at the Cecil Commerce Center, continuing the redevelopment of Cecil Field, a former Naval Air Station.
The total estimated cost of the project is around $200 million.
The project will bring roughly 279 jobs to the Jacksonville area over a six-year period.
Construction was scheduled to be completed before the end of 2010.
Governor Charlie Crist stated that Florida is stepping onto the world stage as a major marketplace for advanced energy technologies.
Governor Charlie Crist noted that the state-of-the-art manufacturing facility ensures competitive battery solutions will be imminently available to U.S. and world markets from Jacksonville.
source_title: Saft America Inc. - Jacksonville, Florida | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Seohan Group announced plans to build a $72 million factory that will produce axles, half shafts, and brake systems for Hyundai Motor Group.
The South Korean company announced Tuesday it would build the plant near Midway, south of Savannah, with plans to hire at least 180 new employees.

Seohan Auto Georgia is the seventh major supplier to locate in the region following Hyundai's May announcement to build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia.
Hyundai's Ellabell site could grow to employ 8,100 workers and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Seohan and six other suppliers have since pledged to invest nearly $2 billion and hire more than 4,600 people.
Seoul-based Seohan already supplies a Hyundai assembly plant in Montgomery, Alabama and a Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Seohan currently makes axles and driveshafts at two plants in Auburn, Alabama that opened in 2008 and 2014.
The company said last year it would invest $13.5 million in the Auburn plants, which jointly employ more than 200 people, to make parts for electric vehicles.
Seohan stated it needs additional capacity to make parts as automakers convert to electric propulsion.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corp. CEO Jung Kee Koo said sustained growth of the EV market over the past few years suggests accelerated changes to the automotive market is inevitable.
Jung Kee Koo stated, "We believe Georgia will be the center of the EV industry, and will be a new frontier for Seohan’s future with limitless opportunities and potential."
The new plant will be located in a business park off Interstate 95 near Midway in Liberty County.
The company plans to begin production at the new Georgia facility in late 2024.
Seohan could qualify for $2.7 million in state income tax credits, calculated at $3,000 per job over five years.
To qualify for the tax credits, workers must make at least $31,300 a year.
The state will also pay to train workers for the new Georgia plant.

Selma Mayor James Perkins Jr. was in office when he helped the EDA successfully bring Seoyon E HWA to Selma in 2005.

facts: SKF USA has announced executive appointments effective Jan. 1, 2017.
George Garner was appointed director of OES truck sales for the SKF Vehicle Service Market (VSM), North America Business Unit.
Ray Bihn will assume Garner’s current position as vice president of automotive sales for the SKF Vehicle Service Market (VSM), North America Business Unit.
In his new role, Garner will be responsible for all heavy-duty OES sales, including Navistar, Daimler, Paccar and Volvo.
Garner will work closely with the SKF OEM sales team to bring the latest product solutions to the heavy-duty OES customers.
Garner will report to Scott Simmonds, vice president of heavy-duty sales.
Garner will be based out of Portland, Oregon.

As vice president of automotive sales, Bihn will be responsible for developing and implementing sales strategies for SKF VSM in the United States and Canada, with a focus on the automotive aftermarket.
Bihn will report to Tom O’Brien, VSM, North America Business Unit.
Bihn will be based out of the SKF office in Elgin, Illinois.

Tom O’Brien stated that both George and Ray have solid backgrounds at SKF as well as in the automotive and heavy-duty markets, respectively, and expressed that the company looks forward to the excellent experience and perspective they’ll bring to their new roles.
Tom O’Brien said the company is pleased to have their continued support at SKF.

The Irapuato site will serve a variety of automotive and non-automotive OEMs and customers.
Initial staffing plans for the Mexico facility call for 220 direct employees, with potential for an additional 220 indirect employees.
Construction of the Mexico facility is part of SRG Global's business strategy to maintain local and regional production capabilities.
SRG Global's other recent investments include its Advanced Development Center in Taylor, MI (opened 2010) and a production facility in Boleslawiec, Poland (opened 2009).
In the automotive sector, SRG supplies front and rear end components including grilles, emblems/nameplates, liftgates, deck lids, and spoilers.
SRG supplies body side parts such as rocker panels, body side moldings, fuel filler caps, wheel flares, door handles, and wheel covers/caps.
SRG supplies interior components including pillars, window surround moldings, cowls, door belt moldings, door panel parts, center consoles, instrument panels, and steering wheels.
The current company was formed by Guardian's acquisition of Siegel-Robert Automotive in 2008.
SRG Global was created in 2009 by combining the trim operations of both companies, with headquarters in Warren, MI.
Kevin Baird serves as president and CEO of SRG Global.
Juan Manuel Oliva is the governor of Guanajuato.
source_title: SRG to open new Mexican molding site

facts: SUHNER Components provides a wide range of precisely manufactured metal parts for industrial applications.
The company specializes in high-quality milled parts, stamped parts, bent parts, and turned parts made from stainless steel, steel, aluminum, and plastic.

SUHNER's Advanced Manufacturing capabilities cover stamped parts from progressive, transfer and 3D transfer technology, flexible drive shafts, spiral bevel gears, components for electric motors, and prototyping.
Mission-critical metal components are applied across mechanical and apparatus engineering, power plants, aerospace, medical technology, and automobiles.
Automotive applications specifically include transmission parts that are permanently exposed to high loads.
Manufacturing these components requires specialized knowledge, advanced materials, and technologies such as CNC technology with 3D metal printing.
The production process begins with a needs analysis to understand specific requirements for mission-critical metal components.
Process optimization focuses on jointly developing and optimizing processes to increase efficiency and create future-proof solutions.
Material selection involves choosing optimal materials based on requirements and application, including stainless steel, steel, aluminum, and plastic.
Manufacturing processes include milling, punching, bending, or turning, utilizing appropriate methods for maximum efficiency and quality.
Finishing steps ensure precision through careful polishing and coating.
SUHNER Components offers an online store for ordering metal parts with a simple ordering process, various payment options, and fast shipping.
The company provides dedicated customer support to assist clients throughout all project phases.

facts: T1 Energy Inc. provides energy solutions for solar modules and cells in the United States, Norway and internationally.
The company manufactures and sells photovoltaic solar modules.
T1 Energy Inc. was formerly known as FREYR Battery, Inc. and changed its name to T1 Energy Inc. in February 2025.
T1 Energy Inc. is headquartered in Austin, Texas.
In 2025, T1 Energy's revenue was $755.30 million, an increase of 25572.84% compared to the previous year's $2.94 million.
Losses were -$380.79 million, which is -15.43% less than in 2024.
T1 Energy received an "A" Grade in Bankability Assessment from Intertek CEA for its G1_Dallas facility.
The Intertek CEA assessment places the G1_Dallas facility among the world's highest-rated solar module manufacturing operations.
Northland initiated coverage of T1 Energy with an Outperform rating and $16 price target.

On June 03, 2026, T1 Energy announced a definitive agreement to acquire Kore Power for $32M.
Kore Power is an established engineering-focused BESS and software solutions provider supporting industrial hy...
Through the acquisition of KORE Power, T1 Energy entered the BESS and Data Center Infrastructure Markets.
Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA and gross margin reflect improved contract mix and operational efficiency.
G2_Austin construction is on track, with comprehensive financing targeted for Q2.

facts: The transportation sector is the largest cause of carbon emissions in Georgia, the Southeast, and nationwide.

Electrifying transportation can realize immediate and deep cuts in carbon emissions that can be deepened by decarbonizing the electricity grid and increasing access to alternatives to personal vehicle-centric mobility, such as public transportation and infrastructure for bicycles and pedestrians.
Electric cars, trucks, and buses do not have tailpipes and do not produce carbon emissions.
Electric vehicles (EVs) are not zero-emission because most electricity powering EVs currently comes from fossil fuels.
Even when accounting for the carbon emissions from those fossil fuel-powered plants, driving an EV in Georgia produces approximately 2.5 times fewer carbon emissions than driving a gas vehicle.
Georgia’s solar market continues to boom and currently ranks #9 nationally.
Drawdown Georgia has determined that the Peach State could eliminate 1 million metric tons of carbon emissions by replacing 250,000 gas passenger vehicles with EVs.
The 250,000 vehicles represent less than 4% of vehicles registered in the state.
There are approximately 6.6 million registered passenger vehicles in Georgia.
Electrifying all registered passenger vehicles presents a total opportunity of approximately 26 million metric tons of emissions reduction, equal to reducing the state’s total emissions by over 20%.
In 2019, the transportation sector accounted for 8.7% of U.S. gross domestic product.
Ford, General Motors, Honda, Hyundai, and Volkswagen have all committed to the electrification of 100% new vehicles between 2030 and 2040.
These five automakers alone have committed over $800 billion to get it done.

Southern Alliance for Clean Energy (SACE) and Atlas Public Policy publish an annual “Transportation Electrification in the Southeast” report tracking the regional EV market across a range of indicators.
The latest SACE and Atlas Public Policy report shows remarkable growth across all six indicators through July 2021.
Georgia is a regional EV manufacturing and supply chain powerhouse, second only to Tennessee in manufacturing investment and jobs.
Georgia also ranks second in the region in EV sales and charging station deployment, trailing only Florida.

Strong EV sales and charger deployments point to a growing EV market, which is vital for Georgia to sustain if it hopes to continue to win EV manufacturing and supply chain investments as manufacturers prepare to locate in the regions where their products are selling.
Governor Kemp created the Electric Mobility Innovation Alliance to bring together industry stakeholders.
The alliance aims to identify the policies and investments needed to grow the electric mobility ecosystem and strengthen Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
Georgia currently imports 100% of the gas and diesel that fuels transportation.
SACE’s report shows when Georgia consumers fill up at the pump, two-thirds of every dollar spent leaks out of the state’s economy.
If all the cars, trucks, and buses in Georgia were electric today, two-thirds of the money consumers and fleet managers would spend on electricity would stay in the local economy.
The result would be a $7.9 billion annual boost to the state’s economy.
The more Georgia’s utilities invest in local renewable energy like solar and battery storage, the bigger the economic boost from electrification will be.
EVs get increasingly cleaner over their lifetime as the electricity grid moves away from coal and fossil gas generation.
Shifting to electric transportation makes electricity generation more locally sourced, keeping more consumer dollars recirculating in Georgia.

Maximum climate and economic benefits result from pairing transportation electrification with efforts to reduce the number of personal vehicles on the road through investments in mass transit and community walk- and bike-ability.
The “reduce, clean, electrify” strategy engages numerous sectors to multiply economic development, job growth, and carbon reduction opportunities.
Georgia is well-positioned to leverage climate solutions to spur economic growth, create jobs, and save consumers money while significantly reducing carbon and other greenhouse gas emissions.
source_title: Electrifying Transportation Moves the Needle on Climate in Georgia

facts: NeoVolta provided an update on its battery manufacturing facility being built in Georgia.
Site acceptance testing is targeted for completion by the end of August 2026.
Production on BESS for residential, commercial and utility-scale applications will begin in Q3.
The effort in Pendergrass, Georgia, is a joint-venture of NeoVolta, LONGi and PotisEdge.
The companies announced the 2-GWh factory earlier this year.
NeoVolta has received a foreign entity of concern (FEOC) compliance opinion from a national law firm.
The FEOC compliance opinion confirms that the Georgia battery manufacturing facility is structured to meet applicable FEOC/PFE eligibility requirements.
Ardes Johnson, CEO of NeoVolta, stated that receiving a FEOC compliance opinion from a top-tier law firm is a landmark milestone for NeoVolta and for NeoVolta Power.
The opinion formally validates that the Pendergrass facility meets the compliance standard the utility-scale market demands.
The opinion removes a key diligence hurdle for customers currently evaluating NeoVolta Power for initial procurement opportunities.
The opinion ultimately positions NeoVolta to compete for the most high-value projects in the U.S. energy storage market.
source_title: NeoVolta BESS manufacturing plant should open in August in Georgia

facts: Taiwanese electric vehicle company Ahamani EV Technology Co., Ltd. has announced plans to enter India’s electric vehicle market by establishing a large-scale battery manufacturing plant.

The strategic investment aims to capitalize on India’s growing demand for electric mobility, which is bolstered by supportive government policies and an increasing consumer shift towards eco-friendly transportation options.
Ahamani EV Technology is actively seeking technology transfer partnerships with leading Indian EV companies.
The company is in advanced discussions with 3-4 major players in the Indian market.
These collaborations are intended to foster knowledge sharing and resource pooling, accelerating the development of advanced EV technologies tailored to the unique needs of the Indian market.
Ahamani has over two decades of experience in EV innovation.
The company specializes in battery technology, motor systems, and smart fleet management.
Ahamani’s renowned UrDa AIOT Mobility System effectively manages electric two-wheeler fleets.
By building a local manufacturing facility, Ahamani aims to enhance India’s self-reliance in EV production, generate employment opportunities, and contribute to the local economy.
Tzu Chi Kung is the Founder & CEO of Ahamani.
Tzu Chi Kung stated that the company has been studying the Indian EV market for the past five years and sees great potential.
Tzu Chi Kung noted that Ahamani’s strong R&D capabilities and successful track record make them ready to work with Indian partners and promote electric vehicles in the country.
Ahamani’s initiatives in battery manufacturing and technology partnerships are poised to play a significant role in advancing India’s journey towards sustainable electric mobility.
source_title: Taiwan's Ahamani EV to Setup Battery Manufacturing Plant in India

facts: Ogeechee Technical College President Lori Durden views the Georgia Industrial Systems and Industrial Robotics Training Center as a gateway to advancement and catalyst for community growth rather than just another campus addition.
The facility's groundbreaking ceremony took place Thursday at the corner of A.J. Riggs Road and Highway 301 South.
Approximately 200 people attended the groundbreaking event.
Event attendees included Technical College System of Georgia Commissioner Greg Dozier, Speaker of the Georgia House Jon Burns, State Sen. Billy Hickman, and Rep. Lehman Franklin.
Ogeechee Technical College began offering industrial robotics training in 2019.
Bulloch County Manager Tom Couch and Jan Moore, the college’s vice president for Economic Development, first outlined the vision for a full-scale robotics training center at Ogeechee Tech.
Governor Brian Kemp approved $23 million, included by Commissioner Dozier in the system’s 2023 budget, to construct the 37,000-square-foot Robotics Training Center in Bulloch County.
The center will provide training to employees of regional manufacturers and companies utilizing automation in supply and warehousing facilities.

Bobby Jones, chairman of the Ogeechee Tech Board of Directors, stated the center will house cutting-edge technology and sophisticated programs to equip individuals with needed skills, creating economic growth and workforce readiness.
In fiscal year 2023, Ogeechee Tech delivered more than 240,000 hours of industrial systems and robotics training.
Once open, the Robotics Center will more than double the college's training capacity and significantly expand the depth of training available.

Georgia Southern University Center for Business Analytics and Economic Research projects the center will yield an economic output of just over $9 million annually within a year of opening, growing to over $15 million within a few years.
Demand for robotics training stems largely from Hyundai's 2,541-acre Bryan County Metaplant, which broke ground in October 2022 to manufacture electric vehicles.
The Hyundai Metaplant is currently under construction just south of I-16 East near Blitchton and has rolled off its first production vehicle.
The Hyundai Metaplant complex is projected to eventually employ 8,500 people on-site.
More than a dozen manufacturing plants being built in southeast Georgia are directly related to the Hyundai Metaplant, including three in Bulloch County.
Ajin USA, a supplier of metal auto body parts to Hyundai, opened its $317 million plant in July at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park next to I-16.
Hanon Systems, a maker of automotive thermal and energy management equipment including air-conditioning components, is building a plant in Bulloch County as a supplier to the Metaplant.
Ecoplastic America Corporation will manufacture injection-molded plastic automotive body parts for Hyundai Motor Group vehicles and is also building a plant in Bulloch County.
The factories for Hanon Systems and Ecoplastic America are expected to come online soon.
The training center has established a partnership between Ogeechee Tech and Southeastern Technical College in Vidalia.
The center will house 12 industrial labs, classrooms, computer labs, and meeting spaces designed for innovative, state-of-the-art programs preparing trainees for current and future workforce demands.

State Sen. Billy Hickman stated the center will serve nine counties, ensure regional manufacturers have access to state-of-the-art training, inspire young students, generate higher-paying skilled jobs, and attract future manufacturers by assuring workforce needs will be met.
source_title: Ogeechee Technical College breaks ground on Robotics Training Center - Statesboro Herald

facts: Two rival South Korean industrial giants were locked in a bitter U.S. trade dispute heading toward a Sunday deadline.
Georgia officials grew increasingly worried a $2.6 billion SK Innovation project in Commerce that could employ 2,600 people would fall victim to the feud.
Most Georgia politicians joined calls urging President Joe Biden to veto a trade commission decision that was in favor of the company’s adversary, LG Energy Solution.
The trade commission decision favored LG Energy Solution, which would have torpedoed the project.
The SK Innovation project is one of the largest economic development deals in state history.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff took a different approach to resolving the dispute.
The manufacturer’s top executive credited Ossoff's approach with helping to end the rift.
Biden announced $2.3 billion in tax credits for critical minerals processing facilities.
Volkswagen is involved in the deal.
The announcement occurred in November 2022.
Atlanta was a location for meetings regarding the deal.
The dispute took two years to resolve.
LG Energy Solution and SK Innovation are the rival South Korean companies.
Commerce, Georgia is the site location for the battery plant.
The project could employ 2,600 people.
The project value is $2.6 billion.
President Joe Biden was urged to veto a trade commission decision.
Critical minerals processing facilities are part of the deal.
Battery components and processed minerals are involved.
source_title: How bitter Korean rivals settled a rift that threatened Georgia battery plant

facts: Hyundai has broken ground on a $5 billion electric car factory near Savannah, Georgia.
Hyundai is partnering with SK On and LG Energy Solution to build three new battery factories in Georgia.
The three new battery factories will have a total annual capacity of 90 GWh.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are considering building two joint venture battery plants in Georgia near the new Hyundai factory, each with an annual capacity of 35 GWh.
The new factories aim to help Hyundai and Kia qualify for the US federal electric vehicle tax credit under the Inflation Reduction Act.
The federal EV tax credit program requires that 40% of battery materials and 50% of battery components be sourced within the United States or from countries with a free trade agreement with the USA.
Under the federal EV tax credit program, cars must be finally assembled in the US to qualify.
The sourcing percentage requirements for the federal EV tax credit will rise over the next 4 years to 80% by 2027.
LG Energy Solution is the principal battery supplier for General Motors.
Hyundai and SK On plan to build a $2 billion battery cell factory in Georgia near the new Hyundai factory.
The start of production for the Hyundai & SK On factory is planned for the first quarter of 2026.
The Hyundai & SK On factory will have an annual capacity of 20 GWh.
SK On will produce pouch-type high-nickel batteries for Hyundai’s new factory near Savannah, its existing factory in Montgomery, Alabama, and a Kia factory in West Point, Georgia.
SK On already operates two battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia and has secured land for two more factories within the state.
Hyundai and Kia have been using SK On’s pouch cells for most of their EVs, including the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 6.
SK On pouch cells will power the IONIQ 7 SUV expected to be released next year.
SK On pouch cells will power the Genesis GV70 EV, which will be produced in the Alabama plant.
The Kia EV6 uses pouch cell batteries from SK On.
The Kia EV9 SUV was introduced in April of 2023 and also uses pouch cell batteries.
Much of the money made available by the Biden administration to support the electric car market will be spent in states like Georgia, Alabama, and South Carolina.

Timothy Fox, an analyst at ClearView Energy Partners, told Bloomberg Green that domestic manufacturing in traditional Republican districts may soften partisan splits on renewables as an implicit long-term strategy for Democrats.
President Biden hopes the IRA and other infrastructure-related laws will form the basis of an industrial policy that revitalizes cities across the country while fighting climate change.
source_title: Hyundai Plans 3 Battery Factories With Annual Capacity Of 90 GWh In Georgia – Candela Green Energy

facts: LaGrange and Troup County sit at the northern end of a 45-mile corridor anticipated to welcome up to 60,000 new residents, along with a wave of new industries and retail stores.
Elected officials asked constituents to raise taxes on themselves to meet anticipated infrastructure and service demands during a recession.
In the mid-2000s, local leaders commissioned Georgia Tech to produce a plan for enhancing services and infrastructure to sustain managed growth into the 21st century.
The completed Georgia Tech plan was presented in summer 2009 and contains 10 goals and 50 strategies.

Kay Durand serves as executive director of the Troup County Center for Strategic Planning (TCCSP), a nonprofit created to coordinate stakeholders across government, healthcare, education, commerce, and development authorities.
The TCCSP board has approximately 12 members who meet monthly to coordinate efforts per the Georgia Tech study goals.

Troup Works is an online program connecting the unemployed with job opportunities throughout the community and is growing monthly, according to Ricky Wolfe, chairman of the Troup County Board of Commissioners.
Funding for the TCCSP project comes from its member organizations.

In 2005, the Pentagon announced the expansion of Columbus's U.S. Army Post Fort Benning mission, bringing 30,000 troops, contractors, vendors, and families to the region (a 40-minute drive down I-185 from Troup County).
In 2006, Kia announced a $1-billion manufacturing plant in West Point, Troup County, promising 30,000 jobs connected to vehicle production, suppliers, retailers, and attendant businesses.
Jeff Lukken, mayor of LaGrange, noted that developers from Florida, Chicago, Maryland, the D.C. area, and across the Eastern U.S. arrived within an 18-month period around 2004.
Local officials approved zoning permits for 11,500 new housing units at a time when LaGrange only had 11,200 houses.

Developers cited LaGrange's location on the shores of West Point Lake, its position as one of only nine metro areas with an intersection of I-85 and I-185, and having more Fortune 500 companies per capita than any other city in the country.
LaGrange and Troup County sit almost halfway between Atlanta and Columbus, near Macon, Birmingham, and Montgomery, which are among the region's five fastest-growing cities.

Everything within Troup County lines is designated as a Tax Allocation District (TAD), which can cut months or years off project launches and ease financing by directing retail sales tax dollars to public coffers.
In July 2010, Troup County voters approved the TAD issue.
A developer secured TAD amenities for a 370-acre site along exits 13 and 14 on I-85 for a mixed-use development named LaGrange Station.

LaGrange Station plans include 1.2 million square feet of retail space predicted to generate $400 million in annual sales when fully built out, potentially opening up to 1,200 new jobs and 550 part-time jobs.

The TAD will use about $15 million in initial funds for road networks, infrastructure, and parks; a developer will float a bond paid off by increased property taxes over approximately 15 years, after which local governments retain all property taxes.

Page Estes, president of the LaGrange-Troup County Chamber of Commerce, stated that residents were leaving the county to shop in Newnan, Columbus, and Auburn/Opelika, prompting chamber interest in the TAD to keep money local.
After Kia's plant opened in 2009, focus groups with Kia executives revealed that new employees struggled to sell their previous homes to take jobs in Troup County.
The rental market in West Central Georgia and East Alabama has been strong as commuters rent locally while maintaining mortgages on their former homes.
Although Kia came online last year and Fort Benning hiring is growing, neither site has yet reached its full hiring stride.
County unemployment remained at 11.5 percent in December 2010, above the statewide figure of 10.2 percent.
Ricky Wolfe attributed the high unemployment rate to the loss of traditional jobs that Kia could not absorb, crediting Kia with preventing staggering unemployment.
High-tech workforce skill requirements complicate employment efforts, and steady losses in the local textile industry have left many unskilled workers needing higher training levels.
Wes Cochran reported there are 70-some-odd Andy Warhol works in LaGrange, claiming more per capita than any town in the country.
The Callaway Foundation, founded by LaGrange native Fuller Callaway in 1943, is a $200-plus million nonprofit and key partner in downtown development.

H. Speer Burdette III, president and general manager of the Callaway Foundation, stated the foundation invests approximately $9 million to $10 million annually and has spent over $25 million on downtown infrastructure and promotion.
In 2000, the Callaway Foundation commissioned a study that led to the development of a 10-screen Carmike Theater downtown with a cupola resembling the old courthouse.

The foundation purchased an old downtown department store, spent $5 million renovating it for a health club, restaurant, and gift shop, and transferred it to the Downtown Development Authority to generate rent revenue.
Plans are underway to introduce loft apartments or condos in the downtown area.
Top employers in Troup County include the Troup County School System, Kia Motors, West Georgia Health Systems, InterfaceFLOR, and the Walmart Distribution Center.
Population data from 2008 shows Troup County at 64,233, LaGrange at 28,401, Hogansville at 2,912, and West Point at 2,644.
Per capita income in 2007 was $28,320 for the county compared to $33,499 statewide.
source_title: LaGrange/Troup County: Good Fortune - Georgia Trend Magazine

The Kia Georgia plant in nearby West Point anchors an automotive supplier network that runs through the region.
The I-85 corridor brings consistent industrial investment.
The Kia Georgia plant in West Point made Troup County a serious manufacturing location.
The supplier network created by Kia Georgia runs through LaGrange.
Automotive supplier facilities operate under OEM quality audit requirements.

Industrial floor scrubbing programs use ride-on and walk-behind floor scrubbers for large-format concrete manufacturing floors in Troup County to prevent buildup, extend floor life, and maintain safety in high-traffic production areas.
Post-construction and pre-occupancy cleaning is available for new manufacturing facilities and industrial buildings in LaGrange before equipment installation or production startup.
Troup County continues to attract new industrial investment along the I-85 corridor.
LaGrange is approximately 65 miles southwest of Alpharetta on I-85, sitting at the heart of West Georgia's industrial corridor.
The Kia Georgia plant in West Point assembles the Telluride, Sportage, and EV6 for North American markets.
Automotive supplier facilities in Troup County require cleaning programs with quality documentation, shift coordination, and industrial floor capability to meet OEM standards.
Manufacturing facilities in Troup County run multiple shifts, requiring cleaning windows built around production schedules and coordinated with plant supervisors before service begins.
LaGrange is on the direct I-85 run through West Georgia, allowing for established crews and locally recruited staff placed on Troup County industrial accounts for consistent nightly coverage.
The MillenniumOS platform generates GPS-verified completion records and digital inspection logs that support facility cleanliness documentation during supplier audits and quality reviews.

Quotes are provided after a walkthrough.

Cleaning is scheduled around production windows, shift changes, and planned maintenance shutdowns.

The primary contact for manufacturing accounts is typically a plant manager or facilities director, with account supervisors interfacing directly rather than routing communication through a call center.
A minimum monthly billing of $2,500 to $3,000 is looked for for standalone manufacturing accounts in LaGrange, though most Troup County industrial facilities exceed that threshold easily.

Millennium offers free facility assessments that include walking the building, building a custom scope, and identifying currently missing services.
